# Research Report: GIS-Based Strategic Tree Trimming for Storm Damage Reduction

## Solution Overview

The City of Miami has developed a systematic approach to tree trimming in public rights-of-way that integrates Geographic Information Systems technology to reduce storm damage.[^3] This initiative represents a protocol-based action within Miami's climate resilience framework, specifically targeting the vulnerability of urban tree canopy to severe weather events.[^2] The approach combines spatial data mapping with strategic vegetation management to minimize infrastructure risks during tropical storms and hurricanes.[^1]

## Technical Components

The system utilizes Geographic Information Systems data to map the city's assets and vulnerabilities, enabling identification of areas most at risk from climate change impacts.[^1] This spatial analysis platform supports the Urban Forestry Management Program's comprehensive tree inventory capabilities, which document all trees located on public property and public rights-of-way.[^4] The technical implementation relies on GIS mapping to inform systematic tree trimming protocols throughout the municipal right-of-way network.[^3] The Urban Forestry team operates with International Society of Arboriculture Certified Arborists who conduct tree risk assessments, tree pruning operations, and maintain detailed spatial inventories of vegetation assets.[^4] These technical capabilities enable data-driven decision-making for tree maintenance activities including pruning schedules, removal priorities, root pruning interventions, and emergency response protocols.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The systematic tree trimming plan was designated as a Phase 1 action within the Miami Forever Climate Ready strategy, scheduled for implementation during Years 0-1 of the initiative.[^3] The Department of Resilience and Public Works leads this protocol-based action in partnership with Florida Power and Light.[^3] The Urban Forestry Management Program, managed by the Department of Resilience and Public Works, comprises a team of ISA Certified Arborists, tree maintenance crews, and administrative staff responsible for executing the systematic trimming plan.[^4] This organizational structure maintains responsibility for the care and maintenance of all trees located on public property and public rights-of-way across the city.[^4] The implementation integrates with broader municipal objectives to create an integrated approach addressing current and future risks from storms, high tides, ground water, and sea level rise while conserving fresh water and protecting Biscayne Bay's health.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

The systematic approach to tree trimming specifically targets reduction of storm damage across Miami's public right-of-way network.[^3] This protocol-based intervention addresses documented vulnerabilities from severe weather events, exemplified by historical impacts such as Hurricane Andrew in August 1992, which struck south Florida with sustained winds of 145 mph and gusts up to 175 mph, causing an estimated $25 billion in damages.[^5] That category 4 hurricane affected an estimated 1.2 million households, with many losing their landscape trees entirely.[^5] The GIS-enabled trimming program enhances the Urban Forestry team's capacity to conduct proactive tree risk assessments and implement preventive pruning operations before storm events.[^4] The systematic approach supports emergency response capabilities by reducing the likelihood of tree-related infrastructure damage during severe weather.[^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This initiative directly addresses Miami's exposure to tropical storms and hurricanes by reducing tree-related infrastructure damage during extreme weather events.[^3] The approach aligns with municipal objectives to reduce current and future risks from storms while managing the compounding effects of high tides, ground water intrusion, and sea level rise.[^2] By utilizing spatial data to identify areas most vulnerable to climate change impacts, the systematic trimming protocol enables targeted interventions in locations facing heightened storm risk.[^1]

## Business Analysis

The partnership structure between the Department of Resilience and Public Works and Florida Power and Light demonstrates a collaborative model for managing vegetation-related infrastructure risks.[^3] This public-private partnership leverages the utility company's interest in maintaining electrical grid reliability alongside municipal objectives for storm damage reduction. The Urban Forestry Management Program's organizational structure, comprising ISA Certified Arborists, maintenance crews, and administrative staff, represents the operational framework supporting ongoing implementation.[^4] The Phase 1 designation within Miami Forever Climate Ready indicates prioritization of this protocol within the city's near-term climate adaptation investment strategy.[^3]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.miami.gov/files/sharedassets/public/v/1/miami-forever-climate-ready-2020-strategy.pdf
- [^2]: https://www.miami.gov/My-Government/Climate-Change-in-the-City-of-Miami/Climate-Change-Action/MiamiForeverClimateReady/Goal4
- [^3]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Miami-US/miami-forever-climate-ready-2020-strategy.pdf

- [^5]: https://www.miamidade.gov/environment/library/reports/trees-damage-assess.pdf

# Research Report: LiDAR and Bathymetric UAV Surveying for Flood Basin Modeling

## Solution Overview

The Tanzania Urban Resilience Program deployed an innovative combination of Light Detection and Range (LiDAR) UAV surveying and bathymetric surveying to support flood modelling within the flood-prone Msimbazi river basin.[^1] This World Bank funded survey project served two purposes: generation of reliable, accurate and fit-for-purpose bathymetric and topographic data of the Msimbazi River and its floodplains for riverine flooding related detailed studies, and rigorous comparison of Digital Terrain Model (DTM) generation by UAV photogrammetry and UAV LiDAR.[^2] The outputs are being used to guide river upgrades that will improve Dar es Salaam's resilience to flooding.[^1]

## Technical Components

The implementation utilized both a photo camera and laser scanner mounted to the same drone, with data obtained through identical flights for both methods.[^4] The LiDAR scanner used was a Velodyne HDL 32e with 16 out of 32 lasers used to construct the point cloud, providing 2 returns per measurement.[^4] The study demonstrated that both UAV photogrammetry and UAV LiDAR are capable of producing high-resolution and high-accuracy DTMs for flood modelling purposes.[^3] The generation of a high-resolution DTM served as a key input for the flood modeling and engineering designs under the Dar es Salaam Metropolitan Development Project.[^3] The UAV LiDAR data was able to penetrate the water surface to some extent, which provided a more accurate representation of the river channel for the Msimbazi River.[^3] In collaboration with Deltares and with funding from GIZ, OMDTZ collected accurate and precise river cross-section surveys using an innovative, low-cost technique developed by the Resilience Academy and OMDTZ.[^5] The organization deployed two units that use the real-time kinematic (RTK) mode of the new, low-cost hardware that are able to provide centimeter precise surveys.[^5] TU Delft student Kirsten van Dongen co-developed a method with OMDTZ to use the hardware, and demonstrated that it could deliver similar accuracy and precision as professional-grade survey equipment.[^5]

## Implementation Details

CDR International in association with Shore Monitoring and Research defined an integral full survey campaign for the lower basin of the Msimbazi River, which was executed in February 2019.[^2] The project area was the lower basin of the Msimbazi River valley at the outfall into the Indian Ocean, where two tributaries (The Sinza River and the Kibangu River) merge with the Msimbazi River.[^4] The survey was conducted for The World Bank and financed by UK aid from the British people, with CDR International BV and Shore Monitoring and Research BV as contractors.[^4] The performance to measure ground level elevation with UAV photogrammetry and UAV LiDAR in a Sub-Saharan African context was investigated for fourteen different types of vegetated areas and three types of urbanised areas in the Msimbazi Basin.[^4] The OMDTZ survey of river cross-sections and hydraulic structures along the Msimbazi River supported the creation of a comprehensive hydraulic model of the area that led to the development of a more realistic flood hazard map of the most vulnerable areas.[^5] After refining their methods and safety precautions, OMDTZ was able to scale up and rapidly collect over 80 cross-sections of the river.[^5] Preliminary insights of the analysis were shared and presented at a master class at the Land and Poverty Conference 2019 in Washington, DC on March 25-29, 2019, and at a workshop on DTM drone applications for Dar es Salaam stakeholders at World Bank Office in Dar es Salaam on April 11th, 2019.[^2] The UAV LiDAR survey conducted for this study was more expensive at the date of the report than UAV photogrammetry.[^4] Compared with a UAV photogrammetry survey, a UAV LiDAR survey costs about 2 to 3 times more.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

The comparative analysis demonstrated that urbanized areas render approximately the same results for both UAV photogrammetry and UAV LiDAR techniques.[^4] UAV photogrammetry is a cost-effective and fast method for generating DTMs in areas with sparse vegetation, though it is not suitable for areas with dense vegetation, as the photogrammetric process cannot penetrate the vegetation to capture the ground surface.[^3] For tall reed and grass areas, photogrammetry errors are in the order of 1m, covering more than 30% of the vegetated area of the basin.[^4] For areas with trees and mangroves, photogrammetry leads to mean errors of approximately 3 m, with maximum errors in DTMs between 5 to 10 m, covering approximately 33% of the vegetated area of the basin.[^4] Based on the results of this comparative analysis it is considered sufficiently accurate to conduct recurrent surveys for aforementioned purpose with UAV Photogrammetry.[^4][^7] LiDAR UAV is considered a more suitable method for DTM generation for flood modelling purposes in Msimbazi type of basins with relatively large coverage of vegetation with variation in type of vegetation, density and heights.[^4] The product of this assignment is the development of a wider flood model that will assist in providing the evidentiary basis for investments in the basin, complementary to the ongoing Tanzania Urban Resilience Program (TURP), implemented by the World Bank.[^5]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The Msimbazi River, which flows through the heart of Dar es Salaam, is particularly prone to flooding, causing widespread damage to infrastructure, property, and livelihoods, and posing a significant risk to human life.[^3] The Msimbazi Basin, home to an estimated 27 percent of Dar es Salaam's population, has historically been a fertile floodplain and the city's main water source.[^5] Flooding has become increasingly severe over the past decade in Msimbazi Basin, with major flood events experienced in seven out of the last 10 years.[^8] Over the past decade, the middle and lower part of the Msimbazi Basin has been experiencing frequent, severe flooding at least twice a year during every rainy season, and in 2019 alone, nine major flood events took place.[^8] The Msimbazi River is one of the major rivers in Dar es Salaam, originating from the Kisarawe highlands, passing through the city's industrial area and the densely populated area of Jangwani, and discharging into the Indian Ocean through the Selander Bridge.[^3]

## Business Analysis

The study will recommend on when to use which surveying methods with respect to purpose, project area size, landuse/vegetation cover and challenges like import of equipment.[^2] The comparative analysis between UAV photogrammetry and UAV LiDAR provides decision-making guidance for future flood modelling investments, with photogrammetry offering cost advantages in sparse vegetation contexts while LiDAR delivers superior accuracy in densely vegetated basins despite higher costs.[^4] The World Bank funding structure, supported by UK aid from the British people, demonstrates the viability of international development finance mechanisms for technology-enabled climate adaptation infrastructure.[^4] The successful deployment of low-cost RTK survey equipment by OMDTZ, validated to deliver similar accuracy as professional-grade systems, suggests potential for scaling cost-effective surveying capabilities across similar Sub-Saharan African contexts.[^5]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/video/2019/05/15/drones-helping-to-understand-flood-risk-in-tanzania
- [^2]: https://cdr-international.nl/lidar-drone-survey-msimbazi-river-dar-es-salaam/
- [^3]: https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/310991567574404522/pdf/Bathymetric-and-Topographic-Surveying-of-Flood-Prone-Rivers-in-Dar-Es-Salaam-Comparative-Case-of-UAV-Photogrammetry-and-UAV-LiDAR-for-Generation-of-Digital-Terrain-Models-for-Flood-Modelling-Purposes-Msimbazi-River-Dar-es-Salaam.pdf
- [^4]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Africa/Dar-es-Salaam-Tanzania/Bathymetric-and-Topographic-Surveying-of-Flood-Prone-Rivers-in-Dar-Es-Salaam-Comparative-Case-of-UAV-Photogrammetry-and-UAV-LiDAR-for-Generation-of-Digital-Terrain-Models-for-Flood-Modelling-Purposes-Msimbazi-River-Dar-es-Salaam.pdf
- [^5]: https://www.hotosm.org/updates/using-low-cost-equipment-to-gather-high-precision-data-for-flood-modeling/

- [^7]: https://iati.fcdo.gov.uk/iati_documents/5714921.odt

# Research Report: Visual Climate Adaptation Platform for Cyclone Risk Assessment

## Solution Overview

CSIRO Australia conducted a demonstration study of the Visual Climate Adaptation Platform (VCAP) in Da Nang, a GIS-based platform enabling users to assess impact, costs and benefits of adaptation options for housing or infrastructure subjected to natural hazards.[^4] The platform was developed based on CSIRO's MetroEngines visual climate adaptation decision-making support platform, with Da Nang serving as a demonstration study of the Geographic Information Systems mapping tools.[^1] This approach focused on assessing potential impacts and damage costs on residential housing subjected to tropical cyclones in two pilot study areas.[^2]

## Technical Components

The city of Da Nang provided historical damage data, which was used with spatial information such as topography, terrain and shielding from the built environment to establish vulnerability models as well as hazard extent and intensity showing which parts of the city would be weakest in a future cyclone event.[^1] The researchers established a vulnerability curve showing what percentage of buildings are likely to be damaged according to predicted wind speeds in Da Nang.[^1] The study used Da Nang Department of Construction's geographical information system database as the source for mapping the city and study wards.[^6] As a result of the demonstration study, levels of impact on houses, spatial damage hot spots, and estimated damage costs due to typhoons of any intensity in the study areas were identified.[^3] A GIS database and a web-portal were developed as part of the VCAP Demonstration Study for Da Nang, ready for further studies.[^3]

## Implementation Details

The pilot study was undertaken in collaboration with the Da Nang Climate Change Coordination Office from March to June 2016, assessing the potential impact and damage costs from tropical cyclones to residential housing in two wards of Da Nang, namely Tho Quang and Hoa Hai.[^3][^4] Dr Chi-Hsiang Wang worked with colleagues Dr Minh Nguyen and Mr Yong-Bing Khoo on assessing the predicted cost of cyclones in the Vietnamese city of Da Nang, one of the cities selected for the 100 Resilient Cities Rockefeller Foundation-funded project.[^1] The VCAP study for Tho Quang ward covered approximately 8,000 houses with a population of 17,000 across an area of 46.5 km2.[^3] Da Nang is now planning to scale up the pilot study to investigate the vulnerability of housing stock to tropical storms in all 56 wards.[^4] Additional funding is needed to support the collection of historical damage data and development of a cadastral GIS for the whole city.[^4] Following data collection and analysis, technical expertise will be needed to expand the functionality of this tool to encompass future projections by digitizing future development plans of Da Nang and modelling future climate change scenarios.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

This study provided valuable insights into damage hotspots that need prioritized investment for adaptation.[^4] The study demonstrated the feasibility and benefits of the VCAP in helping city planners and authorities develop an integrated resilience strategy for natural disaster risk management and climate adaptation.[^2] The VCAP Demonstration Study established a close working relationship between CSIRO and Da Nang CRO teams, received enthusiastic interest from relevant city agencies, and supported two out of four Focus Areas of the City Resilience Strategy.[^3] The Climate Change Coordination Office would like to extend this work to simulate project risk and damage costs to future housing stock under 2050 climate change scenarios.[^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Da Nang is vulnerable to extreme weather events, particularly tropical cyclones, flash floods, and inundation, which are likely to become more frequent and severe in the context of a changing climate.[^4] Typhoons have the greatest impact on housing compared to other sectors making housing one of the most vulnerable sectors to climate extremes in Da Nang.[^7] Housing damage in Da Nang is caused not only by climate hazards, but also by inappropriate housing solutions and poor construction techniques.[^7]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.csiro.au/en/news/all/articles/2017/april/before-the-storm
- [^2]: https://climateadaptationplatform.com/vietnams-resilience-based-climate-adaptation-platform/
- [^3]: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/308526926_A_Visual_Climate_Adaptation_Platform_for_Da_Nang_City_of_Vietnam_-_A_Science-based_Climate_Resilience_Approach
- [^4]: https://medium.com/the-asia-pacific-resilience-prospectus/studies-and-planning-projects-da-nang-vietnam-cad3b5946bbc
- [^5]: https://www.preventionweb.net/news/storm-proof-housing-urban-poor-lessons-da-nang-vietnam
- [^6]: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/272026368_Quantitative_cost-benefit_analysis_for_typhoon_resilient_housing_in_Danang_city_Vietnam
- [^7]: https://cdkn.org/resource/qualitative-insights-into-the-costs-benefits-of-housing-vietnam

# Research Report: Digital Water Meter Reading and Management Platform

## Solution Overview

The City of Ekurhuleni has implemented a comprehensive water meter management system that provides residents with real-time access to their consumption data and enables direct submission of meter readings through digital platforms. This approach addresses water conservation challenges while improving billing accuracy and reducing revenue losses from unmeasured consumption.

## Technical Components

The technical infrastructure includes physical water meters integrated into the municipal billing system, enabling automated revenue generation from water usage.[^1] The digital platform operates through the #MyCOE App, which allows residents to capture and upload images of their meter readings directly for submission to the city.[^2] The City of Ekurhuleni will soon implement a new meter reading system that has been developed to allow its customers to directly send their water and electricity meter readings to the City on time.[^3] SMEC South Africa has been appointed by the City of Ekurhuleni for the management of the City's Water Meter Processes in the following regions within Gauteng Province: Germiston, Katlehong, Boksburg, Edenvale, Vosloorus and portions of Tembisa.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The City of Ekurhuleni installed 2,175 water meters between January and June 2025, which are now integrated into the billing system.[^1] The water meter management project was due to run until June 2023, resulting in a two and a half year appointment for the contracted management services.[^4] Municipal authorities encourage customers to register on the convenient self-service water meter management system to capture their readings from home for accurate, up-to-date and not estimated billing.[^2] The implementation spans multiple regions across Gauteng Province, including Germiston, Katlehong, Boksburg, Edenvale, Vosloorus and portions of Tembisa.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

The implementation has successfully reduced non-revenue water from 29.84% in June 2024 to 28.94% in 2025, which is below the international average of 30% non-revenue water.[^1] The integrated billing system enables revenue generation from water usage through the newly installed meters.[^1] Residents benefit from accurate, up-to-date billing that eliminates estimated charges through the self-service meter management system.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This water metering system addresses climate adaptation challenges by enabling more efficient water resource management during periods of drought and water scarcity. Real-time consumption feedback helps residents adjust their usage patterns in response to climate-driven water availability constraints, building community resilience to extreme weather events that threaten municipal water supplies.

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.ekurhuleni.gov.za/press-releases/service-delivery/water-sanitation/war-on-leaks-leads-to-installation-of-2-175-water-meters-and-reduction-of-water-losses/
- [^2]: https://www.ekurhuleni.gov.za/press-releases/service-delivery/register-on-the-citys-self-service-meter-management-system-for-convenient-billing/
- [^3]: https://www.ekurhuleni.gov.za/campaigns/city-to-launch-new-self-service-meter-reading-system-2/
- [^4]: https://www.smec.com/general/smec-south-africa-appointed-water-meter-management-consultant/

# Research Report: Integrated Metropolitan Meteorological Monitoring Network

## Solution Overview

The Lisbon Metropolitan Area established an integrated meteorological monitoring network in early 2022, consisting of 18 weather stations distributed across each of its 18 municipalities.[^2] This infrastructure supports the CLIMA.AML project, which developed a network of meteorological sensors and a digital information platform to support and promote adaptation to climate change throughout the metropolitan region.[^3] The system enables continuous monitoring of weather patterns and their impact on communities while providing technical and scientific knowledge to support decision-making and increase the resilience and response capacity of the metropolitan territory to climate change impacts.[^1][^7]

## Technical Components

The monitoring infrastructure comprises 18 urban meteorological stations positioned strategically across the municipalities of Almada, Alcochete, Amadora, Barreiro, Cascais, Lisbon, Loures, Oeiras, Odivelas, Palmela, Mafra, Moita, Montijo, Seixal, Sintra, Sesimbra, Setúbal and Vila Franca de Xira.[^6] The implementation includes nine urban measurement micro-sensors designed to study urban heat island effects in eight green spaces of the region.[^4][^5] A freely accessible online platform analyzes all essential data and information to support the monitoring and evaluation of meteorological data, creating continuous monitoring of weather patterns and their impact on communities.[^5][^7] The platform was recently presented at the Pedra do Sal Environmental Interpretation Center in São Pedro do Estoril, allowing real-time monitoring of meteorological data from all 18 municipalities.[^6] The system enables the development of a high spatial resolution model on urban climate variables that facilitates understanding of factors associated with the urban heat island effect.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The Lisbon Metropolitan Area was selected to implement the project within the scope of the Environment, Climate Change and Low Carbon Economy programme, operated by the Secretary General for the Environment and Climate Action.[^8] The initiative operates with a global budget of 193,195.31 Euros, with 85% funded by EEA Grants 2014-2021.[^8] Development was scheduled for completion by the end of the first half of 2023.[^8] AML, as the project's promoter, established an institutional partnership with Biotext (a Norwegian entity), the Municipalities of Almada and Lisbon, as well as Cascais Ambiente and the Center for Studies and Regional and Urban Development (CEDRU).[^8][^5] The project received institutional support from the Portuguese Institute for the Sea and Atmosphere (IPMA), the Norwegian Meteorological Institute and the Network of Municipalities for Local Adaptation to Climate Change.[^5][^8] Institutional cooperation was planned between Portuguese and Norwegian municipalities for sharing good practices and training municipal technicians in the use of the new system, as well as in developing warning thresholds associated with extreme weather.[^7] The study is being carried out in partnership between the Lisbon Metropolitan Area and the Center for Ecology, Evolution and Climate Change (cE3c) of the Lisbon University.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

The collection and processing of data collected in 2022 by the 18 urban meteorological monitoring stations allowed drawing a climate picture of 2022 in the Lisbon metropolitan area.[^2] The average annual temperature in the metropolitan area in 2022, according to data from the weather stations, was 17.56ºC, 1ºC more than the national average in 2022 (16.6ºC).[^2] The maximum temperature recorded was in Alcochete, with 43.33ºC, on July 13.[^2] In 2022, the metropolitan area received 681.69 mm of rainfall (average accumulated value) over 100 days, with 265 days recording no precipitation.[^2] The municipality of Seixal recorded the highest average daily accumulated rainfall value, with 109.60 mm on December 13, which corresponded to 15% of the total rainfall for the year 2022 in that municipality.[^2] The network supports studies focused on potential areas susceptible to the effect of heat and allows the creation of methodologies to mitigate heat waves, replicable in the planning of adaptation to climate change across all 18 municipalities of the metropolitan region.[^4] The project produced nine thematic reports, with the eighth report presenting results of social vulnerability analysis to climate change in the Lisbon Metropolitan Area.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This monitoring network continues the Metropolitan Plan for Adaptation to Climate Change in the Lisbon Metropolitan Area, which was publicly presented in December 2019.[^6][^4] The general objective supports the municipalities of the Lisbon Metropolitan Area in the preparation of Municipal Climate Change Adaptation Plans (PMAAC).[^1] The system's capacity to develop high spatial resolution models on urban climate variables and study urban heat island effects directly addresses extreme heat hazards while enabling the development of warning thresholds associated with extreme weather events.[^4][^7]

## Business Analysis

The project was financed through the EEA Grants Environment Programme under the Environment, Climate Change and Low Carbon Economy program, operated by the General Secretariat for the Environment and Climate Action.[^1][^5] The financial mechanism provided 85% of the 193,195.31 Euro global budget through EEA Grants 2014-2021.[^8] Biotext served as donor project partner in the climate change-related project funded through the EEA/Norway Grants programme.[^3] The implementation model leveraged institutional partnerships across multiple municipal governments and research institutions to distribute technical capacity and knowledge transfer.[^8][^5] The freely accessible online platform design ensures broad public access to meteorological data without direct user costs.[^7] The project developed activities aimed at capacity building of local actors and promoting inter-municipal cooperation to increase resilience and response capacity of the metropolitan territory.[^1]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.eeagrants.gov.pt/media/6607/climaaml_relatorio8_final_23out2023.pdf
- [^2]: https://www.aml.pt/en/third-post-1070/
- [^3]: https://biotext.no/tjenester/korrekturlesing-redigering/
- [^4]: https://www.eeagrants.gov.pt/en/programmes/environment/news/climatelma-installs-sensors-to-assess-the-urban-heat-island-effect/
- [^5]: https://www.aml.pt/en/iniciativas/clima-aml/
- [^6]: https://www.ericeiramag.pt/en/new-platform-shows-weather-data-from-mafra-in-real-time/
- [^7]: https://www.themayor.eu/en/a/view/lisbon-will-study-closely-climate-effects-in-its-metropolitan-area-7178
- [^8]: https://www.eeagrants.gov.pt/en/programmes/environment/news/the-importance-of-the-climaaml-project-in-climatic-risk/
- [^9]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Europe & Middle East/Lisbon-Portugal/lx_contratoclimatico_10___1_.pdf

# Research Report: Smart Water Metering and Automatic Meter Reading System

## Solution Overview

The Water Supplies Department is promoting wider use of smart water meters and Automatic Meter Reading (AMR) System, planned to work in conjunction with the Water Intelligent Network (WIN) to help identify problems.[^4][^5] This approach enables near-real-time water consumption monitoring and early detection of potential water leakage for properties across Hong Kong.[^2] The technology eliminated the need for manual water meter checks, which were previously conducted every four months.[^2]

## Technical Components

The NB-IoT Wireless Smart Water Metering System provides an end-to-end solution spanning system design, development, and implementation.[^2] ASTRI developed a LoRaWAN-based Hong Kong Wireless Smart Water Metering System Standard and its Reference Design to facilitate the retrofitting of smart metering system to existing buildings.[^8] The System Standard is based on WSD requirements, OFCA regulations, and LoRaWAN standard and is the first in Hong Kong.[^8] Cellular technology enables city-wide indoor coverage without additional wiring connected between devices and communication equipment, simplifying deployment and maintenance compared to traditional wired AMI system alternatives, especially for deployment in existing buildings.[^2] ASTRI developed a Smart Water Analysis System that integrates t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) and Principal Component Analysis (PCA) technologies to perform data filtering that identifies anomalies in real-time.[^8] Water consumption data is now updated every half hour, compared to the previous four-month manual check intervals.[^2] Once installed, the smart water meter readers start tracking water usage for eight weeks and transmit the information to a server located in the University of Hong Kong campus.[^7]

## Implementation Details

LPS collaborated with the WSD to deploy the first Public Cellular Network Wireless Smart Water Meter system in Hong Kong.[^2] The Water Supplies Department launched a pilot scheme for Automatic Meter Reading at residential buildings in Un Chau Estate, Sham Shui Po and Sheung Shui Disciplined Services Quarters to review the latest technological advancements and technical performance of AMR water meters in multi-storey high rise buildings as well as its cost effectiveness for large scale implementation.[^9] The AMR pilot scheme was extended in early 2015 to Sheung Shui Area 36 West public housing estate which was under construction at that time.[^9] As of February 2021, the Water Supplies Department had received about 50 related applications involving 110 new buildings and about 30,000 smart water meters.[^6] The Water Supplies Department expected the number of smart water meters installed would gradually increase to more than 18,000 in the next three years, including about 4,000 meters in public housing development projects and about 14,000 meters in private development projects.[^6] The Water Supplies Department is closely monitoring the latest development in the application of wireless communication technology for smart water meters and conducting pilot tests at suitable locations.[^6] Regarding the installation of AMR in existing buildings, as more than three million customer accounts are involved, coupled with the need of cable installation for connecting smart water meters and AMR outstation in the buildings, it is envisaged that the implementation will have greater impact on the public and incur higher costs.[^6]

## Benefits and Impacts

Potential water leakages that were previously found during manual checks can now be detected on two-day intervals, creating a system of alert for proactive maintenance and repairs while saving precious resources.[^2] The automatic meter reading system not only improves the accuracy of meter reading, but also provides customers with timely water consumption data and other useful information, thereby enhancing their awareness of water conservation and prevention of water leakage.[^6] The smart water metering system is expected to raise public awareness in water conservation with an estimated saving of 1.8M m³ of water per year.[^8] The department invited about 1,000 domestic and non-domestic customers of high water consumption in phases to join the programme that enables a taskforce to conduct detailed 'water-usage check-ups' for such customers.[^7] Among the first batch of programme participants in the domestic sector, more than 80% of participants were able to reduce their water consumption.[^7] The Water Supplies Department aimed to reduce water usage by 500,000 cubic metres this year, which is equivalent to 200 Olympic-sized swimming pools.[^7] In the long run, the comprehensive introduction of AMR can save the manual meter reading work and avoid problems arising from the suspension of meter reading work.[^6]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This technology addresses water resource management challenges by enabling early detection of leakage and promoting conservation behaviors among consumers. The system supports climate adaptation by reducing water loss and enhancing the efficiency of water distribution infrastructure through continuous monitoring and real-time data analysis. When the relevant technology becomes mature in the future, the Water Supplies Department will review the situation and explore the technical feasibility of implementing wireless AMR in existing buildings so as to speed up the installation of smart water meters.[^6]

## Business Analysis

The System Standard facilitates a region-wide deployment of wireless AMR system in the future.[^8] The Water Supplies Department is exploring the use of AMR coupled with mobile app to enhance consumers' awareness of their water consumption on a timely basis thereby facilitating behavioural changes to save water.[^4][^5] The Water Supplies Department commissioned the Centre for Water Technology & Policy at the University of Hong Kong to carry out the Water-smart Taskforce Programme, from February this year until early next year.[^7] The taskforce uses a smart water meter reader with high precision and AI (artificial intelligence)-powered technologies to analyse customers' water usage data.[^7] Customers participating in the programme engage in an eight-week water conservation campaign, with the taskforce installing smart water meter readers on the existing meters without requiring any pipe modifications or affecting the water supply.[^7] A company with three hotels which consistently experiences high water consumption has been invited to participate in the programme.[^7] Smart water meter readers continuously monitor the water consumption in hotels, providing granular data for analysis and visualisation on the cloud platform.[^7]

[^1]: Water Supplies Department announcement on Digital Water Office establishment
[^2]: LPS NB-IoT Wireless Smart Water Metering System documentation

# Research Report: Multi-Hazard Risk Assessment and Early Warning System

## Solution Overview

Tbilisi City Hall planned to develop an integrated Multi-Hazard Risk Assessment and Early Warning System that would incorporate high-resolution models for weather and hydrological forecasting.[^3] This approach addresses a critical gap identified by the National Environmental Agency of Georgia, which recognized the lack of high-resolution weather forecast data as a significant challenge for the Georgia Flood Forecasting and Early Warning System (FEWS).[^1] The development of such high-resolution forecasting capabilities was established as a future priority for strengthening the country's climate risk management framework.[^1]

## Technical Components

The technical infrastructure for enhanced forecasting capabilities in Georgia includes multiple integrated systems and hardware investments. A high-performance computer (HPC) was purchased for the National Environment Agency and has been operating since 2021 to enable quality data processing and storage.[^7] Georgia deployed 2 meteorological radars in western and eastern parts of the country with support from the state and international partners.[^8] By the end of 2023, approximately 300 meteorological stations were installed throughout Georgia to significantly improve the quality of weather forecasts and forecasting of expected natural disasters.[^8] The monitoring network modernization included 79 new hydrometeorological stations that were installed, with 77 more planned, significantly boosting data accuracy.[^6] A forecasting platform using Delft-FEWS across 11 selected river basins was established.[^7] Additionally, a flood, landslide, and avalanche forecasting database assessment was developed, along with drought monitoring and forecasting platforms.[^7] A disaster risk information system and a flood forecasting platform were in testing as of the reporting period.[^6] The system builds upon the most complete and integrated almost real-time early warning system for floods and flashfloods that was recently developed and operationalized for the Rioni River Basin, developed under the UNDP project financed by the Adaptation Fund.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The Multi-Hazard Risk Assessment and Early Warning System project was being implemented by the National Environment Agency at the Ministry of Environmental Protection and Agriculture with Italy-Georgia Bilateral funding, in partnership with Tbilisi City Hall.[^3] The broader national scaling-up effort received US$27 million in GCF funding, with the cornerstone initiative focusing on high-tech monitoring and data processing.[^6] The FP068 Multi-Hazard Early Warning System project in Georgia received total GCF financing of USD 27,053,598 in grants, approved on 01 March 2018, with 76% disbursed as of the reporting period.[^5] Co-financing totaled US$38,239,024 from the Government of Georgia and US$5,000,000 from the Swiss Government.[^4] UNDP and the Government of Georgia, with funding from the Green Climate Fund, Swiss and Swedish governments, were implementing a 7-year programme dedicated to reducing the risk of climate-driven disasters in Georgia with an overall budget of US$74 million.[^7] The GCF-financed project scaled up pilot activities and achievements of the UNDP project "Developing Climate Resilient Flood and Flash Flood Management Practices to Protect Vulnerable Communities of Georgia" (Rioni project) financed by the Adaptation Fund from 2012-2017.[^4] The GCF project scaled up prototypes piloted by the Rioni project, including hazard mapping, floodplain modelling, floodplain zoning and early warning systems, to include other river basins and regions of Georgia and to encompass a broader range of key climate-induced hazards.[^4]

To enhance forecasting capabilities, Georgia became a member of the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) in 2021 to improve forecasting capabilities.[^7][^6] A Co-operation Agreement between ECMWF and Georgia came into force on 1 December 2021.[^2] International organisations such as the UN Development Programme (UNDP), the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) and the World Bank supported Georgia to become an ECMWF Co-operating State.[^2] UNDP Georgia provided technical expertise and funding needed to cover the costs of Georgia being an ECMWF Co-operating State, with support drawing on broader assistance funded by the Green Climate Fund and the governments of Switzerland, Sweden and Georgia.[^2] Georgia joined the Boundary Conditions and SAPP Optional Programmes as part of the ECMWF Co-operation Agreement.[^2] In 2025, the programme helped establish a partnership between the National Environment Agency and the European Meteorological Network EUMETNET to enhance forecasting, climate services, and early-warning systems.[^7] Co-operation Agreements offer full access to ECMWF real-time products, archive data and software tools, as well as access to ECMWF training facilities.[^2]

Implementation challenges included outdated capabilities prior to the modernization efforts. According to a World Bank report, the Department of Hydrometeorology was unable to make the best use of weather forecasts available in digital form from leading centers around the world due to outdated hardware and software, and did not have access to sufficient observations and instruments needed to make forecasts for less than six hours ahead.[^9] Despite two newly installed radar systems, precise weather forecasting remained a challenge in a mountainous country hit by several major natural disasters, including the Tbilisi flood of 2015 that killed at least 19 and the deaths of 33 at the popular Shovi resort in 2023.[^9] According to information UNDP Tbilisi office shared, only 10 municipalities in Georgia out of 69 had Emergency Management Actions Plans in one form or another, with few approved, some works in progress, and some not started.[^9]

## Benefits and Impacts

The multi-hazard early warning system is an essential element of the country's climate risk management framework and will serve 1.7 million Georgians currently at risk from climate-induced hazards.[^4] The Georgia Multi-Hazard Early Warning System project was expected to benefit 1,710,000 direct beneficiaries and 2,000,000 indirect beneficiaries.[^5] The project will directly benefit up to 1.7 million people, representing 40% of the population currently at risk from hydrometeorological hazards.[^4] A unified methodology for hazard assessment was created, producing detailed maps for seven major river basins.[^6] The project will significantly improve the National Environment Agency's capacities to meet World Meteorological Organization (WMO) standards.[^4] Levan Davitashvili, Minister of Environmental Protection and Agriculture of Georgia, stated that becoming a Co-operating State of ECMWF would facilitate the enhancement of Georgia's numerical weather prediction capabilities, which in turn would help to improve the accuracy of weather forecasts across the country as well as to produce effective early warnings on natural disasters related to hydrometeorological and geological events.[^2]

The initiative addresses substantial economic risks. Over the last 21-year period, total damages from hydrometeorological hazards were GEL 2.8 billion (US$1.2 billion) at a cost of 152 lives, with 22 deaths occurring in the Tbilisi flash flood of 2015.[^4] Annual average damages to properties from floods were estimated at 116.3 Million GEL (US$51.2 million) without climate change and at 282.7 Million GEL (US$124.4 Million) with climate change.[^4] Georgia's Intended Nationally Determined Contribution (INDC) estimated economic losses from climate-induced hazards without adaptation measures for the period 2021-2030 to be US$10-12 billion, while the cost of adaptation measures was estimated to be US$1.5-2 billion.[^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Georgia faces climate change-related risks including landslides, mudflows, erosion, avalanches, floods, drought, and strong winds that are increasing in frequency, intensity and geographical spread due to climate change.[^5] The "Scaling-up multi-hazard early warning system and the use of climate information in Georgia" project will reduce exposure of Georgia's communities, livelihoods and infrastructure to climate-induced natural hazards through a well-functioning nation-wide multi-hazard early warning system and risk-informed local action.[^4] The project will achieve transformative change in climate risk reduction and management in Georgia by development of a fully-integrated impact-based multi-hazard early warning system.[^4]

## Business Analysis

The implementation follows a multi-layered financing model combining international climate finance with bilateral and national government resources. The scaling up was attained by developing and implementing a nation-wide Multi-Hazard Early Warning System, developing and delivering climate information services, and implementing community-based risk reduction measures.[^4] The project introduced standardized hazard, risk and vulnerability assessment and mapping methods and technologies, and provided critical climate risk information to enable the implementation of nation-wide risk reduction policies.[^4] The project will catalyze a paradigm shift towards climate risk-informed and resilient development.[^4] The implementation structure included three primary outputs: expanded hydro-meteorological observation network and modelling capacities to secure reliable information on climate-induced hazards, vulnerability and risks; multi-hazard early warning system and new climate information products supported with effective national regulations, coordination mechanism and institutional capacities; and improved community resilience through the implementation of the multi-hazard early warning system and priority risk reduction measures.[^4] The Georgia Flood Forecasting and Early Warning System was operated by the National Environmental Agency of Georgia, with George Kordzakhia serving as Head of Hydrological Forecasting Division as of May 2022.[^1]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://european-flood.emergency.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/AM/AM2022/5-3_EFAS%20Annual%20Meeting%202022%20Georgia%20Flood%20FEWS.pdf
- [^2]: https://www.ecmwf.int/en/about/media-centre/news/2021/ecmwf-welcomes-georgia-its-12th-co-operating-state
- [^3]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Europe & Middle East/Tbilisi-Georgia/Tbilisi_Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^4]: https://www.adaptation-undp.org/projects/scaling-multi-hazard-early-warning-system-and-use-climate-information-georgia
- [^5]: https://www.greenclimate.fund/project/fp068
- [^6]: https://www.undp.org/geneva/news/building-climate-resilient-future-georgia-strengthens-its-defenses-against-natural-disasters
- [^7]: https://www.undp.org/georgia/projects/climate-change-disasters
- [^8]: https://mepa.gov.ge/En/News/Details/20780
- [^9]: https://cactus-journalism.ge/index.php?do=full&id=2173&fbclid=IwZXh0bgNhZW0CMTAAAR1JgVTpyD0xGzi24aExmAwbBE1bJdV1xvHXP5Y9anUd6oBn0rdmQ9kU4Qs_aem_8BSpdM5l1rpArqs1N1JySQ

# Research Report: Transit Stations as Multi-Service Community Resilience Hubs

## Solution Overview

Miami-Dade County has developed a specialized story map to analyze optimal locations for transit stations while reimagining these stations as community resilience hubs that offer multiple public services and provide civic spaces.[^1][^2][^3] This approach represents a dual-purpose infrastructure strategy that integrates transportation planning with climate adaptation, enabling transit facilities to serve communities year-round, during disruptions, and throughout recovery periods.[^2] The initiative addresses gaps in underserved areas by providing resources as determined by community needs.[^2]

## Technical Components

The implementation utilizes story mapping technology as a data visualization and communication tool to demonstrate resilience challenges and solutions.[^1] Miami-Dade County collaborated with subject matter experts and GIS professionals to develop the mapping platform, which effectively demonstrates sea level rise impacts and showcases ongoing flood mitigation projects.[^1] The second story map specifically analyzes transit station siting criteria while evaluating how these locations can be transformed into multi-functional resilience hubs.[^1][^2][^3] The technical approach enables the identification of spaces to activate hubs and pods into a multi-layered system through collaborative spatial analysis.[^2]

## Implementation Details

The Atlantic Council's Climate Resilience Center leads the project, partnering with residents, neighbors, community leaders, and community-based organizations.[^2] The strategy is co-created through collaborative engagements, a needs analysis, and a customized site integration plan.[^2] Implementation focuses on identifying gaps and developing strategies to provide underserved areas with resources as determined by the community.[^2] The approach is grounded in neighborhood culture and values through trust and support at all times, not just during disastrous events.[^2] Local organizations and leaders collaborate to identify spaces that can be activated within the hub and pod network system.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

The Climate Resilience Center's Resilience Hub Network Strategy supports Miami-Dade County communities to become better prepared for future challenges while strengthening existing social infrastructure that is vital to communities.[^2] The story mapping approach enables effective visualization and communication of resilience challenges with the community, demonstrating both current vulnerabilities and ongoing mitigation efforts.[^1] The multi-layered hub system provides year-round community services, emergency support during disruptions, and sustained assistance throughout recovery periods.[^2] The initiative addresses resource gaps in underserved areas through community-determined priorities.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The Southeast Florida Regional Climate Change Compact's projections show that by 2040, sea levels in Southeast Florida could rise by 10 to 17 inches, and by 2070, they could rise by 21 to 54 inches.[^4] The dual-purpose transit station and resilience hub strategy directly addresses these flooding challenges by creating elevated, accessible community facilities that can serve as refuge points during climate disruptions while strengthening neighborhood-level adaptive capacity through year-round social infrastructure.[^1][^2]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Miami-US/Resilient305_final.pdf
- [^2]: https://onebillionresilient.org/project/resilience-hub-network-strategy/
- [^3]: https://www.miamidade.gov/global/transportation/transit-oriented-development.page
- [^4]: https://miamidadetpo.org/library/studies/mdtpo-climate-resiliency-study-final-report-2023-06.pdf

# Research Report: Multi-Tool Urban Resilience Assessment Framework

## Solution Overview

Cần Thơ applied a comprehensive suite of analytical tools developed by the 100 Resilient Cities program and complementary technologies to develop its urban resilience strategy.[^2][^4][^6] This multi-tool approach combined standardized resilience assessment frameworks with advanced spatial analysis and stakeholder engagement methods to identify climate vulnerabilities and inform strategic planning.[^4] The methodology enabled systematic evaluation of the city's green and blue infrastructure changes, water management capacity, and community perceptions of resilience challenges.[^2][^4]

## Technical Components

The technical framework incorporated multiple assessment instruments developed by 100RC, including the City Resilience Actions Inventory and City Resilience Perceptions Assessment tools.[^2][^4][^6] Satellite image analysis was deployed to identify changes in green and blue surface coverage across the urban landscape.[^2][^4][^6] The VENSIM modeling tool was applied to calculate the contribution of green infrastructure in improving rainwater storage and infiltration capacity.[^4] The methodology also integrated qualitative research methods, including in-depth interviews and focus group discussions to capture stakeholder perspectives.[^4]

## Implementation Details

Cần Thơ joined the 100 Resilient Cities programme in May 2016, with the programme officially launched in the city at a conference on December 13, 2016.[^3] The city was selected from more than 325 applicants from over 80 countries around the world for this Rockefeller Foundation initiative.[^1] Through the program, Cần Thơ received technical assistance and other essential support and services to develop its resilience strategy.[^1] The Can Tho Resilience Strategy until 2030 was authored by Can Tho City and ISET with funding from The Rockefeller Foundation.[^2] The Can Tho 100RC team identified green infrastructure as one of four focus areas for the city resilience strategy development.[^5] This prioritization emerged from the first phase of the 100RC project, during which the ISET team worked closely with Can Tho city and the Can Tho 100RC team on promoting green infrastructure.[^5]

## Benefits and Impacts

The analytical tools enabled systematic identification of multiple climate and non-climate related shocks and stresses facing the city.[^5] Findings of the Preliminary Resilience Assessment indicated that Can Tho had been experiencing urban flooding, heat waves, water pollution, and lack of green and public spaces.[^5] The assessment process revealed limitations in existing planning and flood management practices while highlighting the advantages and innovative nature of green infrastructure solutions.[^5] Lauren Sorkin, Director of the 100 Resilient Cities programme in the Asia-Pacific region, stated that Can Tho had the chance to play a pioneering role in boosting resilience for cities in Vietnam.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The integrated assessment approach directly addressed climate-related vulnerabilities including urban flooding and heat waves identified through the preliminary resilience evaluation.[^5] The satellite analysis of green and blue surface coverage changes and VENSIM modeling of rainwater storage capacity provided quantitative foundations for climate adaptation planning focused on water management and thermal regulation.[^4] This methodology enabled evidence-based prioritization of green infrastructure interventions to enhance the city's capacity to absorb and respond to climate shocks.[^5]

## Business Analysis

The 100 Resilient Cities programme represented an initiative of the Rockefeller Foundation dedicated to helping cities around the world become resilient to physical, social and economic challenges of the 21st century.[^3] The program provided participating cities with access to knowledge, experience, and assistance from other cities in the network, including Da Nang, the first Vietnamese city admitted to the network two years before Can Tho's 2016 entry.[^3] This network model enabled knowledge transfer and capacity building without requiring direct municipal investment in tool development, as the standardized assessment frameworks were provided through the program partnership.[^1][^2]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://vietnamnet.vn/en/can-tho-joins-100-resilient-cities-E159143.html
- [^2]: https://www.i-s-e-t.org/cantho-resilience-strategy-100rc
- [^3]: https://en.vietnamplus.vn/100-resilient-cities-programme-launched-in-can-tho-post104222.vnp
- [^4]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Asia & Asia Pacific Oceania/CanTho-Vietnam/Can-Tho-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^5]: https://www.i-s-e-t.org/single-post/2018/03/30/green-infrastructure-as-a-focus-area-of-the-can-tho-city-resilience-strategy
- [^6]: https://www.rockefellerfoundation.org/wp-content/uploads/Institutionalizing-Urban-Resilience-A-Midterm-Monitoring-and-Evaluation-Report-of-100-Resilient-Cities.pdf

# Research Report: EXTREMA Heat Alert Mobile Application

## Solution Overview

Paris launched the EXTREMA mobile application in late June 2018 to provide residents and visitors with real-time information about cooling locations during extreme heat events.[^1][^2] This technology-enabled approach combines satellite data, thermal mapping, and an interactive digital platform to help vulnerable populations navigate the city's network of cool islands during dangerous heatwaves.[^3][^4] The initiative emerged following the devastating 2003 heatwave that caused approximately 500 deaths in Paris and 15,000 across France, prompting municipal authorities to develop comprehensive heat adaptation strategies.[^5][^6]

## Technical Components

The EXTREMA system uses real-time satellite data combined with model-based and city-specific information to estimate temperature, humidity, and discomfort index for every square kilometer across Paris.[^7][^8] Temperature estimates are updated every five minutes, providing spatial and temporal resolution unavailable from other services.[^9][^10] Between 2014 and 2016, the City of Paris conducted foundational research on the thermal regulating effect of green and humid spaces, combining field measurements in green spaces and nearby urban areas with aerial thermography maps.[^11] The mobile application evaluates personalized health risk for users at their current location, and when risk levels are high, the system alerts users and provides recommendations with routing instructions to the nearest cooling centers.[^12] The platform provides information about diverse cool spots including air-conditioned rooms, parks, shady streets, fountains, and water availability locations such as public taps, springs, and animal watering holes.[^13] The application was provided in four languages at launch, making it particularly useful for foreign visitors.[^14]

## Implementation Details

The City of Paris developed EXTREMA in collaboration with the Agence Parisienne du Climat, EIVP (École des Ingénieurs de la Ville de Paris), and APUR (Atelier parisien d'urbanisme).[^15] The application was also developed in partnership with France's public health authority and weather agency Météo France.[^16] In the context of the climate adaptation strategy (2015) and the new Paris Climate Plan (2018), the City asked Apur to define, locate, and map cool air itineraries and islands throughout Paris.[^17] The project was implemented in 2018-2019, with the full suite of EXTREMA services released in summer 2018.[^18][^19] Initial investments totaled $852,564 since 2018.[^20] More than 800 cool islands were identified in 2018 as accessible during the day and 150 at night, with the network composed of spaces ranging from parks and forests to swimming pools and museums.[^21][^22] Multiple users downloaded and evaluated the mobile app, making EXTREMA Paris the most downloaded app of the city during its launch week.[^23] In 2022, the option for cool routes was added where users can choose the coolest route to reach their destination.[^24]

## Benefits and Impacts

During the first year of use in 2018, the EXTREMA app was downloaded 6,000 times.[^25] The application lists around 800 to 1,000 different places suitable for those seeking refuge from high temperatures.[^26][^27] Users can create profiles for friends or family members to check if their loved ones are safe during a heatwave, with the system monitoring whether users or family members might be in critical situations.[^28][^29] The City of Paris places an interactive map on its website annually so users can identify the best places to escape the heat.[^30] Cool air itineraries are available to the general public through dynamic maps on the paris.fr site, with separate maps for daytime and nighttime access.[^31] More than 7,000 Parisians have been identified as vulnerable during times of extreme heat, and the EXTREMA project aims to inform those residents of heat risks and how and where to cool off.[^32] The city has added 62 hectares of green space and 4.7 hectares of green roofs to alleviate the urban heat island effect.[^33] By 2020, Paris aimed to ensure all Parisians would be within a seven-minute walk of a cool island.[^34] By 2030, the City plans to create or open to the public at least 300 additional cool islands and routes.[^35] Social benefits include strong public support, with 80% of Parisians supporting initiatives to create more green spaces in the city.[^36]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The EXTREMA system directly addresses extreme heat events, which pose severe consequences for Paris, particularly for elderly residents and those in ill health.[^37] The application proposes an innovative approach that exploits high smartphone penetration in Europe through a mobile application for the public and an administration web hub for local authorities to improve resilience to extreme temperature events.[^38] The initiative aims to prevent disasters like the 2003 heatwave from becoming more common as climate change intensifies urban heat island effects.[^39]

## Business Analysis

Green party councillor Célia Blauel noted that Paris and Athens were the first cities to launch such a cooling app, though Rotterdam also deployed a version of Extrema.[^40] The project received initial investments of $852,564 since 2018, supporting the development and deployment of the mobile application and associated infrastructure.[^41] By 2020, Paris planned to build 100 additional hectares of green roofs, one-third of which would be used to produce fruit and vegetables, representing an integrated approach to climate adaptation and urban agriculture.[^42] The strong public support for green space initiatives, with 80% of Parisians backing such measures, suggests favorable conditions for continued investment in heat mitigation infrastructure.[^43] The multi-agency partnership model involving municipal authorities, public health agencies, meteorological services, and urban planning organizations demonstrates a collaborative governance approach to climate adaptation financing and implementation.[^44]

[^1]: EXTREMA launched in Paris June 29, 2018 Following Rotterdam and Athens, EXTREMA mobile app launched at the city of Paris in the end of June.
[^2]: In 2018, this data will be used to develop a mobile application with real-time information about the closest cool areas, and the coolest routes to take.
[^3]: EXTREMA (project using real-time satellite data, along with other model and city-specific data to estimate the temperature, humidity, and discomfort index for every square kilometre in the city).
[^4]: The network of cool islands is composed by more than 800 spaces, from parks and forests to swimming pools and museums, where residents and visitors can take a break from the city's summer heat.
[^5]: Around 15,000 old people died across France, including 500 in Paris, from the effects of that heatwave.
[^6]: The heat wave of 2003 caused several deaths among elderly Parisians.
[^7]: EXTREMA uses real-time satellite data, along with other model and city-specific data to estimate the temperature, humidity, and discomfort index for every square kilometer in the city.
[^8]: EXTREMA uses real-time satellite data, along with other model and city-specific data to estimate the temperature, humidity, and discomfort index for every square kilometer in the city.
[^9]: Temperature estimates are updated every 5 minutes, providing data at a spatial and temporal resolution that is not available from any other service.
[^10]: The app can tell you, thanks to data that is updated every five minutes, if your grandmother might be in a critical situation, and it can tell you what you need to do
[^11]: Between 2014 and 2016, the City of Paris conducted a study on the thermal regulating effect of green and humid spaces in the Paris area. This study combined field measurements in green spaces and nearby urban areas with aerial thermography maps.
[^12]: The mobile app evaluates the real-time personalised health risk of the user at his/her location, and if high, it alerts him/her and provides recommendations and routing instructions to the nearest cooling centres.
[^13]: The user enters the application environment and by entering information such as their location they can retrieve useful information such as where the cool spots around them are (air-conditioned rooms, parks, shady streets, fountains and more) and where there is water availability (e.g. public taps, springs, animal watering holes, etc.).
[^14]: The application is already provided in 4 languages making it particularly useful for foreign visitors as well.
[^15]: The city of Paris, together with the Agence Parisienne du Climat, the EIVP (École des Ingénieurs de la Ville de Paris) and the APUR (Atelier parisien d'urbanisme) and EXTREMA.
[^16]: One of the aims of the app, which was developed in collaboration with France's public health authority and weather agency Météo France
[^17]: In the context of the strategy of adapting to climate change (2015) and of the new Paris Climate Plan (2018), the City of Paris asked Apur to define, locate and then make a map of the cool air itineraries and islands in Paris.
[^18]: The project was implemented in 2018-2019.
[^19]: In summer 2018 EXTREMA full suite of services will be released.
[^20]: Since 2018 Initial Investments 852,564 USD
[^21]: More than 800 cool islands have been identified in 2018 as accessible during the day and 150 at night.
[^22]: The network of cool islands is composed by more than 800 spaces, from parks and forests to swimming pools and museums, where residents and visitors can take a break from the city's summer heat.
[^23]: Multiple users downloaded and evaluated the mobile app making EXTREMA Paris the most downloaded app of the city that week!
[^24]: Also, in 2022, the option for cool routes was added where the user can choose the coolest route to their reach its destination.
[^25]: This practice seems to be quite successful as an indicator during the first year of use of the app (2018), EXTREMA has been downloaded 6,000 times.
[^26]: Around 800 different places are listed on the app as being suitable for those seeking to take refuge from high temperatures.
[^27]: Around 1000 different places that are suitable for those who seek for a refuge from high temperatures, are listed in the app.
[^28]: Users of the app can create a profile and find the places nearest to them. And they can also create a profile for friends or family so that they can check to see if their loved ones are safe during a heatwave.
[^29]: The app can tell you, thanks to data that is updated every five minutes, if your grandmother might be in a critical situation, and it can tell you what you need to do
[^30]: The City of Paris will place an interactive map on its website every year so that users can identify the best places to escape the heat.
[^31]: Cool air itineraries are available to the general public in the form of dynamic maps on the paris.fr site where you can find a map of the daytime and one for the night time.
[^32]: More than 7,000 Parisians have been identified as vulnerable during times of extreme heat, and this project aims to inform those residents of heat risks, and how and where to cool off.
[^33]: The city has already added 62 hectares of green space and 4.7 hectares of green roofs.
[^34]: By 2020, the goal is for all Parisians to be within a seven-minute walk of a cool island.
[^35]: By 2030, the City will create or open to the public at least 300 cool islands and routes.
[^36]: Social Benefits – 80% of Parisians support the initiatives to create more green spaces in the city, which will lead to a more pleasant living environment
[^37]: In Paris, a natural disaster such a severe heat wave could have serious consequences. The heat wave of 2003 caused several deaths among elderly Parisians.
[^38]: EXTREMA proposes a new innovative and effective approach that aims to exploit the high penetration of smartphones in Europe in the form of a mobile application for the public, and an administration web hub for the local authorities.
[^39]: Health Benefits – Fifteen thousand people died in France during a heat wave in 2003. To prevent such disasters from becoming more common, Paris is protecting its citizens – particularly the elderly and those in ill health – from excessive heat.
[^40]: With (the Greek capital) Athens, we are the first to launch such an app, said green party councillor Célia Blauel, who appeared to have overlooked the fact that the Dutch city of Rotterdam also has a version of Extrema.
[^41]: Since 2018 Initial Investments 852,564 USD
[^42]: Going forward, vegetation must be planted on all new buildings, and, by 2020, the city will build 100 additional hectares of green roofs – one-third of which will be used to produce fruit and vegetables.
[^43]: Social Benefits – 80% of Parisians support the initiatives to create more green spaces in the city, which will lead to a more pleasant living environment
[^44]: The city of Paris, together with the Agence Parisienne du Climat, the EIVP (École des Ingénieurs de la Ville de Paris) and the APUR (Atelier parisien d'urbanisme) and EXTREMA.

# Research Report: IoT Flood Monitoring and Early Warning System

## Solution Overview

TX Intelligence, a subsidiary of Grupo TX, joined forces with AWS and the National Civil Protection System of Panama (SINAPROC) to implement a flood monitoring and early warning solution for the Juan Díaz river basin.[^1] This approach integrates data from climate prediction models to allow the issuance of early warnings in cases of potential flooding.[^1] The system provides real-time information to any user while offering access to historical data stored for sensitivity analyses and monitoring of changes.[^3]

## Technical Components

The implementation uses LoRaWAN (Low Energy Wide Area Network) communication protocol, which operates using LoRa radio frequency technology in a free band spectrum.[^1] Remote IoT sensors can run for multiple years with AA batteries while transmitting data from up to eight kilometers away.[^2] SINAPROC's technical team leveraged extensive studies on the river and its basin to determine the most suitable locations for the different types of sensors, allowing analysis of how rain in the upper part of the basin affects water levels from its source and along its route to create a predictive model based on different meteorological scenarios.[^2] The variety of sensors provides redundant datasets, which help identify false positives, such as when debris blocks a waterway and causes water levels to rise artificially.[^2] Researchers couple the information from IoT sensors with 3D mapping imagery of Panama City, data that is readily available and stored on the AWS Cloud, enabling Grupo TX to use data analytics to understand historical trends, model, and forecast the potential impact of changing weather patterns.[^2] The solution uses multiple AWS products and services including AWS IoT Core, AWS IoT Rule Engine, Amazon Kinesis Data Streams, AWS Lambda, Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose, Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3), Amazon Timestream, AWS IoT Events, Amazon Connect, Amazon Pinpoint, Amazon CloudFront, Amazon Cognito, Amazon DynamoDB, and Amazon DynamoDB Streams.[^2] Predictive river analytics using machine learning can help model river levels using the data collected by IoT devices combined with historical data, making it possible to warn people ahead of time if evacuation is required via a public alert system.[^2]

## Implementation Details

Working together, SINAPROC, Grupo TX, and AWS developed a proof-of-concept for an early warning system for flooding in the Juan Diaz River Basin.[^2] The Juan Díaz river basin covers approximately 322 square kilometers and contains seven rivers that flow through the districts of Panama and San Miguelito before emptying into Panama Bay.[^1] The system had to be capable of installing devices in difficult-to-access areas, some at altitudes greater than 700 meters above sea level and lacking basic services such as electricity and cellular signal.[^1] A dashboard was created for SINAPROC allowing real-time monitoring of all critical metrics and retrospective analysis of historical data.[^1] The project implements a multichannel alert system that notifies various stakeholders, from government authorities to local residents, through phone calls, SMS and emails.[^1] The implementation of LoRaWAN and integration with AWS IoT Core enabled efficient monitoring and early warnings in flood-prone areas.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The dashboard facilitates real-time monitoring and allows for retrospective analysis of historical data, which is invaluable for continuous system improvement.[^1] Grupo TX will be able to train an artificial intelligence model to detect variables that a human may not be able to detect, and as time passes, the model will continue to improve and notifications to citizens in danger zones will be sent faster and more effectively.[^2] The implementation has had a direct impact on the safety of communities and the preservation of ecosystems.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The Juan Díaz river basin has become increasingly susceptible to catastrophic flooding.[^1] A strategic flood risk assessment of the Tocumen and Tapia basins has been developed to understand the key flood mechanisms, create hazard exposure and risk maps, and determine flood risks in four future scenarios.[^4] This technology addresses the urgent need for a system capable of monitoring the conditions of rivers and hydrographic basins in real time while integrating data from climate prediction models for early warning issuance.[^1]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/publicsector/creating-real-time-flood-alerts-cloud/
- [^3]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Latin America & Caribbean/PanamaCity-Panama/Panama-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^4]: https://www.gfdrr.org/en/feature-story/results-resilience-redeveloping-panama-city-waterfront-create-resilient-inclusive-and
