# Research Report: Local Climate Adaptation Tool (LCAT) for Evidence-Based Decision Support

## Solution Overview

The Local Climate Adaptation Tool (LCAT) is a UK-based national evidence-based resource designed to support local decision-makers, including public sector institutions, to take climate adaptation action.[^1] This digital platform brings together complex climate models, adaptation options and community and health impact evidence to help users understand the health implications of climate change in their local area.[^3] The tool has been co-designed with over 50 local authority areas and provides data and evidence to help local areas understand the current and future climate, likely risks and impacts and adaptations needed.[^1]

## Technical Components

This online tool integrates multiple technical components to deliver comprehensive climate adaptation support. The system brings together complex climate models, adaptation options and health impact evidence all in one place.[^11] LCAT was created through undertaking evidence reviews, beginning with the UK Climate Change Risk Assessment and the IPCC 2023 reports, followed by additional literature searches focusing on health and wellbeing impacts.[^5] Using the third UK Climate Change Risk Assessment 2022 (CCRA3) as its starting point, and in consultation with stakeholders, the key risk areas for inclusion in LCAT have been identified as: heat, flooding and drought, extreme storms, coastal security and ocean system changes, and personal security, such as food or energy supply.[^2] The platform pulls together the best available data to provide local climate forecasts and provides the evidence in layers – giving summary overviews and then increasingly more detailed insights to meet the needs of different users.[^10] LCAT uses Climate Just tool to report vulnerability data.[^12] The tool provides information and datasets on vulnerable groups using insight and data from Climate Just to support decision makers to identify who is vulnerable and take a more equitable approach to planning adaptations.[^3] As a new addition in 2025, the tool also includes Deprivation Data for local areas.[^3] The system has been created through accessing data from open-source repositories to ensure LCAT remains free.[^5] The tool identifies key, published evidence on health and well-being outcomes to support the recommendations and advice it will generate.[^2]

## Implementation Details

The Local Climate Adaptation Tool has been co-developed by the University of Exeter and Cornwall Council.[^11] The LCAT project team initially spent a year exploring how local stakeholders were adapting to climate change, how they were working with other agencies to make decisions, and what the barriers were to action.[^7] An initial year was spent exploring how key stakeholders were adapting to climate change, what the barriers were to action and finding a common language that would allow decision making across traditional boundaries.[^3] Stakeholders felt they had limited access to guidance, information and support, and identified a need to be able to access future climate models, which led to the creation of the Local Climate Adaptation Tool.[^7] During the second phase, LCAT developers worked with over 100 professionals, representing around 50 local areas across the UK running co-design workshops and 'teach-in' sessions with keynote speakers.[^3] Through a co-design process, the team developed a decision-making support tool that responds to local needs and challenges, as identified by stakeholders, working with local service providers across the UK.[^7] The prototype was developed for Cornwall and the Isles of Scilly, and it is now being further developed across the country.[^7] In 2022 the Local Climate Adaptation Tool team received funding via Research England (Policy Support Fund) and the Schroder Foundation to develop the tool further to include climate data for most of Scotland, Wales and England, more climate impacts focused on increasing heat, adaptation suggestions, and a new section highlighting local vulnerabilities.[^4] LCAT has secured support from multiple funders including the Local Government Association's Net Zero Programme, the Turing Institute, The Schroeder Foundation and through BlueAdapt, a Horizon Europe project.[^9] The project is currently co-funded to 2026 through BlueAdapt, which has received funding from the European Union's Horizon Europe research and innovation programme.[^5] The Local Climate Adaptation Tool was developed through collaboration between Then Try This, the European Centre for Environment and Human Health at the University of Exeter, Cornwall Council, the NHS and emergency services.[^4] As of February 2023, the prototype health impact maps explored heat (and cold) as well as tested the methodology that was being used for the mapping, with the other risks to be added by mid-2023.[^2] Updated iterations, with more data, content and evidence were published, using the UK's Third Climate Change Risk Assessment (CCRA3) as the evidence starting point.[^3] The full tool launched in February 2024.[^3] LCAT launched in February 2024 and has regular updates.[^11] Over the coming two years the project plans to update and develop LCAT further and run an evaluation, including the development of downloadable data/reports, bringing more data into the tool to provide more localised outputs, highlighting co-benefits, and enhancing the case study offer.[^5]

## Benefits and Impacts

The tool is free and open source, and is aimed at helping councils, the NHS and emergency services as well as local service providers to understand what is likely to happen and prioritise their climate adaptation approaches.[^3] Users can select a local area of interest and see the predicted climate over the coming decades, alongside evidence on the health impacts of climate change from the scientific literature.[^2] The platform allows users to explore local climate models, enables them to see how climate change will impact the health and wellbeing of people in their area, and makes recommendations for actions based on the best available current evidence.[^7] LCAT generates recommendations for appropriate adaptation approaches to support the health and wellbeing of local people, based on the best available peer-reviewed evidence.[^11] The system provides information on how local climates will change, what the likely hazards are, what health and community impacts may occur, who will be most vulnerable and why, and which adaptations to consider.[^5] LCAT makes recommendations about the types of adaptation that should be considered, with the ultimate goal of supporting a multi-agency approach to building resilient and healthy communities.[^12] The tool supports multi-agency professionals in proactively planning mitigation for local climate change impacts, with a joined-up approach at the centre of LCAT.[^7] As well as highlighting impacts across sectors, it uses health as a lens to highlight the health impacts on communities.[^5] The LCAT team released a new guide called Introduction to Climate Adaptation, co-created with Oxfordshire County Council and UK wide decision-makers, to help overcome a lack of knowledge and skills as a key barrier to action.[^6] The tool includes an accompanying learning resource called 'Introduction to Local Climate Adaptation' to provide learning opportunities to build skills and knowledge.[^3] LCAT provides an evidence-based list of adaptation options offering local areas the guidance they need on planning for adaptation in their areas.[^3] The tool was showcased at COP26 in Glasgow, winning Highly Commended in the Climate Challenge Cup.[^3] In July 2024, it was awarded Sustainability of the Year Initiative at the Socitm awards.[^3] The University of Exeter's Local Climate Adaptation Tool won the Sustainability Initiative of the Year award at the 2024 Socitm Awards.[^13]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

LCAT has been designed to help local organisations increase their adaptation response to local climate change, something a recent government report describes as having 'failed to keep pace with the worsening reality of climate risk.'[^10] With little adaptation action happening across the UK, LCAT provides support to local decision-makers in their work to build healthy and resilient communities.[^13] Manchester Climate Change Agency is working with the University of Exeter on a Local Climate Adaptation Tool (LCAT) that is intended to recommend adaptation action that will support improvements in health and wellbeing.[^14]

## Business Analysis

The tool is free and open source.[^11] The Local Climate Adaptation Tool is free and open source as always.[^4] LCAT has been co-developed with, and for, local authorities and local service providers.[^3] The system was designed with, and created for, local UK decision-makers.[^1] LCAT was co-designed with local decision-makers across the four nations of the UK to create a simple online tool that brings together data and advice to understand how local climates will change, what the climate impacts might be for communities, and what action to take.[^6] The outputs from DyME-CHH will feed directly into The Local Climate Adaptation Tool (LCAT) that supports evidence-based adaptation by local authorities and public bodies.[^8] LCAT has since been developed to be used across local authority areas throughout the UK to support climate change adaptation action.[^11] The Local Climate Adaptation Tool has been developed by a team from the University of Exeter and Cornwall Council, with input from more than 50 other local authority areas across the UK.[^10]

# Research Report: Mobile Solar-Electric Systems with Battery Storage for Remote Communities

## Solution Overview

Resilient Power Puerto Rico (RPPR) deployed mobile solar-electric systems with battery storage to remote and underserved communities across Puerto Rico following Hurricane Maria in 2017.[^9] The initiative was founded by Puerto Ricans living in New York City, Jonathan Marvel and Cristina Roig, hours after Hurricane Maria made landfall on September 20, 2017.[^11][^5] This approach targets the most vulnerable communities that were cut off from aid and relief services, providing off-grid renewable electricity to community centers where residents can access power for critical needs during disasters and grid outages.[^2][^3]

## Technical Components

The basic hardware consists of 20 solar panels at 350 watts per panel, generating enough energy to operate a community center with off-the-grid renewable electricity, eliminating the need for fossil fuel-burning generators.[^3] The installations include two Tesla Powerwall batteries, one for backup, each capable of supplying 5 to 6 kWh.[^10] These batteries are super-efficient, occupy very little space, and hang on existing block walls to preserve space for community activities.[^10] The systems provide power for refrigeration, lights, fans, and charging stations for cell phones and other small electronic devices.[^2] RPPR studied the systems that survived Hurricane Maria and determined optimal construction methods, including upgrading bolt diameter from ¼ inch to ⅜ inch, using specific racking systems, and building small parapets or knee walls around the panels.[^6] The organization also developed a mobile emergency management trailer featuring 6 SimpliPhi 'PHI 3.5' Lithium Ferro Phosphate batteries totaling 21 kilowatt-hours, connected to a 5-kilowatt solar array on the roof and an 8-kilowatt inverter.[^8] After Hurricane Ida in August 2021, this trailer was outfitted with additional solar panels stored inside that could be deployed on the ground to increase capacity from 5kW initially to roughly 20kW.[^8]

## Implementation Details

RPPR completed 28 community solar-storage systems installations across the island in the first 11 months of operation, with 38 more in the works.[^7] In the first year, the organization raised money for 25 solar hubs, largely from the diaspora of Puerto Ricans living in New York and Silicon Valley, and that wave of 25 was almost finished.[^3] The initiative received grants of $625,000 to construct 25 of a planned 100 small, commercial-scale solar arrays.[^10] RPPR's Community Solar Energy Initiative (CSEI) deployed 30 solar energy systems to community centers across 22 municipalities in Puerto Rico, including Vieques and Culebra.[^11] The organization partnered with community leaders to assess critical energy needs and identify sites where solar energy systems would generate the greatest impact in communities most impacted by Hurricane Maria.[^11] CSEI facilitated the donation of over 983 solar panels to 30 community groups island-wide as of September 2019.[^11] The organization initially aimed to bring solar power to 100 sites in 100 days, but limited resources—namely, a lack of access to necessary equipment—hindered progress.[^6] The island's limited retail solar infrastructure made it difficult to buy batteries and panels due to very limited availability and long wait times.[^6] RPPR established a warehouse in the small inland city of Caguas to serve as an office, workshop, and educational venue for people to learn about solar.[^6] Key partners include BoxPower, Sunrun, Direct Relief, Blue Planet Energy, SimpliPhi Power, Sonnen, and multiple solar equipment manufacturers and foundations.[^2] The emergency management trailer was developed by Footprint Project in partnership with the Oficina Municipal para el Manejo de Emergencias, with Sail Relief providing logistical and installation support and Puerto Rico Science, Technology and Research Trust providing a safe, fenced area during assembly.[^8] The trailer frame was shipped from Florida and the solar+storage system was installed in San Juan because aluminum was expensive to source in Puerto Rico and there were capacity limitations for welding the trailer locally.[^8] Four volunteers spent at least 80 hours assembling the trailer after it arrived in Puerto Rico.[^8] The inverter cost $4,800 after a discount, and the custom racking totaled $6,200, which was the most expensive line item of the project.[^8] The project benefitted from over $16,000 in donated equipment, with SimpliPhi donating the batteries and Rent.Solar donating the solar modules.[^8]

## Benefits and Impacts

After Hurricane Fiona arrived on September 18, 2022, community members found access to clean water, food, and hygiene supplies at the Resilience Hubs, with power from solar panels and large batteries which powered wifi, charging stations, and refrigeration for medication and essential items.[^1] These Resilience Hubs are prepared to respond in any disaster and can support approximately 2,000 people in each community.[^1] The high-density informal neighborhoods served by the hubs are communities of two, three, or four thousand people that can be serviced with one solar hub.[^3] More than two weeks after Hurricane Fiona, 10% of potable water customers were without access to drinking water, and about 30% of Puerto Ricans (266,000 people) were still without power, demonstrating the critical need for resilient power systems.[^1] The solar systems pay for themselves in about five years based on the electricity being produced offset against the cost of that electricity.[^3] The Vieques Emergency Management Trailer has been utilized by the Vieques emergency management office since 2020.[^4] The trailer was operational and used through the outage in Vieques after Hurricane Fiona.[^4] Shortly after its completion, the trailer was deployed to a water pump in Vieques that lost power during major earthquakes in the southern part of Puerto Rico.[^8] During significant island-wide outages, the trailer is generally stationed at the Vieques OMME office to support emergency operations and communications for state and municipal police, firefighters, paramedics and other first responders.[^8] The trailer can back up the main breaker panel of the Vieques OMME office building and power critical loads including lighting, 2 mini split heat pumps, 4 desktop computers, a radio repeater, refrigerator, and coffee maker.[^8] The solar arrays are not enough to run an air conditioner all day, but will run water filters, pumps, and charging stations for phones and radios.[^10]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Hurricane Maria made landfall in Puerto Rico on September 20, 2017, as a Category 4 storm, causing widespread devastation and leaving the entire island without power.[^2] The Puerto Rico Electric Power Authority (PREPA) estimated that it could take up to a year to restore power to the entire island following Hurricane Maria.[^2] Hurricane Fiona made landfall in Puerto Rico on September 18, 2022, deluging the island with upwards of 30 inches of rain within 24 hours and leaving all residents without electricity.[^1] The mobile solar-electric systems provide critical resilience infrastructure that enables communities to maintain power for essential services during hurricanes, extreme precipitation events, and grid failures that increasingly threaten Puerto Rico's vulnerable populations.[^1][^2]

## Business Analysis

RPPR's three-phase strategy includes relief hubs for the hardest hit and most remote communities, 100 mobile solar kits distributed one each to the 78 municipalities and multiple neighborhoods in bigger municipalities, and ultimately solar electric energy for every household on the island.[^5] The organization's goal is ultimately to have 200 community centers operating with solar panels and batteries to produce and store power simultaneously.[^3] The initiative targets poor people and those in greatest need, allowing the last in line to get power on a decentralized basis.[^3] RPPR partnered with Rocky Mountain Institute (RMI) to launch The Puerto Rico Renewable Microgrid Program, a capacity building program aimed at increasing communities' capacity to assess and address critical energy needs.[^11] Through the CSEI program, RPPR led multiple community-focused workshops on solar energy in Puerto Rico and collaborated with over 15 academic programs in the United States researching topics in island resilience and sustainability.[^11] Mercy Corps supplied 17 Resilience Hubs with solar energy, potable water storage, communication systems, and emergency kits, and has been present in Puerto Rico since the Hurricane Maria emergency in 2017.[^1] In the four months before Hurricane Fiona, Mercy Corps worked closely with Resilience Hub partners and reached one million people across the region through a disaster preparedness campaign.[^1] The organization used a planning study done about 25 years ago by planner Lucilla Fuller Marvel to identify the island's informal communities, which researched and documented who was living where for the first time in the island's history.[^3] RPPR planned to install 100 panels on top of the warehouse in Caguas to operate off the grid and offer that as a prototype.[^6] Clean Energy Group provided technical support and funded the initial feasibility assessment for the emergency management trailer.[^8] The initial resilient power project planned for Vieques was a permanently installed solar+storage system on the OMME office, but due to permitting and municipal approval issues, a permanent installation became increasingly unlikely, leading to the pivot to a mobile solar+storage system that sidestepped interconnection and bureaucratic issues.[^8]

## Sources

# Research Report: Rockaway Peninsula Gas Infrastructure Resilience Program

## Solution Overview

National Grid commenced a program in the Rockaway Peninsula to make its gas system more resilient through accelerated replacement of leak-prone mains and installation of automated meters and other devices.[^3][^4] This infrastructure modernization initiative emerged as a direct response to Superstorm Sandy, which rendered sections of cast iron and low-pressure infrastructure useless, filled with sand and cracked in many places throughout the Rockaway Peninsula area.[^1]

## Technical Components

The initiative employs a robot to seal cast-iron pipes and recondition steel mains from the inside.[^2] The program includes installation of automated meters and other devices as part of the comprehensive infrastructure upgrade.[^3] Under an agreement approved by the New York Public Service Commission (PSC), National Grid partnered with EDF and Google to pilot methane reduction programs that gather and analyze methane data to prioritize system investments and leak repairs.[^2] A pilot project two years prior identified close to a thousand low-level leaks in National Grid's Staten Island service territory alone, demonstrating the scale of monitoring capabilities.[^2]

## Implementation Details

National Grid estimated it would need to reinvest $200 million in infrastructure repairs and upgrades in the wake of Superstorm Sandy, with the worst damage occurring in Rockaway Beach and Staten Island where 27 miles of mains had to be replaced.[^1] The accelerated infrastructure replacement program is expected to generate 380 new jobs, according to the PSC, most of them in the New York City metro area.[^2] The agreement approved by the PSC provides penalties for National Grid if they fail to meet their replacement and leak repair targets, and incentives if they beat them.[^2] This broader recovery effort was part of $15 billion in federal grants appropriated for Sandy recovery and resilience, of which the City had spent $11 billion, or 73%, as of June 2022.[^5]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

In the Rockaway Peninsula area, National Grid's isolation strategy proved especially effective as sections of cast iron and low-pressure infrastructure were rendered useless, filled with sand and cracked in many places during Superstorm Sandy.[^1] This approach directly addresses vulnerabilities to extreme weather events and coastal flooding that can compromise underground gas infrastructure systems.

## Business Analysis

The $200 million infrastructure investment represents a substantial capital commitment to climate resilience infrastructure in the wake of extreme weather damage.[^1] The PSC-approved agreement establishes a performance-based financial model with penalties for National Grid if they fail to meet replacement and leak repair targets, and incentives if they exceed them, creating accountability mechanisms for infrastructure delivery.[^2] The program benefits from federal grant funding allocated for Sandy recovery and resilience efforts.[^5] The initiative's workforce development component, generating 380 new jobs primarily in the New York City metro area, demonstrates the economic co-benefits of climate adaptation infrastructure investments.[^2]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://undergroundinfrastructure.com/magazine/2014/june-2014-vol-69-no-6/features/national-grid-executive-recounts-superstorm-sandy-recovery-process
- [^2]: https://www.edf.org/media/national-grid-will-use-solutions-developed-environmental-defense-fund-and-google-guide-3
- [^3]: https://buildingdecarb.org/wp-content/uploads/BDC-The-Future-of-Gas-in-NYS.pdf
- [^4]: https://www.utilitydive.com/news/northeast-utilities-are-spending-billions-on-resilience-and-the-investment/608378/
- [^5]: https://comptroller.nyc.gov/reports/ten-years-after-sandy/

# Research Report: Urban Tree Canopy Mapping with Geospatial Analysis Software

## Solution Overview

The City of Boulder partnered with 100RC Platform Partners Trimble and DigitalGlobe to map the urban tree canopy, addressing the need to develop a more robust set of urban forest management tools.[^1] Trimble provides geospatial technical solutions and domains to capture, measure, analyze, monitor and share built and environmental information.[^1] This partnership established a baseline urban tree canopy—the first step to advancing tree-canopy development and management strategies.[^2]

## Technical Components

Trimble's eCognition® software integrates geospatial imagery and GIS datasets, and analyzes the data to produce land-cover classifications of specific object types such as trees or buildings.[^2] The software can be programmed to seek out healthy or damaged canopy, allowing officials in Boulder to judge the efficacy of conservation projects.[^4] Trimble presented an initial citywide urban tree canopy analysis and provided free training to city staff on the use of the eCognition software necessary to map changes to the urban tree canopy over time.[^1] The eventual goal is for Boulder to be able to use tools like eCognition on their own, with Trimble providing initial training and support.[^4]

## Implementation Details

Boulder was the first city to work with Trimble through the 100 Resilient Cities partnership.[^4] The urban tree canopy analysis will be published in Boulder's Open Data Catalog.[^1] Trimble and DigitalGlobe are assessing how to replicate and offer similar outcomes to other municipalities based on the work done and lessons learned with Boulder.[^1] This implementation was complemented by a separate heat mapping initiative in which volunteer community scientists traversed their neighborhoods in the morning, afternoon and evening on one of the hottest days of 2022 collecting heat data, which was later analyzed by city staff and local climate scientists.[^3] Boulder was one of several communities chosen to participate in NOAA's Urban Heat Island Mapping Campaign.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The heat mapping data shows that areas with very little vegetation and a high proportion of impervious surfaces—such as roads and parking areas—were significantly hotter, by as much as 17 degrees.[^3] This quantitative evidence demonstrates the critical role of tree canopy in mitigating urban heat island effects. The eCognition software enables officials to monitor canopy health over time and evaluate the effectiveness of conservation initiatives.[^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This geospatial mapping approach directly addresses urban heat island effects by identifying areas with insufficient vegetation cover and elevated temperatures. The baseline urban tree canopy data provides essential information for strategic tree planting and canopy expansion efforts to reduce heat exposure in vulnerable neighborhoods. The integration of heat mapping with tree canopy analysis enables targeted climate adaptation interventions in areas experiencing temperature differentials of up to 17 degrees.[^3]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://apogeospatial.com/the-power-of-trees-decreasing-the-carbon-footprint/
- [^3]: https://bouldercolorado.gov/projects/cooling-our-community
- [^4]: https://nextcity.org/urbanist-news/boulder-resilience-software-100-resilient-cities-rockefeller

# Research Report: Downscaled Regional Climate Modeling for Local Projections

## Solution Overview

The City of Austin's Office of Sustainability collaborated with ATMOS Research, led by Dr. Katharine Hayhoe, to develop geographically specific climate models for Austin through 2100 using the Camp Mabry Weather Station.[^1] Dr. Hayhoe, director of the Climate Science Center at Texas Tech University and chief executive officer of ATMOS Research & Consulting, pulled national modeling data she developed for the 2014 Third National Climate Assessment and scaled down the information to apply locally to Austin.[^2] Global climate model simulations were downscaled to the Austin Camp Mabry weather station using the Asynchronous Regional Regression Model to address local uncertainty resulting from factors that determine how Austin's climate will respond to global scale changes over the coming century.[^1][^2][^5]

## Technical Components

The modeling approach utilized simulations from nine different global climate models to address scientific uncertainty around how much the Earth will warm in response to human emissions and the limitations of global climate models in perfectly representing every aspect of Earth's climate.[^1] The projections were based on two different scenarios for future greenhouse gas emissions known as Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs), used by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) to explore a range of possible futures.[^3] These preliminary projections for Austin were based on a lower scenario where global carbon emissions peak and then decline by end of century, and a higher scenario where continued dependence on fossil fuels means that carbon emissions continue to grow throughout the century.[^1] Historical data in this analysis came from the weather station at Camp Mabry in Austin for the period 1971-2000, with records indicating a summertime average temperature of approximately 84°F and a wintertime average temperature of approximately 53°F from 1943 through 2012.[^3][^1] Climate projections for Austin were averaged over 30-year time scales to address natural variability, which can cause climate variations from year to year or even decade to decade.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The projections developed through this initiative are for the Austin area for the period 2041-2070.[^3] Austin Water is working with a team of climate scientists to develop a set of climate change scenarios for the region, and these scenarios will be used to model the potential effects of climate change on the Highland Lakes and to evaluate the effectiveness of different water management strategies under a range of future climate conditions.[^4]

[^1]: Source document 1
[^2]: Source document 2
[^3]: Source document 3
[^4]: Source document 4
[^5]: Source document 5

# Research Report: CityCore Integrated Urban Governance Platform

## Solution Overview

Shenzhen's Futian District launched an integrated governance platform in April 2022, powered by CityCore, Huawei Cloud Stack's Smart City solution.[^5] This comprehensive system integrates multiple specialized subsystems including smart street management, smart emergency response, smart fire protection, and smart violation detection systems, all operating through a centralized command center.[^2] The platform was recognized at the China Region Award Ceremony for the World's Smart City Expo Congress 2022, where Futian District received the 'World Smart City Award', outcompeting 126 peer smart city projects from 44 cities.[^5]

## Technical Components

The CityCore solution consists of five core platforms: an integration platform, AI platform, CIM platform, blockchain platform, and online video platform.[^5] The online video platform consolidates video resources across all of Futian District, ingesting over 100,000 channels of video and providing 10,000 channels for concurrent video parsing on the AI platform.[^5] The AI platform provides intelligent computing, analysis, and digital services for various departments, supporting multi-scenario applications such as intelligent video discovery, grid patrol, and quick public opinion handling.[^3][^4] The system integrates data from cameras, sensors, alarms and other relevant sources from various government departments to build a comprehensive database on economic activities, public safety, communities' services, transportation, and environmental protection.[^2] The smart street management system has been integrated with data on security monitoring, environmental protection, urban management and other departments.[^2] Encryption technologies have been adopted to protect data from leaks during transmission or storage in AI government systems.[^1] The AI Hub serves as the core of Futian's transformation, helping build a digital twin of the city, intelligent sensors and smart applications.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The platform was launched in April 2022 as part of Futian District's comprehensive digital governance transformation.[^5] The Futian District Smart City Command Center operates as the 'brain of the district', integrating an emergency command centre, operations management centre and comprehensive governance centre.[^2] The implementation established a transparent governance system integrating all four administrative levels from city, district, street, to community through comprehensive event integration and full-cycle governance.[^2] Using the CityCore platform, Futian District built a comprehensive metric system, categorized by five different domains and 10 major topics, that has been embedded into the websites of all its street-level offices and functional departments to enable real-time monitoring of the entire district.[^5] The district launched 'Xiaofu,' an AI assistant for handling government hotline inquiries in July 2023.[^1] To avoid offloading responsibility entirely onto AI, Shenzhen assigned each AI employee a human supervisor, who makes the final decision on each case.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The platform achieved dramatic efficiency improvements, increasing incident distribution efficiency from 4 minutes per enquiry to 50 seconds per enquiry, while the accuracy of intelligent distribution reached 90 percent.[^3][^4] AI assistants achieve over 95 percent accuracy in processing paperwork, reducing review time by 90 percent, according to Li Xiaoming, data management director for the digital governance department of Shenzhen's Futian District.[^1] The smart emergency disposal system links with the daily distribution and disposal system, efficiently handling all kinds of emergencies.[^2] When Internet of Things monitoring equipment verifies there is a fire, the system sounds the alarm and notifies the property management team, automatically notifies the fire brigade and relevant leaders, sends the police fire truck along the fastest traffic route to the best parking position, provides the building map and resident personnel contact information, and monitors the situation in real time.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The integrated platform directly addresses extreme precipitation and flooding hazards through its smart emergency response capabilities. In case of heavy rain, the system evaluates the precipitation forecast, analyzes historical flood data and real-time monitoring from IoT devices, then issues early warnings to areas at risk of waterlogging and notifies emergency personnel.[^2] This automated early warning and response coordination system enables rapid mobilization of resources to vulnerable areas before flooding occurs, reducing response times and protecting communities from climate-related disasters.

## Business Analysis

The implementation represents a comprehensive public sector investment in digital governance infrastructure, with Huawei Cloud Stack serving as the primary technology partner providing the CityCore solution.[^5] The platform's architecture supports scalable deployment across multiple administrative levels, from city to community, enabling replication potential for other municipalities seeking integrated governance solutions.[^2] The recognition at the World's Smart City Expo Congress 2022 positions Futian District as a reference model for smart city implementations globally, potentially creating knowledge transfer and consulting opportunities.[^5] The system's demonstrated efficiency gains—reducing incident processing time by 83 percent and achieving 90 percent distribution accuracy—provide quantifiable return on investment metrics that support business case development for similar implementations.[^3][^4]

## Sources

# Research Report: NotifyChicago Emergency Alert and Community Notification System

## Solution Overview

NotifyChicago is a free municipal service that delivers text message and email alerts to residents regarding both emergency and non-emergency situations.[^7] This mass notification system represents the next phase of AlertChicago, serving as a positive and proactive initiative for educating and informing communities on how to protect themselves and their families from harm.[^2] The platform provides alerts on incidents and conditions including severe weather emergencies, hazardous materials, traffic impacts, and various situations taking place throughout Chicago.[^1][^4]

## Technical Components

The system operates through Rave Alert, a mass notification platform developed by Rave Mobile Safety, a Motorola Solutions brand, with which the City of Chicago had already built a relationship prior to implementing this service.[^5][^6] The technology enables the city's designated personnel to send text messages and email alerts directly to registered phones, emails, pagers, and fax machines.[^2] The platform also provides telephone notification capabilities in addition to text and email delivery methods.[^3] This approach functions as the Office of Emergency Management and Communications' public messaging notification system, integrated into OEMC's broader communications infrastructure.[^1]

## Implementation Details

Residents can register for the service at NotifyChicago.org, where they sign up to receive emergency alerts through their preferred communication channels.[^1] The Office of Emergency Management and Communications operates this system and urges residents to subscribe to receive alerts sent directly to mobile phones or email accounts to stay informed.[^1] The OEMC disseminates preparedness messages through multiple channels including radio and print campaigns, press conferences and releases, social media, and community engagement throughout Chicago, in addition to this notification system.[^1] The Chicago OEMC app is one of only four apps in the state of Illinois from a government authority.[^6] Signing up for the service is free for all residents.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

This approach provides residents with comprehensive situational awareness across a spectrum of conditions, from traffic disruptions to weather emergencies to hazardous materials situations.[^2] The system enables direct communication between municipal emergency personnel and registered residents, ensuring timely delivery of critical information during both routine and emergency events.[^2] The multi-channel notification capability allows residents to receive alerts through their preferred communication method, whether telephone, text message, email, pager, or fax.[^2][^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The notification system directly addresses climate-related hazards by providing alerts for severe weather emergencies, enabling residents to receive advance warning and protective action guidance during extreme weather events.[^1][^4] This communication infrastructure supports community resilience by ensuring residents can access timely information about emergency situations that may result from climate-driven hazards affecting Chicago.

## Business Analysis

The City of Chicago leveraged its existing relationship with Rave Mobile Safety, a Motorola Solutions brand, to implement this mass notification capability.[^5][^6] The free service model eliminates financial barriers to resident participation, supporting broad community adoption without subscription fees.[^2] The platform operates as part of the Office of Emergency Management and Communications' broader public safety infrastructure, representing a municipal investment in emergency preparedness and community resilience.[^1] The Chicago OEMC app represents a relatively unique government technology deployment within Illinois, with only four such applications from government authorities operating statewide.[^6]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/depts/oem/provdrs/edu/svcs/sign_up_for_notifychicago.html
- [^3]: https://its.cmap.illinois.gov/html/inv/el256.htm
- [^4]: https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/depts/oem/provdrs/alertchicago/svcs/notifychicago.html
- [^5]: https://www.ravemobilesafety.com/customer-success-stories/putting-power-hands-people-emergency-information-app/

# Research Report: LPWA Sensor Network for Bridge Structural Monitoring

## Solution Overview

Toyama has implemented a comprehensive sensor network infrastructure to monitor critical municipal assets, including bridges in remote mountainous areas.[^1] The Toyama City Sensor Network consists of an LPWA (Low Power Wide Area Wireless Technology) network that aggregates information from various sensors, with antennas installed in about 100 public facilities in the city covering 98.9% of the resident population.[^1] This infrastructure enables real-time monitoring of structural integrity and automated warning systems for public safety.

## Technical Components

The network utilizes LPWA technology to aggregate information from various sensors and includes a platform that manages data collected from IoT sensors.[^1] Sensors were placed on a bridge in a mountainous area about 20 km away from the Toyama city centre, where indicator lights installed on-site blink to warn users if an abnormality such as an opening or a break occurs in the structure.[^1] An AI-based inspection system was deployed at Yatsuo Ohashi bridge that uses video images to estimate the weights of vehicles traversing the bridge and the resulting structural deflection caused by the vehicles.[^2][^3] The AI then analyzes the data to estimate the bridge's structural integrity and degradation.[^2][^3]

## Implementation Details

The LPWA network was developed in 2018, with antennas installed in approximately 100 public facilities throughout the city.[^1] In FY2019, Toyama started a demonstration experiment that allows private businesses to utilise the sensor network free of charge, with 23 case studies (36 groups) adopted from fields including transportation/people flow, agriculture, remote monitoring/disaster prevention, welfare/nursing care, and performance evaluation/facility management.[^1] NTT DOCOMO and Kyoto University deployed the AI-based bridge inspection system on a trial basis at Yatsuo Ohashi bridge in Toyama Prefecture between December 9, 2019 and September 30, 2020.[^2][^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The bridge monitoring system was demonstrated to lead to labour saving in confirming work needed on bridges, otherwise difficult for city officials to patrol and inspect daily.[^1] The automated warning system provides immediate on-site alerts through indicator lights when structural abnormalities are detected, enhancing public safety for bridge users.[^1] The AI-based inspection approach enables continuous assessment of structural integrity without requiring manual inspections of remote infrastructure.[^2][^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This monitoring infrastructure addresses climate-related risks to critical transportation infrastructure in mountainous terrain, where extreme weather events can cause structural damage to bridges. The real-time detection capabilities enable rapid response to structural degradation that may result from increased precipitation, flooding, or other climate-induced stresses on aging infrastructure.

## Business Analysis

The demonstration experiment model allows private businesses to utilise the sensor network free of charge, attracting applications from diverse sectors including transportation, agriculture, disaster prevention, welfare, and facility management.[^1] This public-private partnership approach enabled 23 case studies involving 36 groups to test applications across multiple industries in FY2019.[^1] The collaboration between NTT DOCOMO and Kyoto University for the AI-based bridge inspection system demonstrates how municipal infrastructure monitoring can leverage partnerships with telecommunications providers and academic institutions to deploy advanced technologies.[^2][^3]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.jlgc.org.uk/en/news_letter/toyama-citys-smart-city-sensor-network/
- [^2]: https://www.docomo.ne.jp/english/info/media_center/pr/2019/1205_00.html
- [^3]: https://www.kyoto-u.ac.jp/en/research-news/2019-12-06

# Research Report: Household Energy Independence Through Microgeneration & Microgrids

## Solution Overview

Wellington City Council is investigating options for incentivising uptake of technology that will build household energy independence, which could include microgeneration, battery packs, solar roofs, small-scale wind, tidal energy and neighbourhood microgrids.[^2][^3] This approach can generate economic opportunity, positioning Wellington as a leader in alternative technologies and building on the city's existing reputation as a low-carbon city.[^2] A more decentralised energy network that includes multiple small-scale generation sources will perform better in an earthquake or a storm and will have an added benefit of reducing reliance on fossil fuels in Wellington.[^2]

## Technical Components

Microgrids are self-sufficient and can operate independently or in conjunction with the larger grid.[^4] These systems can run on different types of renewable energy sources, including solar, wind and hydro power.[^4] In New Zealand, most residential homes would use a solar system between 1.5kW – 4kW.[^1] P2P energy systems allow individuals and communities to generate, share and trade energy among themselves, creating a decentralised energy market and allowing for more efficient energy use and distribution.[^4] The Government expanded the permitted voltage range for New Zealand's electricity networks from +/- 6% to +/- 10% to help manage the changing flow of electricity sent back to the grid from rooftop solar, as well as growing levels of electric vehicle (EV) charging.[^5] Modelling suggests that expanding the voltage range could boost solar investment and overall generation by 507 GWh through increased solar connections.[^5]

## Implementation Details

Wellington City Council does not require a building consent for most solar panel installations, provided the work is undertaken by a suitably qualified person who provides an Energy Works Certificate for the work.[^1] The Government clarified that a building consent is not needed to install rooftop solar panels on existing residential buildings.[^5] Councils are required to process building consents for new homes with solar panels within 10 working days, down from the standard 20 working days.[^5] A 3kW grid-connected solar power system costs around $8,000 in New Zealand and is popular because it helps reduce electricity costs while remaining relatively affordable.[^1] A panel-only no battery 4kWh solar system costs around $10,000.[^1] Battery systems for a standard house cost around $10,000 in New Zealand, and these prices are expected to reduce.[^1] The Wellington City Council will work with Wellington Electricity and the Commerce Commission to investigate incorporating network resilience in price setting mechanisms as part of making the energy network less vulnerable.[^2] Microgrids often require significant amounts of land for the installation of renewable energy sources, such as solar panels or wind turbines, and some communities may resist development due to concerns over land use, visual impact, or potential disruption to wildlife habitats.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

Research from Te Herenga Waka—Victoria University of Wellington shows that microgrids, both grid-connected and off-grid, can be cost effective when optimised and in Aotearoa New Zealand can be on par or even more cost effective than traditional power.[^4] Case studies from Aotea Great Barrier Island, Rakiura Stewart Island and the town of Ohakune in New Zealand demonstrate that implementation of microgrids and P2P energy systems has the potential to improve energy resilience and save communities money in their electricity bills.[^4] 82 percent of Wellington's electricity comes from renewable sources, chiefly from a windfarm located within the boundary of Wellington City.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

A more decentralised network that includes multiple small-scale generation sources will perform better in an earthquake or a storm.[^2] This approach directly addresses Wellington's vulnerability to natural disasters by creating energy systems that can continue operating independently when the main grid is disrupted, enhancing the city's overall resilience to climate-related extreme weather events and seismic activity.

## Business Analysis

Research demonstrates that microgrids can be cost effective when optimised and in Aotearoa New Zealand can be on par or even more cost effective than traditional power.[^4] The Government has implemented regulatory frameworks to support solar adoption, including clarifying that building consents are not needed for rooftop solar panels on existing residential buildings and requiring councils to process building consents for new homes with solar panels within 10 working days.[^5] Wellington's approach can generate economic opportunity, positioning the city as a leader in alternative technologies and building on its existing reputation as a low-carbon city.[^2] Battery system prices for standard houses, currently around $10,000, are expected to reduce, improving the financial viability of household energy independence solutions.[^1] The Council will work with Wellington Electricity and the Commerce Commission to investigate incorporating network resilience in price setting mechanisms, potentially creating financial incentives for decentralised energy adoption.[^2]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://wellington.govt.nz/climate-change-sustainability-environment/sustainable-living/sustainable-homes-and-buildings/renewable-energy/solar-power
- [^2]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Asia & Asia Pacific Oceania/Wellington-New-Zealand/Wellington-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^3]: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12054575/
- [^4]: https://www.wgtn.ac.nz/news/2023/02/cyclone-gabrielle-how-microgrids-could-help-keep-the-power-on-during-extreme-weather-events
- [^5]: https://www.mbie.govt.nz/about/news/supercharging-residential-solar-power

# Research Report: Tidal Monitoring Station Network Expansion

## Solution Overview

Broward County is pursuing an expansion of tidal monitoring stations to provide operationally-sound observations and monitoring capabilities throughout the region.[^3] This initiative aims to enhance data collection infrastructure for tracking critical parameters including water quality, hydrologic conditions, groundwater levels, and precipitation patterns.[^3] The approach addresses the need for improved flood monitoring and emergency response capabilities in a region where high tide flooding in low-lying areas may occur when tidal levels exceed 1.4 ft NAVD88, below the official NOAA threshold of 1.87 ft NAVD88 at South Port Everglades station.[^1]

## Technical Components

The monitoring infrastructure builds upon existing NOAA stations at South Port Everglades, which began operations in 2018, and Lake Worth Pier, which started monitoring in 2010.[^1] The expanded network will support long-term continuous data collection of critical parameters including evapotranspiration in urban areas, water quality with emphasis on temperature, hydrologic and geologic conditions, groundwater quality and levels, precipitation, and groundwater withdrawals.[^3] Complementary subsurface monitoring systems led by Florida International University are being established in Southeast Florida's near-shore and barrier island environments to collect high spatial and temporal resolution data of near-shore saltwater interfaces, water table positions, unsaturated zone moisture conditions and dynamics, and corrosion potential.[^6] The data collection supports modeling efforts aligned with the Southeast Florida Regional Climate Change Compact's Unified Sea Level Rise Projection, which uses 2015 as the starting point for projecting future sea level rise and recommends using NOAA High and USACE High curves for critical infrastructure.[^5]

## Implementation Details

Broward County is identifying funding and partnerships to support the tidal monitoring expansion, with plans to encourage dedicated county, state, and federal funding for modeling efforts that improve knowledge of climate change impacts.[^3] A comparable implementation in Lee County, Florida, demonstrates a viable pathway through public-private partnerships. Fort Myers, which routinely makes headlines for extreme flooding, possessed only one tide gauge until 2024.[^4] Lee County's expansion was conducted through a public-private partnership with Hawaii-based tide technology company Hohonu and funded by Lee County's All Hazards Emergency Fund.[^4] Fort Myers Director of Public Safety Ben Abes attributed the partnership's creation to the 60-year wait for federal tide gauge resources.[^4] As of June 4, 2025, at least 14 tide gauges are in operation throughout Lee County.[^4] In June 2025, local news reported that Lee County had scaled up the availability of tide gauge sensors to help state agencies gather more specific data about local flooding.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

The expanded monitoring network enables expedited emergency alerts for extreme flooding throughout affected counties—a capability likely to save lives as the 2025 hurricane season progresses.[^4] Enhanced data collection supports improved modeling efforts and climate indicator tracking across multiple environmental parameters.[^3] The monitoring infrastructure provides critical information for regions where Fort Lauderdale's seven miles of shoreline and 300+ miles of waterway coastline create extensive vulnerability to coastal flooding.[^2] Most residents of Fort Lauderdale live less than five feet above sea level, making them increasingly vulnerable to tidal flooding as sea levels continue to rise.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This monitoring expansion directly addresses sea level rise impacts and tidal flooding risks in a region where average global sea level has risen by about eight inches over the last 150 years, with current projections expecting an additional 9 to 24 inches of sea level rise within 50 years.[^2] The enhanced observational capabilities support Fort Lauderdale's updated seawall ordinance (ULDR Section 47-19.3), adopted on March 23, 2023, which established regionally-consistent construction standards setting minimum top elevations for seawalls and tidal barriers to ensure both public and privately owned structures contribute to coastal resilience and mitigate the effects of tidal flooding and sea level rise.[^2]

## Business Analysis

The Lee County model demonstrates that public-private partnerships with specialized tide technology companies can overcome decades-long delays in federal resource allocation, with funding secured through dedicated emergency preparedness funds.[^4] The All Hazards Emergency Fund financing mechanism provides a replicable model for Broward County's expansion efforts.[^4] Market dynamics favor partnerships with established tide monitoring technology providers like Hohonu, which has demonstrated capability to rapidly scale sensor networks from single stations to comprehensive county-wide coverage.[^4] Policy support exists through the Southeast Florida Regional Climate Change Compact's unified planning framework and through municipal ordinances like Fort Lauderdale's updated seawall standards that create regulatory drivers for enhanced monitoring data.[^2][^5] The business model leverages multi-level government funding sources, with Broward County actively pursuing county, state, and federal funding streams to support both monitoring infrastructure and the modeling efforts that depend on improved observational data.[^3]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.broward.org/resilience/localconditions/Pages/KingTides.aspx
- [^2]: https://www.fortlauderdale.gov/government/departments-i-z/parks-recreation/sustainability/sustainability-climate-resilience/climate-resiliency
- [^3]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Broward-US/CCAP_2020_ADA.pdf
- [^4]: https://www.eesi.org/articles/view/expanding-tide-gauge-networks-helps-hurricane-stricken-communities-keep-their-heads-above-water
- [^5]: https://1000fof.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/08/1409-broward-sea-level-final.pdf
- [^6]: https://coastalscience.noaa.gov/project/coastal-subsurface-water-table-and-saltwater-intrusion-monitoring-systems/

# Research Report: Storm Drainage Master Plan with Climate-Integrated Modeling

## Solution Overview

The City of Oakland initiated development of an updated Storm Drainage Master Plan that incorporates a comprehensive asset management system and state-of-the-art modeling to evaluate system performance under different storm scenarios while integrating precipitation changes and sea level rise projections.[^2] This initiative addresses the critical need to modernize Oakland's aging infrastructure, as the existing 2006 SDMP had fallen out of date and the storm drainage system required urgent maintenance, repairs, and upgrades.[^2] The updated plan is designed as a living document that will be continuously updated to serve as a vital tool for guiding investment in the City's storm drainage system.[^2]

## Technical Components

The updated master plan incorporates state-of-the-art modeling capabilities that evaluate how the storm drainage system performs under different storm scenarios while incorporating precipitation changes and sea level rise due to climate change.[^2] This technical approach includes a comprehensive asset management system designed to track and manage the city's storm drainage infrastructure.[^2] The modeling framework will support identification of critical maintenance and improvement projects that reduce potentially costly and dangerous flooding.[^2] The City plans to facilitate community engagement through a partnership with an online engagement platform, enabling residents of Oakland neighborhoods to provide information on factors affecting localized flooding and map hot spots for creek or coastal-caused flooding they are already experiencing in their neighborhoods.[^2]

## Implementation Details

The City of Oakland awarded a Professional Services Agreement to Wood Rodgers for engineering services for the Storm Drainage Master Plan Project No. 1005274 in an amount not to exceed three million three hundred ninety-one thousand dollars ($3,391,000).[^3] This agreement was adopted by the Concurrent Meeting of the Oakland Redevelopment Successor Agency and the City Council on November 10, 2020.[^3] The City appropriated $34,332.00 from Development Service Fund 2415 Fund Balance for the project.[^4] The timeframe for the Storm Drainage Master Plan update was designated as near-term, spanning 1 to 2 years.[^2] The Public Works Department leads the initiative, partnering with the Alameda County Flood Control and Water Conservation District.[^2] The Public Works Department was working to identify additional funding sources for the master plan update.[^2] The City plans to explore using an equity checklist to guide the project prioritization process, potentially overlaying socio-economic data with modeling from flooding hotspots and other known stormwater problem areas.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

The updated master plan will be used to identify critical maintenance and improvement projects that will reduce potentially costly and dangerous flooding throughout the city.[^2] The equity-focused prioritization approach addresses the reality that flooding hotspots in Oakland typically coincide with low-income neighborhoods due to the city's topography.[^2] The community engagement platform will enable residents to contribute local knowledge about flooding patterns, creating a more comprehensive understanding of drainage system performance across different neighborhoods.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach directly addresses climate change impacts by incorporating precipitation changes and sea level rise projections into the storm drainage system modeling and planning process.[^2] The updated master plan evaluates how the infrastructure performs under different storm scenarios, enabling the city to prepare for increasingly severe weather events and coastal flooding risks.[^2]

## Business Analysis

The $3,391,000 professional services contract with Wood Rodgers represents the primary capital investment for developing the updated master plan.[^3] The City appropriated additional funding of $34,332.00 from the Development Service Fund 2415 Fund Balance to support project implementation.[^4] The Public Works Department's ongoing efforts to identify funding sources indicate the need for sustained financial support beyond initial planning phases.[^2] The equity checklist approach to project prioritization suggests that funding allocation will consider socio-economic factors alongside technical flooding assessments, potentially positioning the initiative for state and federal environmental justice grants.[^2] The Oakland Green Stormwater Infrastructure Plan, prepared by the Oakland Department of Transportation under Director Ryan Russo and published in September 2019, provides complementary planning context for stormwater management investments.[^1]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://cao-94612.s3.amazonaws.com/documents/Oakland-GSI-Plan-Final-20190930_sm.pdf
- [^2]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Oakland-US/Oakland-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^3]: https://oakland.legistar.com/LegislationDetail.aspx?ID=4681368&GUID=74E1C411-62CF-49E5-9113-6AEE49B4B399&G=undefined&Options=&Search=
- [^4]: https://oakland.legistar.com/gateway.aspx?m=l&id=/matter.aspx?key=36119

# Research Report: Data-Driven Tree Mapping Tool for Urban Forestry Prioritization

## Solution Overview

A data-driven tree mapping tool guides Chicago's urban forestry strategy by integrating multiple environmental, health, and social vulnerability datasets to identify priority areas for tree planting.[^1][^3][^4] The Chicago Department of Public Health developed this community tree tool with support from the Bloomberg Philanthropies Partnership for Healthy Cities, bringing together data on tree canopy, air quality, land surface temperatures, economic hardship, and other factors.[^4] This approach enables the city to work with local nonprofits and community groups to identify priority communities and locations to plant trees.[^4]

## Technical Components

The mapping tool overlays data on tree canopy with air quality, land surface temperatures, economic hardship, and other social vulnerability and environmental factors.[^4] The Morton Arboretum's Chicago Region Trees Initiative combined canopy cover, surface temperature, air quality, flood risk, health, and social vulnerability data into interactive map layers to identify where trees are needed most urgently.[^3][^7] Tree canopy data comes from a LiDAR-based analysis that can identify trees with less than a four-square-foot canopy.[^3] Temperature data were derived from a satellite image that was taken in September of 2021.[^3] The system will be used to identify areas that could benefit from increased canopy cover over the long term, as measured by three-dimensional LiDAR data.[^1] Individual trees are visible as dots on the map, allowing users to see differences between similar developments in different parts of Chicago.[^1] Data from the CDC Places dataset was used to map adult asthma rates across the region, showing the percentage of people 18 years and older who are affected by asthma.[^3] The vulnerable populations data set identifies census block groups that are the most vulnerable by distinguishing those with low incomes, non-English-speaking, and a high proportion of minority populations.[^3] To create the tool, the UChicago research team harmonized multiple datasets into a standardized format for evidence-based decision making.[^1]

## Implementation Details

Mayor Lori E. Lightfoot announced on Arbor Day a new tree equity initiative, Our Roots Chicago, which will plant 75,000 trees over the next five years under the $46 million Chicago Recovery Plan investment expanding Chicago's tree canopy across the city.[^4] The mapping tool will help shape Chicago's plan to spend $46 million planting approximately 75,000 new trees over the next five years, focusing in part on census tracts with relatively lower numbers of trees.[^1] The goal to plant 75,000 trees represents a doubling in tree planting over the previous year.[^4] The initiative is a partnership between the City of Chicago, including the Department of Streets and Sanitation and the Chicago Department of Public Health, and non-profit partners, including Morton Arboretum and Openlands.[^2] HeRoP partnered with Bloomberg Philanthropies, Vital Strategies, and the Chicago Department of Public Health to create the Tree Equity solution.[^9] Bloomberg Associates, a pro bono municipal consulting service, has also supported the development and rollout of the tree equity strategy.[^4] Chicago's Department of Public Health partnered with University of Chicago researchers including health geographer and data scientist Marynia Kolak to develop an advanced mapping tool that combines tree data with metrics related to health outcomes, including air quality, surface temperature and traffic volume.[^1] Dylan Halpern, a senior software engineer at HeRoP, co-led the development of the open-source tool with Kolak.[^1]

The identification of tree planting locations is also supported by a diverse 70-member community-based working group who share a commitment to helping the City expand Chicago's tree canopy and reduce tree removals.[^4] Our Roots Chicago created a 115-member tree equity working group to help identify priority community areas and set long-term goals for expanding the tree canopy.[^5] The Community Tree Ambassador program launched in May, which trains community residents on how to identify potential tree locations on City parkways.[^4][^6] Tree ambassadors are able to survey and submit potential tree planting sites from their mobile devices, providing an efficient way to have more trees planted in a shorter period of time.[^4] The ambassadors are also able to geolocate trees to monitor their progress and performance in priority community areas.[^4] Grow Greater Englewood was allocated $50,000 over two years as part of the Our Roots Chicago tree ambassador program, which they used to host two training events with about 30 people and request about 70 trees.[^5] Volunteers from the Southwest Collective requested more than 700 trees after being part of the Our Roots Chicago tree ambassador program for about a year, covering areas from Garfield Ridge to Archer Heights.[^5]

In 2023, the second year of Our Roots Chicago, the city planted 23,000 parkway trees, with about 8,600 planted in neighborhoods with the highest need.[^5] More than 41,000 trees have been planted in the past two years through Our Roots Chicago, according to data from the city.[^5] The city is on track to meet its goal of planting 75,000 parkway trees by 2026, according to spokesperson Mimi Simon.[^5] On the Southwest Side, the city planted about 500 trees in Brighton Park, 200 in Archer Heights and 90 in Gage Park in 2023 as part of Our Roots Chicago's 22 priority spots.[^5] Chicago recently passed a budget that allocated a total of $188 million in climate and environmental commitments—the most ever spent on the environment in Chicago.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

Working as part of UChicago's Healthy Regions + Policy Lab, Kolak and her colleagues found a relationship between health factors and the amount of canopy cover, with more trees associated with a range of positive outcomes, including better air quality and lower summer surface temperatures.[^1] Chicago's urban forest provides ecosystem services valued at $785.7 million annually in 2020, including $740 million in carbon storage, $15.1 million in air pollutant removal, $11.9 million in avoided runoff, $11.2 million in building energy savings, and $7.5 million in carbon sequestration.[^11] The urban forest sequesters 39,000 tons of carbon annually and stores 3.9 million tons of carbon.[^11] Chicago's urban forest removes 1,300 tons of air pollutants annually and avoids 100 million cubic feet of stormwater runoff.[^11] The initiative aims to expand Chicago's tree canopy and its benefits to all 77 community areas.[^2] The approach also aims to strengthen the urban forestry sector and create green jobs.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This effort tackles tree coverage in communities that fall below Chicago's 16% average thus reducing serious climate change impacts such as urban heat island effects and frequent flooding.[^4] Our Roots Chicago will equitably convey ecosystem benefits to communities disproportionately impacted by the climate crisis by prioritizing planting in historically marginalized and underserved communities on the South and West sides, where the city's current tree canopy demonstrates a shortage of trees.[^4] Currently, the city's average canopy coverage is only 16% which is below the ideal average for cities, and it is even lower in historically under-resourced and underserved community census tracts, often varying from 4% to 10%.[^4] The Chicago region has 23% canopy cover, far below the national average.[^3]

## Business Analysis

The $46 million Our Roots Chicago initiative represents a significant municipal investment in climate adaptation infrastructure, funded through the Chicago Recovery Plan.[^4] The Chicago Region Trees Initiative combined all variables to identify areas that have the highest need for improved tree canopy and its related benefits, enabling CRTI to tailor programs, events, and training programs to improve the urban forest where it is needed the most.[^3] The Chicago Region Trees Initiative is integrating climate change-related goals into a regional tree master plan and updating its recommended planting list to encourage climate-adapted species.[^8] Leslie Brandt, climate change specialist with the Northern Institute of Applied Climate Science and the U.S. Forest Service, partnered with the Chicago Region Trees Initiative to assess the vulnerability of the region's trees to climate change and other factors, and then consider strategies for adaptation.[^8] The assessment showed that some of the least vulnerable trees in the Chicago region are non-native invasive species, while many of the species native to the area are vulnerable to changing conditions.[^8] The Chicago Region Trees Initiative is seeking grant funding to create real-world demonstrations of adaptation that resulted from the workshops.[^8] As the city of Chicago undertakes tree planting initiatives, it plans to do so in partnership with local communities, learning about concerns at a neighborhood level.[^1]

## Sources

# Research Report: Solar Microgrid with Battery Storage for Critical Facility Resilience

## Solution Overview

Berkeley's Energy Assurance Transformation (BEAT) Project establishes a solar-powered microgrid and community resilience hub at the City's Corporation Yard, providing long-duration, clean, and resilient energy supply for critical facilities during grid outages such as PG&E Public Safety Power Shutoffs.[^2] The initiative originated from a $1.5 million California Energy Commission Electric Program Investment Charge grant to research whether creating a sustainable, resilient, clean energy microgrid that would maintain power during outages was feasible.[^3] This approach combines solar generation, battery storage, and electric vehicle charging infrastructure to deliver backup power while advancing the city's Climate Action Plan goals, including the Fossil Free by 2030 target.[^2]

## Technical Components

The system integrates a 1.1 MW solar photovoltaic array installed on carports at the Corporation Yard and on the roof of the Public Safety Building.[^2] A 2.2 MWh Battery Energy Storage System stores solar energy for use during grid outages and to reduce electricity costs.[^2] The infrastructure includes 30 Level 2 EV charging stations and 6 DC fast chargers to charge the City's growing electric vehicle fleet.[^2] A microgrid controller manages the generation, storage, and distribution of energy to ensure reliable power supply.[^2] The project replaces natural gas equipment with all-electric heat pumps for heating and cooling at the Corporation Yard.[^2] Research models demonstrated that clean energy microgrid community designs function by connecting multiple buildings and using clean energy like solar to share energy across facilities during normal operations, with the capability to operate independently from the grid during power outages using solar and battery storage.[^3] Solar panel and energy storage systems were identified as a less restrictive approach to provide microgrid-level resiliency benefits to individual buildings.[^3]

## Implementation Details

The total project cost reached $11.7 million, funded through a California Energy Commission EPIC Grant of $8.7 million and a City of Berkeley contribution of $3 million.[^2] Project partners include the City of Berkeley, TerraVerde Energy, Center for Sustainable Energy, Kisensum Inc., University of California Berkeley, and Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory.[^2] The original BEAT research project was managed by the Office of Energy and Sustainable Development with subrecipients including Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, URS Corporation, Association of Bay Area Governments, Center for Sustainable Energy, Interface Engineering, West Coast Code Consultants, and NHA Advisors.[^1] Based on the BEAT project's recommendations, the City of Berkeley funded several solar plus storage projects at municipal buildings throughout the city.[^3] Implementation faced significant regulatory constraints, as the City found that development of clean energy microgrid communities was severely restricted by California Public Utility Commission code and utility policies.[^3] The Pacific Gas & Electric Company had no interest in adding automatic shut-off switches to cut power to non-microgrid customers and had no legal shutoff agreements in place with non-microgrid customers.[^3] The option of adding new distribution lines proved prohibitively expensive, with construction per mile estimated at $1 million, including capital costs, installation costs, utility charges for operation and maintenance of distribution lines, and the transfer tax of deeding assets to the utility.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The design allows the city to reduce utility energy consumption by 36-43 percent and reduce existing reliance on backup diesel generators during power outages by up to 40 percent.[^1] Research models confirmed that clean energy microgrid community and solar plus storage designs reduced day-to-day energy consumption 36 to 43 percent and reduced diesel generator use during an outage by up to 40 percent.[^3] The system provides a resilient, long-duration power supply exceeding 72 hours for the City's most critical facilities during grid outages, including the Emergency Operations Center, 911 Dispatch Center, and Public Safety Building.[^2] The project reduces the City's electricity costs and demand charges, saving an estimated $100,000 per year.[^2] The Corporation Yard serves as a resilience hub during grid outages by providing a place for community members to access information, charge personal electronic devices, and receive emergency supplies.[^2] The initiative generates clean, renewable energy to power City facilities and charge the City's growing electric vehicle fleet, helping meet Climate Action Plan goals.[^2] The system is made up of solar panels and energy storage that provides Berkeley's critical facilities with clean, reliable backup power in the event of a disaster and isolates the facility from the grid.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This implementation directly addresses grid disruption risks from climate-related events, particularly PG&E Public Safety Power Shutoffs that occur during extreme weather conditions.[^2] Berkeley's Climate Action Plan includes developing and implementing a strategy to maximize local solar and energy storage deployment, with a focus on municipal facilities, schools, and low-income housing.[^4] The plan also includes exploring the feasibility of developing one or more community microgrids to enhance energy resilience, particularly in critical facilities and vulnerable communities.[^4]

## Business Analysis

The financial model demonstrates strong public sector investment with the California Energy Commission providing 74 percent of project costs through EPIC grant funding, while the City of Berkeley contributed the remaining 26 percent.[^2] The $100,000 annual savings in electricity costs and demand charges provides ongoing operational benefits that improve the project's long-term financial viability.[^2] Policy support extends beyond project-specific funding, as Berkeley's Climate Action Plan establishes a goal to partner with East Bay Community Energy to achieve a 100 percent renewable electricity supply for all customers in Berkeley by 2030.[^4] The broader market context for bidirectional electric vehicle integration shows emerging revenue opportunities, with Pacific Gas and Electric operating the nation's first pilot program that allows up to 1,000 residential customers with bidirectional chargers to sell power back to the utility.[^5] International implementations demonstrate significant financial benefits, as bidirectional charging in Denmark earns electric vehicle fleet owners who sell power to the grid $3,000 per vehicle annually, reducing the average total cost of electric car ownership by approximately 40 percent.[^5] California expects to have 12.5 million electric cars on the road by 2035, creating substantial potential for vehicle-to-grid applications.[^5]

[^1]: Office of Energy and Sustainable Development, City of Berkeley
[^2]: Berkeley's Energy Assurance Transformation (BEAT) Project documentation
[^3]: Berkeley Energy Assurance Transformation project research findings
[^4]: Berkeley's Climate Action Plan
[^5]: Pacific Gas and Electric bidirectional charging program documentation
[^6]: Bidirectional electric vehicle technical specifications
[^7]: EV battery backup energy storage capabilities

## Sources

- [^2]: https://berkeleyca.gov/sites/default/files/2022-03/BEAT-Project-Fact-Sheet.pdf
- [^3]: https://the-atlas.com/projects/berkeley-beat-microgrid
- [^4]: https://newspack-berkeleyside-cityside.s3.amazonaws.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/03/2018-12-06-WS-Item-01-Climate-Action-Plan-Update.pdf
- [^5]: https://calmatters.org/environment/2023/07/california-electric-cars-bidirectional-charging/

- [^7]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Berkeley-US/Berkeley_Resilience_Strategy.pdf
