# Research Report: Real-Time Air Quality Monitoring Network for Urban Planning

## Solution Overview

Warsaw has established one of Europe's largest air quality monitoring networks to support evidence-based urban planning and environmental policy implementation.[^3][^8] The comprehensive monitoring system was developed through a partnership between the City of Warsaw and Airly Public, signed on December 30, 2021, with implementation designed to inform spatial development planning and track the effectiveness of clean transport initiatives.[^4][^9] This technology-enabled approach provides real-time air quality data across 164 locations operating 24 hours daily, creating an infrastructure for both immediate public information and long-term policy evaluation.[^3]

## Technical Components

The monitoring network consists of 164 sensors distributed across Warsaw's districts and 17 neighboring municipalities, including Halinów, Izabelin, Karczew, Konstancin-Jeziorna, Lesznowola, Milanówek, Nieporęt, Nowy Dwór Mazowiecki, Otwock, Piastów, Radzymin, Stare Babice, Sulejówek, Wieliszew, Wołomin, Ząbki, and Zielonka.[^4][^8] Each device monitors concentrations of particulate matter PM1, PM2.5, and PM10, as well as nitrogen dioxide (NO2), with sensors located away from streets additionally measuring ozone (O3) pollution.[^3][^8] The Warsaw University of Technology selected installation locations, with half of the sensors positioned at educational institutions and the remainder operating near district offices and busy transportation routes.[^3][^4] Airly Public provided both hardware devices and software platforms that enable the city to create customized tools for resident communication and pollution source analysis.[^3] Data from all reference stations and the 160+ new sensors can be accessed through the Warsaw IoT platform and the Warsaw 19115 mobile application, with information also available on the api.um.warszawa.pl open data platform.[^8] The contract includes provisions for expanding the system with 100 additional sensors in the future.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The monitoring system was deployed through the "Virtual Warsaw Functional Area (Virtual WOF)" project, co-financed by the European Regional Development Fund under the Regional Operational Programme of the Mazovia Province for 2014-2020, with 80% of the purchase financed from EU funds.[^4][^8] As many as 108 devices were installed within Warsaw's districts, while the remaining 56 sensors were deployed across the 17 partner municipalities.[^8] The partnership with Airly Public was extended for an additional 13 months, ensuring operational continuity at least until the end of 2023.[^3] Technical service for the entire system is provided by Airly Public under the contract terms.[^4] The implementation addresses Warsaw's obligation to introduce limited transport zones in pilot version by 2023 and target version by 2026, as required by the Air Protection Program adopted for the Mazowieckie Voivodeship.[^9] The project brings together international and local partners, including Breathe Cities, the Warsaw Air Protection and Climate Policy Department (BOPiPK), and the Warsaw Roads Authority (ZDM), to accomplish critical work in understanding real-world impacts on emissions reductions and public health.[^7]

## Benefits and Impacts

The monitoring network enables the city to locate areas of increased emissions and creates educational value by increasing residents' awareness of pollution sources and harmfulness.[^5] The detailed analysis allows effective measures and initiates organizational changes aimed at reducing air pollution, such as introducing low emission zones or better targeting indicated areas with information about municipal subsidies for furnace replacement.[^4] The system's data directly informed the establishment of Warsaw's first Clean Transport Zone in July 2024, which was adopted based on evidence from Ricardo's experts about health and economic benefits of tackling transport emissions.[^1][^7] The TRUE Initiative's first study in Warsaw in 2022 showed that a very small proportion of the city's fleet—the oldest diesel vehicles—disproportionately contributed to overall emissions, informing policymakers considering the low-emission zone for the city center.[^7] A follow-up study in 2023 evaluated emissions reduction potential of different LEZ designs using real-world data, ultimately helping shape the scope and phasing of the current LEZ scheme that came into effect in 2024.[^7] In 2022, of the three automatic monitoring locations within the proposed Clean Transport Zone boundary, the city had one measured exceedance of the European annual limit value for NO2 and one location identified as at risk of exceeding within 10% of the limit value.[^1] The air quality data is being used for preparation of the Study of the Conditions and Directions of Spatial Development for the Capital City of Warsaw, as well as for monitoring Clean Transport Zone implementation.[^9][^10]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The monitoring system addresses air quality degradation intensified by urban heat island effects and changing atmospheric conditions associated with climate change. Real-time pollution tracking enables adaptive responses to episodic air quality events that may increase in frequency with climate variability. The cross-jurisdictional monitoring approach recognizes that pollution moves across cities, countries, and continents, requiring coordinated regional responses to environmental challenges that transcend administrative boundaries.[^5]

## Business Analysis

The monitoring network represents one of the largest air quality monitoring systems in Europe, with more than 160 devices creating significant market presence in environmental sensing infrastructure.[^8] The public-private partnership model between Warsaw City Hall and Airly Public demonstrates viable financing mechanisms for large-scale environmental monitoring, leveraging 80% EU funding through the European Regional Development Fund to reduce municipal capital expenditure.[^4][^8] The Regional Operational Programme of the Mazovia Province for 2014-2020 provided the policy framework enabling this investment in environmental infrastructure.[^8] The Clean Transport Zone enabled by this monitoring data is estimated to deliver an annual net benefit to Warsaw society valued at 665 million euros (2.9 billion zloty), representing 12% of the city's annual budget.[^1] Health benefits from the Clean Transport Zone are estimated at 330 million euros annually, accounting for mortality rates, morbidity effects, healthcare cost savings, avoided lost productivity, and the value placed on good health and wellbeing.[^1] The 37-square-kilometer Clean Transport Zone, encompassing 7% of the city and including key districts, is being monitored by cameras to enforce compliance.[^1][^2] By 2032, the zone will ban diesel cars over 11 years old and petrol cars over 17 years old, with restrictions implemented in stages gradually tightening to include newer, less polluting vehicles.[^2] Initially, the ban applies to diesel cars over 18 years old and petrol cars over 27 years old, affecting about 3% of the city's private vehicles.[^2] By the final stage in 2032, the LEZ is estimated to reduce NOx emissions by 76% and PM emissions by 87% compared to a scenario with no LEZ.[^6] A comprehensive study assessing emission reductions and health impacts of the Warsaw LEZ is expected in early 2027.[^7]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.ricardo.com/en/news-and-insights/industry-insights/a-groundbreaking-clean-transport-zone-in-warsaw
- [^2]: https://urban-mobility-observatory.transport.ec.europa.eu/news-events/news/warsaw-introduces-polands-first-low-emission-zone-reduce-pollution-2024-07-12_en
- [^3]: https://airly.org/en/warsaw-has-now-launched-the-largest-air-quality-monitoring-system-in-europe-together-with-airly-public/
- [^4]: https://airly.org/en/warsaw-is-the-european-capital-with-the-largest-air-quality-monitoring-network/
- [^5]: https://eurocities.eu/stories/keeping-the-air-clean-in-warsaw/
- [^6]: https://trueinitiative.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/warsaw-lez-true-report-en.pdf
- [^7]: https://trueinitiative.org/insights/new-true-emissions-testing-begins-across-the-warsaw-lez/
- [^8]: https://en.um.warszawa.pl/-/new-sensors-monitor-warsaw-air
- [^9]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Europe & Middle East/Warsaw-Poland/Warsaw_Zielona-Wizja-Warszawy-ENG.pdf
- [^10]: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41597-024-04111-4

# Research Report: Grand Paris Express Automated Metro Network Expansion

## Solution Overview

The Grand Paris Express represents a fundamental transformation of public transport infrastructure in the Île-de-France region, with the completion of this automated underground rail system offering new transport capabilities and bypassing central Paris from suburb to suburb.[^2] This metro rail project, being delivered by Société du Grand Paris (SGP), will expand the Paris Metro network to approximately double its size, involving the design and construction of 200 km of new fully-automated metro across four lines (Lines 15–18) to the north, south, east, and west of Paris.[^12] The expansion is aimed at creating a 'polycentric' transit system by lengthening some existing Métro lines and adding four new ones that run in interconnecting rings around the metropolitan area.[^9]

## Technical Components

The Grand Paris Express will bring 68 new stations to the metro network progressively by 2030, comprising four totally new lines and the extension of one other.[^4] Configured as a small ring and a large ring, the four new metro lines (15, 16, 17, and 18) plus the extension of existing line 14 will shorten the daily commutes of close to 3 million people.[^8] The new metro lines will be 90% underground, provide 68 new stations (an increase of 20% across the network), and will provide connections to three international airports.[^12] Services on the Grand Paris Express will typically operate at a 2–3 minute headway (90 seconds during peak).[^12] The average speed of the trains operating on the Grand Paris Express will be between 55km/h and 65km/h and the maximum speed will be 110km/h.[^7] The metro trains will have a passenger capacity of 950, 1,000, 500 and 350 on Lines 14, 15, 16-17 and 18, respectively.[^7] Six operations centres across the Grand Paris Express network will oversee the safe circulation of trains.[^6] In 2012, Société des grands projets worked with specialised engineering firms to develop its own tool called CarbOptimum to analyse the carbon footprint of current and future projects that are part of the Grand Paris Express.[^1]

Line 14 north extension included the development of an additional 5.8km-long tunnel featuring four new stations from Saint-Lazare to Mairie de Saint-Ouen and became operational in December 2020.[^7] The line was extended further north of Mairie de Saint-Ouen to Saint-Denis Pleyel to connect with the upcoming Lines 15, 16, and 17 by 2024.[^7] Line 14 was also extended south from Olympiades to Maison Blanche, interchanging with Line 7's Villejuif branch, and opened on 24 June 2024.[^11] Line 14 South extension is a 14km-long tunnel from Olympiades to Orly Airport and includes seven new stations at White House, Bicêtre Hospital, Villejuif Gustave Roussy, L'Haÿ-les-Roses, Chevilly-Larue, Thiais-Orly and Orly Airport.[^7] Line 15 South is a 33km-long metro line connecting metro Lines 4, 7, 8, 9 and 13 of the Paris metro and is planned to be completed by the end of 2025.[^7] Line 17 is 26.5km long with nine metro stations between Saint-Denis Pleyel and Le Mesnil-Amelot, will cover 13 municipalities and serve 565,000 passengers daily.[^7] Line 18 is 35km long featuring both underground tracks and roughly 13km of elevated track, with ten metro stations between Orly Airport station to Versailles Chantiers along with a maintenance and storage centre.[^7] Line 18 is planned to be operational in three phases: Massy-Palaiseau to CEA Saint-Aubin by the end of 2026, Massy Palaiseau to Orly Airport by the end of 2027, and CEA Saint-Aubin to Versailles Chantiers by 2030.[^7]

## Implementation Details

Construction of the new lines of Grand Paris Express began in June 2015 and is being carried out in phases up to 2030.[^7] Around 20 tunnel boring machines are concurrently in operation for the Grand Paris Express project.[^13] The new Paris métro, formally titled Grand Paris Express (GPE), will more than double the territory encircling France's capital city with over 120 miles of new tracks, four new underground lines and 68 new metro stations.[^10] Gare Saint-Denis – Pleyel, a new Grand Paris Express station in the northern suburbs of Paris, opened on 24 June 2024 and is expected to transport 250,000 passengers a day.[^4] The RATP remains the operator of Grand Paris Express line 14, including its extensions north and south of Paris.[^6]

Société du Grand Paris (SGP) is a public company established in 2010 dedicated to the development of the new metro network of Greater Paris.[^12] The Artemis consortium formed by engineering companies Artelia, Arcadis and BG Ingenieurs Conseils signed a 16-year €40m ($50m) contract with SGP to serve as the overall project manager and oversee the infrastructure operations for Lines 15, 16 and 17.[^7] The City is involved in the development of public transport in the Île-de-France region, in particular by financing key structural network projects such as the extensions of lines 11 and 14 and of RER (Regional Express Railway) line E to the west.[^2]

A total of €35.6 billion has been earmarked for the Grand Paris Express development, including a provision of €7 billion for risks and contingencies, putting the full cost at roughly €42 billion ($54.34 billion).[^9] Costs for the Grand Paris Express have reached €36 billion ($39 billion), according to the Société du Grand Paris, the state-owned infrastructure agency tasked with designing and implementing the project.[^10] The Société du Grand Paris allocated roughly 35 million euros to commission contemporary art for each of the 68 Grand Paris Express stations, creating a vast, connected gallery available to everyone with a subway ticket.[^10]

Building the Grand Paris Express will generate around 4.4 million tonnes of CO2, and over 70% of those emissions come from using concrete and steel.[^1] The project has committed to a 25% reduction of the greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions caused by constructing the Grand Paris Express by innovating and adapting building methods.[^1] Construction companies are effectively being asked to use concrete with a 40% smaller carbon footprint than conventional concrete.[^1] 90% of the new metro system is built with concrete labelled low-carbon.[^1] Fibre-reinforced concrete is also being used to build the voussoirs on part of Line 16, which consumes half the resources as reinforced concrete and cuts CO2 emissions at concrete plants and steel mills.[^1] The first low-carbon rails were laid on Line 16, and they will be installed on Line 17 and portions of Line 15 South.[^1] The project is also pushing for tracks to be made from recycled scrap metal, a process that emits 60-90% less CO2.[^1] Building the Grand Paris Express has generated nearly 49 million tonnes of rubble.[^1] Ultimately, the project aims to reuse 70% of the earth excavated from worksites.[^1] 56% of excavated earth has been reused.[^1] The 1.4 million of earth excavated for Line 16 will help landscape the La Plaine du Sempin park spanning Chelles and Montfermeil.[^1] 13.5% of excavated earth is transported by barge and by train.[^1] The project will be tracking ecological offsetting measures for 30 years to see how effective they are, and to make any necessary adjustments.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

Close to 3 million passengers will ride on the Grand Paris Express metro lines every day.[^6] The Grand Paris Express network is projected to witness a daily ridership of two million passengers by 2026.[^7] Once the Grand Paris Express expansion is completed, a trip from Orly Airport to the Paris Saclay University Campus will take about 15 minutes, compared to over an hour under current conditions.[^9] After completion of the Grand Paris Express, commuters will be able to travel between destinations within the Parisian Metropolitan area (Île de France) without having to travel into the city of Paris itself to first make a transfer.[^9] With 200 km of tracks, the Grand Paris Express will serve the main business districts (La Défense, La Plaine Saint-Denis), the major hubs of science and higher learning (Paris-Saclay, Institut Gustave-Roussy, La Cité Descartes), three key regional airports (Charles de Gaulle, Orly, Le Bourget), and three high-speed train stations (Massy-Palaiseau, Versailles-Chantiers, and Charles de Gaulle).[^8] 130 universities and higher learning institutions, 408 healthcare centres, and 656 cultural venues will be within a 10-minute walk of the future Grand Paris Express stations.[^8]

Twenty years after the new metro system begins serving passengers, it will be helping cut 10.9 million tonnes of CO2 equivalent of greenhouse gas emissions in Paris region.[^1] The system saves an average of 10,000 tonnes of CO2 equivalent for every 10 km of tunnel.[^1] The Grand-Paris Express project will make a significant contribution to increasing the attractiveness of public transport and make it easier for many metropolitan area residents to stop using their private cars.[^2] These centres will create over 1,000 direct skilled jobs.[^6]

The City of Paris proposed to requalify the ring road as an urban boulevard by 2050, with quieter traffic, reserved lanes for carpooling, public transport and clean vehicles.[^5] The speed limit on the Boulevard Périphérique was reduced to 50 km/h on 1st October 2024.[^3] A lane specifically for vehicles transporting two or more people (VR2+) was implemented on the Boulevard Périphérique on 3rd March 2025.[^3] Between July-August 2025 and July-August 2023, NO2 concentrations on the Boulevard Périphérique decreased by an average of 5.9 μg/m3, while PM10 stabilized at +0.3 μg/m3.[^3] Along the Porte de Vincennes sector of the Boulevard Périphérique, an average drop of 2.7 dB(A) in noise pollution was measured between March and June 2025 compared with 2024, from 79.9 dB(A) to 77.2 dB(A).[^3] From October 2024 to June 2025, daytime speed on the Boulevard Périphérique decreased by 8% and nighttime speed decreased by 18%.[^3] Traffic jams on the Boulevard Périphérique decreased by 11% between March and June 2025 compared to the same period in 2024.[^3] During busy hours on the VR2+ lane from March to June 2025, traffic speed was higher by 8.2 km/h than on other Boulevard Périphérique lanes.[^3]

Harvard University awarded the Grand Paris Express the 2023 Veronica Rudge Green Prize in Urban Design, recognizing design that transforms cities and the lives of its inhabitants in unanticipated and extraordinary ways.[^10]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The Grand Paris Express project involves 200 km of new tracks and 68 stations in the Île-de-France region, which is home to over 12 million inhabitants.[^13] The average return commute for residents in the Paris area is 68 minutes.[^8] Currently one million vehicles per day use the Périphérique, a 35-kilometer ring road separating Paris from the suburbs.[^9] An estimated 10.5 million people live in Parisian suburbs, compared to 2.2 million people in the city of Paris.[^9] The FMGP's proposed measures for the ring road aim to reduce car dependence and promote public transport and soft mobility, as part of a thought-process that includes the ring road in the wider metropolitan area.[^5]

## Business Analysis

The Grand Paris Express is the largest transportation project being developed in Paris, France, being developed by Societe du Grand Paris (SGP) and will serve three million residents per day.[^7] The Metropolitan Forum of Greater Paris (FMGP, Forum Métropolitain du Grand Paris) worked on the future of mobility in Ile-de-France in 2018 and proposed that the ring road be transformed into an urban boulevard.[^5] The Mobility 2030 policy, launched in the framework of the Metropolitan Forum, should lead to an international consultation, under the aegis of the Forum, for the proposal of scenarios to transform the metropolitan area's motorways by 2024.[^2]

[^1]: Société du Grand Paris. "Building a low-carbon metro system."
[^2]: City of Paris. "Climate Action Plan for Paris."
[^3]: City of Paris. "Boulevard Périphérique Traffic and Environmental Monitoring Report 2025."
[^4]: Railway Technology. "Grand Paris Express Metro Expansion Project."
[^5]: Metropolitan Forum of Greater Paris. "The Future of the Paris Ring Road."
[^6]: Société du Grand Paris. "Operations and Employment."
[^7]: Railway Technology. "Grand Paris Express Project Details."

[^9]: The Architect's Newspaper. "Grand Paris Express: Transforming Metropolitan Mobility."
[^10]: Metropolis Magazine. "Grand Paris Express Wins Urban Design Prize."
[^11]: Wikipedia. "Line 14 (Paris Métro)."
[^12]: Arup. "Grand Paris Express Project Overview."
[^13]: Société du Grand Paris. "Project Logistics and Scale."

# Research Report: River Hydraulic Modeling and Water Quality Monitoring System

## Solution Overview

Belgrade faces a critical wastewater management challenge, as the city lacks basic wastewater treatment facilities and discharges sewerage and stormwater directly into the Sava and Danube rivers without any prior treatment.[^2] This approach involves developing a hydraulic model and quality monitoring system for these rivers, carefully calibrated through extensive measurements to provide trustworthy data for determining the scope of subsequent treatment infrastructure.[^1] The initiative is particularly significant given that Belgrade Waterworks' water supply is based on the treatment of raw groundwater, primarily from the River Sava but also from the Danube.[^3]

## Technical Components

The system centers on a hydraulic model and quality monitoring infrastructure for the Sava and Danube rivers, requiring careful calibration through extensive measurements to generate trustworthy data.[^1] This technical framework is designed to assess the natural capacity of both rivers to process nutrients, particularly relevant given the limited remaining heavy industrial activity in the city.[^1] The monitoring system must account for the current situation where municipal and industrial wastewater from Belgrade is discharged without treatment into the Danube River.[^4]

## Implementation Details

Phase I focuses on developing the hydraulic model and quality monitoring system, with the explicit purpose of determining the scope of Phase II and evaluating the extent to which tertiary treatment is necessary.[^1] The phased approach allows for data-driven decision-making regarding whether opportunities exist to rely on the natural capacity of the Danube and Sava rivers to process nutrients.[^1] This implementation strategy acknowledges the limited remaining heavy industrial activity in the city as a factor in assessing natural processing capacity.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This monitoring and modeling approach addresses water quality management in the context of direct wastewater discharge into major river systems.[^2][^4] The initiative is critical for understanding the assimilative capacity of the Sava and Danube rivers, which serve as both wastewater recipients and sources of raw groundwater for Belgrade's water supply.[^3]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://wbif.eu/project-detail/PRJ-SRB-ENV-005
- [^3]: https://www.waterworld.com/drinking-water-treatment/potable-water-quality/article/16200775/renewing-belgrades-water-infrastructure
- [^4]: https://voltecltd.com/service-detail.aspx?id=96

# Research Report: Climate Risk Atlas with Integrated Flood Mapping and Vulnerability Data

## Solution Overview

Mexico City's Climate Action Programme includes the development of a climate risk atlas that integrates comprehensive flood mapping and hazard data from the CDMX Risk and Hazard Atlas.[^2] This approach was designated as action A18 in the PACCM 2014-2020 and serves as a key tool for land use planning and the design of adaptation measures across the city.[^2] The atlas identifies areas and populations most vulnerable to climate change impacts while providing elements for disaster prevention, mitigation, preparedness, and response.[^2][^3]

## Technical Components

The Risk Atlas of Mexico City (RA-GIS) incorporates a comprehensive set of indicators related to hazard, exposure, and social vulnerability.[^4] The system integrates information on floods, ponding, and quadrant mapping from the CDMX Risk and Hazard Atlas.[^2] Flooding events were estimated for return periods of 2, 5, 10, 50, and 100 years, with most flooding concentrated in the central and northern sectors of the city.[^3] The atlas identifies prevalent social risk to multiple hazards including earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, floods, landslides, forest fires, and land subsidence.[^3] A total of 274 shape-file maps were generated in this project.[^3]

## Implementation Details

The climate risk atlas was developed under the responsibility of SPC (Secretariat of Civil Protection) and SEDEMA (Secretariat of the Environment).[^2] Two municipalities in Mexico City—Venustiano Carranza and Azcapotzalco—used the manual to develop their climate risk assessment, with their diagnoses identifying flooding as the main risk.[^4] The assessment included an analysis of social vulnerability and pinpointed priority areas for attention.[^4] The atlas was designed to provide a tool to local and federal authorities and all organizations responsible for disaster prevention and mitigation.[^3] Mexico City initiated the Second Climate Action Program (PACCM) in 2014, continuing actions to increase resilience and combat climate change with a planning tool that integrates, coordinates, and promotes methods to reduce environmental, social, and economic risks posed by climate change.[^7]

## Benefits and Impacts

The indicator for monitoring action A18 is the level of use of the atlas in planning processes.[^2] The atlas acts as a management tool to support implementation of the Climate Action Programme.[^4] Mexico City planned to assess the impacts of flooding on STCM and Metrobus systems and create a portfolio of adjustment measures during 2016-2018, with support from partners including RMS, CTS Embarq, ITDP, and Veolia.[^6] This approach improves knowledge of the potential physical and social impact of local hazards.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The climate risk atlas directly addresses flooding hazards by mapping inundation events across multiple return periods and identifying vulnerable populations and infrastructure.[^2][^3] The system supports Pillar 03 of the CDMX Resilience Strategy, which aims to ensure all citizens have equal access to urban amenities while improving the environment and mitigating risks through sustainable management of natural resources.[^1]

## Business Analysis

The atlas serves as a foundational planning tool that enables municipalities to conduct localized climate risk assessments and prioritize adaptation investments.[^4] The system supports infrastructure planning decisions, including the Calle Verde initiative launched in 2016 to improve drainage, add green spaces, and cultivate a safer streetscape while better preparing the city for climate change effects.[^5] The program repairs drainage pipes under key corridors and repaves streets and sidewalks with permeable pavement to prevent flooding during extreme weather events.[^5] By preventing flooding, the improved drainage network and green infrastructure hinder the spread of waterborne diseases, providing additional health benefits.[^5]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://use.metropolis.org/case-studies/cdmx-resilience-strategy
- [^2]: https://condor.depaul.edu/~dmakagon/student/Mexico_City/MexCityClimateActionPlan.pdf
- [^3]: https://www.preventionweb.net/publication/risk-atlas-mexico-city-mexico-tool-decision-making-and-disaster-prevention
- [^4]: https://mcr2030.undrr.org/news/mexico-city-focuses-solutions-and-scale
- [^5]: https://www.c40.org/case-studies/cities100-mexico-city-reshaping-streets-prevents-flooding-and-ensures-safety/
- [^6]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Latin America & Caribbean/MexicoCity-Mexico/Mexico-City-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^7]: https://www.c40.org/case-studies/cities100-mexico-city-comprehensive-program-increases-resilience/

# Research Report: Hospital Infrastructure Resiliency and Floodproofing Upgrades

## Solution Overview

NYC Health + Hospitals implemented comprehensive resiliency upgrades across multiple hospital facilities following Hurricane Sandy, including installation of backup power systems, elevation and hardening of building systems, and floodproofing of lower levels.[^1][^2][^3] These infrastructure improvements protect critical healthcare facilities serving over one million people annually from coastal flooding, storm surge, and extreme weather events.[^12] The health system secured approximately $1.8 billion from the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) for these resiliency projects across four primary facilities affected by Hurricane Sandy.[^6]

## Technical Components

The resiliency infrastructure includes perimeter flood walls varying in height from 8 feet to 12 feet with integrated flood gates consisting of swing, sliding, and vertical lift configurations.[^4][^10] At Bellevue Hospital Center, the existing perimeter flood wall was raised to an elevation of 17.5 feet NAVD88 by adding concrete to the top of the existing wall, with 13 flood gates installed at openings and 11 air intake louvers on the west side of the Main Hospital Building elevated to the same height.[^4] The flood wall's structural integrity is composed of corrugated sheeting drilled 45 feet underground along the 1800 LF site perimeter wall.[^9] Electrical systems were elevated and hardened, with backup generators installed to ensure operations can continue for at least 72 hours during power outages.[^1][^2] At Bellevue, a new 2.5-megawatt generator was installed on the roof of the Main Hospital Building, fueled by natural gas with a 10,000-gallon diesel fuel backup.[^4] The Ruth Bader Ginsburg Hospital features hurricane-resistant glass curtain wall systems designed to withstand high winds and flying debris on upper floors, while mechanical equipment floors are strategically elevated on the fifth level above projected flood levels with insulated metal panel-clad curtain wall assemblies.[^7] Critical infrastructure on the first floor, including elevators and machine rooms, is protected via concealed fabric and aluminum-plank flood barriers.[^7] Below-grade walls were waterproofed at multiple facilities, and stormwater detention systems were upgraded to manage extreme rainfall events.[^4][^10]

## Implementation Details

A total of 30 resiliency projects were initiated across the four primary affected facilities following Hurricane Sandy in 2012.[^6] The Metropolitan Hospital Flood Wall Resiliency Project was initiated by NYCEDC and NYC Health + Hospitals in 2016 to address flooding vulnerabilities given the hospital's close proximity to the East River and growing climate risks of coastal storm surge and extreme rainfall.[^10] NYCEDC, NYC Health + Hospitals, and Gilbane Building Company celebrated the completion of the Flood Wall at NYC Health + Hospitals/Metropolitan on December 4, 2024.[^9] Gilbane provided construction management at-risk services for the new flood protection system at Metropolitan Hospital's 360,000 SF campus spanning four city blocks, delivering the upgrades on schedule and under budget while exceeding minority and women-owned business participation goals.[^9][^10] FEMA allocated $120 million for Metropolitan Hospital Center in Manhattan, including almost $7 million for electrical repairs and $109 million for a flood wall that protects critical infrastructure on the campus to the 500-year flood level.[^3] The Federal Emergency Management Agency funded the $112 million project, which includes the construction of a perimeter flood wall and an upgraded storm water detention system.[^10] At Coler Specialty Hospital on Roosevelt Island, FEMA allocated $181 million, including replacement of a generator that was destroyed, reimbursement for repairs already completed to the electrical system, and a flood wall that protects critical parts of the campus to the 500-year flood level.[^3] Design for the NYC H+H/Coler flood mitigation project began in March 2024, with schematic design submitted and reviewed with RIOC in Summer 2024.[^8] The facility is currently surrounded by temporary flood barriers, which serve as a critical line of defense against potential flooding events.[^8] Construction of the new Ruth Bader Ginsburg Hospital was funded by $923 million from FEMA and includes upgrades to the rest of the health care campus, including a 4-foot wall to withstand a 500-year storm and flood-resilient power, heating, cooling, and water systems.[^5] The $923 million Ruth Bader Ginsburg Hospital opened in May 2023, offering services to nearly 875,000 residents of South Brooklyn and nearby neighborhoods.[^1] Turner Construction Company, in a joint venture with McKissack & McKissack, completed the FEMA-funded project in 2022, which included new construction of a 10-story 360,000 sq. ft. addition, 40,000 sq. ft. of interior renovations, a new floodwall encircling the campus, and the demolition of damaged structures.[^11] The at least $1.6 billion FEMA package includes reimbursement for repairs to HHC facilities for damage incurred during Hurricane Sandy, and funds for mitigation projects that protect the hospitals from the impact of future extreme weather, in addition to the $142 million HHC already received from FEMA for emergency stabilization measures, partial repairs, and temporary flood barriers.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The protective measures prevent future flood waters from reaching hospital facilities, minimizing damage to infrastructure and allowing facilities to remain operational during hurricane and super storm events.[^10] The innovative flood wall enables Metropolitan Hospital to withstand a one-in-500-year storm event.[^9] At Coney Island Hospital, a new 1,720-foot flood wall protects critical services on the current part of the campus to the level of a 500-year flood.[^3] The new emergency department at Ruth Bader Ginsburg Hospital is double the size of the current space and located on the second floor to sustain flooding and ensure continuity of care during natural disasters.[^5] New generators and quick connect systems allow facilities to operate in a power outage for at least 72 hours.[^2] For PAAP projects in New York, for every dollar spent on repair and restoration efforts, an additional $.85 has been spent on mitigation measures incorporated into the projects, nearly double the $.44 for non-PAAP permanent work grants.[^12] The project delivered a comprehensive flood mitigation system at Metropolitan Hospital, including three new structures and multiple below-grade utility tunnels, in addition to the renovation of the NYC/NY State Emergency Management Chempack Room.[^9] Improvements were made to protect loading docks, harden the Mental Health Building walls, and relocate hospital medical supply services.[^10] Construction teams installed new lighting, erosion controls, fire protections, and security enhancements.[^10] The resiliency upgrades reduce the impact of heavy rain, strong winds, and storm surge on hospital facilities that are critical to the more than one million people who are served every year by this healthcare system.[^12]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

These infrastructure improvements directly address the flooding vulnerabilities that caused severe damage during Hurricane Sandy in October 2012, when the most significant physical damage and serious flooding occurred at three of NYC Health + Hospitals' acute care hospitals—Bellevue, South Brooklyn Health, and Metropolitan—and Coler, a long-term care and rehabilitation facility.[^10] The project protects the hospital campus from significant flooding caused by heavy rain and storm surge, which are becoming increasingly common.[^10] The proposed action is needed to ensure that Bellevue Hospital Center can continue to provide critical medical services to the community during and after future flood events.[^4]

## Business Analysis

NYC Health + Hospitals operates an overall footprint of 20 million square feet in over 75 buildings with over 1,000 pieces of mechanical equipment such as boilers, generators, air handling units, and elevators.[^2] The Climate Resilience Plan recommends a series of infrastructure projects, including installation of additional emergency generators linked to critical HVAC equipment, improving the drainage capacity of roofs and windows, building additional flood barriers, and installing green infrastructure to reduce ponding and heat island effect.[^2] Utility recommendations include assessing generator capacity and increasing capacity where needed, ensuring critical HVAC equipment is connected to back-up generators, and elevating and/or floodproofing critical equipment located below ground and on ground level if located in a flood zone.[^2] Site recommendations include constructing flood barriers and/or stormwater detention systems to prevent flooding during extreme weather events, and installing porous pavement and/or green infrastructure to mitigate ponding, such as bioswales and rain gardens.[^2] Building enclosure recommendations include improving drainage capacity of roof membranes and windows, and replacing roof membranes, windows, and building facades at end of useful life to mitigate against wind-driven rain, improve energy efficiency, and enhance patient and staff comfort.[^2] Senator Chuck Schumer helped secure a large FEMA grant for NYC Health + Hospitals in the aftermath of Sandy, which seeks to protect the City's most vulnerable public hospitals from coastal flooding.[^8] FEMA determined on September 10, 2014, that the proposed flood mitigation action at Bellevue Hospital Center will not have a significant adverse impact on the environment.[^4] A public notice of the availability of the Environmental Assessment for the Bellevue Hospital Center flood mitigation project was published in the New York Post on August 14, 2014, with the public comment period ending on August 29, 2014.[^4]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.healthbeat.org/newyork/2025/09/15/coney-island-hospital-storm-climate-rebuilt/
- [^2]: https://www.nychealthandhospitals.org/pressrelease/nyc-health-hospitals-details-climate-resilience-plan-building-on-existing-efforts-and-commitment/

- [^5]: https://www.nychealthandhospitals.org/pressrelease/south-brooklyn-community-celebrates-future-opening-of-new-ruth-bader-ginsburg-hospital/
- [^6]: https://www.nychealthandhospitals.org/pressrelease/ten-years-after-hurricane-sandy-nyc-health-hospitals-makes-progress-on-majority-of-resiliency-projects/
- [^7]: https://healthcaredesignmagazine.com/projects/ruth-bader-ginsburg-hospital-reimagines-resiliency-with-welcoming-design-in-brooklyn-n-y/62339/
- [^8]: https://www.colercoastalresiliency.com/
- [^9]: https://www.gilbaneco.com/projects/new-york-city-economic-development-corporation-new-york-city-health-hospitals-fema-metropolitan-hospital-flood-wall/
- [^10]: https://edc.nyc/press-release/nycedc-and-nyc-health-hospitals-celebrate-completion-flood-wall-nyc-health
- [^11]: https://www.turnerconstruction.com/projects/new-york-city-economic-department-corporation-coney-island-hospital

# Research Report: Microgrid Infrastructure for Critical Service Resilience

## Solution Overview

This initiative enhances the resilience of Nashville's infrastructure through innovative microgrid systems and self-contained electrical networks designed to safeguard critical services during extreme weather events.[^1] The approach integrates distributed energy resources like microgrids, including solar PV, to enhance the reliability and resilience of the grid.[^6] Using the frameworks and methodologies developed through this project, the city of Nashville and other communities nationwide will have a proven blueprint to achieve energy security and resilience amid escalating climate events.[^6]

## Technical Components

The simulated scenarios included adding a front-of-meter microgrid consisting of 500 kW of solar and 4 MWh of storage.[^2] The project simulated the effects of deploying microgrids in about six different sizes.[^2] The ARCHER project uses advanced modeling, interactive dashboards, and a new resilience metric to help cities figure out where to place distributed energy resources (DER), like solar panels and battery storage, for the biggest impact.[^3] The interactive dashboards developed through the ARCHER project, utilizing the proven L&T-Spark™ platform, provide a cohesive solution for integrating utility and community data, thus enabling enhanced decision-making for utility operators.[^4] These dashboards were designed to integrate utility and community data to support real-time decision-making.[^5] The ARCHER interactive dashboards offer a unified solution for integrating utility and community data, enabling advanced real-time situational awareness and enhanced decision-making for utility system operators.[^6]

## Implementation Details

The ARCHER project is led by EPRI in partnership with TSU, TVA, NES, and the City of Nashville.[^3] A research project involving NES, the Electric Power Research Institute (EPRI), Tennessee State University (TSU), Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) and the city of Nashville examined what would have happened if NES deployed a solar-plus-storage microgrid that served critical services identified by community members.[^2] The project was funded with a $1 million planning grant from the federal Department of Energy (DOE).[^2] Nashville Electric Service (NES) has funding in its budget set aside for resilience projects to help pay for a microgrid that the DOE estimates will cost $70 million to $100 million.[^2] As part of the ARCHER project, TSU established a Community Resilience Advisory Board (CRAB) composed of influential leaders in the North Nashville community to gather insights on which community assets should be prioritized for energy resilience, infrastructure hardening, and power restoration following outages.[^4] Project participants recruited community leaders in Nashville–representing businesses, houses of worship and local organizations–to join a study group, called the Community Resiliency Advisory Board–that met monthly for 3 years to identify the critical services they thought should continue operating during an outage.[^2] On May 30, Tennessee State University partnered with local and federal organizations to conduct a virtual simulation of the March 2020 Nashville tornado.[^4] On the last day of May, a diverse group of collaborators gathered at Tennessee State University (TSU) campus in Nashville for a virtual simulation of the 2020 tornado that tore through the city to test how microgrids, localized energy systems powered by renewables like solar, can keep essential services running when disaster strikes.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach addresses the impacts of extreme weather events that cause prolonged power outages and infrastructure damage. In 2020, a tornado hit Nashville Electric Service's (NES) territory, downing 972 poles and sparking an outage that lasted nine days in certain areas.[^2] The March 2020 tornado caused significant destruction in North Nashville, which is home to TSU, leading to extended power outages for many residents.[^4]

# Research Report: Community Resilience Hubs with Solar Power and Emergency Infrastructure

## Solution Overview

San Juan has established a network of community resilience hubs that transform local community centers into fortified facilities equipped with critical infrastructure and emergency services.[^1][^2] These centers provide safe gathering spaces where residents can access resources and coordinate response and recovery efforts during climate emergencies and disasters.[^1] The initiative involves partnerships with community-based organizations, foundations, and the private sector to deliver comprehensive support services across multiple neighborhoods.[^1]

## Technical Components

The resilience hubs are equipped with solar energy systems, potable water storage infrastructure, communication systems, emergency kits, and health services.[^2] During emergency response situations, these facilities serve as command hubs for communication with federal and Puerto Rico agencies, offering digital communication services for communities and businesses with limited to no access.[^3] Additional services include access to a digital lifeline, such as radio communication and a command center, provision of water, information, and medical services.[^3] The centers feature redundant energy and water services, focused on renewable energy, rain harvesting, and community gardens.[^3] At Parceleras Afrocaribeñas para Transformación Barrial's La Conde, a solar-powered energy hub serving Saint Just, San Antón and Los Mirtos includes safety windows and doors that help it function as a shelter during storms, along with a refrigerator to store medicine.[^4]

## Implementation Details

Mercy Corps' teams in Puerto Rico transformed local community centers into resilience hubs equipped with the necessary infrastructure and services.[^2] The establishment of San Juan's network of community resilience hubs is planned for a medium-term timeline.[^1] Key locations that provide services to communities in various municipalities across the island could be adapted into resilient community centers.[^3] Parceleras Afrocaribeñas developed the action plan for the energy hub in response to power outages affecting the local community.[^4] The organization received a US$20,000 grant from Habitat to help make their plan for the energy hub a reality.[^4] With Takeda's support, Mercy Corps is building more resilience hubs across the Caribbean.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

In 2022, the resilience hubs delivered assistance immediately to people affected by Hurricane Fiona.[^2] The facilities provided reliable access to critical resources in the aftermath of the storm.[^2] During storms, local families can shelter safely inside the energy hub to charge their electrical devices and store medicine.[^4] The resilient community centers provide multiple cross-sector benefits to the community, such as pre-k, educational classes, and workforce training programs while offering a space for disaster relief services and ongoing provision of resources to the community.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

These resilience hubs directly address climate hazards by providing immediate emergency response capabilities during hurricanes and severe weather events. The facilities' proven performance during Hurricane Fiona in 2022 demonstrates their effectiveness in delivering critical resources when traditional infrastructure fails during climate-related disasters.[^2]

## Business Analysis

The initiative operates through a multi-sector partnership model involving community-based organizations, foundations, and private sector entities.[^1] Financial support mechanisms include targeted grants, as demonstrated by the US$20,000 Habitat grant awarded to Parceleras Afrocaribeñas for energy hub development.[^4] The expansion model leverages corporate partnerships, with Takeda supporting Mercy Corps in building additional resilience hubs across the Caribbean region.[^2] This approach enables scalable deployment of community resilience infrastructure through diversified funding sources and cross-sector collaboration.

## Sources

# Research Report: Community-Led Participatory Flood Risk Mapping Using Open-Source Tools

## Solution Overview

Community-led resources mapping represents an effective and replicable model for Lagos to strengthen preparation against flood management challenges.[^1][^3][^4] This participatory approach draws from successful international implementations, particularly the Ramani Huria community-driven mapping project in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania, which began in 2015 to train locals and students to collect and digitize data on infrastructure and flood-prone areas using tools like GPS, drones, and OpenStreetMap.[^7][^1] The methodology proves particularly relevant for Lagos, where the city's low-lying topography—with most land elevated less than 15 meters above sea level—makes flooding especially problematic.[^1]

## Technical Components

The technical infrastructure relies on open source tools that can be operated by anyone with an Android smartphone.[^2] Data collection employs Open Data Kit (ODK) and Open Map Kit (OMK), while map production utilizes QGIS and Java OpenStreetMap (JOSM), all of which are open source software platforms.[^2] Community members, university students, and local government personnel receive training to use JOSM and Field Papers for mapping their communities.[^9] The Dar es Salaam implementation collected drainage data with smartphones using this community mapping approach during 2017–2019.[^8] Data collection encompasses roads, building footprints, flood plains, drainage systems, and key infrastructure including schools, hospitals, and markets.[^9] The Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team with its local partner Open Map Development Tanzania was contracted by the Tanzania Urban Resilience Program of the World Bank to collect large amounts of risk identification data using this methodology.[^8]

## Implementation Details

The Community Participatory Flood Management initiative in Lagos will organize communities around each of the 57 Local Government Areas and Local Council Development Areas (LGAs/LCDAs) to implement the program.[^1] The initiative comprises four components: training communities to carry out comprehensive flood risk assessment, co-producing comprehensive flood risk assessment with trained community members, co-creating flood hazard plans including response protocols, and accessing inputs from the State Government and its partners to implement the flood hazard plans.[^1] Potential partners include the Ministry of the Environment and Water Resources, the Ministry of Health, and agencies under primary actors.[^1] The World Bank served as the main financier of the Dar es Salaam project, with the Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team (HOT) as the primary organization managing projects and OpenMap Development Tanzania (OMDTZ) as the primary local organization carrying out activities.[^2] The Ramani Huria project commenced in 2015 to address the consequences of floods in Dar es Salaam.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

The Dar es Salaam implementation achieved substantial geographic coverage, mapping 49 out of 90 wards—approximately 50% of the city—comprising about 450,000 buildings and 1,700 schools since 2015.[^2] The project mapped 29 wards covering 93 square kilometers for a population exceeding one million people, identifying 1,254 kilometers of waterways and 3,396 kilometers of roads.[^9][^7] University students completing their industrial training demonstrated the scalability of this approach by mapping 228 sub-wards within seven weeks, with nearly 500 students conducting field mapping to create a community asset and threat inventory.[^2] The data collected proves reliable through its participatory approach that incorporates local knowledge.[^2] Communities and government utilize the resulting maps for contingency planning, disaster risk reduction, and urban planning and service delivery.[^9] The Resilience Academy partnered with Delft University of Technology to investigate whether community-mapped drainage data was suitable for creating an urban flood model, validating the technical applicability of this methodology.[^8]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach directly addresses Lagos's acute flood vulnerability, which was demonstrated by the 2022–2023 flood event that resulted in USD 262,500 in damages and displaced 8,000 residents in Lagos State.[^10] The comprehensive mapping of drainage systems, waterways, flood plains, and building footprints enables communities and government agencies to develop targeted flood hazard plans and improve disaster risk reduction strategies.[^9][^1] The methodology's proven effectiveness in Dar es Salaam—another coastal city facing similar flood challenges—demonstrates its suitability for replication in other flood-prone urban environments using the same open source technology platform.[^2]

## Business Analysis

The open source nature of the technology platform significantly reduces implementation costs, as all mapping and data production software can be accessed without licensing fees.[^2] The model leverages existing community capacity and university student participation, creating a cost-effective workforce structure demonstrated by the Dar es Salaam project's ability to deploy nearly 500 student mappers simultaneously.[^2] The World Bank's financing of the Tanzania implementation establishes a precedent for international development bank support for similar initiatives.[^2] The project's suitability for replication in other cities facing floods using the same open source technology creates opportunities for standardized implementation frameworks and knowledge transfer.[^2] Government partnerships spanning multiple ministries and agencies provide institutional support structures, with the Lagos initiative engaging the Ministry of the Environment and Water Resources, Ministry of Health, and agencies under primary actors.[^1]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Africa/Lagos-Nigeria/Lagos-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^2]: https://use.metropolis.org/case-studies/ramani-huria
- [^3]: https://lasbca.lagosstate.gov.ng/wp-content/uploads/2021/05/Lagos_Resilience_Strategy.pdf
- [^4]: https://www.fsdafrica.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/Briefing-Note-1-Flood-Risk-in-Lagos-01.10.21.pdf
- [^5]: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/earth-science/articles/10.3389/feart.2020.00304/full

# Research Report: Climate Risk Atlas and Integrated Information Infrastructure

## Solution Overview

Mexico City has developed a comprehensive information infrastructure that integrates climate risk data, vulnerability assessments, and planning tools to support adaptation decision-making across the metropolitan region. This action helps promote a multifunctional information infrastructure that can reduce risks to the population, ensure continuity of basic services, and improve the environment and quality of life of the inhabitants of CDMX.[^5] The approach encompasses the development of a City Risk Atlas with a set of indicators related to hazard, exposure, and social vulnerability, which acts as a management tool to support implementation of the Climate Action Programme.[^1]

## Technical Components

The City Risk Atlas serves as the core technical platform, incorporating a set of indicators related to hazard, exposure, and social vulnerability to support climate action implementation.[^1] Mexico City developed a Local Climate Action Strategy towards 2050 supported by a Climate Action Programme for the short and medium term, underpinned by a set of resilience actions and indicators to monitor progress and gaps.[^1] The Water Resilience Plan includes generating models of relationship between the lacustrine zone and the Valley of Mexico basin, as well as detailed models of the heritage zone in terms of water.[^4] The Resilience Agency is working with Deltares, a Dutch organization expert in water resource management, together with the Metropolitan Autonomous University (UAM) and academics from National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM) on the Water Resilience Plan.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The CDMX Resilience Strategy covers the whole city/administrative region as its project area.[^3] The Resilience Strategy for CDMX involves multiple stakeholder types including City Government, National Government, Supranational/Intergovernmental Institutions, Private Sector, NGO/Philanthropy, Community/Citizen Groups, and Research Institutes/Universities.[^3] Mexico City's infrastructure does not receive adequate maintenance due to lack of investment, resulting in an inefficient system.[^4] The earthquake in Mexico City resulted in more than 3 million people in the metropolitan area having no water, causing social conflict and vandalism towards water pipes distributing water.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

Under PACCM, the city's resilience, as well as the population's adaptation capacities, are increased, improving quality of life for the 5.6 million city residents most vulnerable to consequences of climate change.[^2] Under PACCM, Mexico City created a territorial planning program that integrates environmental and urban policies resulting in increased conservation of soil and saving 19,242 m3 of water.[^2] PACCM will modernize Mexico City's public transport to become more energy efficient and save 2.7 million kWh of energy.[^2] The Green Challenge has allowed the planting of 27,082,593 trees and plants in Mexico City.[^6] The Green Challenge included the plantation of 16.9 million plants only on conservation land, including the reforestation of 16,505 hectares in forests and rivers.[^6] Additionally, 4,155 inhabitants of rural communities in Mexico City are benefiting in return for their work as brigade members in reforestation and ecological conservation activities.[^6]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Between 2000 and 2015, Mexico City experienced 51 events of extreme rains, floods and landslides that affected 52,331 people.[^6] Venustiano Carranza implemented urban revegetation measures in flood-exposed areas following its climate risk assessment.[^1] Azcapotzalco has taken action to improve and rehabilitate the secondary network of drainage, as well as scale up the creation of green areas.[^1]

## Business Analysis

Mexico City also has a Green Infrastructure Special Programme that acts as a participatory planning instrument for nature-based solutions, founded upon four principles: connectivity, accessibility, functionality, and resilience.[^1] Several initiatives have been launched under Mexico City's Green Infrastructure Special Programme, including 'Planting Parks', a Green Challenge of urban revegetation, river sanitation, as well as the creation of wetlands, pollinating gardens, and urban orchards.[^1] The Water Fund for CDMX aims to strengthen the water supply system and increase environmental protection of water conservation areas.[^5]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://mcr2030.undrr.org/news/mexico-city-focuses-solutions-and-scale
- [^2]: https://www.c40.org/case-studies/cities100-mexico-city-comprehensive-program-increases-resilience/
- [^3]: https://use.metropolis.org/case-studies/cdmx-resilience-strategy
- [^4]: https://www.landuum.com/en/interview/arnoldo-matus-kramer-chief-resilience-officer-mexico-city/
- [^5]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Latin America & Caribbean/MexicoCity-Mexico/Mexico-City-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^6]: https://aiph.org/green-city-case-studies/mexico-city-mexico/

# Research Report: Array of Things Urban Environmental Sensing Network

## Solution Overview

The Array of Things represents an urban sensing infrastructure deployed across Chicago to collect real-time environmental and operational data through a network of interactive, modular sensor boxes mounted on streetlight poles.[^11][^8] This federally funded initiative was developed through a partnership between the City of Chicago, University of Chicago, and Argonne National Laboratory to enable data-driven decision-making for urban challenges.[^4] The datasets gathered make Chicago a truly 'smart city,' allowing the City to operate more efficiently and realize cost savings by anticipating and proactively addressing challenges such as urban flooding and traffic safety.[^11][^1]

## Technical Components

The sensor nodes consist of protective shields containing up to 15 sensors, a computer, two cameras, a microphone, and a cooling fan.[^6] These instruments measure temperature, barometric pressure, light, vibration, carbon monoxide, nitrogen dioxide, sulfur dioxide, ozone, ambient sound intensity, pedestrian and vehicle traffic, and surface temperature.[^1][^8][^13] The nodes include cameras and a microphone, along with sensors for humidity, vibration, magnetic fields, temperature, air pollution, and barometric pressure.[^9] Each node was equipped with an Nvidia graphics processing unit to perform computations on images out in the field and sent only processed data along to the network—a form of edge computing.[^9] The nodes utilize an Argonne-developed technology platform called Waggle, which allows for powerful and secure remote processing of measurements before transmission of data to a central server.[^3] Creek images are collected using a downward-facing camera that captures a 96-dpi image every second.[^7] With continued development, the system may soon be able to collect data on flooding and standing water, precipitation, and wind.[^6] Additional capabilities were developed using the platform, such as methods for interpreting weather and image data to detect standing water and urban flooding.[^1][^3]

## Implementation Details

The National Science Foundation awarded a $3.1 million grant in fall 2015 to support the development of this urban sensing instrument.[^1][^3][^6] Charlie Catlett's initiative received $12 million in National Science Foundation funding over 10 years.[^9] Additional funding was provided by the University of Chicago Innovation Fund, Argonne National Laboratory (which contributed more than $1 million in internal research funding), and the John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Foundation.[^1][^3][^6] The project was first publicly announced in June 2014.[^6] The NSF grant funded the development and installation of 500 sensor nodes, with 50 nodes planned for installation in early 2016, 200 by the end of 2016, and 500 by the end of 2017.[^1][^3] Between 2016 and 2019, the team attached 140 nodes to Chicago streetlights.[^9] In August 2016, the team installed the first current nodes on two light posts on Chicago's near southwest side and activated those nodes in October 2016.[^6] The project launched in 2016, with sensor boxes mounted on light posts collecting real-time data on Chicago's environmental surroundings and urban activity.[^6][^12] To date, more than 140 sensor nodes have been deployed throughout the City of Chicago in a variety of neighborhoods to assess micro-environmental conditions such as weather, air quality, noise levels, and both human and vehicle traffic flow.[^5] By 2018, the goal was to place 500 nodes on traffic poles throughout Chicago, with each node comprising 28 sensors, 2 cameras and a microphone.[^4] The original nodes were retired in September 2021 after many had been in service for 4 years, two years beyond their planned lifespans.[^13] SAGE test units replaced a number of original nodes in late 2021, with many more planned to be replaced in 2022 as the new platform underwent extensive testing and evaluation.[^13] By the end of 2025, the team planned to install 50 of the $10,000 nodes in Chicago, replacing the earlier-generation nodes.[^9]

The project was led by researchers from the Urban Center for Computation and Data at the Computation Institute, a joint initiative of Argonne National Laboratory and the University of Chicago under the direction of Argonne computer scientist Charlie Catlett.[^6][^10] The multidisciplinary team included designers, engineers and scientists at the Computation Institute, the School of the Art Institute of Chicago, Northern Illinois University and the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign.[^1][^10] Efforts to develop the Waggle platform—the software and hardware that powers the project—were led by Argonne Institute of Science and Engineering Co-Director Pete Beckman, together with Rajesh Sankaran and Catlett.[^6][^10] The initiative was executed in partnership with the City of Chicago, which installed nodes onto light posts across the city via its Department of Transportation.[^6] The City's Department of Innovation and Technology served as the lead agency on the project and managed Chicago's data portal, which housed the data for the public.[^6] Civic organization Smart Chicago Collaborative managed community engagement and public outreach.[^6] Other partners included a collective of scientists from twelve universities worldwide, as well as technical advice and support from industry partners such as Microsoft, Cisco, and Motorola Solutions.[^6] In a participatory process, the team at Argonne and local universities worked with everyday Chicagoans and city departments to decide where to place the sensors.[^9] All citizens could submit proposals for where a node should be placed, whether in their neighborhood or elsewhere.[^4] In August 2016, the project released the final version of the project's governance and privacy policies, as well as responses to public feedback and the Civic Engagement Report.[^6][^12] An oversight committee, comprised of academic researchers, city officials and cybersecurity experts, regularly reviewed the project and additional technologies that may be developed and added to it in the future, assisted by an independent security and privacy review team and an external scientific advisory committee.[^3]

In 2021, the team partnered with multiple Chicago community areas, the City of Chicago, Microsoft Research Urban Innovation team, and JCDecaux on project Eclipse to design an experimental air pollution monitoring network with sensors on 115 Chicago bus shelters.[^13] The team partnered with Microsoft Research to install 115 low-cost solar-powered air quality sensors on bus shelters across the city.[^9]

## Benefits and Impacts

The system provides real-time, location-based data about the city's environment, infrastructure and activity to researchers and the public.[^8] All information measured is publicly available for free through the City of Chicago Data Portal and other open data platforms.[^1] Because the data is published openly and without charge, it supports the equitable development of innovative applications, such as a mobile application that allows residents to track their exposure to certain air contaminants, or to navigate through the city based on avoiding urban heat islands, poor air quality, or excessive noise and congestion.[^11][^13] For local government, data generated by the sensors helps make both short-term operations and long-term planning more efficient and effectively realizes cost savings by anticipating floods, traffic safety incidents, and other challenges.[^6] The nodes have been used to assess the safety of at-grade rail crossings, monitor pedestrian crosswalk usage, and detect flooding along the Chicago River.[^9] The nodes allow the city to track vehicle and pedestrian collisions to understand which intersections or traffic flows lead to pedestrian deaths and make urban planning changes accordingly.[^4]

Chicago's current monitoring of temperature data was based on just three sensors across the city, but with temperature data becoming available at hyper-local levels, the city could vastly improve its understanding of micro-level weather patterns and better estimate where to apply salt before snow storms.[^6] Climate researchers have dramatically higher resolution data than currently provided by existing weather stations to study urban microclimates, with benefits for hyper-local weather forecasting and energy efficiency.[^3] The nodes can be used to detect standing water in Chicago's West and South sides during rainstorms, helping city employees identify bottlenecks in the city's sewer system.[^4] Chicago has one of the worst incidences of asthma mortality in the country; the nodes help monitor air pollutant levels across the city and take appropriate measures where necessary.[^4] The resulting data from the bus shelter sensors showed pollution hot spots near industrial corridors on Chicago's South and West Sides in unprecedentedly high resolution.[^9] Kathleen Cagney, who directs the Institute for Social Research at the University of Michigan, used environmental data from the sensors for a study on public health, finding higher asthma rates in places where sensors detected more air pollution.[^9] Nodes support advanced capabilities such as measuring sound levels on busy streets and around public transportation, or assessing air quality in neighborhoods near industrial facilities.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This urban sensing infrastructure directly addresses climate adaptation challenges by enabling the city to anticipate and proactively address urban flooding through real-time detection of standing water and identification of sewer system bottlenecks.[^11][^4] The system measures factors that impact livability such as climate, air quality and noise, serving as a figurative 'fitness tracker' for the city.[^11][^12] The initiative enables scientific investigation of solutions to urban challenges ranging from air quality to urban flooding through comprehensive environmental monitoring.[^1][^3]

## Business Analysis

The project was funded primarily by the U.S. National Science Foundation under grant NSF 1532133, with a $3.1 million initial award and $12 million total over 10 years.[^5][^9][^1] Additional funding was provided by the University of Chicago Innovation Fund, Argonne National Laboratory (contributing more than $1 million in internal research funding), and the John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Foundation.[^1][^3][^6] The newer generation nodes cost approximately $10,000 per unit.[^9] The project was part of the White House Smart Cities Initiative.[^10] Working with the City of Chicago's Department of Transportation and Department of Innovation and Technology, the initiative boosted local research, development, prototyping and the demonstration of new technologies and services.[^1] The project represented the first at-scale research infrastructure deployment enabling researchers to study an urban environment.[^10] Serious questions existed around the sustainability of non-profit funding if the city wanted to expand the project, as the city budget would struggle to take on yet another line item.[^4] The immeasurable value of increased civil engagement from Chicago's citizens, with all citizens able to submit proposals for where a node should be placed, represented an additional benefit beyond direct cost savings.[^4]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://voices.uchicago.edu/compinst/press-releases/national-science-foundation-awards-31-million-array-things-project/
- [^2]: https://www.anl.gov/mcs/array-of-things
- [^3]: https://news.uchicago.edu/story/national-science-foundation-awards-31-million-array-things-project
- [^4]: https://d3.harvard.edu/platform-digit/submission/the-city-of-chicago-and-its-array-of-things-project/
- [^5]: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8991303/
- [^6]: https://www.govtech.com/analytics/What-is-Chicagos-Array-of-Things-Initiative.html
- [^7]: https://www.anl.gov/cels/using-ai-for-water-level-detection
- [^8]: https://datasmart.hks.harvard.edu/solutions/array-things
- [^9]: https://www.technologyreview.com/2022/08/19/1057848/array-of-things-goes-global/
- [^10]: https://www.anl.gov/mcs/article/array-of-things-receives-major-nsf-grant-for-new-sensor-nodes
- [^11]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Chicago-US/Resilient Chicago.pdf
- [^12]: https://www.smartchicagocollaborative.org/work/ecosystem/array-of-things-civic-engagement/
- [^13]: https://arrayofthings.github.io/

metadata.quote: The datasets gathered will make Chicago a truly 'smart city,' allowing the City to operate more efficiently and realize cost savings by anticipating and proactively addressing challenges such as urban flooding and traffic safety.
metadata.context: The datasets gathered will make Chicago a truly 'smart city,' allowing the City to operate more efficiently and realize cost savings by anticipating and proactively addressing challenges such as urban flooding and traffic safety. To date, 100 of the planned 500 sensor boxes have been installed.

# Research Report: Real-Time Air Quality Monitoring and Public Information System

## Solution Overview

The NSW Government provides real-time air quality information through a comprehensive online platform that enables residents to access current pollution levels, forecasts, and health alerts.[^8] This system processes data from Australia's largest air quality monitoring network to deliver hourly updated classifications and daily forecasts for the Greater Sydney Metropolitan Region.[^1][^2] The platform serves as a critical public health tool, offering subscription-based alerts when air quality reaches concerning levels.[^1]

## Technical Components

The NSW Air Quality Monitoring Network operates as Australia's largest system with more than 90 long-term stations, well exceeding the number recommended by the National Environment Protection (Ambient Air Quality) Measure.[^7] The Department currently operates 21 air quality monitoring stations in Sydney, one of which is a temporary research station.[^5] The Alexandria station, which is the 95th station in NSW's air quality monitoring network, records and streams hourly data for carbon monoxide, nitrogen dioxide, ozone, PM10 and PM2.5 airborne particles, sulfur dioxide and visibility, while collecting meteorological data on humidity, temperature, wind speed and direction.[^6] The Ultimo station, situated at the University of Technology Sydney, tracks particle pollution (PM2.5 and PM10) as well as nitrogen dioxide, carbon monoxide and ozone, primarily measuring pollution from vehicles travelling through Broadway.[^1] As part of the Blue Mountains and Lithgow Air Watch Project led by NSW Environment Protection Authority, 12 low-cost air quality (KOALA) sensors were deployed at locations selected by community representatives.[^3] The pollution data is processed to produce an overall air quality rating, ranging from 'Extremely Poor' to 'Good'.[^1] The NSW Air Quality Index provides a simple and easy-to-understand measure of air quality as part of DPIE's data reporting channels.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The NSW Government began monitoring air quality in Sydney in the 1950s and has routinely monitored air pollution in the city since 1951.[^5][^1] The Alexandria rooftop station, a joint partnership between City of Sydney and the NSW Department of Planning and Environment, was located to provide detailed air quality information for the growing population of residents, workers and visitors to the Alexandria/Green Square area.[^6] This station complements readings from the CBD monitoring station at Cook and Phillip Park.[^6] The NSW Government installed an air quality monitoring station at Cook & Phillip Park in the City of Sydney area, with plans to expand to other sites.[^8] The Ultimo station is the third in the City of Sydney.[^1] The Department of Climate Change, Energy, the Environment and Water is collaborating with leading research partners and environment agencies in Victoria, South Australia and Queensland to develop and deploy air pollution sensor networks for real-time air pollution mapping.[^3] This research will support the future integration of data from sensor networks with high-quality data from the NSW Air Quality Monitoring Network, projections from regional airshed modelling and remote sensing information.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

Using data from monitoring stations, the system provides hourly updated air quality categories.[^5] Air quality readings and Air Quality Categories are updated hourly and a daily air quality forecast is made for the Greater Sydney Metropolitan Region at 4 pm every day.[^2] When air quality is 'Poor', an alert and health advice is issued to all those who have subscribed to the NSW Government's free air quality update service.[^1] The Department of Planning, Industry and Environment provides the public with timely and reliable air quality information through the DPIE website, which provides real-time air quality data, air quality forecasts and health advice.[^4] The Alexandria station provides locally specific information during air quality incidents such as bushfires or during heatwaves and supports the NSW air quality alert and forecasting system.[^6] Readings from the Alexandria station are used to assess compliance against the national health-based air quality standards.[^6] The NSW Government website displays detailed pollutant concentrations for all air quality monitoring stations in NSW.[^2] DPIE provides air quality data to a range of government agencies, research institutions and other organisations, with the data used to inform air quality management, policy and research.[^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The monitoring system addresses increasing air quality challenges driven by climate-related events. There have been significant increases in the frequency of dangerous fire weather conditions across NSW since 1973.[^9] NSW experienced extensive bushfires in spring and summer 2019–20, with around 2500 homes destroyed in the state.[^9] Concentrations of ground-level ozone exceeded national air quality standards in Sydney on six or fewer days in 2018 and 2020 but climbed to a record 28 days in 2019.[^7] Between 2018 and 2020, PM10 and PM2.5 concentrations exceeded the national air quality standards on up to 58 days a year in Sydney and up to 151 days a year in regional areas of NSW.[^7]

## Business Analysis

Residents around the state can subscribe to a free Sydney air quality forecast and alerts at airquality.nsw.gov.au.[^1] The public can sign up for air quality updates and alerts from the NSW Air Quality Monitoring Network on Current air quality in New South Wales or the department's website.[^6] The readings from the Alexandria station can be found on the Government's website Air quality concentration data - updated hourly, and also on the new dedicated website Air Quality NSW.[^6] The NSW Government website provides an interactive map to view locations of all air quality monitoring stations across NSW.[^2] The Alexandria station represents a partnership model between City of Sydney and the NSW Department of Planning and Environment.[^6]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://www.airquality.nsw.gov.au/
