# Research Report: LiDAR UAV Surveying for Flood Modeling and River Basin Mapping

## Solution Overview

The Tanzania Urban Resilience Program deployed an innovative combination of Light Detection and Range (LiDAR) UAV surveying and bathometric surveying to support flood modelling within the flood-prone Msimbazi river basin.[^1] This World Bank funded survey project served two purposes: generation of reliable, accurate and fit for purpose bathymetric and topographic data of the Msimbazi River and its floodplanes for riverine flooding related detailed studies, and rigorous comparison of Digital Terrain Model (DTM) generation by Unmanned Aerial Vehicle Photogrammetry and UAV LiDAR.[^2] The outputs were used to guide river upgrades that improved Dar es Salaam's resilience to flooding.[^1]

## Technical Components

The LiDAR system used for this comparison weighed 3.5kg and was deployed with a multi-rotor drone (DJI M600pro), bringing the total take-off weight to just under 15 kg.[^4] For this project, 16 out of 32 lasers were used to construct the point cloud using the Velodyne HDL 32e laser scanner, which provided 2 returns per measurement.[^4] Because all components that determined the accuracy of the final point cloud were attached to the drone, there was no need for ground control points, saving considerable time.[^4] A very positive aspect about the LiDAR survey acquisition was real time control on the measurement of the area.[^4] Because it was a direct measurement technique, the results could be monitored in real time via WiFi/3G/4G connection with the system on a field laptop or even from a computer in an office using 3G/4G only.[^4]

## Implementation Details

CDR International in association with Shore Monitoring and Research defined an integral full survey campaign for the lower basin of the Msimbazi River, which was executed in February 2019.[^2] Shore Monitoring and Research BV and CDR International BV conducted bathymetric and topographic surveying of flood-prone rivers in Dar es Salaam for The World Bank, with a final report dated August 7, 2019.[^3] The entire survey campaign for the Msimbazi River area took place from 14th until 20th of February 2019.[^4] The LiDAR survey was conducted from 7 different home locations throughout the Msimbazi Valley area in Dar es Salaam, which all needed to be assessed on appropriateness, security and possibility to get permission from local leaders of the area.[^4] The multi-rotor with the LiDAR systems could perform flights of 13-15 minutes, after which batteries needed to be changed and therefore the drone had to come home and land in a controlled way regularly.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

UAV LiDAR potentially provided significant cost savings compared to both traditional LiDAR and field surveying methods.[^2] The elimination of ground control points through the integrated system design saved substantial time during survey operations.[^4] The real-time monitoring capability enabled immediate quality control and verification of measurement coverage during field operations.[^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach directly addressed flooding hazards within the flood-prone Msimbazi river basin by generating reliable, accurate bathymetric and topographic data for riverine flooding related detailed studies.[^1][^2] The survey outputs were used to guide river upgrades that improved Dar es Salaam's resilience to flooding.[^1]

## Business Analysis

Preliminary insights of the analysis were shared and presented at a master class at the Land and Poverty Conference 2019 on March 25-29, 2019 in Washington, DC and at a workshop on DTM drone applications for Dar es Salaam stakeholders at World Bank Office in Dar es Salaam on April 11th, 2019.[^2] The World Bank funded this survey project as part of the Tanzania Urban Resilience Program.[^2][^3] The technology potentially provided significant cost savings compared to both traditional LiDAR and field surveying methods, making it an economically viable approach for flood modelling applications.[^2]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/video/2019/05/15/drones-helping-to-understand-flood-risk-in-tanzania
- [^2]: https://cdr-international.nl/lidar-drone-survey-msimbazi-river-dar-es-salaam/
- [^3]: https://documents.worldbank.org/curated/en/310991567574404522/text/Bathymetric-and-Topographic-Surveying-of-Flood-Prone-Rivers-in-Dar-Es-Salaam-Comparative-Case-of-UAV-Photogrammetry-and-UAV-LiDAR-for-Generation-of-Digital-Terrain-Models-for-Flood-Modelling-Purposes-Msimbazi-River-Dar-es-Salaam.txt
- [^4]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Africa/Dar-es-Salaam-Tanzania/Bathymetric-and-Topographic-Surveying-of-Flood-Prone-Rivers-in-Dar-Es-Salaam-Comparative-Case-of-UAV-Photogrammetry-and-UAV-LiDAR-for-Generation-of-Digital-Terrain-Models-for-Flood-Modelling-Purposes-Msimbazi-River-Dar-es-Salaam.pdf

# Research Report: GIS-Enabled Emergency Information Sharing Platform

## Solution Overview

The Common Operational Picture is a GIS-enabled electronic platform that enhances emergency information sharing and support mechanisms for dealing with multiple hazards in Hong Kong.[^6] Launched by the Civil Engineering and Development Department in 2019, this system enables real-time sharing of emergency information among government departments to improve coordination during extreme weather events and natural disasters.[^4][^1] The platform serves as a critical tool for more than 13 government agencies to monitor citywide situations and formulate rapid response plans during emergencies.[^5][^3]

## Technical Components

The Geotechnical Engineering Office manages this common Geographic Information System platform, which facilitates real-time sharing of emergency information including details of landslides, flooding, building damage, and major road incidents among government departments.[^1] The Civil Engineering and Development Department implemented Esri's ArcGIS on Microsoft's Azure cloud platform as part of their IT strategy to manage infrastructure and safety goals.[^5] The platform integrates authoritative data sources including real-time weather information, satellite imagery, real-time traffic with CCTV feeds from the Transport and Housing Bureau, demographic information, and the locations of emergency management shelters.[^5] The system displays the current status of various agencies' emergency operations centers and the locations of all reported emergency incidents across Hong Kong.[^5] The Civil Engineering and Development Department collects and records vast amounts of geospatial slope and geotechnical data which provides crucial support for discharging its duties of keeping slopes safe and protecting lives and properties.[^7]

## Implementation Details

In December 2019, the Hong Kong Civil Engineering and Development Department entered into an Enterprise Agreement with Esri China (Hong Kong) Limited to develop this emergency management system.[^5] The partnership between CEDD, Esri, and Microsoft resulted in the new Common Operating Picture for the emergency management operations of more than 13 government agencies.[^5] The Interdepartmental Task Force on Emergency Preparedness, established in October 2014 and led by the Development Bureau, created this GIS-enabled platform to examine the potential impact of multiple hazards arising from extreme weather events.[^6] The Emergency Monitoring and Support Centre of the Security Bureau makes use of the Common Operational Picture developed by the Civil Engineering and Development Department to monitor the city's situation.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

This IT platform provides a timely overall picture of emergency situations, which greatly improves situational awareness across relevant departments and facilitates decision-making and coordination of emergency response.[^6] The result is greater collaboration and more effective decision-making among the Hong Kong government agencies.[^5] The system enabled enhanced coordination during Super Typhoon Saola approaching Hong Kong in August 2023, when government departments shared emergency information in real-time to improve emergency responses.[^1] The Emergency Monitoring and Support Centre utilizes the platform to conduct real-time citywide monitoring and integrate updates from various departments to swiftly assess risks and formulate response plans and measures.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The platform addresses multiple climate hazards by providing real-time monitoring and coordination capabilities for extreme weather events including typhoons, landslides, and flooding. The Emergency Monitoring and Support Centre is activated when the no. 8 gale or storm signal is hoisted to coordinate resources and emergency response operations across various departments.[^2] The system stands ready for full activation upon the issuance of Tropical Cyclone Warning Signal No. 8, with directorate officers of relevant departments closely monitoring the community in real time.[^3]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.info.gov.hk/gia/general/202308/31/P2023083100803.htm
- [^2]: https://www.info.gov.hk/gia/general/202008/19/P2020081900411.htm
- [^3]: https://www.info.gov.hk/gia/general/202509/21/P2025092100550.htm
- [^4]: https://www.scmp.com/comment/letters/article/3139595/how-much-value-has-open-data-created-hong-kong
- [^5]: https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/leveraging-arcgis-azure-deliver-multi-agency-common-operating-miller
- [^6]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/China/Hong-Kong/HK_Climate_Action_Plan_2030+_booklet_En.pdf
- [^7]: https://www.hkproptech.org/en/insights/articles/Successfulcasesoflocalgeographicinformationapplications

# Research Report: Groundwater Monitoring and Modeling for Sea Level Rise Impacts

## Solution Overview

San Francisco's Port, Department of Building Inspection, and Office of Resilience and Capital Planning are conducting groundwater data collection and modeling efforts to better understand the impacts of rising groundwater at the shoreline, including liquefaction risks.[^7] This approach addresses a critical climate adaptation challenge, as rising groundwater levels in the San Francisco Bay Area could impact twice as much land area as coastal flooding alone over the next century, putting more than 5,200 state- and federally-managed contaminated sites at risk.[^6] Higher sea levels will also increase the elevation of the groundwater table, increasing the susceptibility of some soils to liquefaction during an earthquake.[^7]

## Technical Components

The groundwater data collection and modeling initiative builds upon extensive technical foundations established through regional research efforts. Pathways Climate Institute LLC and the San Francisco Estuary Institute gathered and analyzed multiple data sets and collaborated with city and county partners to analyze and map the existing highest annual shallow groundwater table and its likely response to future sea-level rise.[^4] The project produced web maps showing existing depth to groundwater and future conditions under various sea-level rise scenarios.[^4] The effort produced existing and future condition depth to groundwater GIS data in geodatabase format.[^4] Pathways developed GIS layers of existing shallow groundwater surface that represents the water table at its highest elevation during wet winters, when emergent groundwater is first likely to occur.[^5] The existing groundwater surface was modified in response to sea level rise scenarios to represent a range of future conditions, with sea level rise scenarios selected to pair with sea level rise inundation mapping presented in the Adapting to Rising Tides Shoreline Flood Explorer.[^5] The USGS model MODFLOW was used to estimate the depth of the present-day coastal water table and the future response of groundwater levels to a range of sea-level rise scenarios for the entire California coast.[^2] The extremely high resolution of the model allows it to capture the influences of topography and drainage networks on groundwater levels that could help pinpoint areas where ponding at land surface may occur, or where the water table intersects shallow, sub-surface infrastructure, such as basements, parking garages, buried utilities, road beds, and other infrastructure.[^2] A unique aspect of the MODFLOW model work is that it covers the State of California at high resolution by stitching together overlapping model domains.[^2] The Bay Area groundwater risk maps were based in part on a 2019 analysis led by Hill of more than 11,000 measurements of groundwater levels in the Bay Area.[^6] The groundwater measurements showed places where weird stuff is happening that models wouldn't predict, such as finding extra high groundwater levels on the uphill side of a compacted roadbed, or places where groundwater is surprisingly low because a leaking sewer pipe is allowing groundwater to drain away.[^6] A team of geologists and hydrologists assessed the impacts of sea-level rise on liquefaction by computing changes in liquefaction potential index for 347 boring sites around the San Francisco Bay for groundwater table models developed for current and increased sea-levels of up to 5 meters for the magnitude 7.0 HayWired earthquake scenario.[^3]

## Implementation Details

The groundwater data collection and modeling work emerged from stakeholder engagement for the releases of the USGS HayWired earthquake scenario and the Coastal Storm Modeling System projects, in which local planners and engineers asked where, why, and by how much liquefaction hazards may change due to sea-level rise in the future.[^3] Pathways Climate Institute and the San Francisco Estuary Institute collaborated to extend their groundwater research to better support regional adaptation planning, funded by pro bono contributions from Pathways and a California Resilience Challenge Grant submitted on behalf of four Bay Area counties: Alameda, San Mateo, San Francisco, and Marin County.[^5] The initial effort covered four counties (Alameda, Marin, San Francisco, San Mateo) and was funded by the Bay Area Council's California Resilience Challenge.[^4] The mapping focused on the San Francisco Bay side of each county and did not include the Pacific coastline of Marin, San Francisco, nor San Mateo Counties.[^4] This project included digitizing hundreds of soil borings to fill data gaps and improve the assessment, extensive coordination with city and county staff to ensure the end products meet their needs, and coordination with at-risk communities on messaging and outreach strategies.[^5] City and county representatives provided essential support by gathering data and reviewing depth-to-groundwater maps.[^4] This project included a Technical Advisory Committee with academics Dr. Kristina Hill (Professor at UC Berkeley) and Dr. Kevin Befus (Assistant Professor at University of Arkansas) and scientist Dr. Patrick Barnard (USGS) who are pushing the science of groundwater's response to sea level rise forward.[^5] A research team led by UC Berkeley professor Kristina Hill published a report and a series of searchable maps earlier in 2023 highlighting Bay Area regions that face the highest risk of flooding due to rising groundwater.[^6] Hill and collaborators at the Pathways Climate Institute and the San Francisco Estuary Institute published the groundwater risk report and maps.[^6] U.S. Geological Survey scientists studied the impacts of sea-level rise on liquefaction severity around the San Francisco Bay Area, California for the magnitude 7.0 HayWired earthquake scenario along the Hayward Fault.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The groundwater data collection and modeling efforts provide critical insights into liquefaction risks and infrastructure vulnerabilities. The sensitive sites are artificial fill around the Bay Area margins where the water table is already shallow.[^3] For the HayWired scenario ground shaking, these sites may already have a severe liquefaction hazard, and with 1 meter of sea-level rise, the hazard at more of these sites increases to severe.[^3] As groundwater levels rise due to sea level rise, liquefaction zones are expected to increase in size.[^7] Findings show that low-lying coastal communities and critical infrastructure are most at risk of future rising water tables, including areas in San Francisco Bay, Santa Barbara, Ventura, Port of L.A., Long Beach, Seal Beach, San Diego Bay and San Francisco and San Diego Airports.[^2] According to a 2023 study by the San Francisco Estuary Institute, scientists expect rising groundwater levels to occur many years before they reach the surface in Alameda, Marin, San Francisco and San Mateo counties, based on current groundwater levels and global climate projections.[^1] A new paper, Increasing threat of coastal groundwater hazards from sea-level rise in California, published in Nature Climate Change on August 17, 2020, describes a numerical model that estimates the depth of the present-day coastal water table and the future response of these groundwater levels to a range of sea-level rise scenarios for the entire California coast.[^2] The work is the first to quantify the potential coastal flooding that could result from water table rise along the entire coast of California and serves as a possible model approach for building out an assessment across the entire United States coastline.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This groundwater data collection and modeling initiative directly addresses the compounding climate hazards of sea-level rise and seismic liquefaction risk at the San Francisco shoreline. The approach enables city officials to understand how rising groundwater will interact with existing infrastructure vulnerabilities and earthquake hazards, providing essential data for adaptation planning in low-lying coastal areas where the water table is already shallow.

## Business Analysis

Federal legislative support for groundwater monitoring is expanding, as U.S. Reps. Kevin Mullin, D-San Mateo, and Andrew Garbarino, R-N.Y., introduced legislation in June that would set aside $5 million over the next year for the United States Geological Survey to study and map groundwater rise nationally through 2100.[^1] USGS scientists would also need to recommend mitigation strategies for communities and create a public-facing website displaying their maps, and within three years, the agency would need to submit a report of its findings to Congress.[^1] Regional collaboration models have proven effective, as demonstrated by the California Resilience Challenge Grant funding mechanism that supported the initial four-county assessment.[^5] Last year, the San Francisco Bay Area Planning and Urban Research Association, or SPUR, partnered with local organizations to publish a study about East Palo Alto's flood risk due to groundwater rise.[^1] The pro bono contributions from Pathways Climate Institute alongside public grant funding demonstrate a hybrid financing model that leverages both private sector expertise and government resources to advance critical climate adaptation research.[^5]

[^1]: https://www.mercurynews.com/2023/08/24/rising-groundwater-threatens-bay-area-infrastructure-contaminated-sites/
[^2]: https://www.usgs.gov/news/featured-story/new-usgs-study-projects-increased-risk-coastal-flooding-groundwater
[^3]: https://www.usgs.gov/publications/sea-level-rise-impacts-liquefaction-hazard-san-francisco-bay-area
[^4]: https://www.sfei.org/projects/depth-groundwater-mapping
[^5]: https://www.sfei.org/projects/depth-groundwater-mapping-phase-2
[^6]: https://news.berkeley.edu/2023/03/20/rising-groundwater-threatens-bay-area-infrastructure-contaminated-sites/
[^7]: https://sfport.com/files/2023-11/Port%20Climate%20Action%20Plan%20-%20FINAL%20-%20Nov%202023.pdf

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.kqed.org/science/1997954/lawmakers-push-to-map-groundwater-before-it-swamps-americas-infrastructure
- [^2]: https://www.usgs.gov/news/new-model-shows-sea-level-rise-can-cause-increases-groundwater-levels-along-californias-coasts
- [^3]: https://www.usgs.gov/tools/liquefaction-and-sea-level-rise
- [^4]: https://www.sfei.org/projects/shallow-groundwater-response-sea-level-rise
- [^5]: https://www.pathwaysclimate.com/projects/shallow-groundwater-response-to-sea-level-rise
- [^6]: https://vcresearch.berkeley.edu/news/rising-groundwater-threatens-thousands-toxic-sites-bay-area
- [^7]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/San-Francisco-US/HCR_FullReport_200326_0.pdf

# Research Report: Integrated Real-Time Risk Management and Monitoring System

## Solution Overview

Rome is developing a centralized territorial information technology system to manage multiple risk categories including earthquake risks, hydrological instability, gravitational instability, fire, and extreme climatic events monitored by Rome Civil Protection.[^2] The system includes the creation of a powerful technological infrastructure to manage and integrate all the data and information of traffic, mobility, transport, weather and supervision cameras in real time.[^2] This approach also encompasses the coordination of operational procedures for planning, prevention, intervention and interactive communications with citizens.[^2]

## Technical Components

The technological infrastructure integrates multiple data streams including traffic, mobility, transport, weather monitoring, and supervision camera feeds in real time.[^2] The Territorial Information System in use by the Ufficio comunicazioni, informatica e sistema informativo territoriale di protezione civile enables the analysis, processing and updating of computerized cartography relating to natural and anthropogenic risks present in the territory.[^3] The office contributes to the creation of thematic maps useful for managing events in Roma Capitale, for campaigns aimed at informing citizens, and for updating the General Emergency Plan of Civil Protection.[^3] The development of the platform is supported by the international private sector, including Leonardo and DXC technology providing advanced and innovative AI-driven architecture components within the SIM infrastructure.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The award to commence the design of the system was received in late 2022, after which the design of the system commenced, and the operational phase of the programme commenced in late 2024.[^4] The Head Office serves as the Department in Charge, with partners including Civil protection, Local Police, Departments of Rome, Agencies Investee companies, and External entities.[^2] To date a number of meetings have been held with the Civil Protection in order to assess and define the scope of the Central Office.[^2] It is now necessary to establish a work group to develop an initial feasibility study in order to assess the project from a regulatory, administrative and economic point of view.[^2] The long-term goal is to achieve a Single Operations Center that permanently brings together all the stakeholders of the City Government, including representatives of different Departments, agencies and service companies.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

The Municipal Operations Centre (COC) serves as the Mayor's operational tool for managing emergencies affecting the municipal territory.[^1] It is a permanent structure that is activated in the event of an emergency and is made up of representatives of the various municipal structures and companies that provide essential services including water, electricity, and gas.[^1] The Civil Protection of Roma Capitale is responsible for all activities related to forecasting, prevention, management and overcoming emergencies, supported by the Civil Protection Office.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The centralized system specifically addresses multiple climate-related hazards including extreme climatic events, hydrological instability, and fire risks.[^2] Rome is classified as seismic zone 2 (medium seismicity) and 3 (low seismicity), requiring integrated risk management approaches.[^1] The government has allocated a budget of € 500 million towards developing an advanced and integrated monitoring and forecasting system for the identification and mitigation of hydrological and water-related risks such as droughts, floods, and landslides.[^4]

## Business Analysis

The government has allocated a budget of € 500 million towards developing an advanced and integrated monitoring and forecasting system for the identification and mitigation of hydrological and water-related risks.[^4] From May 2022 onwards, the Italian Ministry of Environment and Energy Security has been implementing the Advanced and Integrated Monitoring and Forecasting System for sustainable and adaptive water management and forecasting.[^4] The development of the platform is supported by the international private sector, including Leonardo and DXC technology providing advanced and innovative AI-driven architecture components within the SIM infrastructure.[^4]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.comune.roma.it/web-resources/cms/documents/BrochureTheCivilProtection.pdf
- [^2]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Europe & Middle East/Rome-Italy/Rome-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^3]: https://www.comune.roma.it/web/it/dipartimento-protezione-civile-uffici-e-contatti.page?contentId=UFF1141826
- [^4]: https://www.waterdiplomat.org/story/2025/10/italys-advanced-and-integrated-monitoring-and-forecasting-system

The first step to complete this action will be the implementation of an integrated risks and emergencies management system. # Research Report: Real-Time Environmental Data Platform for Climate Awareness

## Solution Overview

Ecothes establishes connections between environmental data—including temperatures and air quality measurements—and real-time climate events such as urban floods and heat waves, demonstrating the influence of climate change on daily urban life.[^3] This approach educates public awareness by relating existing environmental data and real-time events with climate change impacts, helping residents understand how they are affected on a daily basis.[^1] The initiative was unveiled through a workshop hosted by the Municipality of Thessaloniki in collaboration with the Aristotle University of Thessaloniki as part of the LIFE SIRIUS European project, titled "Environmental Information in the City of Thessaloniki – Air Pollution & Health" on World Environment Day.[^2]

## Technical Components

The implementation features the "Consolidated Environmental Management System", a specialized web application designed to manage environmental data.[^2] The platform provides real-time data on air quality, offering early warnings to residents, particularly the vulnerable, about the risks of high pollutant levels.[^2] The system simplifies the Air Pollution Index and includes sophisticated early warning mechanisms that consider both air pollution and weather conditions.[^2] Dr. Athina Proyiou from AXON Environmental Ltd demonstrated the platform at the workshop's conclusion.[^2]

## Implementation Details

The workshop took place in the Municipal Council's Conference Room, where municipal officials and academic partners presented the system to stakeholders.[^2] Mr. Angeloudis hailed the new system as a crucial tool for monitoring air quality and safeguarding public health.[^2] The initiative operates as part of the broader LIFE SIRIUS European project framework, representing a collaborative effort between municipal authorities and academic institutions.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

Research shows that 70% of air pollution in Thessaloniki city is caused by car exhaust emissions.[^1] However, in a questionnaire conducted by Resilient Thessaloniki, only 33% of people surveyed indicated that they believe cars are the main source of air pollution in the city.[^1] This awareness gap highlights the educational value of the system in connecting environmental data with public understanding of pollution sources and climate impacts.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The system addresses climate adaptation by linking environmental monitoring data with real-time extreme weather events including urban floods and heat waves.[^3] This approach enables residents to understand the direct impacts of climate change on city life through tangible connections between environmental conditions and daily experiences.[^1]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.climate-kic.org/news/thessaloniki-climathon-attempts-to-change-environmental-behaviors/
- [^2]: https://lifesirius.eu/news/environmental-management-platform-by-the-municipality-of-thessaloniki-a-recap
- [^3]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Europe & Middle East/Thessaloniki-Greece/Thessaloniki-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf

# Research Report: Energy Intelligence Network with Real-Time Demand Response Monitoring

## Solution Overview

This approach leverages enhanced data collection and monitoring infrastructure to enable businesses and institutions to strategically reduce electricity consumption during peak demand periods, thereby lowering costs while strengthening grid stability.[^5] Pittsburgh businesses participating in these programs report return on investment ranging from 5% to 20% of their annual electricity costs, depending on their flexibility and commitment level.[^1] The technology reduces energy costs and emissions while improving the resiliency of electric grid infrastructure.[^5]

## Technical Components

The Energy Intelligence Network expansion involved the installation of plug load monitors to enhance data quality and energy management capabilities.[^3][^5] Field testing of the Intelligent Dashboard for Occupants (ID-O) engaged 80 employees at a major Pittsburgh corporation in online communication, consultation and control supported by plug-in devices manufactured by Plugwise™ that measure the energy consumption of each device and provide digital on-off control.[^2] The system enables real-time monitoring of individual device consumption including laptops, computer monitors, lights and phones, with capabilities for both information visibility and remote digital on-off control.[^2]

## Implementation Details

Pittsburgh City Council authorized a contract with Boss Control LLC to expand the Energy Intelligence Network through the installation of plug load monitors in an amount not to exceed $150,000.00.[^3] The resolution was passed finally by Pittsburgh City Council on October 2, 2018, enacted as resolution number 660 on October 5, 2018, and signed by the Mayor with an effective date of October 8, 2018.[^3] No upfront cost was incurred as part of this contract, with Boss Control LLC instead receiving a portion of the utility cost savings recognized as a result of the Energy Intelligence Network expansion.[^3] Enerlogics has been acting as administrator for the Duquesne Light Demand Response Program since 2016, as part of Phase 3 of the Pennsylvania Act 129 Programs.[^4] Duquesne Light Company and FirstEnergy (Penn Power) serve as the primary utility providers in the Pittsburgh region, each offering distinct demand response opportunities.[^1] PJM Interconnection, the regional transmission organization that coordinates the movement of wholesale electricity in Pittsburgh and surrounding states, administers several demand response programs accessible through utilities or curtailment service providers.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

A CMU study showed that during a nine-month period, using the CMU Intelligent Dashboard ID-O offered significant energy savings for corporations, with savings most visible when laptops, computer monitors, lights and phones were turned off at night and during weekends.[^2] Group C averaged 20.2 percent energy savings with energy use visibility and online control ability but without calendar automation, while Group B with information but no online control averaged 9 percent energy savings.[^2] Enerlogics enabled Duquesne to achieve realization rates in excess of 100% of the targeted demand reduction in the first summer of the program operation (summer of 2017).[^4] Duquesne Light Company offers both emergency and economic demand response options, with varying notification periods and compensation structures, while FirstEnergy Solutions provides demand response programs for businesses including day-ahead notifications and emergency response options.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This system addresses climate-related stress on electrical infrastructure by reducing peak demand during extreme weather events when grid strain is highest. The improved resiliency of electric grid infrastructure helps maintain power reliability during heat waves and other climate-driven demand surges that threaten service continuity.[^5]

## Business Analysis

The performance-based financial model eliminates upfront capital costs for municipal participants, with the service provider receiving compensation only from realized utility cost savings.[^3] Pittsburgh businesses achieve returns ranging from 5% to 20% of annual electricity costs depending on their operational flexibility and program commitment level.[^1] Multiple program structures exist through regional utilities, with Duquesne Light Company offering both emergency and economic demand response options with varying notification periods and compensation structures, while FirstEnergy Solutions provides programs including day-ahead notifications and emergency response options.[^1] The Pennsylvania Act 129 Programs provide regulatory framework support for utility-administered demand response initiatives.[^4]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.myshyft.com/blog/utility-demand-response-programs-for-businesses-pittsburgh-pennsylvania/
- [^2]: https://www.cmu.edu/news/stories/archives/2014/april/april7_workplacedashboard.html
- [^3]: https://pittsburgh.legistar.com/gateway.aspx?m=l&id=/matter.aspx?key=23120
- [^4]: https://enerlogics.com/portfolio/duquesne-demand-response/
- [^5]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Pittsburgh-US/Pittsburgh_Climate_Action_Plan_3.0.pdf

# Research Report: Smart Sewage Network Infrastructure Connection System

## Solution Overview

The Municipality of Jbeil-Byblos is collaborating with 100RC Platform Partner ESRI and Veolia to examine how to plan and build infrastructure connections through a well-maintained sewage network to connect a wastewater treatment plant to houses and buildings.[^1] This approach addresses a critical infrastructure gap in the new city, where a wastewater treatment plant has already been built but lacks the necessary connections to residential and commercial structures.[^1] Planning and building the required infrastructure through a well-maintained sewage network is important for the city's resilience.[^1]

## Technical Components

The implementation utilizes KAPTA™ 3000-AC4 sensors for real-time wastewater network monitoring.[^3] These sensors enable continuous surveillance of the sewage infrastructure to support operational management and maintenance of the network connections being planned between the existing treatment plant and buildings throughout the new city development.[^1][^3]

## Implementation Details

The Lebanese Council for Development and Reconstruction (CDR) serves as the action owner for building wastewater infrastructure in Byblos' new city.[^2] Action partners include Byblos Municipality, Veolia, and the Union of Municipalities, forming a collaborative framework to address the infrastructure connection challenge.[^2] The project focuses on establishing the sewage network infrastructure required to link the already-constructed wastewater treatment plant to residential and commercial buildings in the new city area.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This infrastructure system addresses climate resilience by ensuring proper wastewater management capacity as the city develops, which is essential for managing water resources and preventing contamination during extreme weather events. The well-maintained sewage network infrastructure supports the city's ability to handle wastewater flows under changing climate conditions.[^1]

## Sources

# Research Report: SIATA Integrated Early Warning and Environmental Monitoring System

## Solution Overview

The Early Warning System of Medellín and the Aburrá Valley (SIATA) is an integrated environmental assessment and risk management tool that combines scientific knowledge, research, and technology to identify and predict natural and anthropogenic phenomena.[^2] This approach monitors environmental conditions, identifies threats and emergencies, and informs the decision-making process of response agencies in situations of extreme weather events.[^3] The initiative integrates a communication and education strategy to increase community awareness and participation.[^2]

## Technical Components

SIATA deploys sensors that feed real-time data on hydrometeorological variables and atmospheric conditions to a central system through which the information is analyzed and shared with the relevant parties.[^2] The Seismic Network of the Aburrá Valley comprises 23 stations distributed across the 10 municipalities of the Aburrá Valley, with each station equipped with an accelerograph that records ground motion in three components (two horizontal and one vertical).[^4] The network utilizes triaxial accelerographs from Kinemetrics-Episensor, which have the capacity to register both strong and weak earthquakes.[^4] Information recorded by each station is sent in real-time to the SIATA processing center, where it is stored and processed.[^4] When an earthquake is detected, the system automatically generates a series of products, including instrumental intensity maps (ShakeMaps), which are highly useful for disaster risk management.[^4] The operation and maintenance of the Acceleratorgyro Network of Medellín (RAM) and the Hydrometeorological Network involves regular collection and processing of information stored in each accelerogram to obtain useful tools for the analysis and design of earthquake-resistant structures.[^1] The data is used to create numerical and statistical modeling to understand and identify future risk.[^2]

## Implementation Details

The project stems from an undergraduate thesis from three geologists at EAFIT University in Medellín seeking to measure precipitation patterns in the region.[^2] It was not until 2010 that Aburrá Valley authorities took on the administration of the project.[^2] From 2011 onwards, SIATA created a network of meteorological stations and installed new remote sensors, including a meteorological radar, the first of its kind installed in Colombia.[^2] In 2014, SIATA launched a mobile application to facilitate users' interaction with the data.[^2] Recent projects include a seismology group and an air quality monitoring network in 2016, fire monitoring through thermal cameras in 2017, and the development of new sensors in 2024.[^2] The Área Metropolitana del Valle de Aburrá, in partnership with SIATA, EPM, and the Universidad Nacional de Colombia - Sede Medellín, installed and put into operation a network of 23 seismological stations in the Aburrá Valley with the objective of monitoring seismic threats in the region and evaluating the possible local effects of earthquakes on the territory.[^4] SIATA is a project of the Metropolitan Area of the Aburra Valley (AMVA) and the Mayor's Office of Medellín and has the support and contributions of Medellín's Public Enterprises (EPM) and ISAGEN.[^3] The Strengthening of the Early-Warning Environmental System (SIATA) initiative is part of the 'A City Investment in Care of the Environment' dimension, the 'Medellín Manages Risk' challenge, and the 'Risk Awareness' program under the project 'Strengthening of the SIATA and Innovation for Risk Management'.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The system provides access to information in real time to determine the likelihood of events such as heavy rains, floods, landslides, avalanches, air quality analysis, electrical discharge, and areas where fires may occur.[^3] SIATA designed a mobile application and website where users can find the weather forecast, receive alerts, view precipitation patterns, and access real-time images from the camera network.[^2] The alert system has been instrumental in the construction of large infrastructure projects, as managers can receive timely information about weather phenomena that could significantly impact construction plans.[^2] SIATA conducts education and communication programs and diffuses data through user-friendly platforms.[^2] The SIATA information is publicized through the internet and printed materials for distribution with bulletin boards for a technical audience, and includes maintenance, update and/or replacement of prioritization and quantification equipment.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Given its geographic location, the Aburrá Valley is highly prone to heavy rains, electrical storms, landslides, forest fires, and floods.[^2] From 2016 to 2019, there were 5,577 emergencies caused by natural disasters in the Aburrá Valley, affecting 5,418 homes.[^2] Based on a 2012 study, more than 200,000 inhabitants in the Aburrá Valley are subject to medium or high landslide risk and live in precarious settlements.[^5]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Latin America & Caribbean/Medellín-Colombia/Medellin-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^2]: https://city2city.network/siata-disaster-management-tool-innovation-type-institutional-pioneer
- [^3]: https://use.metropolis.org/case-studies/siata-early-warning-system-of-the-aburra-valley
- [^4]: https://siata.gov.co/sitio_web/application/assets/img/contenido/Noticias/agu2017_red_sismica.pdf
- [^5]: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8068166/

# Research Report: Off-Grid Solar EV Charging Stations with Emergency Power Backup

## Solution Overview

Pittsburgh deployed five portable, solar-powered electric vehicle charging stations manufactured by Envision Solar that operate independently from the electrical grid.[^2][^3][^4] These EV ARC™ units were acquired for $420,000, with a state grant funding half of the purchase, to support the city's transition to a 100 percent fossil-fuel-free municipal fleet by 2030.[^8][^1][^3] The implementation serves dual purposes: providing emissions-free charging infrastructure for the city's growing electric vehicle fleet while simultaneously creating emergency power resources that enhance community resilience during grid failures and disasters.[^4][^2][^6]

## Technical Components

The EV ARC™ charging stations are equipped with 4.3 kW solar arrays and battery packs ranging from 24 to 40 kWh depending on the version.[^11] Each unit generates enough clean solar electricity to power up to 225 miles of electric vehicle driving per day, with energy stored in onboard battery systems for charging day or night and to provide emergency power during grid failure.[^2][^4][^11] The system's solar electrical generation is enhanced by EnvisionTrak™, a patented solar tracking system that causes the array to follow the sun throughout the day, generating up to 25 percent more electricity than a fixed array.[^2][^4][^11] The units fit inside a parking space without reducing available parking and provide Level I, Level II, and DC Fast Charging capabilities.[^1][^11] Each station is equipped with emergency power (E-power) panels that allow first responders and other vital personnel to use the units as emergency generators during disasters or grid outages.[^1][^3] The upgraded EV ARC 2020 design features a re-engineered S-curved column with electronic components and energy storage elevated to the underside of the sun-tracking solar canopy, making the station flood-proof to 9.5 feet.[^11]

## Implementation Details

The City of Pittsburgh deployed the five EV ARC™ units at the Second Avenue Parking Plaza to provide solar-powered charging for the city's fleet of electric vehicles.[^3] The deployment required no trenching, foundations, or installation work of any kind, with setup taking only a few minutes, and the systems can be moved to a different location if the need arises.[^2][^4][^11] Pittsburgh had 10 electric vehicles in its fleet at the time of implementation and intended to increase that number going forward as part of its commitment to transition to 100 percent renewable energy including wind and solar by 2035.[^4] The city also installed a DC Fast Charger at a parking area in the East Liberty location.[^5] Officials planned to place the charging units in sites throughout the city and make them available for public use, though specific locations had not yet been decided.[^9] The first charging stations were designed to charge the Department of Permits, Licenses and Inspections fleet at night and then be open to the public during the day.[^7] The California-based manufacturer Envision Solar had already deployed EV ARC units in more than 100 U.S. municipalities prior to the Pittsburgh implementation.[^11]

## Benefits and Impacts

Fleet vehicles charging from the EV ARC™ units are 100 percent emissions-free, while 70 percent of the power used by grid-connected EV chargers is carbon fuel based.[^1] The stations can be deployed throughout the city in emergency situations, blackouts, or interruptions in electricity supply, which improves the overall resiliency of Pittsburgh and its residents.[^7] The units are immune to grid interruptions, allowing electric vehicles owned by the city to continue operations to serve the needs of city residents during power failures.[^3][^2] Police and first responders can plug in to use the charging stations as emergency generators during outages, ensuring continuity of critical services.[^2] The implementation supports Pittsburgh's third generation climate action plan to reduce the city's greenhouse gas emissions through policies and projects within city limits.[^2][^1] The self-contained and portable nature of the units eliminates the need for trenching or grid connections, avoiding construction disruption and utility bills while providing permanent charging infrastructure.[^2][^11]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The EV ARC™ units enhance Pittsburgh's climate resilience by providing emergency power resources that remain operational during grid failures caused by extreme weather events or other climate-related disruptions.[^4][^6] The flood-proof design to 9.5 feet addresses vulnerabilities to extreme precipitation and flooding events that can compromise traditional grid-connected infrastructure.[^11] This approach supports neighborhood resilience goals by ensuring that critical transportation and emergency response capabilities remain functional during climate-related disasters and power interruptions.[^6]

## Business Analysis

The $420,000 acquisition of five EV ARC™ units was financed through a partnership model with a state grant covering half of the purchase cost, demonstrating how state-level policy support can enable municipal climate infrastructure investments.[^8] The deployment model eliminates ongoing utility bills and avoids permitting, trenching, and construction costs associated with traditional grid-connected charging infrastructure.[^11] The system's portability and rapid deployment capability provides operational flexibility, allowing the city to relocate units as fleet needs and emergency response requirements evolve without additional capital expenditure.[^2][^4] Envision Solar's established presence in more than 100 U.S. municipalities indicates a mature market for off-grid solar charging solutions, with proven technical reliability and municipal procurement pathways.[^11] The integration of emergency power capabilities adds value beyond transportation electrification, creating a dual-use asset that supports both routine fleet operations and disaster response, potentially improving the financial justification for municipal investment in this technology.[^2][^6]

## Sources

- [^4]: https://beamforall.com/city-of-pittsburgh-selects-envision-solar-ev-arc-solar-charging-stations-for-its-fleet-evs/
- [^5]: https://www.pgh-cleancities.org/post/deusa-fleet-success-stories-city-of-pittsburgh

# Research Report: Liquid Fuel Asset Database for Metropolitan Energy Infrastructure

## Solution Overview

The New York City Economic Development Corporation (NYCEDC) developed a database of liquid fuel assets in the New York metropolitan region.[^1][^2][^3] This database system was designed to be piloted with relevant governmental agencies and begin input of data with industry in 2015.[^1][^2][^3] The initiative represents a strategic approach to mapping critical energy infrastructure across the metropolitan area to enhance fuel supply chain resilience.

## Technical Components

The database system was developed to catalog liquid fuel assets throughout the New York metropolitan region.[^1][^2][^3] NYCEDC designed the platform to enable data input from industry partners and facilitate information sharing with governmental agencies.[^1][^2][^3] The technical infrastructure was structured to accommodate collaborative data entry and access by multiple stakeholders across the fuel supply ecosystem.

## Implementation Details

NYCEDC planned to pilot this database with relevant governmental agencies and begin input of data with industry in 2015.[^1][^2][^3] The implementation strategy involved a phased approach, starting with a pilot program to test the system's functionality with government partners before expanding data collection to include industry participants.[^1][^2][^3] The initiative required coordination across multiple governmental agencies and private sector fuel suppliers to populate the database with comprehensive asset information.

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This database addresses climate resilience by providing visibility into liquid fuel infrastructure that is critical during extreme weather events and climate-related disruptions. The system enables better coordination among governmental agencies and industry partners to maintain fuel supply continuity during emergencies. By mapping fuel assets across the metropolitan region, the initiative supports preparedness for climate hazards that could impact energy distribution networks.

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.nyc.gov/assets/sirr/downloads/pdf/Ch_7_Liquid_Fuels_FINAL_singles.pdf
- [^2]: https://www.nyserda.ny.gov/-/media/Project/Nyserda/Files/EDPPP/Energy-Prices/Energy-Statistics/NYS-terminal-resiliency-assessment-final-report.pdf
- [^3]: https://comptroller.nyc.gov/reports/ten-years-after-sandy/
- [^4]: https://www.energypolicy.columbia.edu/publications/improving-regional-situational-awareness-during-fuel-emergencies-new-york-tri-state-areas-lessons/

# Research Report: Wellington Fault Rupture Infrastructure Outage Visualization Tool

## Solution Overview

A web-based visualization tool was developed to enable end users to understand infrastructure outages that would occur across the Wellington region following a rupture of the Wellington fault.[^1] This approach integrates geographic information system analysis with economic modeling to translate seismic hazard scenarios into accessible visual formats for decision-makers and stakeholders. The system addresses the critical need for communicating complex infrastructure vulnerability information in an earthquake-prone urban environment.

## Technical Components

The platform employs GIS analysis to generate outage information and spatial visualizations.[^2][^3][^4] Technical inputs to the MERIT model include temporal data measured in days, spatial data organized by meshblock geographic units, and service level data indicating binary on or off states for infrastructure systems.[^2][^3][^4] This data architecture enables the system to process fault rupture scenarios and translate them into granular outage predictions across Wellington's infrastructure networks.

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This tool directly addresses seismic hazards facing Wellington by enabling visualization of infrastructure system failures following a major earthquake event. The approach supports climate adaptation planning by helping stakeholders understand cascading infrastructure vulnerabilities and restoration timelines in the context of Wellington's high seismic risk environment.

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590061724000024
- [^2]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Asia & Asia Pacific Oceania/Wellington-New-Zealand/Wellington-Lifelines-PBC-MAIN-Combined-20191009.pdf
- [^3]: https://www.gns.cri.nz/research-projects/merit/
- [^4]: https://resiliencechallenge.nz/wp-content/uploads/MERIT-Modelling-Disruption-webinar-slides-March-2022.pdf

# Research Report: Smart Irrigation Controller Rebate and Xeriscape Conversion Program

## Solution Overview

Phoenix Water Services has established a comprehensive residential water conservation initiative that combines financial incentives for smart irrigation technology adoption with support for xeriscape landscape conversion.[^8] This approach addresses the critical challenge of outdoor water consumption, which accounts for over 60% of residential water use in the city.[^4] The program was launched to encourage water conservation while helping customers save money and make the most of available resources.[^2]

## Technical Components

The initiative centers on WaterSense-labeled smart irrigation controllers, which are independently certified to meet specific EPA criteria for efficiency and performance.[^9] These controllers optimize water use in landscape irrigation systems by replacing standard clock-based controllers that lack smart technology capabilities.[^3][^5] To qualify for program participation, existing irrigation controllers must be standard models without smart technology capabilities.[^3] The WaterSense certification ensures that controllers meet rigorous technical specifications designed to reduce water waste caused by inefficiencies in irrigation methods and systems.[^9] Replacing a standard clock-based controller with a WaterSense labeled irrigation controller can save an average home up to 15,000 gallons of water annually.[^9]

## Implementation Details

The City of Phoenix Water Services Department offers financial incentives ranging from $75 to $100 per smart irrigation controller for residential customers.[^1][^2][^3][^4][^5] The program provides up to $100 per controller for customers who purchase WaterSense labeled controllers after July 1, 2023, with one controller allowed per property.[^1] Multiple sources confirm that Phoenix Water offers up to $75 for the purchase and installation of EPA WaterSense-labeled smart irrigation controllers.[^3][^4][^5] The program is open to single-family households or renters who are City of Phoenix water utility customers, with renters required to include landlord approval.[^4] Products must be purchased within 60 days of the application date.[^3] Applications are accepted on a first-come, first-serve basis while funding lasts, with a limit of one smart irrigation controller per premise within 10 years.[^3] Residents can apply through the Water Conservation Incentives webpage at https://phxatyourservice.dynamics365portals.us/#iwantto.[^2][^4] Qualified applicants receive the incentive as a credit on their water bill or as a check mailed to the account on file for their City Services Bill, with processing times up to 90 days.[^1][^3][^4]

The initiative is complemented by a Grass Removal Program that provides $2 per square foot for homeowners who pay a city services bill and own their home.[^6] A minimum of 250 square feet of grass must be removed to qualify, with grass required to be alive and healthy at a minimum of 75% density of the area to be removed.[^6] Areas of bare soil or weeds must be less than 25% of the grass removal area.[^6] Pre-approval of applications is required, with applicants required to wait until approval and issuance of a Notice to Proceed before removing any grass, allowing up to 3 weeks for review.[^6] Applicants have 6 months from the date of the Notice to Proceed to complete landscape conversion work.[^6] Projects over 5,000 square feet are approved on a case-by-case basis, funds permitting.[^6] Water features and pools are prohibited from being installed in the area of grass removal, and bare soil after grass removal is also prohibited.[^6] Grass removed through the program cannot be reinstalled while ownership of the property is maintained.[^6] The incentive payment is processed after final approval in the form of a check in the mail, with applicants allowed 1-2 billing cycles to receive the check.[^6]

During the spring 2023 semester, the City of Phoenix Water Services Department partnered with an honors undergraduate landscape architecture student to develop residential xeriscape design plans for three standard lot sizes.[^7] The partnership continued from fall 2023 through spring 2024 to gather feedback from residents, experts, and Home Owner's Associations on irrigation behaviors and xeriscape practices to inform a series of irrigation design plans to supplement the previously created xeriscape plans.[^7]

## Benefits and Impacts

The smart irrigation controller technology delivers substantial water conservation outcomes by addressing outdoor water waste. Residential outdoor water use in the United States accounts for nearly 8 billion gallons of water each day, mainly for landscape irrigation, with experts estimating that as much as 50 percent of this water is wasted due to overwatering caused by inefficiencies in irrigation methods and systems.[^9] The WaterSense labeled controllers installed through this program can save an average home up to 15,000 gallons of water annually.[^9] Phoenix Water Planning Division deputy director Max Wilson noted that over 60% of residential water use occurs outdoors, and the introduction of smart irrigation controllers can significantly reduce this number by optimizing water use.[^4] Phoenix residents have voluntarily reduced per-person water use by more than 30% since 1990, demonstrating the community's commitment to conservation efforts.[^7]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This water conservation initiative addresses Phoenix's vulnerability to drought conditions and water scarcity intensified by climate change. By reducing outdoor water consumption through smart irrigation technology and xeriscape landscaping, the program builds resilience against prolonged dry periods and increasing temperatures that drive higher evapotranspiration rates. The approach directly mitigates climate-related water stress by optimizing irrigation efficiency and promoting drought-tolerant landscaping practices.

## Business Analysis

The program operates through a municipal utility incentive model, with the City of Phoenix Water Services Department providing direct financial subsidies to residential customers. The incentive structure ranges from $75 to $100 per controller, delivered either as water bill credits or mailed checks.[^1][^2][^3][^4][^5] This subsidy model reduces the upfront cost barrier for homeowners adopting water-efficient technology. The grass removal component offers $2 per square foot incentives, creating additional financial motivation for landscape conversion.[^6] Some utilities offer rebates to help customers water smarter outdoors, indicating broader market support for irrigation efficiency programs.[^9] The first-come, first-serve application process with funding limitations suggests budget-constrained program administration.[^3] The 10-year limit on controller replacements per premise indicates long-term program planning and equipment lifecycle considerations.[^3] The partnership with university landscape architecture students for design plan development represents a cost-effective approach to technical assistance delivery.[^7]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://northcentralnews.net/2024/community/city-offers-water-incentives/
- [^3]: https://www.phoenix.gov/administration/departments/waterservices/supply-conservation/save-water/conservation-incentives/toilets-smart-controllers.html
- [^4]: https://www.azfamily.com/2023/10/03/financial-incentives-offered-phoenix-residents-who-want-conserve-water/

# Research Report: C4I4 Integrated Emergency Response and Resilience Center

## Solution Overview

The C4I4 (Centro de Control, Comando, Comunicación y Cómputo, Inteligencia, Investigación, Información e Integración) serves as an emergency response and resilience facility for disasters and crises in Santa Fe, Argentina.[^3] This facility is part of Santa Fe, Argentina's resilience strategy developed through the 100 Resilient Cities program pioneered by the Rockefeller Foundation.[^3] The provincial government of Santa Fe invested more than 35 million dollars in the SICAD 911 System of Control, Attention and Dispatch.[^1]

## Technical Components

The C4I4 is a 20 thousand-square-meter four-story building, with an emergency light system capable of providing 48 hours of emergency lighting; an independent water-pumping system, and twice the average optical fiber capacity.[^3] The Santa Fe Ministry of Security awarded Motorola Solutions to deliver a state-of-the-art, integrated end-to-end emergency management system for multi-agency coordination and to shorten response times to emergency calls from the public.[^2] The new emergency management system includes Motorola Solutions' DIMETRA TETRA Consoles, which provide always-on digital radio voice communication and constant connection between police officers in the field and the command and control center.[^2] The new integrated system, called Control, Attention and Dispatch System for Police Emergencies (SiCAD), provides 17,500 police officers of the Province of Santa Fe to be fully coordinated and connected across 10 cities including Rafaela, Reconquista, Rosario, City of Santa Fe and Venado Tuerto.[^2]

## Implementation Details

Governor Perotti stated the 35 million dollar investment in the system makes it the most modern in Argentina today.[^1] The provincial government of Santa Fe invested more than 35 million dollars in the SICAD 911 System of Control, Attention and Dispatch.[^1] The Santa Fe Ministry of Security awarded Motorola Solutions to deliver a state-of-the-art, integrated end-to-end emergency management system for multi-agency coordination and to shorten response times to emergency calls from the public.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

This approach enables multi-agency coordination and shortened response times to emergency calls from the public.[^2] The integrated system provides 17,500 police officers of the Province of Santa Fe to be fully coordinated and connected across 10 cities including Rafaela, Reconquista, Rosario, City of Santa Fe and Venado Tuerto.[^2] The DIMETRA TETRA Consoles provide always-on digital radio voice communication and constant connection between police officers in the field and the command and control center.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The facility serves as an emergency response and resilience facility for disasters and crises in Santa Fe, Argentina.[^3] This implementation is part of Santa Fe, Argentina's resilience strategy developed through the 100 Resilient Cities program pioneered by the Rockefeller Foundation.[^3] The building's emergency light system capable of providing 48 hours of emergency lighting and independent water-pumping system enhance operational continuity during climate-related disasters.[^3]

## Sources

- [^3]: https://resilientcitiesnetwork.org/downloadable_resources/Network/Santa-Fe-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf

# Research Report: Climate Resilient Home Platform for Residential Adaptation

## Solution Overview

The Climate Resilient Home platform serves as an interactive educational and outreach tool designed to provide homeowners, builders, municipal staff, and elected officials with a comprehensive roadmap for making investment and policy decisions that improve residential climate resilience.[^1][^2] This approach delivers tangible, actionable guidance on preparing homes for the impacts of wildfire, extreme weather, flooding, and environmental changes through both new construction and retrofitting strategies.[^1] The system was developed through a 2019 partnership between eight Alberta municipalities—Edmonton, St. Albert, Wetaskiwin, Spruce Grove, Leduc, Devon, Stony Plain, and Strathcona County—and the All One Sky Foundation as part of the Climate Resilience Exchange project.[^1]

## Technical Components

The climateresilienthome.ca website provides detailed technical specifications covering multiple building components, including rooves, windows and doors, exterior walls, foundations, and landscape design elements.[^1] The platform incorporates cost estimation functionality that calculates both the effort and expense associated with installing various resilient features, enabling green developers and homeowners to make informed financial decisions.[^3][^4] This digital tool translates complex climate adaptation strategies into clear, actionable instructions for addressing multiple climate change impacts including flooding, wildfire, extreme weather events, and broader environmental shifts.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The Climate Resilience Exchange project was funded through the Federation of Canadian Municipalities' Municipalities for Climate Innovation Program's Climate Adaptation Partner Grants.[^1] The All One Sky Foundation led the initiative, which produced multiple resources and tools addressing climate change impacts on invasive species management, water security, trees and forests, and home resilience across the Greater Edmonton Region.[^1] The collaborative effort brought together eight municipal governments to develop standardized approaches for improving residential climate adaptation throughout the region.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

A climate resilient home designed using this approach demonstrates enhanced capacity to cope with climate change events including storms, floods, heatwaves, and extreme cold conditions.[^2] These improvements deliver multiple benefits: reduced property damages, lower operating costs, and enhanced comfort and safety for occupants.[^2] The platform's cost estimation features enable users to evaluate the financial implications of resilience investments before implementation, supporting more strategic resource allocation by both individual homeowners and municipal policymakers.[^3][^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This solution directly addresses multiple climate hazards by providing specific guidance on protecting residential structures from wildfire, extreme weather events, flooding, and changing environmental conditions.[^1] The comprehensive approach recognizes that climate resilient homes must withstand diverse threats including storms, floods, heatwaves, and extreme cold, requiring integrated design strategies across multiple building systems.[^2]

## Business Analysis

The project secured funding through the Federation of Canadian Municipalities' Municipalities for Climate Innovation Program's Climate Adaptation Partner Grants, demonstrating the availability of federal support mechanisms for municipal climate adaptation initiatives.[^1] The platform's cost estimation functionality addresses a critical market need by enabling green developers and homeowners to evaluate the financial feasibility of resilience investments, potentially accelerating adoption rates.[^3][^4] The multi-municipal partnership model distributes development costs across eight participating jurisdictions while creating standardized guidance applicable throughout the Greater Edmonton Region, improving cost-effectiveness compared to individual municipal approaches.[^1]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://changingclimate.ca/map/climate-resilient-home-adaptation-tool-2/
- [^2]: https://www.climateresilienthome.ca/
- [^3]: https://www.allonesky.ca/climate-adaptation-and-resilience-planning
- [^4]: https://www.allonesky.ca/regional-climate-adaptation-collaborative

# Research Report: LiDAR Elevation Data System for Sea-Level Rise Vulnerability Mapping

## Solution Overview

Broward County coordinates with agency partners to ensure the coordinated acquisition of, access to, and informed use of LiDAR and other high-resolution elevation data for resiliency and adaptation planning, studies, stormwater modeling, asset management, and other purposes.[^1] The County updated its LiDAR database with the 2018 USGS data set and continues to partner with municipal, state, and federal agencies to collect and maintain LiDAR data refinements for countywide planning.[^1] This elevation data system enables improved analysis and mapping capabilities for identifying areas vulnerable to sea-level rise by utilizing the most recent LiDAR data.[^1]

## Technical Components

The project specifications were designed to meet or exceed USGS Lidar Base Specifications for Quality Level 1 Lidar at 8 pulses per square meter, with all flights planned to reach an average aggregate nominal pulse density of 10 pulses per square meter and an average aggregate nominal pulse spacing not to exceed 0.35 meters.[^3] Dewberry deployed Leica Geosystems' linear-mode sensors, TerrainMappers, and ALS80s, along with Riegl VQ1560 and VQ1560i sensors for the Florida peninsula lidar project.[^3] The lidar point cloud data includes classified ground data, building rooftops, overpasses and bridges, a hydrologically flattened digital terrain model, 3D breaklines, and associated metadata.[^3] Within Fort Lauderdale specifically, a vehicle traveled throughout City streets, as well as along County and State roads within City limits, to capture data with a focus on street curbs, drainage swales, alleyways, and sidewalks using street level imagery and LiDAR elevation survey technology.[^4] Broward County uses LiDAR elevation data in the development of land-use policies and modeling.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The Florida Division of Emergency Management, Florida Department of Transportation, Florida Department of Environmental Protection, and five Florida Water Management Districts worked in cooperation with the U.S. Geological Survey to maintain and update high-resolution elevation data through a $22 million lidar project covering the Florida peninsula.[^3] The Florida Legislature appropriated $15 million in funds to FDEM, which entered into a joint funding agreement with USGS to match funds under the USGS Supplemental for Hurricane Irma totaling $7.6 million to acquire quality level 1 specification lidar data for roughly 35,000 square miles of the Florida peninsula.[^3] The estimated cost for quality level 2 lidar completion in Florida is $0.88 million with a payback period of 0.08 year.[^2] Broward County completed a Seawall Modeling Study in August 2018, prepared by Cummins Cederberg Coastal & Marine Engineering.[^5] The information gained from the Fort Lauderdale LiDAR assessment is used to guide operations and maintenance activities and help the City plan for future capital improvement projects.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

The National Enhanced Elevation Assessment conservatively estimated that availability of lidar data would result in at least $10.88 million in new benefits annually to the State of Florida.[^2] The 2017 Florida Statewide Lidar Assessment indicated that the state would realize a net benefit of more than $20 million per year for Quality Level 1 topographic lidar acquired every two to three years.[^3] The Florida Statewide Lidar Assessment, conducted in 2017, exceeded the NEEA-identified benefits by almost $10 million, reporting at least $20 million in new benefits annually to the State with a need for topographic lidar coverage at quality level 1.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This elevation data system improves analysis and mapping capabilities for identifying areas of the County vulnerable to sea-level rise by utilizing the most recent LiDAR data.[^1] The approach supports updating maps of potential impacts of sea-level rise to the natural and built environments at two and 3.3-foot levels, with this information considered in long-term planning.[^1] The high-resolution elevation data enables stormwater modeling and asset management critical for addressing coastal flooding and precipitation-related hazards.[^1]

## Business Analysis

The estimated cost for quality level 2 lidar completion in Florida demonstrates a rapid payback period of 0.08 year against expected annual benefits of $10.88 million.[^2] The 2017 Florida Statewide Lidar Assessment indicated that the state would realize a net benefit of more than $20 million per year for Quality Level 1 topographic lidar acquired every two to three years, establishing a strong financial case for recurring data acquisition.[^3] The Florida Legislature's appropriation of $15 million in funds to FDEM, combined with USGS matching funds totaling $7.6 million under the USGS Supplemental for Hurricane Irma, demonstrates a public-sector joint funding model for large-scale elevation data acquisition.[^3] This multi-agency partnership approach, involving state emergency management, transportation, environmental protection agencies, and water management districts working cooperatively with federal geological survey resources, represents a collaborative financing mechanism for maintaining critical climate adaptation infrastructure data.[^3]

[^1]: Broward County Climate Change Action Plan
[^2]: Florida Statewide Lidar Assessment
[^3]: Florida Peninsula Lidar Project
[^4]: Fort Lauderdale LiDAR Assessment
[^5]: Broward County Seawall Modeling Study

## Sources

- [^1]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Broward-US/CCAP_2020_ADA.pdf
- [^2]: https://pubs.usgs.gov/publication/fs20233037/full
- [^3]: https://www.xyht.com/lidarimaging/lidar-data-for-floodplain-models/
- [^4]: https://www.fortlauderdale.gov/Home/Components/News/News/6502/16
- [^5]: https://www.broward.org/Climate/Documents/BC_SeawallModelingStudy_Aug2018.pdf

# Research Report: KATWARN Mobile Emergency Alert and Warning System

## Solution Overview

The Berlin Fire Department operates KATWARN, a technology-enabled disaster information and warning system that delivers alerts through smartphones, SMS, and email.[^5][^4] This free-of-charge mobile warning platform was developed by the Fraunhofer Institute for Open Communication Systems (FOKUS) in Berlin to inform people through their mobile devices about emergencies and provide guidance on protective actions.[^2] The system functions as a general warning mechanism designed to inform and advise people in case of a broad range of dangers, including extreme weather situations, large fires, chemical spills, and shootings.[^2]

## Technical Components

The platform disseminates local, state, and federal hazard alerts via smartphone app and SMS service, with information provided by multiple authorized senders including fire department control centers, the police, the German Weather Service (DWD), flood and earthquake control centers, as well as linked external warning systems.[^6][^2] Citizens receive their information through SMS, email, or push messages from an app.[^2] The system is linked to other platforms including the German Weather Service (Deutscher Wetterdienst DWD) and the modular warning system of the Federal Government (MoWaS/NINA-App).[^6] Registration is done through a postal code, and users must select specific regions, counties, and cities to receive warnings for those particular locations.[^2] The warnings are precisely targeted to the postal code zones at risk.[^1] The KATWARN roaming technology enables international use, with messages displayed across borders on national apps.[^6]

## Implementation Details

KATWARN has been in use in Germany since 2010, initially as an SMS and email service and since 2012 additionally as a smartphone app.[^3] As a uniform system throughout Germany, it is operated on local, state, and federal levels.[^3] The population warning system was developed by Fraunhofer FOKUS on behalf of the German public insurance companies and CombiRisk GmbH as a contribution to the common good.[^3] In September 2015, the disaster warning system expanded to Wall AG's digital advertising surfaces in Berlin, where warnings approved by the fire department control center and the German Weather Service are immediately played unchanged from Wall AG's technical control center on the advertising surfaces.[^4] However, more people need to be interested in the use of these disaster information and warning systems offered by the Berlin Fire Department.[^5]

## Benefits and Impacts

The system sends alerts about fires, storms, or other sudden emergencies via SMS, email, or smartphone app and provides tips on what to do.[^1] The advantage of this approach is that users can be precisely informed about dangers in a particular location, given that at the time of registering for the app one has to select specific regions, counties, and cities.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This warning system addresses climate-related hazards by providing alerts for extreme weather situations including storms and other sudden emergencies that may increase in frequency and intensity due to climate change.[^2][^1] The platform's integration with the German Weather Service enables timely dissemination of meteorological warnings that help residents prepare for and respond to climate-driven weather events.[^6][^2]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.berlin.de/katastrophenschutz/warnung-und-information/englisch/public-safety-warnings-and-infos/artikel.1470288.en.php
- [^2]: https://www.dw.com/en/saving-lives-with-warning-apps/a-19430125
- [^3]: https://www.katwarn.de/en/partner.php
- [^4]: https://www.fokus.fraunhofer.de/en/espri/projects/en_katwarn.html
- [^5]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Europe & Middle East/Berlin-Germany/adapting_to_the_impacts_of_climate_change_in_berlin.pdf
- [^6]: https://www.katwarn.de/en/areas.php
