# Research Report: UAV LiDAR and Photogrammetry for Flood Modeling Digital Terrain Models

## Solution Overview

A World Bank-funded comparative study evaluated UAV photogrammetry and UAV LiDAR technologies for generating Digital Terrain Models to support flood modeling in the Msimbazi River basin.[^1][^2][^3] This rigorous comparison aimed to provide guidance on when to apply each acquisition method based on project requirements, area characteristics, and operational constraints.[^3][^4] The initiative served dual purposes: producing reliable bathymetric and topographic data for riverine flooding studies while systematically comparing drone-based surveying technologies in a Sub-Saharan African context where such meticulous analysis had been lacking.[^3][^4]

## Technical Components

The UAV LiDAR system consisted of a Velodyne HDL 32e laser scanner weighing 3.5 kg mounted on a DJI M600pro multi-rotor drone, with total take-off weight just under 15 kg.[^2] The multi-rotor configuration enabled flights of 13-15 minutes duration before requiring battery changes and controlled landings, contrasting with photogrammetry fixed-wing platforms capable of hours-long flights covering hundreds of kilometers.[^2] The survey campaign executed multiple technical tasks including setting out Ground Control Points and Benchmarks, RTK-GNSS River bathymetry profile measurements, UAV LiDAR Survey, UAV Photogrammetry Survey, and water level and salinity measurements.[^4] DTMs were generated at multiple grid resolutions including 0.25 x 0.25 m, 0.50 x 0.50 m, 1.0 x 1.0 m, and 2.0 x 2.0 m, with no interpolation performed between points and grid cells.[^2] The comparison methodology employed advanced GIS spatial analysis, with vegetation types mapped and metrics assessed for respective vegetation classes.[^4] Performance evaluation covered fourteen different types of vegetated areas and three types of urbanized areas within the basin.[^2]

## Implementation Details

CDR International in association with Shore Monitoring and Research defined and executed the integral survey campaign for the lower basin of the Msimbazi River from February 14th until February 20th, 2019, with only water level logger retrieval occurring after this period.[^2][^3] The project targeted the Lower Basin of the Msimbazi River, a highly vulnerable area to flooding requiring accurate hydraulic flood modelling for identification and design of appropriate mitigation measures.[^2] The Tanzania Urban Resilience Program deployed this innovative combination of LiDAR UAV surveying and bathymetric surveying to support flood modeling within the flood-prone basin.[^5] More than 300 students and locals were trained to use drones, GPS, and mobile apps to collect data, transforming everyday citizens into urban planners.[^7] UAV LiDAR survey costs were approximately 2 to 3 times more than UAV photogrammetry survey at the time of the report.[^2] Preliminary insights were shared at a master class at the Land and Poverty Conference 2019 in Washington, DC (March 25-29, 2019) and at a workshop on DTM drone applications for Dar es Salaam stakeholders at the World Bank Office on April 11th, 2019.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The survey of river cross-sections and hydraulic structures along the Msimbazi River supported creation of a comprehensive hydraulic model that led to development of a more realistic flood hazard map of the most vulnerable areas.[^6] Flood modeling comparison using a 10-year return period rainfall event demonstrated that using the LiDAR DTM versus the 2016 DTM resulted in design level differences of 30-40 cm in the Jangwani area, which would have caused protective measures to be designed too low—a large impact.[^2] In dense tree areas, the LiDAR ground point cloud contained approximately 10,000 points with uniform spatial distribution, while the photogrammetry ground point cloud had approximately 50,000 points but with poor spatial distribution concentrated in patches.[^2] For mangrove and tree areas covering approximately 33% of the vegetated basin area, photogrammetry showed mean elevation errors of approximately 3 m, with maximum errors in DTMs between 5 to 10 m.[^2] For tall reed and grass areas covering more than 30% of the vegetated basin area, photogrammetry showed elevation errors in the order of 1 m compared to LiDAR.[^2] The analysis concluded that although LiDAR UAV survey was more expensive, it was considered a more suitable method for DTM generation for flood modeling purposes in Msimbazi-type basins with relatively large coverage of vegetation with variation in type, density, and heights.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The Msimbazi Basin, home to an estimated 27 percent of Dar es Salaam's population, has experienced increasingly severe flooding over the past decade, with major flood events in seven out of the last 10 years.[^6][^9] Each year, Msimbazi flooding leads to fatalities and destroys critical infrastructure.[^6] Outputs from this surveying initiative will be used to guide river upgrades that will improve the city's resilience to flooding.[^5]

## Business Analysis

UAV-mounted LiDAR scanners potentially provide significant cost savings compared to both traditional plane-based LiDAR and field surveying methods as equipment becomes smaller and lighter while UAVs can fly longer and carry more weight.[^4] The analysis recommended that removing uncertainties at a higher initial cost with LiDAR will easily get paid off in subsequent study and design stages by avoided additional study time and delays, and limitation of need to over-dimension designs of measures, increasing cost-efficiency.[^2] The study provided recommendations on when to use which surveying methods with respect to project area size, land-use/vegetation cover, and challenges like import of equipment.[^3][^4] The project demonstrated deployment of real-time kinematic (RTK) mode using new, low-cost hardware capable of providing centimeter-precise surveys, with TU Delft student Kirsten van Dongen co-developing a method that delivered similar accuracy and precision as professional-grade survey equipment.[^6] The DEM from 2000 may not accurately represent current topography of the basin, especially in areas with significant development, and the Pugu Hills are not well represented, highlighting the value proposition of updated high-resolution surveying.[^8]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.worldbank.org/en/programs/tanzania-urban-resilience-program
- [^2]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Africa/Dar-es-Salaam-Tanzania/Bathymetric-and-Topographic-Surveying-of-Flood-Prone-Rivers-in-Dar-Es-Salaam-Comparative-Case-of-UAV-Photogrammetry-and-UAV-LiDAR-for-Generation-of-Digital-Terrain-Models-for-Flood-Modelling-Purposes-Msimbazi-River-Dar-es-Salaam.pdf
- [^3]: https://cdr-international.nl/lidar-drone-survey-msimbazi-river-dar-es-salaam/
- [^4]: https://cdr-international.nl/portfolio/tanzania-worldbank-topographic-and-bathymetric-surveying-of-flood-prone-rivers-in-dar-es-salaam/
- [^5]: https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/video/2019/05/15/drones-helping-to-understand-flood-risk-in-tanzania
- [^6]: https://www.hotosm.org/updates/using-low-cost-equipment-to-gather-high-precision-data-for-flood-modeling/
- [^7]: https://www.gfdrr.org/en/feature-story/msimbazi-basin-long-term-integrated-approach-flood-risk-management-and-urban
- [^8]: https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/532691567573088477/pdf/Development-of-a-Hydrological-and-Hydrodynamic-Model-of-the-Msimbazi-Basin-Inception-Report.pdf
- [^9]: https://www.worldbank.org/en/country/tanzania/brief/msimbazi-basin-development-project

# Research Report: Interactive Story Maps for Sea Level Rise Visualization

## Solution Overview

Miami-Dade County has developed story maps to help visualize data and communicate various resilience challenges with the community.[^2][^3][^4] This approach can be explored through the Sea Level Rise Strategy ArcGIS Hub, which provides access to interactive visualizations and downloadable documentation.[^1] By collaborating with subject matter experts and GIS professionals, the County has been able to effectively demonstrate sea level rise as well as showcase what projects are underway to address the flooding.[^2]

## Technical Components

The initiative utilizes ArcGIS Hub as its primary platform for hosting and delivering interactive story maps.[^1] Through collaboration with subject matter experts and GIS professionals, the County has developed visualization tools that effectively demonstrate sea level rise conditions and communicate ongoing flood mitigation projects.[^2] The Sea Level Rise Strategy ArcGIS Hub serves as the central repository where community members can view the story maps and download printable versions of related documentation.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The Miami-Dade Board of County Commissioners unanimously approved the recommendations of its Sea Level Rise Task Force in 2015, which called for an Enhanced Capital Plan that has evolved into the County's Sea Level Rise Strategy.[^1] The County developed multiple story maps to address different resilience planning needs.[^2] A second story map was developed to analyze both where to site transit stations and how stations could be reimagined to also serve as community resilience hubs, offering multiple public services and providing civic spaces.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

This approach enables the County to effectively demonstrate sea level rise impacts to community members while simultaneously showcasing what projects are underway to address flooding challenges.[^2] The transit-focused story map provides analytical capabilities for identifying optimal station locations while exploring how these facilities could function as community resilience hubs that offer multiple public services and provide civic spaces.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The story maps directly address sea level rise visualization and flood communication challenges facing Miami-Dade County.[^1][^2] This technology-enabled approach helps the community understand both current sea level rise conditions and the County's strategic response through its Enhanced Capital Plan and Sea Level Rise Strategy.[^1]

## Business Analysis

The Sea Level Rise Strategy evolved from the Enhanced Capital Plan that was called for by the Sea Level Rise Task Force, whose recommendations received unanimous approval from the Miami-Dade Board of County Commissioners in 2015.[^1] This institutional support demonstrates policy backing for technology-enabled resilience communication approaches. The ArcGIS Hub platform provides a centralized digital infrastructure for distributing resilience information to the community while maintaining accessibility through downloadable printable versions.[^1]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Miami-US/Resilient305_final.pdf
- [^3]: https://www.route-fifty.com/emerging-tech/2017/02/miami-flood-mapping-resiliency/135605/
- [^4]: https://resilient305.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/R305-First-Annual-Progress-Report_October_pages-1.pdf

# Research Report: Urban Heat Response Data Platform

## Solution Overview

City Tech Collaborative partnered with Microsoft, Esri, d3i Systems, and the City of Chicago to create a replicable, scalable study of urban heat spots over time and assessment of site-specific heat interventions through the Urban Heat Response project.[^1] This initiative brings together a broad coalition of stakeholders to explore data-based approaches to mitigate the negative impacts of extreme heat events.[^2] The approach makes weather and climate information based on NASA environmental data accessible through Esri's ArcGIS mapping and analytics platform hosted in Microsoft's Azure Cloud, enabling urban planners and municipal officials to measure the impact of heat interventions and plan effective, customized interventions for neighborhoods or even individual buildings using previously inaccessible data.[^3]

## Technical Components

The technology integrates advanced environmental data from NASA to quantify urban heat islands and assess urban heat reduction interventions.[^3] NASA and Microsoft met with the City of Chicago, Esri, City Tech Collaborative, and D3i Systems to create this tool to incorporate advanced environmental data into urban design and infrastructure planning.[^8] The urban heat visualizations are based on NASA satellite images that show heat concentrations and change over time, with satellites passing over a city two to three times per month providing approximately six images per summer.[^6] The group explored several types of satellite data, but at 30 meters, Landsat had a finer resolution for capturing the urban heat interventions.[^7] Through cutting-edge analytical power provided by Microsoft's Azure Cloud and mapping functionality provided by Esri's ArcGIS, NASA environmental data is incorporated into a visualization dashboard to provide deep insights to urban planners and government officials evaluating the need for and impact of urban green spaces and other mitigation measures used to manage urban hotspots.[^8] More than 20,000 global cities use Esri's ArcGIS mapping and spatial analytics software, which provided an essential platform to host urban heat island assessment data.[^1] d3i Systems' expertise in supercomputer-based research using advanced models of Earth's atmosphere, oceans, and land masses helped the team understand heat trends and the effect on the Earth's surface.[^1] The team is investigating the potential benefits of using data collected via Chicago's Array of Things initiative—an urban measurement project comprised of hundreds of nodes installed around Chicago to collect real-time data on the city's environment, infrastructure, and activity.[^8]

The City of Chicago partnered with the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) and CAPA Strategies on a community science heat mapping campaign to better understand how heat is distributed across Chicago.[^4] On July 27th and 28th, 2023, 110 volunteers drove predetermined routes across the city, collecting temperature and humidity data using specially designed thermal sensors mounted to their vehicles.[^4] The routes were designed to capture the diversity of land uses and land covers in Chicago, including parks, industrial corridors, and residential neighborhoods.[^4] Data was collected in the morning, afternoon, and evening to capture the temperature fluctuations throughout the day.[^4] During the three separate one-hour collection periods, volunteers collected 121,114 individual temperature and humidity measurements along 10 traverse routes.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The Urban Heat Response pilot project timeline is 1-5 years and is owned by City Tech Collaborative with partners including Microsoft, NASA, NOAA, ComEd, University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, Mayor's Office, OEMC, CDPH, DPD, DFSS, and MOPD.[^2] In fall 2019, several groups came together for a pilot project to evaluate urban heat interventions, with the City of Chicago serving as the pilot city.[^7] City Tech Collaborative managed the partnership and project, which also included Microsoft, with Azure cloud computing and other tools; Esri, with ArcGIS mapping and other software; and d3i Systems, with Earth observation research and expertise, including the use of Landsat data.[^7] The prototype was slated for testing in Chicago in early 2020.[^3][^6] The partners, Microsoft, Esri, systems engineering and advanced analytics firm d3i Systems and the City of Chicago continued their work to devise the tool through the end of that year.[^6]

Phase 1 of the pilot focused on use case definition, data gathering, and value definition to generate a detailed framework for implementation.[^2] Phase 2 of the pilot committed to building a minimum viable product necessary to drive decision-making regarding future extreme heat episodes.[^2] The COVID-19 pandemic delayed efforts to integrate urban heat island assessment into the ArcGIS mapping and analytics platform, but project participants are determined to expand access to this capability in the near future.[^1] Once the heat mapping tool is live, it should be accessible to cities of all sizes because it will build on Esri software many urban planners already use.[^6] Following the proof-of-concept period, the tool could be expanded to include any city for which satellite data is available.[^6]

## Benefits and Impacts

The average temperature differential between the warmest and coolest places in the city at the same time was 14.9°F.[^4] The largest temperature differential was observed during the afternoon at 16.2°F.[^4] Five of the intervention sites, including Millennium Park, had statistically significant decreases in surface temperature, while one had a gain in temperature.[^7] On a hot day in August, a green roof located on the top of Chicago City Hall measured almost 80 degrees Fahrenheit cooler than the neighboring conventional roof.[^5]

Urban planners, emergency management directors and local policymakers can use the data to identify urban hotspots and quantify the impact of urban heat interventions or land use policy changes.[^6] The enhanced mapping and analytics capability allows stakeholders to zero in on past interventions such as tree planting and the creation of greens spaces and understand the impact on the surrounding climate over time.[^5] These same planners and officials are able to quantify the heat reduction outcomes from previous interventions, transforming how the city plans and executes critical emergency response services.[^8] The enhanced capability has the potential to quantify heat reduction efforts and transform how cities plan and execute critical emergency response services.[^5] The pilot informs and improves decisions on near-term planning as well as long-term planning regarding infrastructure and resilience investments.[^2]

The tool is scalable to other applications and other cities, with the potential to better inform how cities plan and implement future green spaces, cooling shelters, utility-sponsored programs for low-income customers, public health interventions, and weather-related emergency response, ensuring that resources get to the communities and populations that need them most.[^8][^5]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Extreme heat events are responsible for more annual fatalities in the U.S. than any other form of extreme weather.[^2] The Midwest heat waves of 1995 and 1999 together claimed more than 1,300 lives, with an estimated 739 in Chicago alone.[^2] In July 1995, Chicago experienced a five-day streak of extreme heat resulting in over 700 heat-related deaths.[^8] During past extreme heat events, Chicago's ability to respond has been hindered by its lack of available data, with present day challenges persisting due to gaps in data, isolated datasets, and other issues surrounding data accessibility.[^2] City officials are working with NASA scientists to better understand the temporal and spatial evolution of the city's urban heat island.[^3]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.citytech.org/city-tech-and-partners-use-nasa-environmental-data-to-evaluate-urban-heat-interventions
- [^2]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Chicago-US/Resilient Chicago.pdf
- [^3]: https://www.citytech.org/urban-heat-response
- [^4]: https://www.chicago.gov/content/dam/city/depts/cdph/environment/heat_watch/Summary-Report-Heat-Watch-Chicago_CAPA-12.15.2023.pdf
- [^5]: https://www.citytech.org/urban-heat-response-pilot
- [^6]: https://www.smartcitiesdive.com/news/city-tech-collaborative-targets-2020-for-putting-heat-mapping-tool-in-urban/562618/
- [^7]: https://www.usgs.gov/centers/eros/news/collaborations-quest-examine-effects-urban-heat-actions-chicago
- [^8]: https://usgeo.gov/jazz-observatory/2023/stories/heatmappingtool.html?category=Health

# Research Report: Tide Height Modeling and Storm Surge Warning System

## Solution Overview

Mumbai Maritime Board's modelled tide height data serves as a critical input for predicting high-tide timings and issuing storm surge warnings during extreme weather events.[^1][^5][^6] This approach addresses the city's vulnerability as an island metropolis where high population density and coastal location create extreme risk from rising sea levels and coastal flooding.[^9] The technology integrates hydrodynamic models and storm surge calculations to assess tide and storm surge impacts on the coastal city.[^4]

## Technical Components

The system utilizes hydrodynamic models and storm surge models to calculate tide and storm surge impacts on Mumbai as an island city with connectivity to the sea.[^4] Mumbai Maritime Board's modelled tide height data provides the foundation for predicting high-tide timings and generating storm surge warnings during extreme weather events.[^1][^5][^6][^7][^8] A web GIS-based decision support system was built to calculate the vulnerability and risk of elements exposed to flood.[^4] However, modelled data has limited accuracy in predicting future risk projections compared to monitored data.[^1] The Indian National Centre for Oceans Information Services (INCOIS) tide gauge data is not used by any department or agency in Mumbai for planning decisions, despite the city's tide gauge—installed by the British East India Company in the late 1800s—being the only observatory in the country offering more than a century's worth of sea level measurements.[^1][^7]

## Implementation Details

The Municipal Corporation of Greater Mumbai approached the Ministry of Earth Sciences (MoES) to develop an Integrated Flood Warning System for Mumbai referred to as IFLOWS-Mumbai.[^4] This initiative represents a joint effort between the Ministry of Earth Sciences (MoES) and the Municipal Corporation of Greater Mumbai (MCGM), also referred to as Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation (BMC).[^2][^3][^4] The Flood Warning System was formally launched jointly by Honorable Shri Uddhavji Balasaheb Thackeray, Chief Minister of Maharashtra, and Dr. Harsh Vardhan, Union Minister for Health & Family Welfare, Science & Technology, Earth Sciences, on June 12, 2020, at 12:30 hours in Mumbai.[^4] In the two years preceding this launch, researchers conducted studies to provide real-time weather information by measuring the city's rainfall, drainage capacity, topography, land use, infrastructure development, population, lakes, creeks, and river bathymetry data for all rivers including Mithi, Dahisar, Oshiwara, Poisar, and Ulhas.[^3] The system incorporates weather models from National Centre for Medium Range Weather Forecasting (NCMRWF) and India Meteorological Department (IMD), field data from rain gauge network stations established by Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology (IITM), MCGM, and IMD, plus thematic layers on land use and infrastructure provided by MCGM.[^4] The land topography, land use, infrastructure, and population data provided by MCGM was integrated into a Decision Support System to accurately estimate flood levels at ward level using thematic layers in GIS.[^4] Mumbai became only the second city in the country after Chennai to receive the IFLOWS system.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The integrated approach enables authorities to issue storm surge warnings during extreme weather events by predicting high-tide timings using Mumbai Maritime Board's modelled tide height data.[^1][^5][^6][^7][^8] The web GIS-based decision support system calculates the vulnerability and risk of elements exposed to flood conditions.[^4] The system provides ward-level flood level estimates by integrating land topography, land use, infrastructure, and population data through thematic layers in GIS.[^4] BMC is recommended to integrate monitored data into planning and decision-making for improved accuracy, given that modelled data has limited accuracy in predicting future risk projections.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This system directly addresses Mumbai's extreme vulnerability to coastal flooding and rising sea levels, which pose significant risks given the city's high population density and coastal location.[^9] The approach responds to the city's increasing flood periodicity, exemplified by the August 29, 2017 flood that brought Mumbai to a standstill.[^4] Machine learning model projections of sea level rise impacts reveal that Mumbai faces the highest flood risks, particularly under high emission scenarios.[^9]

## Business Analysis

The implementation represents a government-led initiative involving coordination between the Ministry of Earth Sciences (MoES) at the national level and the Municipal Corporation of Greater Mumbai at the municipal level.[^2][^3][^4] The formal launch by the Chief Minister of Maharashtra and Union Minister for Health & Family Welfare, Science & Technology, Earth Sciences demonstrates high-level political support for the flood warning infrastructure.[^4] The system leverages existing institutional capacity from multiple national agencies including National Centre for Medium Range Weather Forecasting (NCMRWF), India Meteorological Department (IMD), and Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology (IITM), creating a collaborative framework for flood risk management.[^4] The recommendation that BMC integrate monitored data into planning and decision-making suggests ongoing opportunities for system enhancement and accuracy improvements.[^1]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Asia & Asia Pacific Oceania/Mumbai-India/Mumbai Climate Action Plan 2022.pdf
- [^2]: https://www.nccr.gov.in/sites/default/files/iflmumbai_2.pdf
- [^3]: https://www.shankariasparliament.com/current-affairs/iflows-mumbai-integrated-flood-warning-system
- [^4]: https://www.pib.gov.in/Pressreleaseshare.aspx?PRID=1630928
- [^5]: https://www.mcgm.gov.in/irj/go/km/docs/documents/Environment/Climate/MCAP%20Summary%20for%20Policymakers.pdf
- [^6]: https://www.thenatureofcities.com/TNOC/2022/04/04/developing-a-successful-climate-action-plan-for-mumbai/
- [^7]: https://science.thewire.in/environment/indian-coast-sea-level-rise-tide-gauge-satellite-altimetry-complexity/
- [^8]: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6346595/
- [^9]: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-13394-5

# Research Report: Electric Vehicle Fleet with Solar Microgrids and Battery Storage

## Solution Overview

Los Angeles is implementing a comprehensive electric vehicle transition strategy that integrates municipal fleet electrification with renewable energy infrastructure to enhance climate resilience while reducing emissions.[^2] This approach combines the deployment of electric buses, light-duty vehicles, and charging infrastructure with co-located solar photovoltaic systems, battery storage, and microgrids designed to provide backup power during outages.[^2] The initiative represents one of the nation's most ambitious municipal electrification programs, encompassing multiple city departments and transit agencies working toward aggressive zero-emission targets.[^1][^2]

## Technical Components

The technical infrastructure centers on advanced microgrid systems that integrate multiple energy components for optimal performance. At LADOT's Washington Bus Yard, the implementation includes 1.5 megawatts of rooftop and bus solar canopy paired with a 4.5MWh energy storage system provided by Apparent, powering five Proterra 1.5-megawatt fleet chargers with 104 remote EV charging dispensers.[^1] The microgrid utilizes Apparent's intelligent grid operating system (igOS™) platform to integrate Proterra Energy's charging infrastructure with energy generation, coordinating how and when electric buses are charged with energy generated from solar, drawn from storage, or supplied by the utility.[^1] At seven-and-a-half megawatts of EV charging power, this installation represents one of the largest fleet charging installations in the United States.[^1] The charging infrastructure deployment extends beyond transit facilities, with the Bureau of Street Lighting installing 1,000 publicly available curbside Level 2 EV chargers using CFI grant funding.[^5] For the LAPD's electric fleet, Greenlots supplied 100 Level 2 chargers and four DC fast chargers, integrating its software with LAPD's fleet management program and utilizing SKY technology that reduces charging load based on priority when buildings near peak capacity, allowing optimal EV charging while avoiding overloading.[^4][^8] The BMW i3 vehicles serving as non-emergency fleet vehicles are electrified by lithium-ion batteries capable of 80-100 miles of emission-free driving.[^8]

## Implementation Details

The electrification program operates across multiple timelines and scales, with LADOT establishing the goal in 2019 to achieve a 100% electrified bus fleet by 2028.[^1] LADOT operated 29 EV buses in its fleet with 30 additional zero-emission buses expected to be in operation by Summer 2022.[^1] LA Metro and LADOT set targets to fully electrify their bus fleets by 2030, with Metro pushing to convert its fleet of 2,000-plus buses all-electric by that deadline.[^2][^9] The city established procurement guidelines requiring 50% EV procurement for all new light-duty fleet vehicles, increasing to 80% by 2025 as called for in the Los Angeles Sustainable City pLAn.[^4] The LAPD unveiled 100 BMW electric cars added to its fleet in June 2016, with plans to acquire 500 electric vehicles by 2021.[^8] The BlueLA Carsharing Pilot Project, running from October 2015 to March 2025 and operated by the City of Los Angeles Department of Transportation, includes construction and installation of 100 carshare stations with 500 charge points and operation of an electric vehicle carshare program with 300 electric vehicles in disadvantaged communities.[^7] As of the reporting period, the BlueLA project had 40 carsharing sites installed with 60 more to be installed in 2024, 200 charge points installed with 300 more to be installed in 2024, and 152 Electric Chevy Bolts currently deployed with 150 more planned for 2024.[^7]

The program involves extensive partnerships and significant funding commitments. LADOT was awarded a $6 million grant by the California Energy Commission to install the solar and storage microgrid at the Washington Bus Yard.[^1] LADOT selected Proterra and Apparent to install the EV-charging microgrid where it manages EV charging and overall energy use for more than 100 electric buses.[^1] Greenlots partnered with the City of Los Angeles, Los Angeles Police Department, and BMW of North America on the electric vehicle program announced in June 2016 to help the City achieve its clean transportation goals as part of Mayor Garcetti's Sustainability pLAn.[^4] The Bureau of Street Lighting, alongside LA County and LA Metro, and Los Angeles World Airports each secured a $15 million Charging and Fueling Infrastructure grant from the Federal Highway Administration to build out EV charging in Los Angeles.[^5] Los Angeles received nearly $80 million in new federal funding to electrify Metro buses to reduce air pollution, following nearly $900 million in federal funding announced earlier in 2024.[^6] The Low or No Emission Grant funding allowed Metro to procure battery-electric buses, charging equipment, and supporting infrastructure to provide zero-emission service along bus routes operating from Metro's Bus Division 7 in West Hollywood.[^6] The BlueLA Carsharing Pilot Project received a grant amount of $4,669,343 with resource contributions of $26,524,394, for a project total of $31,193,737.[^7] The BlueLA project involves a public-private partnership with the City providing rebates, fee waivers, and in-kind services while BlueLA provides infrastructure and vehicle investment.[^7]

Implementation challenges exist despite the ambitious scope. Metro faces roadblocks preventing it from reaching its electric bus goals, including a lack of chargers and a shrunken pool of manufacturers that can deliver electric buses.[^9] The zero-emission buses funded by federal grants replace compressed natural gas buses that have exceeded their useful service life, creating more sustainable transportation options ahead of the 2028 Olympic and Paralympic Games hosted in Los Angeles.[^6] The City's numerous different fleets are all piloting and actively pursuing medium and heavy-duty electric vehicle opportunities, including a recent pilot at the Port of Los Angeles testing trucks, electric utility bucket trucks, and a procurement opportunity for electric buses to be used on the airfield at LAX.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

The electrification initiative delivers substantial environmental and operational benefits across multiple metrics. The BlueLA project achieved estimated quantifiable benefits including GHG emission reductions of 5,102 MTCO2e, NOx reductions of 1,837 lbs, PM2.5 reductions of 10 lbs, and fossil fuel-based transportation fuel use reductions of 496,131 gallons.[^7] The BlueLA carshare service reduced an estimated annual GHG emissions by 2,234.11 metric tons, equivalent to 2,469,325 pounds of coal burned or 251,391 gallons of gasoline consumed.[^7] Since project launch, the BlueLA carshare program had over 565,000 trips taken and more than 18.4 million miles traveled by 4,250+ members.[^7] The BlueLA project service areas include the Los Angeles neighborhoods of Echo Park, Hollywood, East Hollywood, Silverlake, Wilshire Center, Mid-City, Westlake, Koreatown, Pico-Union, Downtown, Boyle Heights, South LA, Chinatown, Rampart Village, MacArthur Park, and Jefferson Park.[^7]

The solar and storage microgrid paired with 104 EV chargers supports LADOT's adoption of electric buses as the agency transitions to a fully electric fleet by 2028.[^1] By delivering clean solar energy generation and storage capacity, the project reduces greenhouse gas emissions, lowers LADOT's electricity costs, and provides emergency back-up power that enables the agency to continue to operate in an outage.[^1] LADP now operates the largest municipal EV fleet in the nation.[^2] The LAPD's installation represents the largest electric vehicle fleet and charging installation to ever serve a police department.[^4][^8] Nearly 16,000 EV chargers have been installed in Los Angeles over the past two years, representing a 77% increase in the city's available charging infrastructure since Mayor Bass assumed office.[^5] Los Angeles has the most EV charging stations in the country.[^5]

The broader transportation electrification aligns with citywide renewable energy goals. LADWP will supply 55% renewable energy by 2025, 80% by 2036, and 100% by 2045.[^3] The Eland Solar & Storage Center, a large-scale solar installation and battery storage system serving LADWP, began commercial operations in November 2024 and will supply power to more than 250,000 homes.[^5] Over 400 zero-emission heavy-duty trucks have been added to the Ports Drayage Truck Registry, moving forward the joint Ports' Clean Air Action Plan goal of all trucks serving the Ports being zero emission by 2035.[^5] LADWP secured $8 million in grant funding from the U.S. Department of Energy to strengthen zero-emission hydroelectric infrastructure, improve grid resilience, and promote equitable access to clean energy.[^5]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The integrated approach directly addresses climate resilience through backup power capabilities that maintain critical transportation services during grid disruptions caused by extreme weather events or other climate-related emergencies. The microgrid's energy storage system provides emergency backup power that enables LADOT to continue operations during outages, ensuring transit continuity when communities need it most.[^1] By co-locating renewable energy generation with vehicle charging infrastructure, the system reduces dependence on centralized grid power that may be vulnerable to climate-related disruptions while simultaneously reducing emissions that contribute to climate change.[^2]

## Business Analysis

The program operates through diverse financial mechanisms combining federal grants, state funding, and public-private partnerships. The California Energy Commission provided $6 million for LADOT's Washington Bus Yard microgrid installation, while the Federal Highway Administration awarded $15 million CFI grants to multiple city agencies for charging infrastructure expansion.[^1][^5] Federal transit funding totaling nearly $80 million supports Metro's bus electrification, supplementing nearly $900 million in federal funding announced earlier in 2024.[^6] The BlueLA project demonstrates the public-private partnership model, with the City providing regulatory support through rebates, fee waivers, and in-kind services valued at $26,524,394, while private partners contribute infrastructure and vehicle investment, supported by $4,669,343 in grant funding.[^7]

The market dynamics reflect Los Angeles's position as a national leader in EV infrastructure deployment. Los Angeles has the most EV charging stations in the country, with nearly 16,000 chargers installed over the past two years representing a 77% increase since Mayor Bass assumed office.[^5] The order of electric vehicles aligns with Mayor Garcetti's Sustainable City pLAn released in April 2015, which includes a goal that more than half of all new city vehicles in Los Angeles be fully electric.[^8] The City established ambitious targets to increase the percentage of zero-emission vehicles in the city to 25% by 2025, 80% by 2035, and 100% by 2050, with plans to convert all City fleet vehicles to zero emission where technically feasible by 2028.[^3] However, implementation challenges include manufacturer capacity constraints and infrastructure gaps, with Metro facing a shrunken pool of manufacturers that can deliver electric buses and insufficient chargers to meet electrification timelines.[^9] The program's operational benefits include reduced electricity costs through solar generation and intelligent charging management that optimizes energy use while avoiding peak demand charges.[^1][^4]

[^1]: Los Angeles Department of Transportation. (n.d.). *LADOT awarded $6 million grant for solar and storage microgrid to power electric bus fleet*.

[^2]: City of Los Angeles. (n.d.). *Climate adaptation and resilience plan*.

[^3]: City of Los Angeles. (n.d.). *Los Angeles Sustainable City pLAn targets*.

[^4]: Greenlots. (2016). *Greenlots partners with City of Los Angeles and LAPD on electric vehicle program*.

[^5]: Office of Mayor Karen Bass. (2024). *Mayor Bass announces major clean energy milestones*.

[^6]: Metro. (2024). *Metro receives federal funding for electric bus procurement*.

[^7]: California Air Resources Board. (n.d.). *BlueLA Carsharing Pilot Project progress report*.

[^8]: BMW Group. (2016). *LAPD unveils 100 BMW i3 electric vehicles*.

[^9]: Los Angeles Times. (2024). *Metro faces challenges in electric bus transition*.

## Sources

- [^3]: https://plan.mayor.lacity.gov/las-green-new-deal/targets
- [^4]: https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/greenlots-to-provide-lapd-with-the-largest-ev-fleet-charging-network-to-serve-a-police-department-300281352.html
- [^5]: https://mayor.lacity.gov/news/delivering-results-2024-bass-highlights-unprecedented-green-year-la
- [^6]: https://mayor.lacity.gov/news/major-funding-secured-secretary-buttigieg-and-mayor-bass-announce-new-funding-electrify-buses
- [^7]: https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/lcti-bluela-carsharing-pilot-project

# Research Report: Reservoir Water Level Monitoring and Early Warning System

## Solution Overview

Da Nang planned to build warning systems linked to water level at Truc Dong and Hoc Khe reservoirs as part of investing in hydro-meteorologic monitoring and early warning systems for flood, flash flood and reservoir breakdown.[^1] This initiative was designated as Action 4.7 and also included building an early warning system for Tra reserve.[^1] The city budget allocation for this action was 600 million VND by 2020, with 2,000 million VND from other sources.[^1]

## Technical Components

The automatic hydro-meteorological monitoring network consists of weather-proof enclosures containing data loggers, rechargeable batteries, sensors for air temperature, air humidity, solar radiation, wind speed, water level with attached solar panels and mounted upon masts located at fixed ground stations.[^2] To capture changes in weather and stream flow in the basin, the 5-minute and half-hour recording frequency options were set for meteorological and hydrological variables, respectively.[^2] All hydro-meteorological data was transmitted every 30 minutes to the data server at the data processing centre via Global System for Mobile Communications (GSM)/General Packet Radio Service (GPRS) network.[^2]

## Implementation Details

A total of 20 meteorological stations and five hydrological stations have been built in VGTB river basin.[^2] The city budget allocation for Action 4.7 was 600 million VND by 2020, with 2,000 million VND from other sources.[^1] The implementation included developing a proposal and roadmap for investment of the monitoring system, encompassing warning systems at Truc Dong and Hoc Khe reservoirs as well as an early warning system for Tra reserve.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

These devices help to improve data availability, connectivity, and usefulness, and facilitate timely flood warning delivery to the hardest-to-reach communities.[^3] The results showed that the performance of flood simulation at hourly time step has significantly improved during flood events in September and November 2015.[^2] The enhanced monitoring capabilities enable more accurate and timely warnings for vulnerable populations in remote areas.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach directly addresses flooding, flash flood, and reservoir breakdown risks through enhanced monitoring and early warning capabilities.[^1] The system's ability to capture rapid changes in weather and stream flow conditions enables improved flood simulation performance during extreme precipitation events.[^2]

[^1]: Climate Change Adaptation Plan for Da Nang period 2015-2020, vision to 2030
[^2]: Improving Flood Forecasting in the Mekong River Basin Using Satellite-Based Observations and Neuromorphic Computing
[^3]: Flood Early Warning System in the Mekong River Basin: A Review

## Sources

- [^3]: https://www.i-s-e-t.org/technological-improvements-to-flood-early-warning-systems-in-vietnam

# Research Report: RESCCUE Web-Based Urban Resilience Modeling Platform

## Solution Overview

The RESCCUE Toolkit represents a comprehensive web-based platform developed through a four-year European Union Horizon 2020 research and innovation project that ran from May 2016 to October 2020.[^1] This approach aims to help urban areas around the world become more resilient to climate change, improving their capability to anticipate, prepare for, respond to and recover from significant multihazard threats with minimum damage.[^5] The platform was developed and implemented for three case studies: Barcelona, Bristol and Lisbon, with the Barcelona implementation created in close collaboration with the Barcelona City Council and other local stakeholders.[^1]

## Technical Components

The RESCCUE Toolkit includes a set of innovative datasets, methodologies, models and tools to analyse urban resilience based on a multisectoral approach.[^5] Mathematical modelling was performed using a large set of software packages and tools including GIS, Infoworks ICM, DIgSILENT PowerFactory, TransCAD, MOHID Studio, SIMGES, and HBV hydrology model.[^2] The platform takes advantage of the existing HAZUR tool and methodology as the basis of further software developments able to perform the assessment, management and planning of urban resilience in an integrated way.[^9] In the holistic approach, the resilience assessment tool (HAZUR) is used to analyse the relations and the cascading effects among the different urban services.[^9] The resilience assessment framework defines resilience as a combination of four dimensions: absorptive capacity, adaptive capacity, restorative capacity, and transformative capacity.[^1] The toolkit is connected to a database that stores all information related to the resilience assessment, including climate change projections, resilience indicators, and adaptation strategies.[^1] This database was designed to be flexible and scalable so that it can be easily adapted to other cities.[^1] The platform includes a user management system that allows definition of different user roles and permissions to control access to different modules and functionalities.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The RESCCUE project was funded by the European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme under grant agreement No 700174.[^3] The project consortium was led by Aquatec (SUEZ Advanced Solutions) and included three city councils (Barcelona, Lisbon and Bristol), UN-Habitat, urban services companies (Endesa, EDP, Aguas de Portugal, and Wessex Water), research centres (Cetaqua, FIC, LNEC and IREC), universities (Exeter and EIVP), and SMEs (Hidra and UrbanDNA).[^4] The three cities included as pilot sites served as validation platforms of the improved tool, where integrated analyses of urban resilience were performed throughout the project.[^9] Main implementing actors for developing the resilience assessment tool included Ajuntament de Barcelona (Urban Ecology), RESCCUE project partners (Aquatec, Cetaqua, UN-Habitat), and urban service operators (Aigües de Barcelona, Endesa, etc.).[^3] The tool was co-developed with service operators and integrated into their management processes.[^3] The RESCCUE approach was implemented in three EU cities with support from UN-Habitat to disseminate results among other cities belonging to major international networks.[^7]

## Benefits and Impacts

The resilience assessment tool was used to assess the resilience of the water, energy, and waste services in Barcelona and to identify the cascading effects between them.[^3] The system focuses on cascading failures that involve several urban functions, given the interdependencies existent between the several city services.[^7] The urban resilience management tool HAZUR Manager was improved and tested so it can be easily deployed in any other city around the world.[^9] By constantly monitoring key infrastructures, the tool is able to detect which infrastructures are affected when shocks occur and supports response efforts.[^9] The RESCCUE Resilience Action Plan of Barcelona was built based on RESCCUE's template and guidelines and on the results obtained using the tools and approaches developed in RESCCUE.[^6] This thematic plan contributes to the city's global resilience plan.[^6] The project published several papers in different peer-reviewed journals, as well as a special issue in the Sustainability Journal entitled 'Integrated assessment of climate change impacts and urban resilience: from climate and hydrological hazards to risk analysis and measures'.[^5]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The RESCCUE Toolkit for Barcelona focuses on four climate hazards: heavy rains and floods, droughts and water scarcity, sea level rise and coastal floods, and heat waves.[^1] These tools assess urban resilience from a multisectorial approach for current and future climate change scenarios, including multiple hazards and cascading effects.[^7] Recent climate projections for the city of Barcelona show a relevant increment of the maximum rainfall intensities for the period 2071–2100.[^10]

## Business Analysis

The project generated models and tools to deliver a framework enabling city resilience assessment, planning and management by integrating into software tools new knowledge related to the detailed water-centred modelling of strategic urban services performance into a comprehensive resilience platform.[^8] The RESCCUE approach uses the water sector as the central point of the approach to help cities around the world become more resilient to physical, social, and economic challenges.[^7] The platform aims to improve urban resilience through a set of models and software tools that assess climate change impacts in strategic urban sectors, such as water, transport, energy supply and solid waste, then interconnects them to assess urban resilience for the current state and a wide range of potential future scenarios.[^9] The RESCCUE Toolkit is accessible as a web-based platform at http://toolkit.resccue.eu/.[^1] The validation platform formed by three EU cities (Barcelona, Lisboa and Bristol) allowed testing the innovative tools developed in the project and disseminating their results among other cities belonging to major international networks.[^8]

## Sources

# Research Report: Wellington Digital Climate Information Hub and Community Action Map

## Solution Overview

Wellington City Council developed an online information hub to connect residents with comprehensive resources about living in the city, with a particular focus on climate action and resilience.[^2] This digital platform serves as a centralized access point for climate science tools, community engagement resources, and funding opportunities.[^2] The initiative builds upon the Council's award-winning digital city model tool, which creates accessible and engaging experiences for the public to explore Wellington's changing urban landscape.[^1]

## Technical Components

The platform is owned and operated by Wellington City Council using software licensed from Social Pinpoint.[^2] The technical infrastructure includes an interactive Community Climate Action Map that allows users to find local climate action groups in Pōneke, connect with initiatives, or add markers to showcase projects.[^2] The system incorporates climate science tools that enable residents to explore potential hazards affecting their homes and communities.[^2] The implementation integrates with Wellington's Digital Twin tool, developed as part of the Our Changing City project, which visualizes the past, present, and future of Pōneke to raise awareness of climate change impacts.[^3]

## Implementation Details

The Council won an award for innovation from the prestigious Bloomberg Global Mayors Challenge in 2022 and received US$1 million, which was used to deliver Wellington's digital city model tool.[^1] Kelly Henderson, Innovation Lead at Wellington City Council, shared a showcase video about the Digital Twin the team developed, outlining how the Our Changing City project utilized the model to show temporal changes in Pōneke.[^3] The digital city model tool was designed to create accessible and engaging experiences for the public to explore the changing shape of Wellington.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The Climate Action Info Hub provides access to over 60 funding opportunities to support community climate action projects in Wellington.[^2] The platform enables residents to explore potential hazards affecting their homes and communities through integrated climate science tools.[^2] The interactive Community Climate Action Map facilitates connections between local climate action groups and initiatives across Pōneke, allowing users to showcase their projects by adding markers to the map.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The digital platform addresses climate adaptation by providing residents with tools to explore potential hazards affecting their specific locations and communities.[^2] The integrated Digital Twin visualization demonstrates the impacts of climate change on Wellington's urban environment across past, present, and future timeframes, enhancing public awareness and understanding.[^3]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://wellington.govt.nz/climate-change-sustainability-environment/climate-change/what-were-doing-about-climate-change/our-climate-action-areas/action-area-community-action
- [^2]: https://www.letstalk.wellington.govt.nz/climate-action-info-hub
- [^3]: https://www.smartcitiescouncil.com/news/post/wellington-s-digital-twin

# Research Report: Multi-Technology Roof Assessment and Retrofit Program

## Solution Overview

In 2015, the District's Department of General Services (DGS) launched a comprehensive roof assessment initiative that evaluated over 250 acres of District-owned roofs for their suitability for solar photovoltaics (PVs), solar thermal, green roofs, daylighting, or cool roofs.[^1][^2][^4] This approach was designed to mitigate emissions and adapt to four local climate change-related challenges: extreme heat events, extreme precipitation, sea-level rise, and storm surge.[^1] The assessments identified dozens of sites that could benefit from roof improvement projects, allowing DGS to install over 150,000 square feet of measures across the District.[^2]

## Technical Components

The implementation encompasses multiple roof technologies tailored to specific building characteristics. Solar photovoltaic installations generate renewable energy on suitable structures, while cool roofs reflect solar radiation to reduce heat absorption.[^1][^4] The Department of General Services manages a portfolio of green roofs at various District-owned facilities.[^3] Many of these green roofs were installed by Furbish Company, LLC and contain proprietary systems including EcoCline® and SmartSlope®.[^3] The assessment process analyzed each roof across the 250-acre portfolio for its technical suitability for solar thermal systems, daylighting applications, and vegetation-based installations.[^2][^5][^6]

## Implementation Details

The 2015 assessment covered over 250 acres of District-owned roofs, systematically analyzing each property for optimal roof technology applications.[^1][^7][^8] Following the comprehensive evaluation, DGS installed over 150,000 square feet of measures across dozens of identified sites throughout the District.[^2] Furbish Company, LLC served as a key implementation partner, deploying proprietary green roof systems across multiple District-owned facilities.[^3] The program has created more than 300 jobs and employed dozens of local companies throughout its implementation.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The initiative is expected to avoid 350,000 metric tons of CO2 emissions over 20 years.[^1] Economic benefits total $40.2 million in projected savings, with $25 million attributed to solar PV installations and $15.2 million from cool roof implementations.[^1] Beyond emissions reductions and cost savings, the program has generated substantial employment impacts, creating more than 300 jobs while engaging dozens of local companies in the installation and maintenance work.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach directly addresses Washington, D.C.'s four identified local climate change-related challenges: extreme heat events, extreme precipitation, sea-level rise, and storm surge.[^1] The multi-technology strategy combines emissions mitigation with adaptation measures, using cool roofs to reduce urban heat island effects during extreme heat events while green roofs manage stormwater during extreme precipitation events.[^1]

## Business Analysis

The program demonstrates a compelling financial model with $40.2 million in projected savings distributed across solar PV installations ($25 million) and cool roof implementations ($15.2 million).[^1] The initiative has stimulated local economic development by creating more than 300 jobs and contracting with dozens of local companies for installation and maintenance services.[^1] The municipal implementation model leverages District-owned property assets, with DGS managing the ongoing portfolio of green roofs and other roof technologies across various facilities.[^3] The proprietary systems deployed by Furbish Company, LLC, including EcoCline® and SmartSlope® technologies, represent specialized market solutions within the green infrastructure sector.[^3]

## Sources

# Research Report: HAZUR® Urban Risk Assessment and Resilience Planning Methodology

## Solution Overview

The HAZUR® methodology represents a structured, qualitative risk assessment framework designed to evaluate interconnected urban services and infrastructures within cities, identifying risks, assessing cascading impacts, and establishing priorities for improvement.[^9] This approach was developed by Cetaqua, the Water Technology Centre, in collaboration with UN-Habitat, and forms part of the City Resilience Profiling Tool (CRPT) that supports cities in developing resilience strategies.[^1] The methodology has been applied in several cities around the world, including Barcelona, Bristol, Lisbon, Bogota, Amman and Mexico City.[^1]

## Technical Components

The HAZUR® methodology engages multidisciplinary teams of city stakeholders in a series of structured sessions, using a qualitative process to stimulate collaborative problem-solving among participants as they identify potential hazards and operational vulnerabilities in the urban network.[^9] This approach draws inspiration from well-established methodologies in industrial safety, such as Hazard and Operability Studies (HAZOP), as well as from business continuity planning and strategic urban management.[^9] The system incorporates several analytical techniques, including 'What-If' scenarios and cross-service impact assessments, utilizing participatory modeling, domino-effect simulations, and the assessment of climate adaptation measures on urban service operations.[^9] The RESCCUE project uses the HAZUR tool and methodology as the basis for software developments able to perform the assessment, management and planning of urban resilience in an integrated way.[^3] In the holistic approach, the resilience assessment tool is used to analyse the relations and the cascading effects among the different urban services.[^3] The urban resilience management tool HAZUR Manager is being improved and tested so it can be easily deployed in any other city around the world, and by constantly monitoring key infrastructures, the tool will be able to detect which infrastructures are affected when shocks occur.[^3]

## Implementation Details

The HAZUR® workshop in Barcelona was held on the 20th and 21st of June 2017 at the Cetaqua facilities.[^1] The workshop was organized by Cetaqua and UN-Habitat, with the support of the Barcelona City Council.[^1] The workshop was attended by 25 participants from different organizations, including the operators of the critical services, the city council, the civil protection agency, the meteorological agency and the research centres.[^1] Organizations participating in the Barcelona HAZUR® workshop included Aigües de Barcelona, AMB, Barcelona Cicle de l'Aigua, Barcelona City Council, Cetaqua, Civil Protection, Endesa, Gas Natural Fenosa, Port de Barcelona, Servei Meteorològic de Catalunya, Telefónica, TMB, UN-Habitat, and Universitat Politècnica de Catalunya.[^1] The workshop was divided into two days, with the first day dedicated to the identification of the hazards, the services and the potential impacts, and the second day dedicated to the risk assessment.[^1] An initial resilience assessment with HAZUR has been made in Barcelona, which helped identify improvements of the tool currently under development.[^3] The Barcelona Resilience Action Plan was developed within the framework of the RESCCUE project (Resilience to cope with climate change in urban areas), a research and innovation project funded by the European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme under grant agreement No 700174.[^2] The RESCCUE project using HAZUR® methodology involves a consortium of 18 partners coordinated by Aquatec – SUEZ Advanced Solutions.[^6] CETAQUA, CENTRO TECNOLOGICO DEL AGUA, FUNDACION PRIVADA received a net EU contribution of €979,949.61 for participation in the RESCCUE project.[^5]

## Benefits and Impacts

Through the HAZUR process, cities can consolidate essential data, analyze service interdependencies, anticipate crisis impacts, and develop proactive management strategies.[^9] Through HAZUR, cities can gain a deeper understanding of vulnerabilities and critical infrastructures, as well as the complex interdependencies between urban services, facilitated by a common language, clear visuals, and insights from key stakeholders.[^9] The results of the risk assessment in Barcelona show that the main risks are related to floods, droughts and heat waves, specifically the impact of floods on the water supply and sanitation services, the impact of droughts on the water supply service, and the impact of heat waves on the energy and health services.[^1] The HAZUR® implementation in Barcelona identified that the impact of heat waves on energy services has a risk level of 5 (very high risk).[^1] In Barcelona, participants identified a list of 10 hazards including floods, droughts, heat waves, sea level rise, storm surges, wildfires, earthquakes, terrorist attacks, industrial accidents, and cyber-attacks.[^1] By identifying the vulnerabilities of urban services, the resilience assessment tool will help prioritize investments to improve their robustness and reduce the risk of failure.[^2] Local administrations across Europe are utilizing HAZUR to inform resilience strategies, and the technique is frequently incorporated into university-level courses on smart cities and urban resilience.[^9]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Climatic drivers and pressures affecting the urban water cycle, such as droughts or heavy rains, can produce critical direct impacts on strategic urban services (water supply, wastewater and stormwater drainage, wastewater treatment, solid waste, telecommunication, energy supply, transport, etc.) and cause cascading collateral impacts on other services.[^4] Recent climate projections for Barcelona show a relevant increment of the maximum rainfall intensities for the period 2071–2100.[^10] RESCCUE focuses on the cascading failures that involve several urban functions, given the interdependencies existent between the several city services.[^4]

## Business Analysis

RESCCUE was born in May 2016 as Europe's first large-scale innovation and urban resilience project, aimed to improve the capability of cities to anticipate, prepare for, respond to, and recover from significant multi-hazard threats with minimum damage.[^8] The RESCCUE project received funding from the European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme under grant agreement No 700174.[^1] RESCCUE aims to deliver a framework enabling city resilience assessment, planning and management by integrating into software tools new knowledge related to the detailed water-centred modelling of strategic urban services performance into a comprehensive resilience platform.[^5] The RESCCUE project is developing a set of tools and methodologies to assess urban resilience and to support the development of resilience strategies, with these tools being tested and validated in three case study cities: Barcelona, Lisbon, and Bristol.[^2] The RESCCUE validation platform is formed by 3 EU cities (Barcelona, Lisboa and Bristol) that will allow testing the innovative tools developed in the project.[^5] The HAZUR® models and tools will be validated in Barcelona, Lisbon and Bristol, three cities carefully selected by their representativeness of the European diversity in terms of climate type and city characteristics.[^6] The RESCCUE tools allow urban resilience assessment from a multisectorial approach, for current and future climate change scenarios, including multiple hazards and cascading effects.[^4] Action 6.2 aims to develop a resilience assessment tool for urban services based on the methodology being developed within the RESCCUE project, to assess the resilience of the water, energy, and waste services in Barcelona and identify cascading effects between them.[^2] Action A2.1 under RESCCUE includes the improvement of the drought indicators system and the Drought Protocol of the Ter-Llobregat system.[^7] Action A2.2 under RESCCUE includes the implementation of a real-time flood forecasting and warning system for the main critical points of the city's drainage system.[^7]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://toolkit.resccue.eu/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/Barcelona-Resilience-Action-Plan_Toolkit.pdf
- [^3]: https://thesourcemagazine.org/resccue-a-european-model-for-urban-climate-change-resilience-planning/
- [^4]: https://www.mdpi.com/2073-4441/10/10/1356

- [^6]: https://toolkit.resccue.eu/project-overview/
- [^7]: https://toolkit.resccue.eu/wp-content/uploads/2020/12/D4.6.pdf
- [^8]: https://urbanresiliencehub.org/articles/urban-living-holistic/

# Research Report: Smart Water Pressure Management Infrastructure

## Solution Overview

Beginning in late 2017 and continuing through 2018, Cape Town's municipal government significantly expanded its investment in advanced water pressure management infrastructure, deploying smart process controllers and zone-by-zone pressure regulation mechanisms across the metropolitan water distribution network.[^1][^2][^4] This technological approach emerged as a critical response to the city's dual challenge of increased water demand driven by population growth, urbanization, and economic development, alongside heightened water scarcity resulting from climate change.[^1] The implementation proved instrumental in helping consumption drop below 500 megalitres per day during the peak of the 2015-2018 drought crisis.[^2]

## Technical Components

The pressure management infrastructure centered on the Regulo PRV controller, an electronic device retrofitted to existing hydraulic pressure reducing valves throughout the distribution network.[^5] This controller recorded inlet, outlet and critical point pressures, flow rate and valve position at 15-minute intervals, providing granular operational data for system optimization.[^5] The Regulo featured a dedicated internal modem and battery, enabling deployment in underground chambers without requiring external power supplies or protective kiosks.[^5] Utilitec Services, a Technolog group company, partnered with municipal authorities to install and commission over 100 of these advanced pressure control systems across the metropolitan area.[^5] The Technolog software platform, Utilisoft, provided a web-based interface that allowed city water managers to remotely monitor and adjust pressure settings across all automated zones.[^5] Municipal authorities had been expanding pressure testing protocols with the aim of using results to implement additional automated pressure zones across the metro, a process that had been ongoing for many years but was significantly accelerated in response to the drought emergency.[^6]

## Implementation Details

Cape Town established 170 pressure management zones covering 68 percent of the water network as of June 2021, building on a well-established programme that by some accounts encompassed over 200 pressure management zones.[^3][^5] The advanced pressure management programme had been underway since 2007, with municipal authorities planning to install a further 50 systems in 2017 as part of the drought response acceleration.[^5] The city raised R1 billion through a Green Bond listed on the Johannesburg Stock Exchange to fund sustainability infrastructure projects, including upgrades to reservoirs and water pressure management systems.[^3] Complementing the pressure management technology, municipal crews visited more than 4,000 households for leak detection and repairs, while replacing 258 kilometers of water pipes to reduce burst frequencies and water losses.[^1] The implementation enabled city officials to adjust water pressure remotely and work more efficiently by creating automated zones that could be managed from central control facilities.[^6]

## Benefits and Impacts

The pressure management systems achieved water savings of 70 million liters per day at the peak of the 2018 drought, with the intensified investment in late 2017 and early 2018 contributing to a reduction in consumption by a further 50 megalitres daily by early 2018.[^2][^3] Overall water consumption in Cape Town declined by more than 50 percent, from a peak exceeding 1.2 billion liters per day in February 2015 to just over 500 million liters per day in February 2018.[^4] The over 100 advanced pressure control systems installed across the network resulted in an estimated saving of 21 million cubic meters of water per annum, translating to a financial saving exceeding £5.5 million annually.[^5] In the Gugulethu district metering area specifically, implementing advanced pressure control reduced average daily flow from 11,000 cubic meters per day to 7,000 cubic meters per day, achieving savings of 4,000 cubic meters daily.[^5] The programme also generated significant infrastructure cost avoidance, as the advanced pressure control systems resulted in a substantial reduction in burst frequencies, saving an estimated £1.2 million per annum in repair costs.[^5] The comprehensive water conservation program postponed and possibly eliminated the need for an additional water supply scheme that would have cost $85 million—five times more than the conservation project investment.[^1] Beyond water savings, the programme delivered climate mitigation co-benefits by saving 58,000 tons of CO2 emissions each year.[^1] The demand management approach proved highly effective despite population growth exceeding 30 percent between 2001 and 2011, with water demand growing at an average of 1.78 percent compared to an average growth rate exceeding 4 percent before implementation.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The pressure management technology directly addressed Cape Town's vulnerability to extreme drought conditions, demonstrated during the 2015-2018 period when the city endured a one-in-400 year drought that brought approximately 4.6 million residents to the brink of "day zero"—the point when municipal water supplies would have been exhausted.[^3] The system's ability to reduce consumption by lowering the rate at which water flows to properties, while simultaneously reducing leaks and pipe bursts by maintaining pressure within tolerable levels for aging pipework, provided a dual adaptation benefit during the crisis.[^6] This technological approach enabled the city to extend available water supplies during unprecedented climate-driven scarcity while building long-term resilience against future drought events intensified by climate change.

## Business Analysis

The financial model demonstrated exceptional cost-effectiveness, with the water conservation program avoiding $85 million in capital infrastructure costs for new water supply schemes while generating annual operational savings exceeding £5.5 million from reduced water losses and £1.2 million from decreased pipe burst repairs.[^1][^5] The R1 billion Green Bond issued on the Johannesburg Stock Exchange represented an innovative financing mechanism that enabled the city to fund pressure management upgrades alongside other sustainability infrastructure projects, demonstrating how municipal green bonds can support climate adaptation investments.[^3] The public-private partnership model with Utilitec Services and Technolog provided technical expertise and proprietary controller technology while allowing the municipality to retain operational control through the web-based Utilisoft platform.[^5] The programme's expansion trajectory, from an established baseline since 2007 to accelerated deployment of 50 additional systems in 2017 and over 100 systems by 2018, reflected a scalable implementation model that could be adjusted based on drought severity and available capital.[^5] The substantial cost avoidance relative to traditional supply-side infrastructure investments positioned pressure management technology as a financially attractive demand-side alternative for water-stressed municipalities facing climate-driven scarcity.

[^1]: Source 1
[^2]: Source 2
[^3]: Source 3
[^4]: Source 4
[^5]: Source 5
[^6]: Source 6

# Research Report: Heat Risk Story Map and Vulnerability Index

## Solution Overview

Manchester developed a heat mapping tool to enhance understanding of the city's exposure and vulnerability to heat over time.[^5] The tool gives insights into how urban heat risks vary across the city and within communities, identifying areas where high temperatures and heatwaves could have the biggest impact on people's health and wellbeing.[^5] This approach emerged from collaborative efforts to address growing concerns about the impact that hotter summers will have on Manchester and its inhabitants.[^7]

## Technical Components

The Manchester Heat Risk Story Map combines climate information about extreme heat events with socio-economic data to understand future heat risks in UK cities, demonstrated through ArcGIS Storymaps.[^2] The system incorporates data from the Manchester City Pack, which provides a non-technical summary of Manchester's future climate and associated impacts using the latest UK Climate Projections (UKCP) data.[^2] A Heat Vulnerability Index for Greater Manchester was developed using novel indicators and an improved method of index construction, assessing vulnerability at the census unit of Lower Super Output Area across the metropolitan region of Greater Manchester.[^4] The technical framework draws on methodologies demonstrated in comparable implementations, such as the Keep Bristol Cool mapping tool, which was developed by experts from the Met Office and the Tyndall Centre for Climate Change Research, University of Manchester working with Bristol City Council to map urban heat risk.[^5]

## Implementation Details

In 2021, work was undertaken by Manchester Climate Change Agency (MCCA) to further understand the city's climate risks and vulnerabilities, resulting in the publication of Manchester's climate risk: a framework for understanding hazards and vulnerability.[^7][^1] The framework identifies Manchester's weather-related hazards and projects how climate change will affect these hazards, establishing a framework to support the comprehensive assessment of the city's vulnerabilities to climate change.[^7] In 2022, Manchester Climate Change Agency worked with the Met Office, Manchester City Council and Manchester Metropolitan University to further understand the impacts of a changing climate on Manchester.[^2] This collaboration produced the Manchester Heat Pack working with Manchester City Council, Manchester Climate Change Agency and Manchester Metropolitan University to provide information on how extreme heat events in the city may change this century due to climate change.[^3][^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

The Manchester Heat Pack provides information on how extreme heat events in Manchester may change this century due to climate change, the impacts, and how to build resilience to extreme heat.[^2] The tool gives insights into how urban heat risks vary across the city and within communities, identifying the areas where high temperatures and heatwaves could have the biggest impact on people's health and wellbeing.[^5] Similar implementations demonstrate the value of this approach, as the Keep Bristol Cool mapping tool demonstrates how current heat vulnerability varies across different neighbourhoods and how climate change may increase temperatures in the future, serving policy makers and practitioners such as urban designers, landscape architects, and emergency planners.[^6][^5]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This system directly addresses the growing threat of extreme heat events in Manchester, where droughts, heatwaves and wildfires represent future risks of particular concern for the city and its inhabitants.[^7] The framework developed for understanding Manchester's vulnerability to climate change enables comprehensive assessment of the city's capacity to respond to these climate threats.[^1][^7]

## Sources

- [^3]: https://www.metoffice.gov.uk/research/approach/collaboration/spf/ukcrp-outputs
