# Research Report: City Water Resilience Approach Framework Implementation

## Solution Overview

Cape Town became the first city to apply the City Water Resilience Approach (CWRA) to embed urban water resilience into the city's systems, following the severe 2014-2017 drought.[^4] This framework-based assessment tool was developed in Cape Town and became available for deployment in cities around the world in 2019, performing the necessary function of assessing changes in the urban water system during the implementation of the Cape Town Water Strategy.[^6] The approach helped the city move from a reactive, crisis-management mode to a more proactive and strategic approach to building water resilience.[^2]

## Technical Components

The City Water Resilience Framework (CWRF) includes a set of resilience goals, sub-goals and indicators, categorised across four dimensions: infrastructure and ecosystems, health and wellbeing, planning and finance, and leadership and strategy.[^5] The framework was co-created through research and a robust peer-review process with eight partner cities and consultation with global experts.[^5] Using this framework, both a 'city characterisation' report mapping Cape Town's water resources and identifying elements impacting water resilience, and a 'water resilience profile' detailing a series of actions that the city can take to address these, were created.[^4] The approach adopts a nature-first ethos, prioritising interventions that don't rely on hard infrastructure where natural factors can play a role.[^4] The CWRA includes five key stages towards developing a city's water resilience: understand the system, assess urban water resilience, develop an action plan, implement the action plan, and evaluate, learn and adapt.[^4]

## Implementation Details

Cape Town joined a group of 100RC member cities, including Mexico City, Miami, Amman, Greater Manchester, and Thessaloniki in developing the City Water Resilience Framework in 2018.[^6] The framework was developed by engineering consultancy Arup and funded by the Rockefeller Foundation.[^6] A Water Resilience CoLab was hosted in Cape Town in September 2018, during which local stakeholders engaged with stakeholders from Addis Ababa, Los Angeles and Mexico City and formulated several concrete actions that form part of the city's strategy.[^6] The City conducted a full assessment of Cape Town's water resilience using the CWRF, providing information on the city's relative water resilience strengths and vulnerabilities.[^6] The City committed to repeat the CWRF assessment every three years to determine changes, which will be used to inform future decision-making.[^6] Evidence from the application of CWRF in Cape Town and Greater Miami and the Beaches informed the cities' key water planning and strategies by integrating water resilience factors and activities.[^5]

## Benefits and Impacts

The CWRA was applied in Cape Town in the aftermath of the drought to help the city learn from the experience and to build a more water-resilient future.[^2] The resilience assessment highlighted the city's strengths in areas such as water conservation and demand management, but also identified weaknesses in areas such as the diversity of water sources and the integration of water and land use planning.[^2] Based on the findings of the assessment, the city developed a water resilience action plan that includes a range of actions, such as diversifying the city's water supply through the development of new sources, including groundwater, desalination, and water reuse.[^2] The Cape Town Water Strategy of 2019 captures lessons from the drought and makes a firm commitment to a 'whole-of-society' approach to make Cape Town a truly water-sensitive city by 2040.[^4] Cape Town is striving to be a water sensitive city by 2040 that optimises and integrates the management of water resources to improve resilience, and enhance competitiveness and liveability for the prosperity of its people.[^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

During the 2014–2017 drought, the overall dam levels supplying Cape Town dropped from 92.5 to 23%.[^7] Annual rainfall in the Western Cape had been steadily decreasing in the last few decades, with 2017 having the lowest annual rainfall since 1933.[^7] Low levels of rainfall led to the worst drought in the region since 1904.[^7] Cape Town released its notice of 'Day Zero' in January of 2018, originally predicted to be in April 2018, when dam levels would hit 13.5% and citywide water rationing would be enforced.[^7] At that point, taps would shut off to residents and water distributed through communal standpipes, limited to 25 l per person per day, as per the World Health Organization's minimum short-term emergency survival recommendations.[^7] Analysis of the physical data finds only a slight direct relationship between rainfall and dam levels (r2 = 0.3), suggesting a more complex narrative for the decrease in water supply, including increased water use and management decisions.[^7] During the same time period from 1996 to 2017, Cape Town's dam storage capacity only increased by about 15%, and rainfall remained highly variable.[^7]

## Business Analysis

Cape Town's Resilience Strategy includes an action to become a water-sensitive city as part of its Connected, Climate-Adaptive City pillar.[^1] The strategy also includes an action to build a data-smart city as part of its Capable, Participatory City pillar.[^1] The City of Cape Town serves as both water services authority and water services provider, responsible for providing water and sanitation services to residents and businesses in its jurisdiction.[^3] The Breede-Gouritz Catchment Management Agency is responsible for water resource management at a catchment level for the catchments that supply Cape Town with water.[^3]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://resource.capetown.gov.za/documentcentre/Documents/City%20strategies%2C%20plans%20and%20frameworks/Resilience_Strategy.pdf
- [^2]: https://www.resilienceshift.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/10/CWRA-Methodology-Final.pdf
- [^3]: https://www.arup.com/globalassets/downloads/insights/c/city-water-resilience-approach/characterisation-report/ccr-cape-town.pdf
- [^4]: https://worldgbc.org/case_study/cwra-cape-town/
- [^5]: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2210670721007630
- [^6]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Africa/CapeTown-SouthAfrica/Cape-Town-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^7]: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/water/articles/10.3389/frwa.2022.910149/full

# Research Report: Integrated Flood Forecasting and Modeling System for River Basins

## Solution Overview

A sophisticated flood forecasting and modeling system was developed for the Msimbazi River Basin to address recurring urban flooding challenges.[^1][^2][^3] This integrated approach combines hydrological modeling, hydraulic simulation, and real-time forecasting capabilities to predict flood events and assess their impacts on vulnerable communities.[^3][^5] The system represents a collaborative effort to create more accurate flood hazard mapping and enable timely early warnings for areas experiencing frequent severe flooding.[^5][^7]

## Technical Components

The technical architecture integrates multiple specialized modeling platforms and data sources to deliver comprehensive flood forecasting capabilities. SOBEK models river channels in 1D and inundation areas in 2D, with the 1D and 2D areas fully coupled.[^2] The 1D component propagates floods in river channels while the 2D component models the inundation of land surface areas in terms of 2D flood propagation.[^2] The SOBEK hydraulic flood model for the Msimbazi River uses a 20m resolution grid suitable for flood modeling, with LiDAR DTM 2019 data processed and resampled to this resolution.[^2] A cascaded model composed of wflow sbm hydrological and SOBEK 1D2D hydraulic model was set up and calibrated for the Msimbazi River within the Tanzanian Urban Resilience Program.[^2] The wflow sbm model was used to assess the catchment area of the Msimbazi river and the river discharges.[^2] The Delft-FEWS system includes data feeds from observed hydro-meteorological data from TAHMO and forecasted meteorological precipitation from the NOAA Global Forecast System (GFS), a hydrological wflow model, a 1D2D urban flood model in SOBEK, and a Delft-FIAT impact assessment model.[^3] The hydrodynamic model included detailed schematization of a community mapped drainage network and compared simulated flood depth with citizen's reports on flood depth for models run with and without the community mapped drainage network.[^1] Available baseline data for model development included rainfall gauge data from 5 stations covering the period 1985-2018 with daily temporal resolution, though some gaps exist in the data.[^4] Discharge data from 2 gauges covering the period 1985-2018 with daily temporal resolution is available, though some gaps exist in the data.[^4] Water level data from 2 gauges with 15-minute temporal resolution is available for 2018, representing a short period of record.[^4] A 30-meter resolution Digital Elevation Model from the 2000 Shuttle Radar Topography Mission is available, though the 2000 DEM may not accurately represent the current topography, especially in areas that have undergone significant development, and the Pugu Hills are not well represented.[^4] Land use data from 2016 based on RapidEye imagery is available, though significant development since 2016 may have changed land use patterns.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The hydrological and hydrodynamic model of the Msimbazi Basin was developed for the Tanzania Urban Resilience Program Dar es Salaam Metropolitan Development Project.[^4] Royal HaskoningDHV in association with CDR International and Hydro-Exuberance prepared the Inception Report for the Msimbazi Basin model development in September 2019.[^4] The German International Cooperation Agency (GIZ) worked with OpenMap Development Tanzania (OMDTZ) and Dutch knowledge institute Deltares to collect cross-section surveys, extending an existing flood model for the Lower Msimbazi to include a much larger upstream portion of the river.[^5] In collaboration with Deltares and with funding from GIZ, OMDTZ collected accurate and precise river cross-section surveys using an innovative, low-cost technique developed by the Resilience Academy and OMDTZ.[^5] After the first field exercise to refine methods and safety precautions, OMDTZ was able to scale up and rapidly collect over 80 cross-sections of the river.[^5] More than 300 students and locals were trained to use drones, GPS, and mobile apps to collect data for the Msimbazi Basin Development Project.[^6] A new survey of the Msimbazi river cross-sections was required as the 2010 data was limited to a few sections and was not sufficient for developing a detailed hydrodynamic model.[^4] The framework for urban flood modeling with community mapped data was demonstrated and validated on a case study in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania.[^1] State-of-the-art urban flood models combine detailed geometric data on sewer pipes and drainage channels with digital terrain data to model flood events, combining both overland flow and channel flow through channels and pipes.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

Delft-FEWS runs operationally at Deltares to provide a forecast every hour for the following 8 hours on river depth, flooded area, number of buildings affected per neighborhood (ward), flooded area of the bus depot, and flooded bus stops.[^3] A lead time of 8 hours was chosen because it takes water approximately 8 hours to flow from the very upstream parts of the Msimbazi River into the city of Dar es Salaam, which is enough time for stakeholders to act on forecasted floods while avoiding many false alarms and misses.[^3] The OMDTZ survey of river cross-sections and hydraulic structures along the Msimbazi River supported the creation of a comprehensive hydraulic model of the area that has led to the development of a more realistic flood hazard map of the most vulnerable areas.[^5] Community Water Watch (CWW) is a community-designed and -operated flood early warning service for Dar es Salaam, Tanzania that uses hydrological and hydrodynamic models built upon locally-collected map data.[^3] GFDRR supported real estate assessments showing that flood protection in the Msimbazi Basin could unlock up to $900 million in investment.[^6]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Each year, Msimbazi flooding leads to fatalities and destroys critical infrastructure.[^5] Over the past decade, the middle and lower part of the Msimbazi Basin has been experiencing frequent, severe flooding at least twice a year during every rainy season, and in 2019 alone, nine major flood events took place.[^7] The integrated modeling system addresses extreme precipitation and flooding hazards by providing hourly forecasts with sufficient lead time for emergency response and community preparedness actions.[^3]

# Research Report: Downscaled Climate Modeling System with Multi-Scenario Projections

## Solution Overview

The Istanbul Metropolitan Municipality developed a comprehensive climate modeling approach that integrated downscaled global climate models with thirty years of city-specific meteorological data from stations operated by the General Directorate of Meteorology (MGM).[^3][^4][^5] This data-driven methodology supported the municipality's commitment to achieving carbon neutrality and climate resilience by 2050 in alignment with the Paris Agreement.[^2] The technical framework enabled detailed climate projections across multiple emissions scenarios to inform long-term adaptation planning.

## Technical Components

The modeling system utilized downscaled global climate models combined with national data inputs and outputs from MGM-operated meteorological stations, providing three decades of localized climate information for Istanbul.[^1][^3][^6] This approach incorporated four distinct climate scenarios—RCP 2.6, RCP 4.5, RCP 6.0, and RCP 8.5—as defined in the IPCC's 5th Assessment Report published in 2013.[^1] In 2017, the MGM compared results from three different global climate models (HadGEM2-ES, MPIESMMR, and GFDL-ESM2M) to generate forecasts for regional climate changes in Türkiye according to RCP scenarios 4.5 and 8.5 covering the period from 2016 to 2099.[^1] The technical infrastructure integrated historical meteorological observations with advanced climate modeling outputs to produce city-specific projections.

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The climate modeling revealed that across all scenarios, İstanbul is expected to experience higher average temperatures between 1 and 4.5°C per year under RCP 8.5, with a faster temperature increase projected for summer months compared to winter.[^1] These projections directly informed the municipality's strategic goal of transforming Istanbul into a carbon-neutral and climate-resilient city by 2050.[^2]

## Sources

# Research Report: Air Quality Hotspot Forecasting and Early Warning System

## Solution Overview

Mumbai is implementing an air pollution hotspot-based forecasting system designed to provide ward and zonal-level air quality predictions across the metropolitan region.[^4] The Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology (IITM) through its System of Air Quality Forecasting and Research (SAFAR) program serves as the lead agency for developing this Air Quality Forecasting and Early Warning System, with support from the Maharashtra Pollution Control Board (MPCB) and the Municipal Corporation of Greater Mumbai (MCGM).[^4] This approach aims to inform the public and guide emergency response measures through spatially granular air quality forecasts.[^4]

## Technical Components

The Air Quality Early Warning System (AQEWS) integrates multiple data streams including weather forecasts, satellite-based Aerosol Optical Depth (AOD) measurements, and ground-level monitoring from 24 air quality monitoring stations across Mumbai, Mahape, Vasai, Nerul, and Kalyan.[^5] Within Greater Mumbai specifically, the system utilizes 20 air quality monitoring stations at locations including Navy Nagar, Malad West, Borivali East, Deonar, Powai, Mulund West, Bandra Kurla Complex, and Chakala.[^5] IITM has integrated satellite AOD data—which measures the hazy effect aerosols create in polluted air—along with historical data from a network of 420 air quality monitoring stations across India to power these early warning systems.[^5] The forecasting system relies on pinpoint accuracy, starting with precise weather forecasts as a foundation, according to IITM scientist Sachin Ghude.[^5] Mumbai currently operates 28 Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring System (CAAQMS) stations, with five managed by the Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation (BMC), nine by IITM, and 14 by MPCB.[^1] Each CAAQMS station costs ₹9 crore and monitors PM2.5 and PM10 concentrations, as well as levels of carbon monoxide, sulphur dioxide, nitrogen oxides, ammonia, and ozone.[^1] These stations also measure wind direction, wind speed, ambient temperature, relative humidity, solar radiation, and rainfall.[^1] All CAAQMS stations connect to a server, with readings accessible through the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) website or CPCB's Sameer app.[^1] The performance of these early warning systems can be enhanced with Low-Cost Sensor (LCS) devices, which can provide an even more granular picture of air pollution and support hyperlocal mitigation measures within short time frames.[^5]

## Implementation Details

The Mumbai Climate Action Plan targeted implementation of the Air Quality Forecasting and Early Warning System for the short-term period of 2022-2025.[^4] The AQEWS for Mumbai was rolled out during the India Clean Air Summit (ICAS) in 2023, organized by the Centre for Air Pollution Studies (CAPS) at the Center for Study of Science, Technology and Policy (CSTEP) in Bangalore.[^5] The system became accessible through a dedicated web portal at ews.tropmet.res.in/mumbai/index.php.[^5] Since 2022, the number of monitors in Mumbai increased by 50% to 30, providing better ground-level reporting that helps identify local pollution sources.[^2] An additional 15 CAAQMS stations were planned for installation in the Mumbai Metropolitan Region (MMR) before the start of winter, with work orders issued for phased delivery starting the following month.[^6] The expansion plan allocated three additional stations each to Thane, Vasai-Virar, and Kalyan-Dombivali areas, while two stations each were designated for Mira-Bhayander, Ulhasnagar, and Bhiwandi Nizampur areas.[^6] Each of these new stations carries a cost of Rs 9 crore.[^6] Mumbai's Climate Action Plan also proposed expanding the real-time air quality monitoring network to cover all wards of the city by 2025, with MPCB and MCGM as lead agencies and IITM (SAFAR) as supporting agency.[^4] The city aimed to establish a network of 50 real-time air quality monitoring stations by 2025 as part of its climate action plan targets.[^4] The MCAP included developing a high-resolution emissions inventory and source apportionment study for Mumbai in the short-term period of 2022-2025, led by MPCB and the National Environmental Engineering Research Institute (NEERI).[^4] The plan also proposed increasing the number of monitoring stations such as CAAQMS equipped with low-cost indigenous sensors and real-time monitoring technology based on CPCB criterion for site location by 2022-2027.[^8] Currently, Mumbai has 11 MPCB CAAQMS and 9 SAFAR CAAQMS stations installed, with 5 SAFAR CAAQMS stations proposed.[^8] The MPCB operates 12 mobile vans positioned to monitor pollution levels at various sites, which help monitor pollution in hotspots since AQMS cannot be set up overnight.[^9] These mobile vans are stationed near heavy traffic junctions like Wadala and Andheri, as well as near the Deonar and KanjurMarg dumping grounds.[^9] Data from the mobile vans is shared with local ward offices for action against any polluting body.[^9] The BMC currently has only one mobile van for monitoring pollution levels at pollution hotspots or from places where specific complaints are received, which was dysfunctional and being repaired.[^9] The BMC planned to consolidate with each ward and department to assess commitment from each level, after which a comprehensive action plan would be drawn up in conjunction with the requirements of forecasting models.[^7] The broader SAFAR program implementation plan included installation of meso-urban observation networks in major metropolitan cities and development of high-resolution dynamic emission inventories of air pollutants for proposed metropolitan cities.[^3] Technical infrastructure included wind profilers, radiometers, and wind lidar for continuous upper air observations.[^3] The original SAFAR program budget requirement totaled 90.00 crores over the period 2012-13 to 2016-17, with allocations of 25.00 crores in 2012-13, 30.00 crores in 2013-14, 20.00 crores in 2014-15, 10.00 crores in 2015-16, and 5.00 crores in 2016-17.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The forecasting system provides AQI forecasts similar to weather warnings, which Mumbaikars had not previously been able to access.[^5] The forecasts are expressed in anticipated hourly levels of PM2.5 and PM10, and an accompanying map provides users with a spatial and temporal distribution of air pollution across the larger MMR area over the next few days.[^5] MPCB officials stated that before winter, when the city's AQI usually worsens, having more stations in place would enable better monitoring and identification of areas which record poor AQI and allow for mitigation accordingly.[^6] The expanded monitoring network provides better ground-level reporting that helps identify local pollution sources.[^2] The Mumbai Climate Action Plan created a detailed climate profile and vulnerability assessment using satellite imagery, identifying areas and parameters at most risk, ranging from mapping mean surface temperature, vegetation cover, water connection prevalence, to pollutant concentration and flood prone areas.[^7]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The air pollution forecasting system addresses climate adaptation needs by enabling proactive responses to air quality deterioration events that can be exacerbated by climate conditions such as temperature inversions and changing wind patterns. The system's ability to provide ward-level granularity allows municipal authorities to implement targeted emergency response measures in vulnerable neighborhoods before pollution episodes intensify. The integration of meteorological forecasting with air quality modeling supports climate-informed public health protection strategies.

## Business Analysis

The objective of developing SAFAR in major metropolitan cities of India including Delhi, Pune, Chennai, Kolkata, Mumbai, Bangalore, and Hyderabad falls under studies of Atmospheric Chemistry and Climate.[^3] IITM coordinates and leads the effort for the development of the SAFAR system and continues its effort in improving the skill of forecasts.[^3] The deliverable is to develop and dedicate the System of Air Quality Forecasting & Research (SAFAR) to the nation as an operational service in at least 6 metropolitan cities.[^3] The plan of augmenting the number of monitoring stations is part of a larger project where more than 50 monitoring stations are intended for installation across Maharashtra.[^6] At present, only two stations are operational in Thane (Kasarvadavali and Upvan Fort) while there is one each in Bhiwandi (Gokul Nagar), Kalyan (Khadakpada), Ulhasnagar (Siddhi Vinayak Nagar), Virar (Vilanj), and Mira Bhayander (Bhayander West) areas.[^6] The capital-intensive nature of CAAQMS stations at Rs 9 crore each requires sustained government funding commitments.[^1][^6] The operational model relies on multi-agency coordination between IITM, MPCB, and MCGM, with IITM providing technical expertise and forecasting capabilities while state and municipal agencies manage monitoring infrastructure and enforcement actions.[^4]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://healthpolicy-watch.news/despite-1-billion-expenditure-indias-air-quality-is-still-appalling-but-improvements-are-possible/
- [^3]: https://www.moes.gov.in/programmes/metropolitan-air-quality-and-weather-service
- [^4]: https://mumbaifirst.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/MCAP_MumbaiFirst_2022.pdf
- [^5]: https://www.hindustantimes.com/cities/mumbai-news/mumbai-residents-can-minimize-exposure-to-air-pollution-with-new-early-warning-system-developed-by-iitm-101693076968071.html
- [^6]: https://indianexpress.com/article/cities/mumbai/mumbai-metropolitan-region-to-get-15-new-air-quality-monitoring-stations-10130188/
- [^7]: https://citizenmatters.in/the-mumbai-climate-action-plan-awaits-your-suggestions/

# Research Report: Real-Time Storm Warning System with Meteorological Network

## Solution Overview

Buenos Aires developed a Storm Warning System (SAT) within the framework of the Hydraulic Plan to anticipate accurately and in real time the arrival of storms, southeast blows and different extreme climate phenomena.[^4] Through the analysis of big data, this approach generates georeferenced information at the AMBA level regarding extreme meteorological events, with an accuracy of around 4 km and up to 2 hours in advance.[^4] The system allows the city to analyze and evaluate storm forecasts, warns of probable flooding, and improves the management of emergency situations.[^5]

## Technical Components

The SAT is equipped with a network of 34 automatic meteorological stations, of which 10 were already transmitting data at the time of reporting, a radar, a hydrometeorological forecasting model, a GIS database, and a support platform.[^3] All 34 meteorological stations have solar panels and batteries that provide autonomy in case of possible power outages.[^1] The meteorological stations can measure the speed, flow, and water level in the City's streams and the water level in the Río de la Plata.[^1] The meteorological radar is located in the Buenos Aires district of Merlo, which allows it to detect possible storms with a northwest-south direction that most affect the City of Buenos Aires.[^1] The system includes a satellite reception antenna that receives information captured by the American meteorological satellite GOES-R, launched in 2016, which provides storm forecasts, real-time lightning activity mapping, and tornado warnings.[^1] The GOES-E Satellite maps storms, tornados and hurricanes in real time.[^4] The datacenter and hardware and software solutions that integrate the SAT operations center were already installed at the CUCC.[^3] The city installed sensors in over 30,000 storm drains that measure and alert the city if flooding is predicted.[^2] The monitoring system consists of a network of interconnected sensors that measure speed, direction and level of water in the sewage drains and feeds the data to city IT systems that are tracking additional meteorological, hydrological, and environmental parameters, setting off an alarm if flooding is predicted.[^2] The monitoring system measures changes in rainfall, temperature, humidity, wind direction and speed, and stream water level to generate community alerts and inform real-time emergency decisions.[^6] Buenos Aires' stormwater network operation is complemented by hydrometeorological sensors that transmit data to a control center, providing information on main problems in the City's water basins including flow contained in conduits and water level in each one.[^5]

## Implementation Details

Buenos Aires' hydro-meteorological monitoring system has been operational since 2014.[^2] The initial cost of the monitoring system was 200 Million USD.[^2] The system was funded through Grants, Bonds & Loans.[^2] The installation of the meteorological radar in Merlo was being finalized at the time of reporting.[^3] So far, 30 information collection points had been installed, with more sensors under development.[^6] Buenos Aires incorporated 46 sensors to the hydrometeorological network throughout the City to support the Storm Early Warning System.[^5] The Centro Único de Coordinación y Control (CUCC), located in the Chacarita neighborhood, receives all information from the radar, stations, and satellite through fiber optic connections and radio links to avoid cuts and interruptions.[^1] The CUCC integrates the emergency management node where Civil Defense, the Guard of Assistance and Emergencies, Firefighters, City Police, SAME, the Dirección del Cuerpo de Agentes de Control de Tránsito y Transporte and the 108 Immediate Social Assistance line work in a coordinated manner.[^1] The Early Warning Systems for Storms and Heat Waves initiative has a medium-term timeframe and was in progress with new resilient actions as of the strategy publication in September 2018.[^4] The Flood Risk Management Support Project includes implementation of a Comprehensive Hydrometeorological Observation, Surveillance, and Alert System (SIHVIGILA) which will increase the capacity for severe weather forecasting and improve emergency management and recovery.[^9] Studies were carried out jointly by the Ministry of Health, the National Meteorological Service, the National University of Entre Ríos, the National University of La Matanza and the University of Buenos Aires to establish the statistical relationship between extreme temperatures and increased mortality.[^8]

## Benefits and Impacts

The monitoring system enables emergency management by pre-empting social and medical assistance needs, predicting and monitoring weather phenomena, automatically operating pumping stations and floodgates, issuing warnings to key institutions and the public, and sending cleaning crews to compromised drains.[^2][^6] The CUCC transmits alerts to Civil Defense, SAME, Firefighters, and City Police to coordinate necessary measures including traffic alerts, deployment of prevention agents, and intensification of drain cleaning work.[^1] When combined with other weather reports and citizen alerts on social media, the city can now determine in real time which areas need immediate support.[^2] In 2014, Buenos Aires experienced the most rainfall ever registered in the city's history – but unlike in 2013, the city did not flood.[^2] The city saw an uptick in almost all satisfaction indices including the storm water drains index (19 to 56).[^2] The Buenos Aires hydro-meteorological monitoring system protects approximately 6 million people, including residents and daily commuters.[^6] During the warm period from October 2021 to March 2022, 987 daily alerts were issued for extreme heat (615 yellow, 205 orange and 167 red).[^8] During the EWS for Extreme Cold Temperatures test period, from 9 June to 20 September 2021, 239 daily alerts were issued for extreme cold (197 yellow, 37 orange and 5 red).[^8] Under this system the weather alerts are issued by the Argentinian National Meteorological Service, and the Ministry of Health issues recommendations for health care.[^8] The collection of large amounts of information by the SAT system offers a large database of climate and atmospheric data for the City, and this big data will allow updating the City's meteorological models to better guide future hydraulic works.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

In April 2013, Buenos Aires suffered a record rainfall, causing more than $300 million in damages, and the hydro-meteorological monitoring system helps prevent such catastrophes in the future.[^6] Almost 70% of the population of Buenos Aires concentrates in the main basins running across the City (Maldonado, Vega and Medrano streams) and faced with an extreme event, 25% of the basin might be flooded.[^4] CABA's aging population, urban development, and high population density increase both exposure and vulnerability to extreme heat and heavy rainfall hazards.[^7] In the summer of 2013–2014 Argentina experienced record heatwaves and preventable deaths.[^8] The mortality risk during heatwaves increased in 13 of the 18 provinces analysed in Argentina.[^8] Buenos Aires faced blackouts and traffic disruptions affecting hundreds of thousands due to peak energy demand during the extreme heat event.[^7]

## Business Analysis

In 2011, Buenos Aires unveiled a Hydraulic Master Plan intended to combat the increasing frequency of floods and their associated social and economic damage.[^10] The project will upgrade the city's stormwater drainage network to provide protection against 10-year rainfall events.[^10] These projects will benefit about 700,000 people living in the target basins, 10% of whom are in the most critical areas.[^10] Almost 50% of the urban population in Buenos Aires is employed in the informal economy and a large proportion of urban livelihoods are sensitive to climate shocks due to employment disruption and exposure to heat.[^7] Buenos Aires has a real-time Information System that allows the city to anticipate the arrival of different weather phenomena.[^5]

## Sources

- [^1]: http://buenosaires.gob.ar/desarrollo-urbano-y-transporte/noticias/el-sistema-de-alerta-de-tormentas-sat-una-herramienta
- [^2]: https://the-atlas.com/projects/hydro-meteorological-monitoring-system-improves-service
- [^3]: http://buenosaires.gob.ar/desarrollourbano/desarrollo/programas-y-acciones/sistema-hidrometeorologico-de-observacion-vigilancia-y-alerta
- [^4]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Latin America & Caribbean/BuenosAires-Argentina/Buenos-Aires-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^5]: http://buenosaires.gob.ar/climateaction/storm-and-flood-preparedness
- [^6]: https://goexplorer.org/buenos-aires-monitoring-climate-data-for-flood-prevention/
- [^7]: https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/consecutive-extreme-heat-and-flooding-events-in-argentina-highlight-the-risk-of-managing-increasingly-frequent-and-intense-hazards-in-a-warming-climate/
- [^8]: https://heathealth.info/resources/early-warning-system-for-extreme-temperatures-in-argentina/
- [^9]: https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/press-release/2016/06/22/mayor-proteccion-contra-inundaciones-tres-millones-argentinos-banco-mundial
- [^10]: https://www.c40.org/case-studies/cities100-buenos-aires-flood-prevention-in-low-income-communities/

# Research Report: Inclusive Sign Language Early Warning System for Deaf Communities

## Solution Overview

An inclusive early warning system was developed and piloted in Da Nang to address critical gaps in disaster communication for the deaf and hard of hearing (DHH) community.[^1] The approach leverages widely-adopted mobile messaging technology combined with sign language interpretation to ensure that approximately 1,500 DHH individuals residing in the city can receive timely disaster alerts.[^1] This initiative addresses a significant vulnerability, as 85% of DHH respondents rely on family members, friends, or neighbors for warning information, making them highly dependent and vulnerable, especially if they live alone.[^1]

## Technical Components

The core technical infrastructure utilizes the Zalo messaging application, Vietnam's most popular messaging platform, to deliver disaster warnings through dedicated community groups.[^1] The system incorporates multiple communication channels including Zalo groups with sign language videos and SMS alerts to ensure redundant message delivery.[^1] A survey found that 80% of DHH respondents in Da Nang owned a smartphone and 65% had internet access at home, providing the necessary technological foundation for this digital approach.[^1] The Da Nang Association of the Deaf manages the dedicated Zalo group in coordination with the city's disaster risk reduction office, with volunteer sign language interpreters on standby to facilitate communication.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The proposed model was piloted with a group of 50 DHH individuals in Da Nang over a two-month period, which included a simulated typhoon warning drill.[^1] The Da Nang Association of the Deaf managed the Zalo group for the pilot, and a volunteer sign language interpreter was on standby.[^1] This pilot implementation operates within a broader context of disaster management capacity building, as Da Nang has been enhancing the application of digital technologies and data systems to effectively improve disaster response capacity.[^6] The city has gradually transformed digitally, applied science, technology, and databases into reality, especially in releasing forecast, alerts and warnings.[^7] Municipal authorities are also working with the Korean International Cooperation Agency (KOICA) on a complementary project to establish an Integrated Center for Controlling Green and Smart Urban Resilience (ENSURE Center) in Da Nang.[^3][^4] This broader KOICA initiative includes consulting for establishing an urban disaster response system and strategy, building an integrated emergency reporting management system, and building an integrated urban disaster management system.[^2] The KOICA project aims to prepare a rapid disaster response system by establishing a unified disaster report number and a network of related organizations such as the police, fire, public security, and emergency services to strengthen the disaster response capacity of Da Nang City.[^5]

## Benefits and Impacts

The pilot evaluation demonstrated exceptional reach and effectiveness, with 100% of DHH participants receiving the warning during the simulated drill.[^1] Among participants, 88% received the alert from Zalo first while 12% received it from SMS first, demonstrating the multi-channel approach's redundancy.[^1] User preference data revealed overwhelming support for the visual communication method, as 95% of pilot participants preferred Zalo with sign language video as their channel for future warnings, while only 5% preferred SMS.[^1] These results indicate that the technology-enabled approach successfully overcomes previous communication barriers that left DHH individuals dependent on informal networks for life-saving information.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Da Nang is frequently affected by tropical storms originating from the South China Sea between September and December, with Typhoon Noru in 2022 causing widespread flooding and damage in Da Nang and surrounding provinces.[^1] The inclusive early warning system directly addresses the vulnerability of DHH residents to these recurring climate hazards by ensuring they receive timely alerts through accessible communication channels during the critical typhoon season.[^1]

## Business Analysis

The implementation leverages existing widely-adopted consumer technology infrastructure, as the Zalo platform already has high penetration rates among the target population, potentially reducing deployment costs compared to custom-built systems.[^1] The partnership model involves coordination between the Da Nang Association of the Deaf, the city's disaster risk reduction office, and volunteer sign language interpreters, creating a community-based operational structure.[^1] This approach aligns with broader municipal investments in digital disaster management systems, as evidenced by KOICA's official development assistance project supporting the establishment of smart integrated urban disaster management infrastructure.[^2] The KOICA initiative represents a knowledge transfer model that commercializes Daegu City's disaster information system for Da Nang City, Vietnam's sister city, accumulated from social disasters in the past.[^5] Chairman of the People's Committee of Da Nang city Huynh Duc Tho worked with Director of KOICA in Vietnam Kim Jin-oh on November 12, 2019 regarding the disaster risk reduction project.[^4] KOICA Director Kim Jin-oh stated that the ENSURE center will help Da Nang take the lead in managing risks and forecasting natural disasters, noting that Da Nang boasts great potential for smart city development because of its good IT platform and special attention to preventing natural disasters.[^3][^4]

# Research Report: Coastal Storm Evacuation Route Camera Monitoring System

## Solution Overview

The New York City Department of Transportation installed cameras and traffic detection equipment on select coastal storm evacuation routes including Woodhaven Boulevard, Flatbush Avenue, and Hylan Boulevard.[^2][^3][^4] This infrastructure enables multiple city agencies to monitor conditions and coordinate emergency response during coastal storm events. The system provides critical visibility along key evacuation corridors vulnerable to coastal flooding.

## Technical Components

The implementation includes cameras and traffic detection equipment deployed along three major evacuation routes: Woodhaven Boulevard, Flatbush Avenue, and Hylan Boulevard.[^2][^3][^4] NYC Emergency Management, DOT, NYPD, and DEP monitor traffic camera feeds to quickly identify flooding and respond immediately.[^1] The camera network provides real-time visual data that enables rapid assessment of road conditions during storm events.

## Implementation Details

The Department of Transportation installed the monitoring equipment on select coastal storm evacuation routes rather than across the entire evacuation network.[^2][^3][^4] The three instrumented corridors—Woodhaven Boulevard, Flatbush Avenue, and Hylan Boulevard—represent strategically important routes for coastal storm evacuations. Multiple city agencies participate in monitoring operations, including NYC Emergency Management, the Department of Transportation, the New York Police Department, and the Department of Environmental Protection.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The camera system enables NYC Emergency Management, DOT, NYPD, and DEP to quickly identify flooding conditions and respond immediately.[^1] This multi-agency monitoring approach provides coordinated situational awareness during coastal storm events. The traffic detection equipment supports both flood identification and traffic management along critical evacuation corridors.[^2][^3][^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach directly addresses coastal flooding risks during storm events by providing real-time visibility along evacuation routes. The system enables rapid identification of flooded roadways, supporting emergency response decisions and public safety during coastal storms and hurricane events.

## Sources

# Research Report: HAZUR® Urban Resilience Assessment Methodology and Platform

## Solution Overview

The holistic approach for resilience assessment was implemented using the HAZUR® methodology and tool.[^11][^8][^12] This methodology analyses the cascading effects that have collateral impacts on other strategic urban services and the city.[^11] The HAZUR® approach is based on a method and software (as a service) to help city decision makers and urban resilience professionals make fully informed and structured choices to make their cities more resilient analyzing the interdependencies between different city services, monitoring the city and simulating cascade effects in case of impacts that may affect the city.[^1]

## Technical Components

The HAZUR® methodology has been developed by Cetaqua, the Water Technology Centre, in collaboration with UN-Habitat and has been applied in several cities including Barcelona, Bristol, Lisbon, Bogota, Amman and Mexico City.[^2] In the holistic approach, the resilience assessment tool (HAZUR) is used to analyse the relations and the cascading effects among the different urban services.[^10] The RESCCUE project took advantage of the existing HAZUR tool and methodology as the basis of further software developments able to perform the assessment, management and planning of urban resilience in an integrated way.[^10] The approach of the project is holistic and multisectoral and utilizes the HAZUR® tool that enables consideration of urban interdependencies in decision making.[^3] These tools assess urban resilience from a multisectorial approach, for current and future climate change scenarios and including multiple hazards.[^8] The RESCCUE tools allow urban resilience assessment from a multisectorial approach for current and future climate change scenarios, including multiple hazards and cascading effects.[^9] The information generated by sectorial and integrated models was employed to feed a more holistic analysis to provide a general overview of Barcelona's resilience in the case of extreme rainfall events.[^7] A comprehensive flood risk assessment including direct, indirect, tangible and intangible impacts has been performed using cutting edge sectorial and integrated models to analyze the resilience of different urban services (urban drainage, traffic, electric and waste sectors) and their cascade effects.[^7]

## Implementation Details

The HAZUR® tool was used in Barcelona as one of three validation cities for the RESCCUE project.[^1] The HAZUR® workshop in Barcelona was held on the 20th and 21st of June 2017 at the Cetaqua facilities, organized by Cetaqua and UN-Habitat with support of the Barcelona City Council.[^2] The workshop was attended by 25 participants from different organizations, including operators of critical services, the city council, the civil protection agency, the meteorological agency and research centres.[^2] In Barcelona, participants identified 10 hazards including floods, droughts, heat waves, sea level rise, storm surges, wildfires, earthquakes, terrorist attacks, industrial accidents, and cyber-attacks.[^2] Participants identified 10 critical services: water supply, sanitation, energy, transport, telecommunications, health, food supply, waste management, public safety, and emergency services.[^2] The RESCCUE project was led by Aquatec – SUEZ Advanced Solutions and counted with 18 partners including UN-Habitat, Bristol, Barcelona and Lisbon City Councils, utilities such as Endesa, EDP, Aguas de Portugal and Wessex Water, four research centers: Cetaqua, FIC, LNEC and IREC, The University of Exeter and École des Ingénieurs de la Ville de Paris and SMEs such as Opticits, Hidra and UrbanDNA.[^5] Ajuntament de Barcelona (Barcelona City Council) was a partner in the RESCCUE project.[^3] Since 2016, RESCCUE has been developing methodologies and tools to support cities increase their resilience, with Barcelona, Bristol, and Lisbon serving as testing platforms.[^6] The RESCCUE Project is an H2020 research project with four years of implementation.[^9] The RESCCUE project received funding from the European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme under grant agreement No 700174.[^12] The possible approaches were ranked by their cost-efficiency in terms of CAPEX and OPEX to evaluate their benefits potential.[^8]

## Benefits and Impacts

The results of the HAZUR® risk assessment in Barcelona show that the main risks are the impact of floods on water supply and sanitation services, the impact of droughts on water supply service, and the impact of heat waves on energy and health services.[^2] According to the obtained results, Barcelona could suffer a significant increase of socio-economic impacts due to climate change if adaptation measures are not adopted.[^7] The impacts have been geographically distributed showing the specific situation of each district of Barcelona for current and future scenarios, providing information essential for the justification and prioritization of the implementation of adaptation measures.[^7] Key results of RESCCUE include Climate Change Scenarios and Risk Assessment, Climate Change Adaptation Strategies Prioritization Methodology, and Resilience Action Plans (RAP) for the three RESCCUE Cities including Barcelona.[^4] The RESCCUE project aimed to elaborate Resilience Action Plans (RAP) for the research pilot cities with contributions of local project partners and stakeholders.[^3] With the support of UN-Habitat, RESCCUE disseminated results among other cities belonging to major international networks.[^9]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Barcelona faces urban flooding, Combined Sewer Overflow during heavy storm events, drought, heat waves and sea level rise.[^1] Recent climate projections for Barcelona show a relevant increment of the maximum rainfall intensities for the period 2071–2100.[^7] RESCCUE not only assessed urban resilience under current climate scenarios but also provided projections on the future climate at local scale, facilitating thus the evaluation of climate change impacts in urban services.[^5]

## Business Analysis

The RESCCUE project aimed to deliver a framework for assessment, planning and management of urban resilience and develop a portfolio of adaptation strategies to test in selected pilot cities.[^3] RESCCUE aimed to deliver a framework enabling city resilience assessment, planning and management by integrating into software tools new knowledge related to the detailed water-centred modelling of strategic urban services performance into a comprehensive resilience platform.[^8] The project took the water sector as a starting point for the analysis of interconnectedness of urban systems.[^3] The three RESCCUE cities were selected due to their strong involvement with urban resilience as demonstrated by their selection and participation in the 100 Resilient Cities program funded by the Rockefeller Foundation.[^6] The three research sites Barcelona, Bristol and Lisbon are members of the '100 Resilient Cities' organization pioneered by The Rockefeller Foundation.[^5] The validation platform was formed by 3 EU cities (Barcelona, Lisboa and Bristol) that allowed testing the innovative tools developed in the project and disseminating their results among other cities belonging to major international networks.[^8] RESCCUE is an H2020 research project that aimed to help cities become more resilient to physical, social, and economic challenges, using the water sector as the central point of the approach.[^6]

## Sources

- [^3]: https://climate-adapt.eea.europa.eu/en/metadata/projects/resilience-to-cope-with-climate-change-in-urban-areas-2013-a-multisectorial-approach-focusing-on-water

# Research Report: CoRDEES Smart-Grid Energy Management Platform

## Solution Overview

The City of Paris has developed a data-driven approach to optimize energy management in urban districts through the CoRDEES (CoResponsibility in District Energy Efficiency & Sustainability) project.[^1] This initiative represents a comprehensive smart-grid technology implementation designed to address the significant gap between theoretical and actual energy performance in sustainable building developments.[^1] The system operates within Paris's broader climate data strategy, which includes multiple projects aimed at developing new ways to use data in response to climate change-related challenges.[^6]

## Technical Components

The CoRDEES project deployed a Community Energy Management Platform, an interoperable and multi-user system that monitors, consolidates, and analyzes energy data in real time across all buildings (electricity and heat) and public facilities including electric vehicle stations, street lighting, and automated waste collection.[^3] This platform consolidates all energy data from 12 real estate programs, tracking consumption, peak consumption, production, level of autonomy, and storage capacity.[^5] The facilitator uses this data analysis platform with systemic, real-time analysis to improve the coordination of production and demand.[^6] At the end of February 2018, a key implementation step began with the deployment of sensors (instrumentation) in buildings.[^8] By April 2019, the project delivered a BtoC community portal V1, and by June 2019 developed energy services for operators and end-users.[^4] The final version of the Community Energy Management Platform was completed by October 2019.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The CoRDEES project was implemented in the Clichy Batignolles eco-district, a 54-hectare area under construction in Paris.[^4][^3] The European Union's Urban Innovative Actions Initiative awarded the project €4.3 million, with a total ERDF budget of EUR 4,364,796.48 granted.[^1][^4] The partnership included Ville de Paris, Une autre ville (UAV) private company, PARIS BATIGNOLLES AMENAGEMENT (PBA) public/private company, ARMINES research center, and EMBIX private company.[^4][^5] The subsidy contract was signed by the Mayor of Paris in April 2017, followed by a kick-off event and press conference in May 2017.[^4] On May 31, 2017, a hundred people including real estate operators, developers, architects, energy experts, and representatives of the state and city of Paris gathered to publicly launch the project on the 7th floor of lot 7 of the development zone of Clichy Batignolles.[^8] The Parisian and European stakes of the project were presented by Jean Louis Missika (Deputy Mayor of Paris), Jean François Danon (Director of Paris Batignolles Aménagement), and Raffaele Barbato (representative of the UIA program).[^8] On March 24, 2017, the Paris municipality and its stakeholders entered the first step of the program, with 50 professionals from 20 companies gathering.[^8] In June 2017, the project held its first workshop with inhabitants and local associations to involve them in the project and imagine future services.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

The CoRDEES project aimed to reach the energy efficiency objective of 50 kWh/m² and less than 90% of CO2 emissions through its integrated approach.[^3][^4] This community-based model aims to enlist end users to help monitor and manage energy at Clichy-Batignolles to ensure that buildings achieve the carbon dioxide reductions they were designed to.[^1] The approach addresses a critical challenge, as data from the occupied buildings at Clichy-Batignolles suggests significant discrepancy between the theoretical and the actual energy performance, despite the project coming very close to net-zero carbon emissions on paper.[^1] Within Paris's broader data initiatives, the DataCity Paris program helped save up to 10 percent on the city's annual street lighting electricity bill and cut greenhouse gas emissions by analyzing data on street usage to identify off-peak times where the lights can be dimmed.[^2] In the second round of DataCity challenges, the City of Paris specified it wanted solutions to help achieve its target outlined in the Paris Climate Plan to reduce energy consumption in its buildings by 10 percent by 2020 through behavioural action.[^7]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The CoRDEES project addresses climate adaptation challenges by creating a new energy ecosystem designed to manage the expected 30% increase in overall energy consumption by 2030 in Grand Paris Metropole.[^3] This approach directly responds to the need for real-time energy management systems that can bring energy use in line with Paris's ambitious climate targets while accounting for the performance gaps that emerge in occupied sustainable buildings.[^1]

## Business Analysis

The project introduced innovative governance structures including the Urban Energy New Deal, which collectively defines energy commitments and contractual, financial, and regulatory conditions for successful implementation.[^3] By October 2019, standard clauses for new economic conditions and contracting schemes were in place, along with the Urban Energy New Deal and Urban Sustainability Trustee Facilitator.[^4] The project introduced a new actor called the Urban Sustainability Trustee Facilitator to provide technical recommendations, ensure stakeholder cooperation, empower end users, and coordinate the creation and implementation of services.[^4] This platform, led by a neighborhood energy facilitator, tests a new collaborative governance that involves the co-responsibility of actors involved in energy performance.[^5] The project must define a new business model that will ensure the energy facilitator becomes a permanent post.[^6] The CoRDEES approach operates within Paris's broader innovation framework, where DataCity enables the city to experiment in small sections without having to make a large purchase through a co-production model where the city is not the only potential buyer and never the main leader of the project but a co-leader.[^7] DataCity is managed by a special team within NUMA and operates as a meeting point of city government, corporates, start-ups, and technical partners working together throughout a nine-month programme to define urban challenges and develop solutions using data.[^7] The City of Paris became one of the first cities to introduce an open data policy in 2010, making all structured data accessible by open license to promote their reuse and generate new applications.[^7] C40 Cities Climate Leadership Group, a network of 91 of the world's cities committed to tackling climate change, partnered with tech firm NUMA to launch DataCity C40 in cities around the world, with the initiative based on data city programmes in Paris and Casablanca.[^2] Anne Hidalgo, Mayor of Paris and Chair of C40, stated that DataCity was conceived in Paris and every year the project allows the city to move faster on key issues for Paris and other cities around the world.[^2]

## Sources

- [^8]: https://www.uia-initiative.eu/en/news/cordees-officialy-launched

# Research Report: YMCA Community Resilience Centres with Backup Power Systems

## Solution Overview

The YMCA of Greater Toronto developed its own Resiliency Strategy to guide efforts in supporting and enhancing community resilience, applying a climate resilience lens to strategic actions and priorities.[^2][^4] The organization is working to turn its facilities into a network of Community Resilience Centres by installing high-efficiency backup power systems to reduce the carbon footprint of its owned buildings while allowing these locations to remain operational during extended electricity grid power failures.[^1] These Community Resilience Centres allow YMCA facilities to generate their own electricity on-site and continue to support the community even if the electricity grid is compromised in an emergency.[^2][^4]

## Technical Components

The implementation involves installing high-efficiency combined heat and power energy systems at existing and new Centres of Community to allow them to generate their own electricity on-site.[^2] The YMCA integrates other resilience-building and low-carbon microgrid measures including solar panels, battery energy storage systems, electric vehicle chargers, advanced controls, and backup generators where combined heat and power energy systems are not possible.[^2][^4] The organization is installing high-efficiency power generation equipment throughout its portfolio including solar panels and energy storage systems.[^6] IESC's design team completed preliminary engineering work to size major pieces of equipment and worked with Combined Heat and Power and backup Natural Gas generator contractors.[^4] The design team investigated the implementation of microgrid systems at YMCA sites to understand the challenges that might need to be overcome.[^4] IESC developed performance criteria to provide assurance that Engineering Procurement and Construction organizations will deliver a robust and long-term functional system while providing flexibility to ensure costs are as efficient as possible.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The Cooper Koo Family YMCA, located at 461 Cherry Street in the West Don Lands of Toronto, was developed in partnership with Waterfront Toronto, Infrastructure Ontario, and the Province of Ontario.[^1] This LEED Gold building houses a large green roof and a micro-grid including seven electric vehicle charging stations, a battery back-up and a small solar array.[^1] The YMCA is working to install a high efficiency combined heat and power generator along with additional solar panel and battery capacity at the Cooper Koo Family YMCA, with enough capacity to remain operational in a blackout.[^1] Working with its climate strategy consultants, Mantle314, the YMCA received funding from Natural Resources Canada to explore strategies for developing a resilient, low-carbon community energy system in the West Don Lands.[^1] Mantle Developments worked with The YMCA of Greater Toronto to design facilities that can function as community reception centres during climate emergencies.[^5] The YMCA at The David Braley Vaughan Centre in Vaughan and The Steve & Sally Stavro Family YMCA in Toronto achieved LEED Platinum certification in February 2024.[^3] The Steve & Sally Stavro Family YMCA project includes a range of sustainable design strategies and materials including a green roof, bicycle parking, locally-produced low-emission materials with high recycled content, and certified wood.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

Both YMCA centres have been designed with climate resilience in mind and can act as public community reception centres during climate emergencies.[^3] The City of Toronto's Resilience Strategy references the YMCA of Greater Toronto for their work to "increase community resiliency and protect vulnerable populations."[^6] The facilities are designed to remain operational during extended electricity grid power failures, allowing them to continue supporting the community even when the electricity grid is compromised in an emergency.[^1][^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The Community Resilience Centres address climate emergency preparedness by ensuring facilities can function as public community reception centres during climate-related events.[^3][^5] The high-efficiency backup power systems and microgrid infrastructure enable continuous operations during extended electricity grid failures that may result from extreme weather events or climate-related emergencies.[^1][^4]

## Business Analysis

In 2024, the YMCA of Greater Toronto launched a new green building policy to ensure that all new construction and renovation projects are designed and built to the highest sustainability standards.[^8] The organization received funding from Natural Resources Canada to explore strategies for developing a resilient, low-carbon community energy system in the West Don Lands.[^1] The implementation involves partnerships with Waterfront Toronto, Infrastructure Ontario, the Province of Ontario, and climate strategy consultants Mantle314.[^1][^5] The organization's Resiliency Strategy guides the effort to increase community resiliency and protect vulnerable populations through strategic actions and priorities that apply a climate resilience lens.[^2][^4]

# Research Report: Multi-Dimensional Urban Resilience Assessment Framework

## Solution Overview

Huangshi adopted a comprehensive suite of assessment tools provided by 100 Resilient Cities (100RC) to evaluate and strengthen the city's resilience approach across multiple dimensions.[^1][^2][^3] The Resilient Action Inventory, Resilience Perception Assessment, Asset and Risk Tool, and Opportunity Assessment tools were implemented to assess contributions to the 12 dimensions of the urban Resilience Framework.[^4][^5] These tools enabled systematic identification of the most threatening weaknesses, risk factors, and hidden dangers while profiling a wide variety of alternative recovery pathways.[^1]

## Technical Components

The implementation incorporated four distinct assessment instruments provided by 100RC to evaluate Huangshi's resilience approach.[^1][^2][^3] The Resilient Action Inventory tool assessed the city's resilience actions across the 12 dimensions of the urban Resilience Framework.[^4][^5] The Resilience Perception Assessment tool measured stakeholder perceptions of resilience capacity.[^1][^2] The Asset and Risk Tool identified the most threatening weaknesses of risk and hidden dangers within the urban system.[^1] The Opportunity Assessment tool profiled a wide variety of alternative recovery options and resilience-building pathways.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The assessment tools were adopted to systematically evaluate Huangshi's resilience approach across the 12 dimensions of the urban Resilience Framework.[^1][^2][^3] The implementation involved applying the Resilient Action Inventory, Resilience Perception Assessment, Asset and Risk Tool, and Opportunity Assessment tools to assess the contributions of the city's resilience strategies.[^4][^5] This comprehensive assessment approach enabled identification of the most threatening weaknesses of risk and hidden dangers while profiling alternative recovery scenarios.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The assessment tools enabled systematic identification of the most threatening weaknesses of risk and hidden dangers within Huangshi's urban system.[^1] The implementation profiled a wide variety of alternative recovery pathways, providing decision-makers with multiple options for resilience-building interventions.[^1] The tools assessed contributions across all 12 dimensions of the urban Resilience Framework, offering a comprehensive evaluation of the city's resilience approach.[^1][^2][^3]

## Sources

# Research Report: Municipal Analytics Office for Climate Resilience Data Tracking

## Solution Overview

The Office of Performance and Accountability (OPA) was established by executive order on Mayor Mitch Landrieu's first day in office in May 2010 to serve as the city's home for data-driven improvements.[^1][^2] This municipal analytics office supports the work of the Mayor's Office of Resilience and Sustainability through the collection and monitoring of data related to resilience-building projects.[^1][^4] Since initiating its successful STAT programs in 2010, OPA has led a transformation in the improvement, tracking, and reporting of public service delivery, with notable successes in using analytics to reduce fire risk and address blight.[^1][^2]

## Technical Components

The office runs the City's data analytics initiatives, including BlightStat, ResultsNOLA which evaluates the performance of city departments, and NOLAlytics which helps those departments conduct their own data analytics projects to support their missions.[^3] Based on successful models piloted in Baltimore and New York City, the STAT model enables city departments to set goals and track progress, creating a culture of transparency and accountability.[^1] With the help of Richard Todd, a graduate student from Princeton's Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs, OPA developed a toolkit for departmental engagement around data that includes presentation tools, an analytics typology, a website, a form for departments to express interest, and sustained programming of departmental engagement.[^2]

## Implementation Details

After finalizing this typology and accompanying toolkit in the summer of 2016, OPA issued an enterprise-wide call for proposals, supported by an 'office hours' session open to any public employee.[^2] This outreach led to over 20 new project proposals and a new structure for OPA's long-term engagement throughout the city.[^2] In one project, OPA developed a predictive model that identified which parts of the city were most at risk for fires and fire fatalities.[^3] The City used that information to target its campaign to distribute smoke alarms to vulnerable households.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The data collection and monitoring capabilities that OPA provides to the Mayor's Office of Resilience and Sustainability enable evidence-based tracking of resilience-building projects across the city.[^1][^4] This analytical infrastructure supports the systematic evaluation of climate adaptation initiatives and their effectiveness in reducing urban vulnerabilities.

## Sources

- [^2]: https://datasmart.hks.harvard.edu/news/article/effective-analytics-outreach-departments-new-orleans-1017
- [^3]: https://medium.com/what-works-cities-certification/new-orleans-from-blightstat-to-preventing-fire-fatalities-6c245f535de0
- [^4]: https://datasmart.hks.harvard.edu/news/article/how-new-orleans-defied-division-performance-management

# Research Report: UAV LiDAR and Bathymetric Survey for Flood Modeling

## Solution Overview

A comprehensive bathymetric and topographic surveying project was conducted on the Msimbazi River in Dar es Salaam for the World Bank, comparing UAV Photogrammetry and UAV LiDAR technologies for generation of Digital Terrain Models for flood modelling purposes.[^1] This World Bank funded survey project served two purposes: generation of reliable, accurate and fit for purpose bathymetric and topographic data of the Msimbazi River and its floodplains for riverine flooding related detailed studies, and rigorous comparison of Digital Terrain Model generation by Unmanned Aerial Vehicle Photogrammetry and UAV LiDAR.[^3] The Tanzania Urban Resilience Program utilized an innovative combination of Light Detection and Range (LiDAR) UAV surveying and bathometric surveying to support flood modelling within the flood-prone Msimbazi river basin.[^5]

## Technical Components

CDR International in association with Shore Monitoring and Research executed a full survey campaign for the lower basin of the Msimbazi River in February 2019.[^3] The UAV photogrammetry achieved ground point densities of approximately 70 points per square meter across the project area, compared to 57 points per square meter for LiDAR.[^2] The project area covered the lower basin of the Msimbazi River valley at the outfall into the Indian Ocean, where two tributaries (The Sinza River and the Kibangu River) merge with the Msimbazi River.[^2] The data comparison methodology included selection of typical terrain types, performance assessment, point cloud statistics, land use analysis on a basin scale level, and validation with GNSS data for typical terrain types.[^1] The German International Cooperation Agency (GIZ) worked with OpenMap Development Tanzania (OMDTZ) and Dutch knowledge institute Deltares to collect cross-section surveys, extending an existing flood model for the Lower Msimbazi to include a much larger upstream portion of the river.[^6] OMDTZ collected accurate and precise river cross-section surveys of the Msimbazi River using an innovative, low-cost technique developed by the Resilience Academy and OMDTZ, in collaboration with Deltares and with funding from GIZ.[^6] TU Delft student Kirsten van Dongen co-developed a method with OMDTZ to use low-cost, dual-frequency GNSS receiver hardware, and demonstrated that it could deliver similar accuracy and precision as professional-grade survey equipment.[^6] OMDTZ deployed two units that use the real-time kinematic (RTK) mode of the new, low-cost hardware that are able to provide centimeter precise surveys.[^6]

## Implementation Details

The bathymetric and topographic surveying of flood-prone rivers in Dar es Salaam was conducted on the Msimbazi River from February 14th until 20th, 2019.[^2] The Msimbazi Basin project required a new survey of river cross-sections to be carried out as soon as possible, as the 2010 data with limited sections was insufficient for detailed hydrodynamic modeling.[^9] OMDTZ rapidly collected over 80 cross-sections of the Msimbazi River after refining methods and safety precautions during a first field survey with Deltares associates Bobby Russell and Hessel Winsemius.[^6] More than 300 students and locals were trained to use drones, GPS, and mobile apps to collect data in the Msimbazi Basin.[^8] At the time of the report, UAV LiDAR surveys cost approximately 2 to 3 times more than UAV photogrammetry surveys.[^2] A professional-grade set of survey equipment with dual-frequency Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) units or acoustic profiling equipment costs tens of thousands of dollars.[^6] The study recommended on when to use which surveying methods with respect to purpose, project area size, landuse/vegetation cover and challenges like import of equipment for the Sub-Saharan African context.[^3] Preliminary insights of the Msimbazi River UAV survey analysis were presented at the Land and Poverty Conference 2019 in Washington, DC on March 25-29, 2019 and at a workshop for Dar es Salaam stakeholders at World Bank Office in Dar es Salaam on April 11th, 2019.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The OMDTZ survey of river cross-sections and hydraulic structures along the Msimbazi River supported the creation of a comprehensive hydraulic model of the area that led to the development of a more realistic flood hazard map of the most vulnerable areas.[^6] The product of this assignment was the development of a wider flood model that assisted in providing the evidentiary basis for investments in the basin, complementary to the ongoing Tanzania Urban Resilience Program (TURP), implemented by the World Bank.[^6] Outputs were used to guide river upgrades that improved Dar es Salaam's resilience to flooding.[^5] Community-generated data from drones, GPS, and mobile apps now guides the Msimbazi Basin Development Project's design, informing the layout of a new flood-resilient city park, the placement of green infrastructure like terracing and mangrove buffers, and the alignment of upgraded transport corridors.[^8] The usefulness of a model built this way was demonstrated by using the model to investigate the impact of drains blocked by solid waste on flooding.[^11] Based on this study, for monitoring of morphologically dynamic river systems recurrent UAV Photogrammetry surveys are considered sufficiently accurate.[^2][^8] The Government of Tanzania established the Msimbazi Basin Development Project (2022-2028) with a $200 million credit from the World Bank Group's International Development Association (IDA) to strengthen flood resilience and integrate urban development efforts in Dar es Salaam.[^7] The Msimbazi Basin Development Project will help reduce the flood exposure of over 300,000 people, including many low-income communities, while providing access to better infrastructure and services.[^7]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The Msimbazi Basin is home to an estimated 27 percent of Dar es Salaam's population and has historically been a fertile floodplain and the city's main water source.[^6] Over the past decade, the middle and lower part of the Msimbazi Basin has been experiencing frequent, severe flooding at least twice a year during every rainy season, with nine major flood events taking place in 2019 alone.[^7] Each year, Msimbazi flooding leads to fatalities and destroys critical infrastructure.[^6]

## Business Analysis

The bathymetric and topographic surveying project was funded by the World Bank and served as part of the Tanzania Urban Resilience Program.[^3][^4] The German International Cooperation Agency (GIZ) provided funding for the collaborative work with OMDTZ and Deltares to collect cross-section surveys.[^6] The Government of Tanzania established the Msimbazi Basin Development Project (2022-2028) with a $200 million credit from the World Bank Group's International Development Association (IDA).[^7] The innovative low-cost surveying technique developed by the Resilience Academy and OMDTZ provided a cost-effective alternative to professional-grade survey equipment that costs tens of thousands of dollars.[^6] At the time of the report, UAV LiDAR surveys cost approximately 2 to 3 times more than UAV photogrammetry surveys, making photogrammetry a more economical option for monitoring morphologically dynamic river systems.[^2]

## Sources
