# Research Report: District Energy Systems and Microgrid Network

## Solution Overview

Pittsburgh has developed an extensive network of district energy systems and microgrids designed to modernize the city's aging electrical infrastructure with advanced clean energy technologies.[^6] This approach involves multiple interconnected facilities that provide heating, cooling, and backup power to critical facilities including hospitals, universities, government buildings, and commercial properties across several neighborhoods.[^1][^2][^4] The initiative operates under a memorandum of understanding with the U.S. Department of Energy to transform how energy is produced, transported, and consumed throughout the city.[^6][^12]

## Technical Components

The district energy infrastructure comprises multiple interconnected systems with substantial generation capacity. The overall Pittsburgh system operates with 998,000 lbs/hr steam capacity and 32,534 tons chilled water capacity.[^8] The North Shore Energy Center consists of two interconnected steam and chilled-water plants serving 30 buildings and more than six million square feet of space, with a total capacity of 14,234 tons chilled water, 25.5 MMBTU/hr hot water, and 297,000 lbs/hr steam.[^5][^7] The distribution network includes a 13,200-ft steam distribution system and a 13,200-ft chilled water distribution system.[^7] The Allegheny Health Network facility at Wexford Hospital provides all heating, cooling, and primary power needs via steam boilers, hot water boilers, chillers, and a 2.0 MW natural gas generator, along with emergency backup power.[^1][^2] The Allegheny County District Energy System utilizes seven state-of-the-art steam boilers and one domestic water heater to serve six government buildings including the Allegheny County Courthouse and City-County Building.[^1][^2][^11] The Uptown system was designed to operate in island mode during grid outages to ensure continuous power supply to critical facilities like UPMC Mercy during major storms or other emergencies.[^4] The Pittsburgh International Airport Microgrid operates independent of the electric grid through a 21.25 MW natural gas power plant, making it one of the most resilient airports.[^2] The distribution piping system consists of a network of underground pipes installed in a combination of direct-buried and tunnel applications to deliver steam, hot water, and chilled water to customers.[^4] The thermal energy storage tank stores chilled water produced during off-peak hours when electricity rates are lower, which can then be used to meet cooling demand during peak hours.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The NRG Uptown EcoDistrict project, which broke ground on March 7, 2017, involved a construction cost of $50,000,000.[^4][^5] The central plant was located on property owned by UPMC Mercy, adjacent to the existing Mercy Hospital boiler plant.[^4] The project represented a partnership between NRG Energy, the City of Pittsburgh, the Urban Redevelopment Authority of Pittsburgh, Allegheny County, UPMC Mercy, and Duquesne University.[^4] Cordia announced the financial closing on three microgrids and district energy systems in Allegheny County from Essential Utilities, Inc., with the acquisition closing at $165 million on January 30, 2024.[^1][^2] The strategic partnership created an arrangement that interconnected Duquesne University's district energy system to Cordia's existing Uptown district energy system.[^3] Cordia acquired Duquesne University's on-campus energy system and leveraged excess capacity at the Gibbon Energy Plant, the first combined heat and power plant in western Pennsylvania.[^11] The plant generates 80 percent of the campus' electricity needs, approximately 5 MW, using combined heat and power technology.[^10] The system has a total capacity of 181,000 lbs/hr steam, 6,150 tons chilled water, 6,000 tons ice storage, and 5.5 MW electricity generation.[^10] Effective July 1, 2023, Cordia began offering steam heat services to buildings in Pittsburgh's central business district in addition to the Northshore and Uptown neighborhoods it currently serves.[^9] The seamless transfer of district steam services from PACT to Cordia advanced the city's climate goals and ensured reliable, economical energy for downtown businesses.[^3] Distribution for the Downtown Pittsburgh District Energy system is owned and maintained by Duquesne University.[^11] In 2015, Mayor Peduto signed a Memorandum of Understanding with the National Energy Technology Laboratory on behalf of the U.S. Department of Energy on joint efforts to design 21st Century energy infrastructure for Pittsburgh.[^6]

## Benefits and Impacts

The North Shore system has achieved a 99.99% reliability rating, consistently delivering dependable energy while maintaining a strong presence in Pittsburgh's North Side community.[^7] The interconnection between Duquesne University and the Uptown system enhanced the efficiency, reliability, and resiliency of both systems while reducing their environmental impact.[^10] The integration increased efficiencies for both current and future clients through the ability to prioritize operation of the most environmentally and cost-efficient equipment at either facility to meet customer loads.[^10] The connection made operating both facilities more efficient and reduced risk through redundancy if either system is required to temporarily cease operations for maintenance or other reasons.[^3] The new energy center delivers steam, chilled water, and backup power to UPMC Mercy and additional future customers with higher efficiency, lower carbon emissions, and lower capital and operating costs compared to multiple, stand-alone systems.[^5] Cordia's upgrades expect substantial water conservation through condensate return, recycling and reusing water in a way that has not been implemented in downtown Pittsburgh before.[^9] The plant can also isolate from the grid if needed, allowing the campus increased energy self-sufficiency.[^10] Cordia's modernization efforts result in a more resilient, efficient energy system for downtown Pittsburgh.[^9] Cordia Pittsburgh won the 2025 IDEA System of the Year Award from the International District Energy Association.[^8]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Temporary power outages are common in the city and are likely to increase as the energy grid is strained during extreme weather events.[^6] The district energy systems address this vulnerability by providing backup power and the ability to operate independently from the main grid during outages, ensuring critical facilities maintain continuous operations during major storms or other emergencies.[^4] The modernization of Pittsburgh's aging electrical grid with 21st century clean technologies enhances the city's overall energy resilience while reducing environmental impacts.[^6]

## Business Analysis

Cordia has provided reliable heating, cooling, and electricity to Pittsburgh customers for over two decades, supporting major hospitals, universities, and commercial buildings with resilient, low-carbon energy.[^8] The North Shore Energy Center serves 30 buildings and more than six million square feet of space, with customers including PNC Park, The Carnegie Science Center, and Allegheny General Hospital.[^5] The Uptown system provides steam, hot water, and chilled water to customers including UPMC Mercy, Duquesne University, and other commercial and residential buildings in the Uptown Corridor, with UPMC Mercy serving as the first and anchor customer.[^4][^5] The outcomes of the MOU with National Energy Technology Lab help modernize delivery of utility services through new business models and markets, grow technology research and development opportunities and product manufacturing, reduce environmental impacts, enhance resilience and security through integrated district-based microgrid solutions, address affordability for consumers, and encourage workforce development.[^6] Technologies developed at the GRID Institute seek to be deployed alongside the City of Pittsburgh in a manner that builds off of the District Energy Initiative's current progress.[^12] The new power laboratory will itself be a significant utility distribution level microgrid, where new technologies can not only be developed, but demonstrated in early stage applications and tested/certified for accelerated field deployments.[^12] This transformation relies on 21st Century Energy Infrastructure designs, which leverage advanced technology and design techniques to modernize energy infrastructure, create new business models and markets, and expand technology research and development opportunities.[^12]

## Sources

# Research Report: Seoul Safety App: Real-Time Disaster Information and Emergency Response

## Solution Overview

In 2018, Seoul launched a safety portal that provided real-time disaster information and began the Seoul Safety App service.[^8][^6][^9] This digital infrastructure represents a comprehensive approach to disaster preparedness and emergency response, integrating multiple technological systems to protect residents from various hazards including floods, fires, and other emergencies. The platform has evolved to incorporate advanced data analytics, artificial intelligence, and mobile technologies to deliver timely warnings and coordinate emergency services across the metropolitan area.

## Technical Components

The technical architecture encompasses multiple interconnected systems designed to collect, analyze, and disseminate disaster information. In 2023, Seoul created a cutting-edge digital platform with big data and AI for disaster response that provides disaster response support and digital operation control function where various disaster information is integrated.[^1] The platform integrates and manages diverse administrative information owned by the Seoul Fire Headquarters, analyzing the information with AI and big data to deliver advanced digital information from the 119 reporting to on-site response stages.[^1] The city developed a comprehensive flood forecasting system and an early warning network to better prepare for increasingly frequent and intense rainfall events.[^2] Starting in 2023, Seoul became the first in the country to operate a flooding forecast and warning system designed to alarm and guide citizens in advance so that they may acknowledge the situation and evacuate.[^10] The Seoul intelligent disaster prevention and safety system is based on the population density data collected by security cameras.[^6] The technology includes an intelligent people counting and crowd detection system that automatically detects crowd density and informs of signs of risk.[^6] The Republic of Korea has been providing information to the public through wireless emergency alerts using mobile phones.[^3] In Korea, the WEA service based on cell broadcast service technology has been implemented since 2005, and local governments and central authorities have had the power to send such messages since 2017.[^3] They send warning messages to individual cell phones connected to the base stations of mobile service providers in a designated area.[^3]

## Implementation Details

The implementation unfolded through multiple phases beginning with pilot programs in 2017. The Ansimi app began as a pilot service in 2017 before its implementation across all of Seoul in October 2018.[^5] In May 2017, four autonomous districts of Eunpyeong-gu, Seodaemun-gu, Seongdong-gu and Dongjak-gu first adopted the Anshimi program, and it was extended to all 25 districts with full service starting from October 25, 2018, following one year and five months of preparation and improvement period.[^7] Anshimi uses the control tower operated by each autonomous district to monitor risk situations in real time by linking a total of 39,463 CCTVs installed in Seoul for crime prevention as of the end of June 2018 with smartphone applications.[^7] In 2022, Seoul's goal was to create Seoul 119 big data center that manages 119 dispatches and fire administration information, make a 3D map of disaster-prone facilities including nursing homes, and ensure firefighters can use it when rescuing lives.[^1] The Seoul 119 big data center makes an infrastructure integrating information about 119 calls and dispatches, field information, and disaster data from related agencies, and serves as a public data provider via big data platforms of the National Fire Agency and the SMG.[^1] Seoul launched the central disaster safety and countermeasure headquarters on May 15, 2023, which operated around-the-clock until October 15, 2023.[^10] The SMG plans to develop an algorithm that guides paths for disaster prevention patrol by analyzing each region's time slots where disasters are most likely to take place.[^1] The algorithm for the preventive patrol paths generates big data encompassing fire, rescue, first aid, life safety, and dispatch data accumulated over the past two decades.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The system has demonstrated substantial adoption and operational effectiveness since deployment. The Anshimi application recorded 16,483 downloads during the pilot period from May 2017 to September 2018, with 3,614 people joining the service and using it 7,055 times as of September 2018.[^7] As of September 2023, the Ansimi app had 224,604 total downloads and 211,481 recorded cases of app service use.[^5] When using Ansimi in emergency situations, the app automatically alerts the relevant district CCTV control center, and a police patrol is dispatched to the area by the control center.[^5] The Anshimi application has photo taking and video shooting functions, which means even situations in the blind spot of the CCTVs can be transmitted to the integrated control center through images or videos to ensure a quick response.[^7] Starting in November 2023, users of the Ansimi app became able to view the locations of more than 23,000 emergency call booths.[^5] Emergency Ready App provides multilingual support in English, Chinese, and Japanese for 36 services, including disaster alerts, safety guide, and civil defense shelter locations.[^4] The Korean central authorities and local governments issued warning messages about various hazards, including typhoons, fires, fine dust, and infectious diseases.[^3] Since 2020, WEA messages increased dramatically due to COVID-19, amounting to nearly 50 times more than the previous year.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This integrated digital infrastructure directly addresses climate-related hazards through specialized forecasting and early warning capabilities. The comprehensive flood forecasting system and early warning network specifically target increasingly frequent and intense rainfall events that pose growing risks to urban populations.[^2] The flooding forecast and warning system operates to alarm and guide citizens in advance so that they may acknowledge the situation and evacuate when flooding is highly likely due to downpours.[^10]

## Sources

# Research Report: Satellite-Enabled Air Pollution Monitoring for Public Health

## Solution Overview

The Urban Healthy Living pilot was funded by the UK Space Agency's Space for Smarter Government Programme, which adapts satellite-enabled air pollution monitoring to support public health programmes and healthcare delivery in an urban setting.[^3][^4][^5] Belfast is part of a joint project funded by the UK Space Agency's Space for Smarter Government that's using satellite technology to help planners and residents better understand urban air pollution.[^2] The project demonstrated the use of a space-enabled technology for traffic related air pollution monitoring and aimed to spur innovative interventions in healthy living and disease prevention.[^3]

## Technical Components

Working in both Belfast and the English town of Bicester, the team uses machine vision cameras and air quality sensors to gather traffic and air quality data.[^2] The project focuses on levels of nitrogen dioxide, PM2.5 and PM10.[^2] Using a blend of technology, the project produced air pollution models that were visualised on 3D maps, making complex information accessible.[^3] In Belfast this monitoring occurs along the Westlink, Albert Bridge and Ormeau Road.[^2] Dowds Group were asked to provide a monitoring strategy for the proposed air quality monitoring at Belfast Transport Hub.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The project is being delivered by a collaborative network of SMEs that includes Geospatial insight, Earthsense, 3DEO and Redshift.[^2] Facilitated by Professor Alan Wells, the project also includes NI Government Departments, health officials and council staff, including Belfast's air quality officers and Smart Belfast consultants.[^2] Working with clinicians from the Belfast Health Trust, the team studies the relationship between local air quality and respiratory and circulatory diseases, and with Type II diabetes.[^2] The team examines the impact of the 'school run' on local air quality particularly along walking, cycling and bus routes.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

The initiative prototyped a routing map that could allow clinical patients to better self-manage their respiratory conditions by selecting the routes and modes of transport that limit their exposure to pollutants.[^3] Perhaps using a phone app, the project allows residents to make more informed choices about cycling and walking routes.[^2] The project's research led to some important conclusions which are highly relevant for policy makers and city planners; that air pollution hovers long after traffic dissipates, and that weather is a determining factor.[^3] Those with COPD and asthma are much more severely affected by air pollution levels, and air pollution is increasingly linked with non-respiratory diseases including diabetes and reduced cognitive function.[^3]

## Sources

# Research Report: Advanced Electrochemical Desalination & Biomimetic Membrane Technology

## Solution Overview

Singapore's national water agency PUB is advancing electrochemical deionisation and biomimetic membrane technologies to dramatically reduce the energy intensity of seawater desalination.[^1][^2] Traditional desalination methods currently use about 3.5 kilowatt-hour (kWh) of electricity per cubic metre of water because high pressure is needed during the reverse osmosis process to push water through the polymeric membranes to filter out salts.[^3] These innovative approaches draw inspiration from natural systems—mangrove trees and aquaporin proteins found in living organisms—that have evolved over millions of years to extract freshwater from seawater using negligible amounts of energy.[^4][^5]

## Technical Components

The electrochemical deionisation (EDI) technology uses an electric field to remove dissolved salts from water, with the potential to be more energy-efficient than reverse osmosis, especially for water with low salinity.[^6] Evoqua's low-pressure electrodialysis membrane technology enables water output quality to be tuned by power adjustments to minimize footprint and energy consumption.[^7] Biomimetic membranes are inspired by nature and mimic the biological membranes in living cells to transport water, with the potential to be more permeable and require less energy.[^8] PUB is working with the Nanyang Environment And Water Research Institute (Newri) at Nanyang Technological University (NTU) to develop membranes which are enhanced with aquaporin proteins or synthetic biomolecules that help to improve their permeability.[^9] Aquaporins are proteins found in living organisms that enhance the transfer of water through a cell membrane, while synthetic biomolecules are a new innovation that replicate the effect of these aquaporins at a lower cost.[^10] The principle of such applications is adopted from plant and animal life which have been extracting sea water for their survival evolved over millions of years, using negligible amounts of energy.[^11] Mangrove trees, which grow in seawater, have the unique ability to filter out high concentrations of salt from the water they take in and can also transport water from their roots up to the rest of the plant with minimal resistance.[^12] Developing membranes that mimic these abilities can save a significant amount of energy because lower operation pressure would be required to operate the desalting process.[^13]

## Implementation Details

Research and development of the electrochemical desalination technology began in 2008 when Evoqua, then Siemens Water Technologies, won a global competition for a three-year matching grant funded by the Singapore Environmental & Water Industry Programme Office.[^14] Back in 2008, Siemens won a grant from Singapore's Environment & Water Industry Programme Office to build a demonstration unit that could desalinate seawater to drinking water quality, using just 1.5 kWh of power for each cubic metre of water that it produced.[^15] Since December 2010, the Siemens demonstration unit has been treating 50 m³ of seawater per day at a PUB facility in Singapore.[^16] The unit is successfully producing drinking water from seawater using approximately 50% of the energy required by the most efficient desalination technology available today.[^17] EDI desalination modules have been used at a demonstration plant in Tuas since March and are able to treat 3,800 cubic metres of sea water per day.[^18] The rate of energy consumption of the Tuas EDI demonstration plant is 2.4kwh per cubic metre of water treated, according to PUB's assistant chief executive Harry Seah.[^19] PUB's assistant chief executive Harry Seah hopes to reduce the rate of energy consumption of desalination at the Tuas demonstration plant to 1.8kwh per cubic metre of sea water by next year, down from 2.4kwh per cubic metre.[^20] PUB said it aims to scale up its operations at the Tuas demonstration plant to 10,000 cubic metres of sea water per day by next year.[^21]

Singapore's national water agency PUB and Evoqua Water Technologies agreed to a memorandum of understanding (MOU) to further develop electrochemical desalination technology that aims to improve energy and cost efficiencies in seawater desalination.[^22] Evoqua has been presently engaged in a demonstration with PUB of initial feasibility of advanced electrochemical desalination technology.[^23] PUB and Evoqua will explore the design, construction, installation and evaluation of a system comprising Evoqua E-Desal equipment to produce drinking water from seawater with an eventual capacity of 1 million gallons per day (mgd).[^24] PUB and Evoqua will commission the initial phase of the demo project by December 31, 2014.[^25] The production costs of the EDI technology have been reduced to a fraction of its original cost from half a million dollars to about $40,000, according to Dr Qiao Xiangyi, Evoqua's research and development manager.[^26]

PUB is trialling new technology including ceramic membranes and biomimetic membranes at PUB's research and development plant in Tuas to see if these are more durable, cost-efficient and energy-saving compared with the usual polymeric membranes used in desalination.[^27] Biomimetic membranes developed by Newri are able to achieve at least a 50 per cent enhancement in water permeability compared with commercial ones for seawater desalination.[^28] A 10 m³/day pilot to scale up Newri's aquaporin-based biomimetic membranes is currently being constructed at the Tuas R&D Facility and will be ready in Sept this year, with the pilot lasting for six months.[^29] A new desalination integrated validation plant will be located in Pasir Ris by next year and will integrate, configure and optimise technologies including the ceramic and biomimetic membranes to validate if lower energy consumption and better performance can be achieved for desalination.[^30]

Biomimetic membranes have also been used in the production of Newater and are currently being piloted at the Ulu Pandan Newater plant at a capacity of 14 m³/day.[^31] Aquaporin completed the Living Lab Project to develop the world's first biomimetic low-energy Aquaporin Inside® CLEAR membrane at demonstration scale, with membranes installed at the Kranji NEWater Factory (KNF), operated by PUB, Singapore's National Water Agency.[^32] The Aquaporin CLEAR membranes will continue to be operated and monitored at KNF, while delivering NEWater standard permeate into PUB's distribution network.[^33] The Environment and Water Industry Programme Office (EWI) is investing $470million from Singapore's National Research Foundation in water R&D.[^34]

## Benefits and Impacts

In 2009, PUB and Evoqua Water had shown using proof-of-concept EDI models that they could achieve an energy consumption rate of 1.65kwh per cubic metre of sea water.[^35] After 12 months of continuous operation benchmarked against parallel concurrently operated trains with other commercial membranes, the Aquaporin Inside® CLEAR biomimetic membranes achieved up to 20% energy savings for the energy-intensive reverse osmosis treatment stage, while consistently meeting the stringent water quality standards of NEWater.[^36] Following the successful 12-month demonstration, Aquaporin will work with PUB to explore further collaboration opportunities to expand the use of low-energy membranes to other NEWater factories.[^37] PUB aims to reduce the system-level energy consumption of desalination to less than 2kWh per cubic metre.[^38] Singapore is hoping to reduce current energy requirements for membrane desalination from 3.5kWh down to 0.8kWh/m³.[^39] With further breakthroughs in research and development, the long-term target set by Singapore is to reduce desalination energy consumption to 0.8kWh/m³.[^40]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Currently, up to 30 per cent of Singapore's water needs is desalinated by reverse osmosis, a process which uses about 3.5 kilowatt hours (kwh) to produce 1 cubic metre of desalinated water.[^41] PUB is part of Singapore's GreenGov.SG movement that aims for the public sector to achieve net zero emissions around 2045, which is earlier than the national target of 2050.[^42] PUB, Singapore's National Water Agency, is leading research and development for the water sector in Singapore to improve energy efficiency and reduce the carbon footprint of water treatment processes.[^43]

## Business Analysis

Singapore is exploring the use of ultra-permeable membranes, blue energy (e.g. pressure-retarded osmosis) and ceramic membranes to reduce energy consumption in the desalination process.[^44] One of the technologies which PUB is looking into to achieve that target is the use of Biomimetic membranes/Biomimicry in desalination plants.[^45] The Environment and Water Industry Programme Office (EWI), which spearheads the growth of Singapore's water industry, is investing $470million from Singapore's National Research Foundation in water R&D to promote research and development in this field.[^46] The production costs of the EDI technology have been reduced to a fraction of its original cost from half a million dollars to about $40,000, demonstrating significant progress in commercial viability.[^47]

# Research Report: Hazard & Risk Explorer: Multi-Hazard Climate Information Platform

## Solution Overview

The City of Vancouver launched the Hazard & Risk Explorer in 2024, an interactive online platform designed to improve public access to information on climate hazards through a multi-hazard and risk story map.[^1][^2] This initiative addresses the absence of an accessible way for the City to share multi-hazard information that would help communities understand risk and prepare for impacts.[^2] The platform was identified as an action in the Resilient Vancouver Strategy to improve public awareness of climate hazards, with implementation led by the Vancouver Emergency Management Agency (VEMA) and a target completion date of 2025.[^2]

## Technical Components

The Hazard & Risk Explorer enables users to explore four key hazards: earthquake, extreme heat, wildfire smoke, and coastal flooding.[^1] The platform contains spatial data indicating inequitable distribution of risk across the City, allowing users to navigate through each hazard to learn about the risks, how to prepare, and steps the City is taking to mitigate and prepare.[^1][^2] This system builds on the work of Vancouver's Climate Change Adaptation Strategy, which provides the City's overall approach to prepare Vancouver for five climate hazards including extreme heat, poor air quality, drought, extreme rainfall, and sea level rise.[^3] The platform's development was informed by a Hazard, Risk, and Vulnerability Analysis (HRVA) completed in 2024, a high-level study of the hazards that may impact Vancouver and the potential consequences to people, property, environment, economy, and critical infrastructure.[^5]

## Implementation Details

The initiative was led by VEMA with a target completion date of 2025.[^2] A comparable implementation in Metro Vancouver's North Shore region demonstrates the technical scope of such projects, where Resilience Mapping Canada developed a disaster and climate risk profile story map for eight hazards between August 2023 and July 2024 for North Shore Emergency Management and Partner Municipalities and First Nations.[^4] That project included an introductory video integrated into the land acknowledgement, 16 maps of hazard exposure and historic events, many figures and photographs highlighting sections of the story's narrative, and information and links to local alerts and notifications, household preparedness guides, and evacuation guide.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

The Hazard & Risk Explorer helps users create a plan to protect those who are disproportionately impacted, prepare their home and gather supplies for each hazard, and find cleaner air spaces, cooling centres and other City resources.[^3] The platform has potential to improve equity via access to information, though care must be taken to ensure information cannot be used for harm such as blue-lining.[^2] To enhance accessibility, Vancouver provides printable hazard fact sheets available in 12 languages including English, French, Traditional Chinese, Simplified Chinese, Tagalog, Punjabi, Vietnamese, Farsi, Spanish, Korean, Portuguese, and Japanese.[^5]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach directly addresses Vancouver's experience with climate hazards, including the devastating heat dome in 2021 followed by atmospheric rivers, which demonstrated the urgent need to prepare the city for a changing climate.[^6] The platform specifically targets earthquake, extreme heat, wildfire smoke, and coastal flooding hazards, while building on broader adaptation planning for extreme heat, poor air quality, drought, extreme rainfall, and sea level rise.[^1][^3]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://retooling.ca/resources/city-of-vancouvers-hazard-risk-explorer/
- [^2]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Vancouver-CA/vancouver-climate-change-adaptation-strategy-2024-25.pdf
- [^3]: https://vancouver.ca/images/web/one-day-enews-nov-2024.html
- [^4]: https://resiliencemapping.ca/northshore-storymap
- [^5]: https://vancouver.ca/home-property-development/hazards-that-could-affect-our-city.aspx
- [^6]: https://vancouver.ca/files/cov/vancouver-climate-change-adaptation-strategy-2024-25.pdf

# Research Report: Multifunctional Park-and-Ride Energy and Water Management Hubs

## Solution Overview

Warsaw has developed an integrated approach to transforming parking facilities into multifunctional energy hubs that combine renewable power generation, electric vehicle infrastructure, and sustainable water management.[^1] The eco-park-and-ride facility at Połczyńska incorporates permeable materials, photovoltaic systems, and nature-based water retention systems as part of this comprehensive retrofit strategy.[^1] This initiative serves as a testbed for future retrofitting plans at all other 15 Park+Rides already operational in Warsaw, as well as at upcoming sites in the city.[^3]

## Technical Components

Parking facilities certified under the Warsaw Green Building Standard (WGBS) now use permeable paving materials and integrate nature-based retention solutions, such as 'dry rivers' or rain gardens, alongside photovoltaic panels and heat pumps.[^1] The Połczyńska Park+Ride project specifically includes installing photovoltaic modules producing clean electricity up to 192 kWp in total.[^3] The system incorporates four charging points for electric vehicles, each with 22 kW capacity, powered by means of two stations with the power of 2×22 kW each.[^3][^8] The charging stations feature Type 2 sockets, LED panels for device operation and signals of the charging process and its completion, and charging process protection from unauthorized interference such as plug removal, plug connection to another vehicle, or switching the charging process off.[^8] The project includes constructing an energy bank for storage of electricity, which is critical for better balancing between the supply and consumption of electricity since solar energy is not available 24/7.[^3] An integrated energy management system connects all installations on-site to ensure that energy is not wasted, but rather utilized in the most efficient manner possible.[^3] The clean renewable energy produced by the site's PV modules assists in powering EV chargers as well as other on-site installations.[^3]

## Implementation Details

The comprehensive retrofit of the Połczyńska Park+Ride site integrated low-carbon transportation, clean renewable energy, sustainable water management, and green infrastructure.[^3] The retrofitting was completed in 2022, with initial investments of US$ 13,200 in 2020 for preparatory works, US$ 263,250 in 2021, and US$ 697,600 in 2022, totaling a budget of US$ 974,050.[^3] The Public Transport Authority (ZTM) announced a tender for energy audit, concept, and feasibility study of a project concerning nine P+R parking lots to determine how to power them with green energy, with plans to seek co-financing through EU funds.[^4] The first four electric vehicle chargers were made available on a P+R car park in Warsaw in December 2017.[^8] New charging stations were installed gradually at Park & Ride facilities, with the first one placed in the Metro Młociny I P&R facility, which houses two stations where four electric cars can be charged simultaneously.[^5][^7] The Warsaw City Council adopted new Regulations for the Park & Ride Facilities that include free charging for electric cars, free parking spaces for bicycles, mopeds and motorcycles, and Eco Cards for electric vehicle users.[^7] The chargers can be used based on a dedicated Eco Card that enables electrical vehicle owners to leave their vehicles free of charge on P+R car parks outside the opening hours of those facilities and to charge them for free.[^8] The cards are issued on a first-come first-served basis, with night charging available only with Eco cards.[^5][^7] The Public Transport Authority plans to provide other electrical vehicle chargers in other car park facilities in the foreseeable future.[^8]

## Benefits and Impacts

The project reduced CO2 emissions by 33 tonnes per year starting in 2022.[^3] Financial savings are expected to reach around US$ 10,000 per annum, including income from charging fees for electric vehicles that is under consideration.[^3] The Białołęka Library, designed with solar panels, natural lighting, and spaces shaped by greenery, became the first municipal building to be certified under the Warsaw Green Building Standard (WGBS).[^1] Warsaw selected building retrofit and e-mobility as their first priorities for replication strategy, since it stayed in the scope of one department.[^2] The city aims through its plan to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by 40% by 2030 and achieve yearly savings.[^9]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The integration of permeable paving materials and nature-based retention solutions such as 'dry rivers' or rain gardens addresses stormwater management challenges associated with extreme precipitation events.[^1] These green infrastructure elements work alongside renewable energy systems to create climate-resilient parking facilities that manage both energy and water resources sustainably.[^1][^3]

## Business Analysis

Warsaw's Green Vision roadmap outlines smart energy solutions such as Vehicle to Grid (V2G) and Vehicle to Building (V2B) to connect electric cars to the power grid during electricity peak times.[^6][^9] Electric vehicles and charging points feature prominently in the city's plans to improve its urban environment, with smart lampposts and a new energy management system planned as part of smart city initiatives.[^2] The city plans to build a photovoltaic farm and install photovoltaic panels on buildings as part of its broader Green Vision plan.[^6][^9] The municipal financing model for the Połczyńska project involved phased investments over three years, with the total budget of nearly US$ 1 million supporting the integration of multiple climate adaptation technologies.[^3] The Public Transport Authority sought to leverage EU funds for the modernization of parking lots, demonstrating a public-private financing approach to scaling the initiative across multiple facilities.[^4]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://netzerocities.eu/2025/09/16/building-for-the-future-how-warsaws-green-building-standard-is-changing-the-city/
- [^2]: https://sharingcities.eu/city-warsaw/
- [^3]: https://www.c40.org/case-studies/polczynska-park-and-ride/
- [^4]: https://www.emta.com/news/parking-lots-powered-by-green-energy-in-warsaw/
- [^5]: https://warszawa19115.pl/en/-/eco-friendly-p-r-facilities
- [^6]: https://www.arup.com/projects/warsaw-green-city-climate-and-action-plan/
- [^7]: https://en.um.warszawa.pl/-/eco-friendly-p-amp-r-facilities
- [^8]: https://www.emta.com/news/first-electric-vehicle-chargers-installed-at-a-pr-car-park-in-warsaw/

# Research Report: Fiber Optic Network Expansion for Digital Equity and Connectivity

## Solution Overview

The City of Los Angeles is partnering with internet providers to expand Wi-Fi access to all residents, with particular emphasis on vulnerable populations.[^2][^4] This approach addresses the digital divide affecting 15% of Angelenos who live in households without broadband subscriptions, a gap that disproportionately impacts low-income households and individuals who identify as Black or Latino.[^6] The Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors unanimously passed a series of motions beginning in 2021 to address the hundreds of thousands of households in the County estimated to be without home internet service.[^5]

## Technical Components

The Bureau of Street Lighting installed 1.5 miles of underground fiber optics cable stretching from Leimert Park Plaza to the Hyde Park Metro Station as part of the Crenshaw Community Connectivity Pilot.[^3][^8] Building on existing city-owned street lighting assets, the Bureau worked with Cisco to deploy cloud-managed outdoor access points and Cisco Ultra-Reliable Wireless Backhaul (URWB), enabling free Wi-Fi at public spaces along the corridor.[^3][^6] The technology uses fiber communications infrastructure and wireless backhaul technology provided by Cisco, with Presidio serving as the technology integrator.[^9][^6] The Bureau is using a small portion of the total capacity of this fiber optics infrastructure to pilot one year of free public WiFi at public spaces along the corridor.[^8] The Bureau's infrastructure, including electrical power and light poles, provides a foundation on which the city is adding other services, including broadband internet infrastructure.[^7] The Wi-Fi infrastructure was designed as a platform to support future smart cities use cases, such as data-driven traffic management, increased pedestrian and public safety, improved emergency response and environmental sensing technology.[^3]

## Implementation Details

In 2023, the Bureau of Street Lighting launched a project to extend broadband internet access to underserved and unserved communities in the city.[^7] The Bureau worked directly with the Digital Inclusion unit to prepare and launch infrastructure pilots funded through the American Rescue Plan Act (ARPA), aiming to bring digital equity to Los Angeles.[^7] In partnership with the Los Angeles Department of Water and Power, the Bureau is actively working to bridge the digital divide utilizing Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) funds to develop the infrastructure needed to provide basic internet access.[^2] The project objectives include providing 300 public Wi-Fi access points in the highest need communities, improving equitable and affordable access from telecommunications companies for all residents and businesses, and providing connected innovation zones for residents and businesses to build on this access.[^2] The project focuses on areas where the digital divide is high and more than half of the population has low to moderate incomes.[^2]

The city of Los Angeles, in partnership with Cisco and community organizations Destination Crenshaw, the California Community Foundation and Digital Equity LA, launched a pilot program to provide free internet access across major streets, parks and public gathering spots along LA's Crenshaw Corridor.[^6] The Crenshaw Community Connectivity Pilot represents a public-private partnership among the city of Los Angeles, Cisco and a number of neighborhood nonprofits.[^9] The Bureau partnered with the LA Public Library to provide public outreach and education related to the broadband infrastructure project.[^7] This work is made possible through partnerships with community organizations like Destination Crenshaw.[^7] Destination Crenshaw and other community partners are working with the Bureau to realize the full potential of this infrastructure as a platform and starting point for more bandwidth, better city services, new economic opportunities, and additional capabilities.[^8]

The County of Los Angeles' Internal Services Department and WeLink Communications have partnered to bring affordable and reliable broadband internet to underserved areas in East Los Angeles/Boyle Heights and South Los Angeles.[^5] Through an extensive competitive process, the County selected WeLink as the partner for these first two Community Broadband Networks areas.[^5] Construction was expected to begin in Summer 2024, with internet service rolling out beginning in Fall 2024.[^5] Funding for this project has been provided in part through a grant for Local Agency Technical Assistance from a program administered by the California Public Utilities Commission.[^5] The City released an RFP to select a service provider to implement the Digital Inclusion & Bridging the Digital Divide program, with the City anticipating executing a contract with the selected provider in Q3 2022.[^10]

## Benefits and Impacts

The Crenshaw Community Connectivity Pilot offers internet access across the Crenshaw Corridor and benefits residents, businesses and visitors to the Crenshaw, Hyde Park, Park Mesa and Leimert Park neighborhoods.[^3][^6] The public WiFi network is called 'DestinationCrenshawWifi' and requires no password, though users must agree to terms and conditions when logging on.[^8] Three community connectivity pilots were launched that are bringing digital equity to communities in Los Angeles with the greatest need through infrastructure.[^7] The Bureau is working to bring community wi-fi to communities in Los Angeles, including Crenshaw and Canoga Park, through fiber construction.[^7]

WeLink offers a low-cost home internet plan starting at $25 per month to 50,000 qualified households in South Los Angeles and East Los Angeles/Boyle Heights, surpassing the initial goal in 2022 of serving 12,500 households.[^5] WeLink offers services across 68 square miles, where there are over 275,000 households and small businesses in East Los Angeles/Boyle Heights and South Los Angeles.[^5] WeLink offers internet speeds ranging from 500 megabits per second to 2 gigabits per second – up to 20 times faster download speed and up to 100 times faster upload speed than the new Federal Communications Commission broadband benchmark.[^5] WeLink offers plans starting at $65 per month to households without qualification requirements, with monthly rates fixed until at least September 2027.[^5] Every WeLink plan includes unlimited data, a Wi-Fi router, parental controls, and multilingual customer support without credit checks or cash deposits.[^5] The Internal Services Department was tasked with finding ways to provide reliable broadband internet to low-income communities where over 20% of households do not subscribe to home internet.[^5]

Los Angeles Mayor Karen Bass stated that the Crenshaw Corridor initiative shows what's possible when the public, private and nonprofit sectors come together with the community to bridge the digital divide.[^9] During the January 2025 wildfires, AT&T announced that postpaid and prepaid customers with billing zip codes in affected areas would receive unlimited talk, text and data from January 8 through March 31, 2025.[^1] On February 14, AT&T announced an expanded offering of up to three months of internet service for free via credits in upcoming billing cycles for impacted customers who keep their service.[^1] T-Mobile enabled Starlink Direct-to-Cellular service over impacted areas of Southern California, providing basic texting including to 911 and delivering wireless emergency alerts.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Expanded Wi-Fi access supports climate adaptation by enabling vulnerable populations to access critical emergency communications, real-time hazard alerts, and digital resources during climate-related disasters. The infrastructure's capacity to support future smart cities applications, including data-driven traffic management, improved emergency response, and environmental sensing technology, positions it as a foundational platform for climate resilience monitoring and response.[^3] The demonstration of emergency connectivity services during the January 2025 wildfires, when telecommunications providers extended unlimited service and emergency texting capabilities to affected areas, illustrates how digital infrastructure serves as essential lifeline infrastructure during climate-driven disasters.[^1]

## Business Analysis

The implementation leverages multiple funding mechanisms including Community Development Block Grant funds, American Rescue Plan Act funding, and grants from the California Public Utilities Commission's Local Agency Technical Assistance program.[^2][^7][^5] The public-private partnership model brings together municipal agencies, technology providers like Cisco, telecommunications companies, and community organizations to share implementation costs and operational responsibilities.[^6][^9] WeLink's pricing structure demonstrates a sustainable business model with low-cost plans at $25 monthly for qualified households and standard plans at $65 monthly with rates fixed until at least September 2027, providing predictable revenue streams while maintaining affordability.[^5] The County's competitive selection process for broadband partners and the city's RFP approach for service providers indicate structured procurement frameworks designed to ensure cost-effectiveness and service quality.[^5][^10] The infrastructure investment creates a platform for future revenue-generating smart city applications beyond basic connectivity, including traffic management, public safety enhancements, and environmental monitoring services.[^3]

## Sources

# Research Report: IoT-Enabled Fire Detection and Emergency Response System

## Solution Overview

VeeSafety deployed more than 300 sets of intelligent 4G smoke detectors in Shenzhen Shiyan Langxin Old Village to build a 24/7 fire prevention and control system for this century-old village.[^1] When the Internet of Things (IoT) monitoring equipment verifies there is a fire, it will sound the alarm and notify the property management team, automatically notify the fire brigade and relevant leaders.[^2][^3] This approach represents a technology-enabled fire prevention system that enhances emergency response capabilities in vulnerable urban areas.

## Technical Components

The implementation utilizes intelligent 4G smoke detectors as the primary hardware infrastructure, with more than 300 units installed throughout the village.[^1] The system operates as an urban fire remote integrated monitoring system that serves as the core technological platform.[^1] When IoT monitoring equipment verifies a fire, the technology automatically triggers multiple notification pathways, including audible alarms, property management team alerts, fire brigade notifications, and communications to relevant leaders.[^2][^3]

## Implementation Details

The smart smoke sensing project of Langxin Old Village adopts the 'government-enterprise collaboration' model, where the subdistrict office provides basic data and VeeSafety is responsible for equipment development and operation and maintenance.[^1] The deployment involved installing more than 300 sets of intelligent 4G smoke detectors across the century-old village to establish continuous fire prevention and control capabilities.[^1] VeeSafety serves as the primary technology partner responsible for equipment development and ongoing operation and maintenance, with the urban fire remote integrated monitoring system functioning as the core platform.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The system provides 24/7 fire prevention and control capabilities for Langxin Old Village.[^1] When fire incidents occur, the IoT monitoring equipment automatically executes multiple response actions simultaneously, including sounding alarms, notifying property management teams, alerting the fire brigade, and informing relevant leaders.[^2][^3] This automated multi-channel notification approach enables rapid emergency response coordination across multiple stakeholder groups.

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Fire risk represents a climate-related hazard that can be exacerbated by extreme heat conditions and drought periods. This IoT-enabled fire detection and response system enhances community resilience by providing early warning capabilities and automated emergency coordination for fire incidents in vulnerable urban settlements.

## Business Analysis

The implementation demonstrates a government-enterprise collaboration model where public sector entities and private technology providers share responsibilities for system deployment and operation.[^1] The subdistrict office contributes basic data resources while VeeSafety assumes responsibility for equipment development, deployment, and ongoing operation and maintenance activities.[^1] This partnership structure enables municipalities to access advanced fire prevention technology without bearing full development costs, while private sector partners gain opportunities to deploy and operate monitoring systems in urban environments.

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.vstarcam.com/news/56857.html
- [^2]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/China/Shenzhen/[ENG] Shenzhen SDG report - full report_updated.pdf
- [^3]: https://www.tyeefire.com/tyees-intelleigent-fire-alarm-and-evacuation-solution/

# Research Report: Green Light District Heritage Retrofitting Initiative

## Solution Overview

Amsterdam has established a strategic collaboration with Climate KIC, the EU's innovation platform for scaling up proven climate solutions.[^4] This partnership serves as a key mechanism for advancing climate innovation through the Green Light District initiative, which brings together heritage preservation and sustainable retrofitting expertise.[^1][^2] The approach demonstrates how European cities can leverage pan-European networks to accelerate climate adaptation implementation while sharing lessons learned across the Climate-KIC community.[^2]

## Technical Components

The Green Light District initiative integrates heritage preservation with sustainable retrofitting and energy efficiency technologies.[^2] Specialists from Slovenia, Portugal and the Netherlands, supported by EIT Climate-KIC, have joined forces in a pan-European project on sustainable heritage related to the Green Light District approach.[^2] The technical framework combines expertise from multiple disciplines to address the unique challenges of implementing climate solutions in historic urban environments.

## Implementation Details

The Green Light District founding partners consist of De Gezonde Stad, De Groene Grachten, NV Zeedijk, TU Delft, Rooftop Revolution, Gemeente Amsterdam and EIT Climate-KIC.[^1] This multi-stakeholder collaboration brings together academic institutions, municipal government, community organizations, and European innovation platforms to deliver the initiative.[^1] The lessons learned and positive outcomes from the Green Light District project are shared within the Climate-KIC community, enabling knowledge transfer across European cities.[^2]

## Business Analysis

Climate KIC, a member of the European Institute of Innovation and Technology (EIT), has secured €99 million across 19 Mission projects from Horizon Europe Missions funding, emerging as the top beneficiary of this funding mechanism.[^3] When Horizon Europe Missions launched in 2021, Climate KIC quickly positioned itself as a key vehicle for delivering this new type of research programme.[^3] In 2021, Climate KIC's budget stood at €39.1 million – including €1.8 million from membership fees and €6.7 million from external funders.[^3] In 2024, following the signing of a Memorandum of Cooperation with the EIT, Climate KIC entered a new phase as a financially sustainable member of the EIT Community, continuing to align with the EIT model while operating with greater autonomy.[^3] This financial trajectory demonstrates the platform's capacity to mobilize substantial European funding for climate innovation initiatives like Amsterdam's Green Light District collaboration.

## Sources

- [^1]: https://degezondestad.org/projecten/green-light-district/
- [^2]: https://pocityf.eu/news/green-light-district-project/
- [^3]: https://www.eit.europa.eu/news-events/news/climate-kic-secures-largest-share-horizon-missions-funding
- [^4]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Europe & Middle East/Amsterdam-Netherlands/Amsterdam-roadmap_climate_neutral.pdf

# Research Report: DC Circulator Electric Bus Fleet with Grid-Resilient Infrastructure

## Solution Overview

The District of Columbia Department of Transportation operates the DC Circulator bus line with six routes serving Washington, D.C., and Rosslyn, Virginia.[^4] The District will continue to electrify the DC Circulator fleet and build out infrastructure to keep them running in the event of a grid failure.[^8] DDOT has committed to a fully electric DC Circulator bus fleet by 2030, 15 years earlier than the 2045 deadline imposed by the DC Clean Energy Omnibus Amendment Act of 2018 and the Clean Energy DC Plan.[^2]

## Technical Components

In the spring of 2018, the D.C. Department of Transportation took delivery of 14 American-made Proterra battery electric buses for the DC Circulator fleet, making it one of the largest electric bus fleets in the country.[^5] The new Proterra E2 Catalyst Electric Buses are manufactured in the United States and run on 100 percent battery electric propulsion.[^1] The Proterra electric buses are powered by 4 large battery packs under the floor and after charging overnight, the buses can operate for a full 10-hour shift.[^5] The South Capitol Street facility was designed to use a solar photovoltaic (PV) canopy and a battery to provide electricity to the chargers.[^4] GPTech examined different options for the solar PV canopy and bus charging system, including battery storage and standalone microgrid capabilities.[^4] The DC Circulator fleet consists of electric, clean diesel, and hybrid (diesel and electric) buses.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The District of Columbia Department of Transportation's DC Circulator transit program in April 2018 placed 14 Proterra Battery Electric Buses into service as part of a two-year pilot.[^7] The new electric DC Circulator buses officially hit the streets on May 1, 2018.[^1] This initiative was supported by Proterra, Pepco, and the District Department of Transportation.[^1] The pilot proved to be a crash course in the challenges of building and operating BEB infrastructure and chargers.[^7] DDOT planned to purchase an additional 18 electric buses in the coming fiscal year after 2018, with a long-term goal to electrify the entire DC Circulator fleet.[^5] The DC Circulator received about $10 million for bus electrification in 2022.[^6] City Administrator Kevin Donahue stated that the DC Circulator cuts were necessary to pay for $100 million in costs related to 'the need for electrification.'[^2] Mayor Bowser's proposal to cut funding for WP-AM, EM-LP, and RS-DP lines was driven by the need to pay for new electric buses and charging facilities.[^2] The facility was designed to charge 46 buses, an increase over the current 14 charging stations.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

Over their 12-year lifetime, the 14 Proterra buses will reduce fuel and maintenance costs by more than $6 million.[^1] With the Catalyst E2 buses, the DC Circulator will displace more than 88,900 gallons of diesel per year and eliminate more than 243,980 pounds of CO2 emissions annually.[^1] The DC Circulator electric bus fleet displaces nearly 89,000 gallons of diesel per year and reduces CO2 emissions by a quarter million pounds.[^5] The battery-electric vehicles will bring clean, quiet, zero-emission transportation to more than 4.8 million annual riders and the Circulator's 189 drivers across all six distinct Circulator routes.[^1] The D.C. Circulator buses transport more than 4.8 million passengers each year and travel six routes around D.C. and into Virginia, stopping at popular monuments, museums, attractions and transportation hubs along the way.[^5]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The infrastructure buildout includes capabilities to keep electric buses running in the event of a grid failure, addressing climate-related disruptions to energy systems.[^8] The solar photovoltaic canopy and battery storage system examined by GPTech provides standalone microgrid capabilities that enhance operational resilience during extreme weather events that could compromise grid reliability.[^4]

## Business Analysis

The District should continue to support WMATA's transition to zero-emission buses and work to fully electrify the DC Circulator fleet on an accelerated timeline.[^3] As of 2021, the adoption of electric MHDVs in the District was in its infancy, with only a handful of electric trucks and buses in operation, primarily in demonstration projects, though the District's own fleet included a small number of electric buses operated by the DC Circulator.[^3] The financial model demonstrates significant operational savings, with the 14 Proterra buses reducing fuel and maintenance costs by more than $6 million over their 12-year lifetime.[^1] The DC Circulator received approximately $10 million for bus electrification in 2022, supporting the accelerated transition timeline.[^6] The commitment to full fleet electrification by 2030 positions the program 15 years ahead of the 2045 deadline imposed by the DC Clean Energy Omnibus Amendment Act of 2018 and the Clean Energy DC Plan.[^2]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://mayor.dc.gov/release/mayor-bowser-celebrates-arrival-new-electric-circulator-buses
- [^2]: https://ggwash.org/view/90507/the-dc-circulators-electrification-dilemma
- [^3]: https://sustainable.dc.gov/sites/default/files/dc/sites/sustainable/page_content/attachments/Transportation%20Electrification%20Roadmap.pdf
- [^4]: https://www.greenpwrd.com/projects/south-capitol-street-circulator-site-5gs82
- [^5]: https://afdc.energy.gov/case/3071?text
- [^6]: https://www.sierraclub.org/dc/blog/2023/03/wmata-has-worst-bus-electrification-record-among-large-us-transit-agencies
- [^7]: https://gwrccc.org/news/two-year-pilot-report-of-circulator-bus-to-be-unveiled-during-august-25th-virtual-workshop/

# Research Report: Airport Sustainability & Resilience Framework with Climate Action Plan

## Solution Overview

Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport developed a comprehensive sustainability and resilience framework centered on environmental, social, and governance performance disclosure and strategic planning.[^2][^5] The airport's vision is to be the most sustainable airport in the world by reducing impact on the environment, building resiliency and efficiency into operations, facilitating cleaner transportation, improving the communities around the airport, while driving the economic prosperity of the region.[^1] This approach positions the facility as a leader among U.S. airports in transparency and climate action through systematic reporting and implementation of sustainability initiatives.[^2]

## Technical Components

The sustainability framework encompasses multiple integrated planning documents that guide technical implementation across the airport's operations.[^4] The Climate Action Plan and Sustainable Management Plan were updated to provide strategic direction, while additional specialized plans under review include the Energy Management Plan, Water Master Plan, and Zero Waste Plan.[^4] The airport pursues certifications across numerous projects and rating systems, including LEED, Parksmart, and Envision standards.[^1] The South Deicing Complex achieved Envision Verification, demonstrating the application of sustainable infrastructure design standards.[^3] The airport was the first to earn LEED for Communities certification, establishing a precedent for comprehensive sustainability assessment at the facility level.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The sustainability contract was established in April 2023, with a joint venture team providing ongoing support for strategic planning and implementation.[^4] Since inception, the joint venture has applied for and received over $11,000,000 in sustainability grant funding from the FAA and FTA.[^4] RS&H worked with the airport to develop the 2022 ATL Flying Forward ESG report in collaboration with airport leaders across the organization.[^2] In July 2023, the City of Atlanta released its third Environmental, Social, Governance and Prosperity Report, which highlights the priorities, performance, risks, and opportunities at the airport across the ESG+P criteria.[^5] The South Deicing Facility earned an Envision Verified Award in May 2025, marking a recent milestone in sustainable infrastructure development.[^1] The joint venture continues to support the airport as a sustainable leader by identifying opportunities to demonstrate new technologies and implement pilot projects to demonstrate efficiency and innovation in sustainable development.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

The 2022 ATL Flying Forward ESG report addressed the airport's priorities, performance, risks, and opportunities across environmental, social and governance elements, providing investors with comprehensive disclosure of sustainability performance.[^2] In 2022, Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport was on the forefront of U.S. airports that disclosed their environmental, social, governance and prosperity performance to investors.[^2] The sustainability goals include net zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050, 100% clean and renewable energy by 2035, and 90% diversion of waste generated at the airport from landfills by 2035.[^1] The airport is committed to the City of Atlanta's 2019 Resolution No. 10-R-3783 to transition to 100% Clean and Renewable Energy by 2035, as well as the ACI 2050 Net-Zero goal.[^1] The City has set a goal for the airport to achieve net zero carbon emissions by 2050.[^5]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The sustainability framework builds resiliency and efficiency into airport operations through systematic planning and infrastructure improvements.[^1] The approach addresses climate adaptation through comprehensive environmental performance management, renewable energy transition, and waste reduction strategies that enhance operational resilience.[^1] The integration of climate action planning with sustainable management practices positions the facility to respond to evolving environmental conditions while maintaining operational continuity.[^4]

## Business Analysis

The joint venture secured over $11,000,000 in sustainability grant funding from the FAA and FTA since April 2023, demonstrating successful access to federal financing mechanisms for airport sustainability initiatives.[^4] The airport's commitment to the City of Atlanta's 2019 Resolution No. 10-R-3783 and the ACI 2050 Net-Zero goal provides a clear policy framework supporting long-term sustainability investments.[^1] The disclosure of environmental, social, governance and prosperity performance to investors in 2022 established transparency mechanisms that support access to sustainable finance and impact investment opportunities.[^2] The pursuit of multiple certifications across LEED, Parksmart, and Envision rating systems creates market differentiation and demonstrates alignment with industry sustainability standards.[^1]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.atl.com/sustainability/
- [^2]: https://airport-world.com/sustainability-2-0/
- [^3]: https://www.rsandh.com/resources/news/multiple-rsh-aviation-projects-receive-envision-sustainability-awards/
- [^4]: https://cerm.com/hartsfieldsustainability
- [^5]: https://www.buyatlantabonds.com/city-of-atlanta-ga/about/esg-program/i31

# Research Report: AI-Powered Smart City Platform for Rapid Public Issue Response

## Solution Overview

In April 2022, Futian District officially launched the Smart City Governance for Quick Public Opinion Handling platform, designed to help the district government receive and handle public demands.[^1][^2] Residents participate in social co-construction by reporting hazards, reflecting demands and putting forward suggestions through the Smart Futian Mobile Portal.[^3] The main goal of this platform has been to enable fast responses to and fast closures of issues reported by the district's residents.[^3]

## Technical Components

The platform was powered by CityCore, Huawei Cloud Stack's Smart City solution.[^3] The AI platform provides intelligent computing, analysis, and digital services for various departments.[^1] It supports multi-scenario applications such as intelligent video discovery, grid patrol, and quick public opinion handling.[^1] The AI platform provides intelligent computational and analytical capabilities and digital services to power applications such as the intelligent discovery of events and incidents from video, grid cell-based inspection, and quick response to public opinions and sentiments.[^3] The platform uses AI to offer several key features, including the de-duplication of events across all channels, and distribution of E2E services.[^2]

## Implementation Details

Futian District's new government service platform was launched in April 2022.[^3] On the platform, citizens can submit questions, complaints, opinions, and suggestions or ask for help via multiple channels.[^1][^2] The platform enables the government to quickly respond to the general public, handle requests efficiently and provide timely feedback.[^1][^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

With the platform, Futian managed to improve their incident distribution efficiency from 4 minutes per enquiry to 50 seconds per enquiry.[^1][^2] The accuracy of intelligent distribution has reached 90 percent.[^1][^2]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://e.huawei.com/en/case-studies/solutions/computing/shenzhen-futian-huaweicloud-2023
- [^2]: https://govinsider.asia/intl-en/article/futian-district-shenzhen-a-success-story-of-urban-governance-with-ai
- [^3]: https://www.huaweicloud.com/intl/en-us/about/blogs/20230310.html

# Research Report: Air Quality Flag Program for Schools and Communities

## Solution Overview

The Metro Public Health Department and Metro Nashville Public Schools are participating in the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's Air Quality Flag Program to help protect people's health from outdoor air pollution.[^1] This approach, which started in January 2019, involves participating schools raising a flag each morning based on the color of the Air Quality Index (AQI) to show the expected amount of air pollution for that day.[^1][^2] Rose Park Magnet Math and Science Middle School is among five STEAM Metro Nashville Public Schools to begin raising a brightly colored flag to help their students, staff and members of the community be aware of daily air quality conditions.[^1]

## Technical Components

Nashville has four air quality sensors, all positioned in the east and northeast parts of Davidson County.[^2] Managed by the Metro Health Department, they report air pollutant concentrations to the EPA hourly.[^2] Each morning participating schools raise a flag based on the color of the Air Quality Index (AQI) to show the expected amount of air pollution for that day.[^1]

## Implementation Details

Rose Park Magnet Math and Science Middle School joins J.T. Moore Middle School, Madison Middle School, Stratford STEM Lower Campus, Whitsitt Elementary School and 16 other MNPS schools already participating in the program.[^1] The Nashville Breathes project started in January 2019.[^2] Strategic partners that helped make the Nashville Breathes plan a reality include MNPS STEAM Advisory Board, Metro Nashville Public Health Department, American Lung Association (Tennessee Chapter), TN Asthma Coalition, and Metro Nashville Public Schools.[^2] Students at Rose Park Middle School presented the air quality flags and a student-made art installation to promote clean air on Thursday, February 6 at 1:30pm.[^1] ENCORE students at Rose Park Middle took the lead by including their school community and Metro Council in the air quality awareness drive.[^2]

## Business Analysis

The Climate Pollution Reduction Grants (CPRG) program provides $5 billion in grants to states, local governments, tribes, and territories to develop and implement ambitious plans for reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and other harmful air pollution.[^3]

[^1]: Source 1
[^2]: Source 2
[^3]: Source 3

## Sources

# Research Report: Disaster Assessment and Assistance Dashboard (DAAD)

## Solution Overview

The Disaster Assessment and Assistance Dashboard (DAAD) was deployed in San Francisco as a pilot after receiving endorsement by FEMA to be part of its open data initiative.[^1][^2][^10] San Francisco became the first city to test the app, and pending budget approval, the platform was slated for official purchase in June.[^5] Mark Farrell, San Francisco's District 2 county supervisor, stated that the city adopted the dashboard because of its ability to centralize help for residents.[^3][^18]

## Technical Components

DAAD draws information from numerous hubs comprised of FEMA's own open data APIs (application programming interfaces), city and regional information, and on-the-ground citizen feedback.[^19] When fully developed, the dashboard will harness disaster information from more than 100 application programming interfaces (APIs) and will be filled with hundreds of data sets to help citizens and emergency crews assess current situations.[^22] In 2014, a cohort of concerned Bayview residents worked closely with City staff and Appallicious to develop San Francisco's first daily-use Digital Community Resilience Platform.[^6] Following San Francisco's public-facing launch, a mobile version and formats for multiple languages were in the works.[^9][^13]

## Implementation Details

Appallicious CEO Yo Yoshida indicated that the company would likely pilot the Disaster Assessment and Assistance Dashboard with the city of San Francisco for 90 days before formally offering it to communities.[^11] In the coming weeks after July 2014, San Francisco residents and businesses would have a chance to submit information to the beta version of DAAD as the dashboard was introduced to the public by city and county departments.[^4][^21] During the pilot, the San Francisco Fire Department's Neighborhood Emergency Response Team (NERT), an emergency training program for citizens, worked with Appallicious to provide feedback and inspire crowdsourced engagement.[^7][^12] The Office of Resilience and Recovery partnered with the San Francisco Public Libraries to conduct community outreach meetings at the City's 29 libraries, building on the work already being done in the libraries to reimagine these neighborhood spaces as gathering sites.[^14] ORR and Appallicious piloted a new community-level dashboard in 29 neighborhoods as part of ORR's strategic outreach partnership with the San Francisco Public Libraries.[^15][^16][^17] Once released to cities, a freemium version of the app would be available for basic use.[^20]

## Benefits and Impacts

San Francisco Supervisor Mark Farrell stated that the Disaster Assessment and Assistance Dashboard helps build communication and collaboration among all stakeholders involved in planning and recovery on a citywide basis, allowing the city to learn lessons, create standards and become more efficient through all phases of disaster—planning, response and recovery.[^8] For San Francisco, the platform was an integral tool in the city's resiliency plans for all kinds of disasters, large or small.[^5] San Francisco's Neighborhood Empowerment Network (NEN) developed a model for building community resilience that works with residents, community organizations, and faith-based groups to build local capacity and foster collaboration.[^23]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The dashboard serves as a comprehensive disaster management tool designed to support San Francisco's resilience planning across multiple hazard scenarios. By centralizing disaster information and enabling collaboration among stakeholders, the system enhances the city's capacity to prepare for, respond to, and recover from climate-related emergencies and other disasters affecting urban communities.

## Sources
