# Research Report: Port Electric Charging Infrastructure with Backup Power Microgrid

## Solution Overview

The Port of Oakland is implementing a comprehensive electric charging infrastructure system integrated with backup power capabilities to protect operations from power reliability challenges while transitioning cargo handling to zero emissions.[^2][^3] In October 2024, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency awarded the Port a $322 million grant to transition its cargo handling operations to zero emissions, representing the largest federal investment to date in cutting emissions from seaport operations in the Bay Area.[^1][^5] This initiative combines electric vehicle charging infrastructure with microgrid technology, fuel cells, and battery storage to create a resilient power system capable of maintaining critical port operations during grid outages.[^3][^4]

## Technical Components

The infrastructure deployment includes 145 heavy-duty and Class 8 electrical chargers at 7 locations for yard, dockside, on-road, and transit vehicle use, increasing the number of zero-emission vehicles that can be supported from 50 to over 1,000 throughout the seaport.[^4] The charging network comprises 31 direct current fast chargers for drayage trucks and 20 Level 2 chargers for yard tractors and forklifts.[^2] The DCFCs provide a power output of at least 150 kW and can charge a drayage truck in 1-2 hours, while the Level 2 chargers deliver 19.2 kW and charge yard tractors and forklifts overnight.[^2] The system incorporates a smart charging management platform to optimize charging schedules and minimize demand charges.[^2] The project includes a 5-megawatt fuel cell installation to provide resilient, zero-emissions power to the Port, capable of operating during grid outages to ensure critical equipment continues functioning.[^3] The fuel cell will be powered by renewable natural gas or hydrogen and integrated with the Port's microgrid to manage energy use and reduce grid reliance.[^3] Battery Energy Storage Systems are deployed at 6 locations for clean energy storage and to provide charging for vehicles and port operations during rolling blackouts and times of peak energy demand.[^4] The implementation includes 6 substation upgrades for electric grid modernization to support the Port's transition to zero emissions, accommodate future zero-emission vehicle needs, and ensure a resilient local electrical grid.[^4] Solar generation infrastructure provides increased green energy capacity for electric vehicles and other facilities and equipment.[^4] The new electrical infrastructure was designed to withstand natural disasters such as earthquakes and floods and is less susceptible to power outages.[^3]

## Implementation Details

The project deploys 663 pieces of zero-emissions equipment fueled either by battery or hydrogen fuel cell.[^5] The initial phase includes 61 pieces of zero-emission and near-zero emission cargo-handling equipment and heavy-duty trucks, along with supporting charging infrastructure, at the Oakland Seaport and at freight facilities that support operations.[^2] Equipment deployment consists of 31 battery-electric Class 8 drayage trucks manufactured by Volvo Trucks North America with a range of up to 275 miles on a single charge and equipped with 565 kWh batteries capable of charging at up to 250 kW, 10 battery-electric yard tractors, 10 battery-electric forklifts, and 10 renewable diesel-powered top handlers.[^2] The Port requested $52 million in PIDP funding to support a $119 million infrastructure project that will construct critical infrastructure to support the transition to 100% zero-emissions cargo handling operations.[^3] Infrastructure costs total $24,000,000, including $12,400,000 for 31 DCFCs, $800,000 for 20 Level 2 chargers, and $10,800,000 for site preparation and installation.[^2] Equipment costs total $70,000,000, including $24,800,000 for 31 battery-electric Class 8 trucks, $8,000,000 for 10 battery-electric yard tractors, $3,000,000 for 10 battery-electric forklifts, $15,000,000 for 10 renewable diesel top handlers, and $1,200,000 for scrappage of 61 units.[^2] The truck charging infrastructure component has a total cost of $15,000,000, with $6,000,000 from PIDP Grant, $5,000,000 from Port Funds, $2,000,000 from Terminal Operator Funds, and $2,000,000 from Other Grant Funds.[^3] Electrical infrastructure upgrades total $45,000,000, with $20,000,000 from PIDP Grant, $15,000,000 from Port Funds, $5,000,000 from Terminal Operator Funds, and $5,000,000 from Other Grant Funds.[^3] The fuel cell installation component has a total cost of $20,000,000, with $10,000,000 from PIDP Grant, $5,000,000 from Port Funds, and $5,000,000 from Other Grant Funds.[^3] The fuel cell will be located at the Port's new truck service center in the North Gateway area of the Oakland Seaport.[^3]

The Port applied for $52,000,000 in funding from the EPA Clean Ports Program to implement the Port of Oakland Zero- and Near-Zero Emission Freight Facility Clean Ports Project, with the total project cost of $104,000,000 and the remaining $52,000,000 coming from a combination of public and private cost share.[^2] Cost share contributions include $10,000,000 from the Port of Oakland, $5,000,000 from the Bay Area Air Quality Management District's Carl Moyer Program, $25,000,000 from Port Tenants, and $12,000,000 from Technology Providers.[^2] Forum Mobility committed to providing $6,000,000 in private capital investment toward developing a heavy-duty electric truck charging depot in or near the Port.[^2] Zeem Solutions committed $6,000,000 in private investment toward establishing a charging and fleet operations depot in the Oakland area to serve drayage truck operators.[^2] GSC Logistics committed $7,500,000 toward purchasing 15 battery-electric Class 8 drayage trucks and 15 DC fast chargers.[^2] TraPac committed $6,000,000 toward purchasing 10 battery-electric yard tractors, 10 battery-electric forklifts, and 20 Level 2 chargers.[^2] The Bay Area Air Quality Management District authorized a conditional award of $5,000,000 in cost-share funding from its Carl Moyer Program for the purchase of eligible zero- and near-zero emission equipment.[^2] The California Transportation Commission voted to approve a $42 million grant for the Green Power Microgrid Project, with the total project cost of $60 million and the Port providing approximately $18 million in funds.[^4] Matched by the port and local partners, the total investment will be close to half a billion dollars.[^1]

The project is being implemented over a five-year period from 2024 to 2029.[^2] Equipment procurement occurred from Q3 2024 to Q4 2026, and infrastructure construction will occur from Q1 2026 to Q4 2027.[^2] The implementation follows a phased approach, with electrical infrastructure upgrades being the first component implemented because they are critical to supporting other project components.[^3] The Port's existing electrical infrastructure is over 50 years old, nearing the end of its useful life, and not designed to support large-scale deployment of zero-emissions equipment, which will significantly increase power demand.[^3] Key partners include TraPac Container Terminal, which handles approximately 25 percent of the Port's total container volume and will deploy 10 battery-electric yard tractors, 10 battery-electric forklifts, and 20 Level 2 chargers.[^2] GSC Logistics, a leading drayage and logistics provider, will deploy 15 battery-electric Class 8 drayage trucks and 15 DCFCs at its facility near the Port.[^2] Shippers Transport Express will deploy 15 battery-electric Class 8 drayage trucks and 15 DCFCs at its facility near the Port.[^2] Eagle Rock Aggregates will deploy one battery-electric Class 8 drayage truck and one DCFC at its bulk aggregate facility.[^2] Forum Mobility and Zeem Solutions will develop and operate charging depots for drayage trucks serving the Port, located in close proximity to provide charging services to small fleets and independent owner-operators.[^2] The Port partnered with ARCHES (Alliance for Renewable Clean Hydrogen Energy Systems) to bring in clean hydrogen fuel for the project.[^5] The Port joined forces with Caltrans and the Metropolitan Transportation Commission to submit the grant application.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

The electrification effort will reduce more than 69,000 tons of yearly greenhouse gas—the equivalent of burning more than 160 Statues of Liberty's weight in coal—emitted by drayage trucks, cranes, forklifts, and tractors.[^1] The project is estimated to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by more than 5,000 metric tons of carbon dioxide equivalent per year and criteria air pollutants by more than 25 tons per year.[^2] Lifetime emission reductions are estimated at 251.6 tons of NOx, 3.8 tons of PM2.5, and 16.5 tons of ROG.[^2] The microgrid will supply backup power to help insulate the Port from regional power outages and allow the Port to provide resilient power to critical facilities, such as the Port's emergency operations center and data center.[^3][^4] The project serves as a key implementation measure of the Port's Seaport Air Quality 2020 and Beyond Plan, which was adopted by the Board of Port Commissioners in 2019 and sets a goal of achieving zero emissions from all Port operations by 2050.[^2] The project invests in electric and hydrogen equipment, battery storage and charging infrastructure, the elimination of existing diesel equipment, and community engagement activities.[^5] The EPA's Clean Ports Program grant came with a mandate for community engagement at every stage of the electrification project, from planning and design to execution and outreach.[^1] Pacific Environment was part of a coalition of environmental and community organizations that was in conversation with the Port while it was drafting its grant proposal, particularly around electrifying drayage trucks.[^5] The West Oakland Cultural Action Network is one of four community-based organizations working with the Port to inform residents about the effort to electrify heavy-duty trucks and tractors that haul containers.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The integrated charging infrastructure and microgrid system directly addresses climate-driven power reliability challenges by creating resilient backup power capabilities that can maintain critical port operations during grid outages.[^3][^4] The fuel cell installation and battery energy storage systems provide zero-emissions power during rolling blackouts and times of peak energy demand, protecting the Port from the impacts of changing electric power reliability associated with climate stresses on regional energy infrastructure.[^3][^4] The project is located in a federally designated non-attainment area for ozone and particulate matter, and the new electrical infrastructure was designed to withstand natural disasters such as earthquakes and floods while being less susceptible to power outages.[^3]

## Business Analysis

The project demonstrates a comprehensive public-private financing model combining federal grants, state funding, port capital, and private sector investment. The EPA Clean Ports Program provided $322 million in October 2024, while the California Transportation Commission approved a $42 million grant for the microgrid component.[^1][^4] Private sector partners committed $25,500,000 in cost-share contributions, with Forum Mobility and Zeem Solutions each providing $6,000,000 for charging depot development, GSC Logistics contributing $7,500,000 for trucks and chargers, and TraPac committing $6,000,000 for electric equipment.[^2] The Bay Area Air Quality Management District authorized $5,000,000 in conditional funding from its Carl Moyer Program for eligible zero- and near-zero emission equipment purchases.[^2] The financing structure leverages multiple grant programs including PIDP funding requests totaling $52 million to support the $119 million infrastructure project.[^3] The Port's clean air and electrification project has so far avoided EPA funding freezes that occurred after Donald Trump's second inauguration and remains on track.[^1] The project represents a replicable model for port electrification, with technology providers Forum Mobility and Zeem Solutions developing charging depot operations that can serve small fleets and independent owner-operators, creating a scalable business model for heavy-duty vehicle charging infrastructure.[^2] The smart charging system implementation to optimize charging and minimize demand charges demonstrates operational cost management strategies essential for long-term financial sustainability.[^2]

## Sources

# Research Report: NYC Community Air Quality Citizen Science Monitoring Toolkit

## Solution Overview

The New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene developed a community air quality citizen science toolkit to empower residents in monitoring local air pollution through accessible technologies and data-sharing platforms.[^2][^4] This approach builds on the success of the New York City Community Air Survey, which the Health Department established in 2007 and began collecting data in December 2008 as the largest ongoing urban air monitoring program of any US city.[^6] The initiative aims to increase community awareness of local air quality, its determinants, and air pollution-related health risks while expanding citizen participation in acquiring, interpreting, and communicating air quality data.[^4]

## Technical Components

The toolkit incorporates multiple monitoring technologies and data platforms to support community-based air quality assessment. The New York City Community Air Survey, conducted by the Health Department and Queens College (CUNY), uses monitors that check pollutants causing health problems, including fine particles, nitrogen oxides, sulfur dioxide, ozone, and elemental carbon as a marker for diesel exhaust particles.[^3] Survey monitors use a small battery-powered pump and filters to collect air samples, with monitors mounted 10 to 12 feet off the ground on public light poles, utility poles along streets, and in some parks.[^3] The Community Air Monitoring Project for Environmental Justice deployed Aeroqual AQY1 monitors from July 2019 to July 2020, which use a laser particle counter to measure PM2.5 and electrochemical sensors to measure NO2 and O3.[^5] These monitors recorded data at one-minute intervals, with data transmitted wirelessly to a central server for storage and processing.[^5] The C-SensLab training program at CUNY's Advanced Science Research Center in Harlem provides hands-on experience building low-cost, arduino-based sensors to learn about scientific principles behind environmental air quality parameters.[^1] A pilot study in Brooklyn's Williamsburg neighborhood utilized AirBeam2 low-cost monitors, with data automatically uploaded every second onto the AirCasting mobile app.[^6] NYC Open Data provides modeled air pollution raster data from the survey, while the Air Quality Hub on the Environment & Health Data Portal includes information such as PM2.5 data feeds and data stories.[^3] Participants in the C-SensLab program learn to use open-source data analysis tools to graph and interpret air quality data as they identify patterns and trends.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The New York City Community Air Survey represents a collaboration between the Health Department and the Barry Commoner Center for Health and the Environment at Queens College of the City University of New York.[^6] Survey air pollution measurements are taken at various city locations during each season, with routine sites spread around the city to capture different neighborhood characteristics, including residential, commercial, and industrial areas.[^3] Every survey site is monitored for a two-week period during each season, and after each period, monitors are collected and taken to a laboratory for analysis.[^3] Since 2008, the program has studied how pollutants from traffic, buildings such as boilers and furnaces, and other sources impact air quality in different neighborhoods.[^3] To better understand potential sources of emissions in low-income neighborhoods, an additional 15 environmental justice sites were included in the survey.[^3] The Community Air Monitoring Project for Environmental Justice is a partnership between the New York City Environmental Justice Alliance and four member organizations: UPROSE, The Point CDC, El Puente, and WE ACT for Environmental Justice.[^5] This community-led initiative involved partner organizations in all aspects of the project, from selecting monitor locations to analyzing data and developing policy recommendations.[^5] One monitor was deployed in each of the four communities from July 2019 to July 2020, for a total of four monitors.[^5] Monitors were sited in locations of community concern, such as near major roadways, industrial areas, and sensitive receptors like schools and parks.[^5] The C-SensLab training program operates as a 6-week train-the-trainer program based at CUNY's Advanced Science Research Center in Harlem.[^1] A pilot study focusing on personal exposure to fine particulate matter using low-cost sensor technology was conducted in collaboration with El Puente in Brooklyn's Williamsburg neighborhood.[^6] As part of data acquisition conducted in 2019, high school students gathered data at their schools, schoolyards, and playgrounds using low-cost monitors, attending workshops, training sessions, and research activities as part of their science class curriculum and portfolio.[^6] The Barry Commoner Center for Health published a report documenting the Air Quality Citizen Science Research Project in NYC in October 2020, providing toolkit and case studies.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

The citizen science toolkit pilot study aims to help develop methods for citizen air quality monitoring that can be scaled to other communities and develop strategies for disseminating research findings to the public, community, policymakers, and stakeholders.[^4] The air quality monitoring conducted in Williamsburg was incorporated into El Puente's 'Our Air/Nuestro Aire' grassroots campaign.[^6] The main goals of this campaign included sharing the collected data with the community, engaging academic partners to develop a set of policy and urban design solutions, and incorporating findings into a 5-point policy platform.[^6] The Community Air Monitoring Project for Environmental Justice measured concentrations of fine particulate matter, nitrogen dioxide, and ozone across four environmental justice communities over a one-year period.[^5]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This community air quality monitoring approach addresses climate adaptation by enabling communities to track air pollution patterns that may intensify with climate change, including increased ground-level ozone formation during heat waves and particulate matter concentrations affected by changing weather patterns. The toolkit's focus on environmental justice communities provides critical data for populations disproportionately vulnerable to both air quality degradation and climate impacts.

## Business Analysis

The citizen science toolkit leverages low-cost monitoring technologies to expand air quality surveillance beyond traditional regulatory networks, creating a scalable model for community-based environmental monitoring. The approach combines institutional resources from the Health Department and CUNY with community-based organizations to distribute implementation costs and build local capacity. The C-SensLab train-the-trainer model creates a multiplier effect by preparing community members to lead subsequent monitoring initiatives.[^1] The use of arduino-based sensors and open-source data analysis tools reduces financial barriers to participation while maintaining scientific rigor.[^1] The integration of monitoring data into grassroots campaigns and policy platforms demonstrates a pathway for translating community-generated data into actionable policy recommendations.[^6] The partnership structure between NYC-EJA and member organizations provides a replicable framework for community-led environmental monitoring that can be adapted to other cities facing similar air quality and environmental justice challenges.[^5]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://comsenslab.commons.gc.cuny.edu/course-schedule/
- [^2]: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/344575711_Air_Quality_Citizen_Science_Research_Project_in_NYC_Toolkit_Case_Studies
- [^3]: https://www.nyc.gov/site/doh/data/data-sets/air-quality-nyc-community-air-survey.page
- [^4]: https://commonercenter.org/news.html
- [^5]: https://nyc-eja.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/CAMP-EJ-2020-Report-Final-021821-Reduced.pdf
- [^6]: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9321294/

# Research Report: Heat-Stress-Avoidant Navigation App for Vulnerable Populations

## Solution Overview

The Heat Adaptation for Vulnerable Population Groups (HEAL) project developed a heat-stress-avoidant navigation application to help at-risk groups and others navigate the city more safely during periods of extreme heat.[^3] This initiative was funded by the Baden-Württemberg Foundation from 2021 to 2024, focusing on practical solutions to mitigate heat stress.[^1] The project was initiated with the objective to transcend academic boundaries and address the pressing problem of heat stress in Heidelberg, emphasizing practical applications.[^2]

## Technical Components

The HEAL app is a web-based routing application designed to facilitate heat-adapted mobility, calculating alternative routes that steer away from main roads with little shade and suggesting routes through parks and shaded areas instead.[^2] The application was developed by modeling heat conditions across the city using statistical models based on real-time sensor data (in Heidelberg only) and sun exposure models.[^2] The project team identified and modeled areas with increased heat stress and developed statistical prediction models based on sensor data and existing climate analysis maps.[^1] The HEAL app calculates alternative routes that steer users away from main roads with little shade, directing them through parks and shaded areas instead, and provides detailed information about the type of path, surface and gradient along the chosen route.[^1] The HEAL project and the routing app are largely based on open data from OpenStreetMap (OSM), a global community project that collaboratively maps the built and natural environment throughout the world.[^2] To further refine the routing approach, the project team simulated 10,000 pedestrian trips in Heidelberg at different times of the day to analyse variations in route selection based on heat exposure, helping to optimize the algorithm.[^3]

## Implementation Details

The HEAL routing app was originally launched in Heidelberg during the summer of 2024.[^2][^3] The project brought together experts from the Heidelberg Institute for Geoinformation Technology (HeiGIT) as well as the Geoinformatics Department and the TdLab Geography (Transdisciplinarity Lab), both parts of the Institute of Geography at Heidelberg University.[^1] A key component of HEAL is its transdisciplinary approach, which actively involves local stakeholders such as the Office for Environmental Protection, the Academy for Older People and senior and family community/network members.[^3] To ensure the navigation App addresses real-life challenges and is easy to use, the project team organised five interactive city walks, surveys, interviews (mobile and static), participatory and mental mapping.[^3] A heat workshop at the Bahnstadt district engaged pupils from a secondary school in discussions on heat-related health risks and potential solutions as part of the HEAL project.[^3] The HEAL app for Heidelberg is available at https://heal.openrouteservice.org/ and the HEAL app for Worms is available at https://worms.heal.openrouteservice.org/.[^2] Initially developed for Heidelberg, the HEAL app is now also available in Worms.[^2] The project has been designed from the beginning to make the approach and the routing application transferable to other cities in Germany, aiming to extend the benefits of this research beyond Heidelberg.[^2][^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The measures within this project ensure safe mobility and support daily activities during heat events, primarily focusing on pedestrians, but also accommodating cyclists.[^3] The public has access to information on heat adaptation strategies in the form of brochures of Heidelberg and to the HEAL app to identify routes with lower heat stress in Heidelberg.[^1] The HEAL project launched a routing app to help citizens avoid heat stress, supporting climate adaptation and public health during hot weather periods.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Surface temperatures in Heidelberg reached almost 53°C during July 2022 on the 'Promenade' in Heidelberg's 'Bahnstadt' district.[^3] The HEAL project complements the broader Heat Action Plan by providing innovative solutions for heat-stress-resilient mobility in Heidelberg.[^3] This navigation system specifically addresses heat protection challenges by enabling vulnerable populations to avoid extreme heat exposure during daily activities.[^4]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://giscienceblog.uni-heidelberg.de/2024/07/25/staying-safe-in-the-heat-routing-app-for-heidelberg/
- [^2]: https://heigit.org/a-routing-app-to-avoid-the-heat-heal-app-now-available-also-for-worms/
- [^3]: https://climate-adapt.eea.europa.eu/en/mission/solutions/mission-stories/enabling-heat-stress-resilient-mobility-with-the-heal-navigation-app-in-germany-story88
- [^4]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Europe & Middle East/Heidelberg-Germany/heidelberg_nzc_ccc_ok.pdf

# Research Report: Advanced Metering Infrastructure for Water Conservation and Leak Detection

## Solution Overview

Houston Public Works is implementing an Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) system to replace more than 95% of existing aged systems and improve leak detection capabilities across the city's water distribution network.[^1] This technology upgrade addresses the end-of-life status of the city's 20+ year old legacy automated meter reading system while enhancing operational efficiency and water conservation efforts.[^1] The initiative serves nearly 3 million residents across a four-county, 600-square-mile service area.[^2]

## Technical Components

The Sensus AMI System encompasses network infrastructure, Software as a Service (SaaS) platforms, and smart sensing capabilities deployed throughout Houston's water distribution system.[^1] Houston Public Works will deploy 500,000 new smart water meters and sensors over a five-year period, with devices capable of measuring water pressure and temperature in addition to consumption.[^3] The SaaS tool enables centralized monitoring through dashboards that provide reports on customer water usage, leaks, water pressure readings, and other data in nearly real time.[^3] Smart sensors and data analytics allow the department to more quickly detect and fix problems, such as water main leaks that cause water pressure to drop.[^3] The utility plans to integrate acoustic leak detection and pressure monitoring of water mains using the fixed network infrastructure.[^2] Houston Public Works has partnered with Microsoft Azure to use cloud-based data analytics tools to gain insights on sensor data and create a digital twin, a real-time digital representation of its infrastructure.[^3] The system includes virtual connect and disconnect features that eliminate the need for expensive truck rolls, effectively handling 1,000 work orders each day that don't require field deployment.[^2] Remote reading devices attached to water meters transmit water usage data back to the city, enabling accurate and automated billing.[^5]

## Implementation Details

The contract with Sensus USA Inc. has estimated spending authority of $6,122,748.99 for Houston Public Works in FY21 and $31,916,227.96 in out years, totaling $38,038,976.95.[^1] Initial funding of $18,592,348.99 comes from $17,614,638.99 from the Water and Sewer System Consolidated Construction Fund and $977,710.00 from the Water and Sewer System Operating Fund.[^1] Houston City Council authorized an additional appropriation of $16,054,610.00, consisting of $14,054,610.00 from the Water and Sewer System Consolidated Construction Fund and $2,000,000.00 from the Dedicated Drainage and Street Renewal Capital Fund.[^7] The scope of work requires Sensus USA Inc. to provide all labor, materials, equipment, and supervision for installation of the Sensus AMI System network and Software as a Service, with software maintenance and upgrades covered under the SaaS service agreement.[^1] Installation of the individual meter reading devices and infrastructure will be completed by Houston Public Works field personnel and/or contractors.[^1] Twenty prospective proposers downloaded the solicitation document, and proposals were received from Aclara Technologies LLC, Badger Meter Inc., Itron, Inc., Sensus Americas, Smart Earth Technologies, LLC, Neptune Technology Group, Inc., and Core & Main.[^1] Sensus USA Inc. received the highest overall score and was deemed the best qualified vendor to perform the requirements as outlined in the RFP.[^1] The request for proposal was issued with a 12% M/WBE participation goal, and Sensus USA Inc. submitted a Good Faith Effort with 1.88% MWBE approved by the Office of Business Opportunity.[^1] Crystal Communications was designated as the certified MWBE subcontractor for Radio Network Installation, representing 1.88% of the contract.[^1] The replacement of the automated meter reading system was presented to the Transportation, Technology & Infrastructure Committee on April 2, 2020.[^1] As of December 2024, Houston Public Works had successfully replaced over 100,000 remote reading devices across the city.[^5] The department identified approximately 125,000 nonfunctioning remote reading devices that needed replacement as part of the Water Bill Improvement Plan launched on April 1, 2024.[^5] Houston Public Works encountered over 15,000 meters that were inaccessible due to obstacles such as landscaping, debris piles, concrete driveways, and locked gates during the replacement effort.[^5] The city identified 273,000 remote reading devices that have not failed yet but will eventually need repairs.[^9] Houston will spend about $8.5 million to replace more than 25,300 aging water meters, with some meters more than 20 years old according to documents the City Council unanimously passed.[^11]

## Benefits and Impacts

The AMI system helps improve customer service, automate processes, protect revenue, improve water quality, identify breaks and outages, increase reliability, segment the network through district metering, and evaluate asset health.[^4] The replacement enables Houston Public Works to maintain continuity of the automated meter reading process and the continued timely and accurate billing of customer water and sewer consumption while minimizing incremental increases in operating costs.[^1] Project success will be measured through increased system availability resulting in fewer manual and estimated meter reads.[^1] The system allows the department to phase out its existing joint use contract with CenterPoint Energy, eliminating the need to mount hardware on CNP poles, with cost savings realized incrementally over the 10-year replacement period resulting in approximately $173,000.00 annually beginning in FY2028.[^1] Houston's 2024 Water Conservation Plan sets a five-year goal to reduce water loss to 18 gallons per capita per day and a ten-year goal to reduce it to 15 gallons per capita per day.[^6] Houston Water's water loss in 2022 was 15.6 percent, or 28 GPCD.[^10] During 2023 to May 2024, the city lost 36 billion gallons of water due to leaks alone—enough water to supply the city of Fort Worth for a year.[^11] Houston ranked first among Texas water utilities with the highest total volume of water loss in 2019, losing 32,870,203,000 gallons, representing 16% water loss at 147 gallons per capita per day.[^12] Many remote reading devices stopped functioning due to age, damage, or extreme weather conditions, leading to billing inconsistencies, and as meter readers aged and failed, Houston Public Works began estimating water usage using an algorithm that wasn't consistently accurate.[^5][^11]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The AMI system enhances Houston's climate resilience by enabling rapid detection and response to infrastructure failures that could be exacerbated by extreme weather events. The department will purchase equipment and services from Sensus USA Inc. to implement a flood monitoring system that leverages the communications platform deployed as part of the Advanced Metering Infrastructure system, building upon a successful pilot deployment conducted in 2022.[^7] Houston Public Works is installing sensors that measure water flow rates in drainage ditches and water levels at highway underpasses to predict flooding based on rainfall and water velocity, determining how quickly an area downstream will potentially flood.[^3]

## Business Analysis

The City of Houston was the first major city in the U.S. to use in-ground automated water meter reading technology, deploying an automated meter reading system in 1998 to curb the expense of more than 200 meter readers, before migrating to AMI starting in 2010.[^2][^4] The department started to deploy the fixed network AMI system in September 2010, which included network administration application software, cell control units, repeaters and communication modules dispersed around the greater Houston area.[^2] The September 2010 deployment allowed 54,000 accounts to be read over the fixed network, and more than 310,000 of the total population of 465,000 metered accounts transmit over the fixed network.[^2] The city aimed to expand the fixed network footprint and achieve close to 100 percent network coverage of its service territory by mid-2014.[^2] All of Houston Water's customers are metered and billed based on usage.[^10] The Water Bill Improvement Plan was launched on April 1, 2024, with the goal of eliminating inaccurate water billing and addressing system-wide issues by enhancing transparency, updating technology, and improving customer service.[^5] Migrating to a Software as a Service solution simplifies IT management and improves redundancy and security.[^3] Houston Public Works plans to submit the updated Water Conservation Plan to TCEQ by May 1, 2025, following a City Council vote on September 25, 2024.[^6] The City of Houston's Water Conservation Plan includes measures to determine and control water loss as a required strategy, along with a program for universal metering and meter repair/replacement as required by TCEQ.[^6] Houston Water conducts annual water audits and has a proactive leak detection and repair program.[^10]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://houston.novusagenda.com/agendapublic/CoverSheet.aspx?ItemID=20589&MeetingID=453
- [^2]: https://waterfm.com/houston-we-have-a-solution/

- [^4]: https://www.xylem.com/en-gp/resources/webinars/going-beyond-ami-the-city-of-houstons-holistic-approach/
- [^5]: https://www.houstonpublicworks.org/improvewaterbills
- [^6]: https://www.houstontx.gov/council/committees/servicedelivery/2024_08_28/water_conservation_plan.pdf
- [^7]: https://houston.novusagenda.com/agendapublic/CoverSheet.aspx?ItemID=30062&MeetingID=702
- [^8]: https://www.houstonpublicmedia.org/articles/news/city-of-houston/2024/06/25/491785/as-city-of-houston-continues-to-address-water-bill-issues-some-customers-could-pay-more-as-new-equipment-is-installed/
- [^9]: https://www.houstonpublicmedia.org/articles/news/city-of-houston/2024/12/11/508483/houston-public-works-most-of-125000-malfunctioning-non-operational-water-meters-replaced/
- [^10]: https://www.houstonpublicworks.org/sites/g/files/nwywnm456/files/doc/003-houston_water_conservation_plan_02212025.pdf
- [^11]: https://www.houstonchronicle.com/politics/houston/article/water-meter-reader-replacement-21018055.php

# Research Report: Real-Time Benzene Alert and Monitoring System (RABITS)

## Solution Overview

The Houston Health Department developed and operates the Rapid Alert Benzene Information: Time Sensitive (RABITS) system to inform where and when elevated benzene concentrations are detected so the department can respond more quickly with more specific monitoring or enforcement.[^8] Akash Gulati's benzene monitoring framework serves as a cornerstone of this alert system.[^6] This approach will hold organizations responsible for their fugitive emissions and create heightened public awareness of the risks of extended benzene exposure.[^6]

## Technical Components

The RABITS system was developed by Raun and her team as an alarm system that can notify them when benzene levels rise.[^5] Akash Gulati mapped all the benzene point sources in Houston and used wind direction data from stationary monitors to identify a subset of point sources that could be responsible for benzene emissions.[^6] The Houston Health Department operates and maintains an ambient air monitoring network for Houston that follows U.S. EPA protocols and quality assurance/control procedures, funded through grants from the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency and the TCEQ.[^4] Nine stationary air monitors and a mobile unit attached to an electric vehicle will be installed in communities including Meadowbrook, Allendale, Pecan Park and Park Place to detect levels of cancer-causing chemicals in the air, including benzene, ethylene oxide, formaldehyde and 1,3-butadiene.[^2] A community-focused mobile air monitoring platform approach will be utilized by the community to monitor for air toxic emissions.[^7] The department's original mobile lab will undergo an upgrade to its air monitoring equipment through a grant funded by the Environmental Defense Fund.[^3] Nine community locations will be selected by the community for yearlong sampling with passive samplers for benzene and 1,3-butadiene monitoring, including homes, Milby High school, and Houston Botanic Garden.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The Houston Health Department received the Notice of Award of the Enhanced Air Quality Monitoring Grant in May 2023.[^1] The department received $500,000 in grant money from the EPA for air monitoring in communities in East Houston.[^2] The City of Houston's community monitoring targets four hazardous air pollutants that potentially pose risk to communities: 1,3-butadiene, benzene, formaldehyde, and ethylene oxide.[^7] The monitoring area is in close proximity to TPC and Goodyear, which are two large manufacturing plants that are the largest emission sources of 1,3-butadiene in Houston.[^1] The study on benzene monitoring thresholds was commissioned by the Houston Health Department and is awaiting peer review.[^9]

## Benefits and Impacts

The monitoring effort will provide information to inform where elevated levels originate, provide data for regulators to compare against fenceline monitoring and use in evaluation of the effectiveness of fenceline monitoring, and provide a baseline of concentration levels prior to TPC expansion.[^1] The City of Houston is concerned about the levels of toxic air pollutants, specifically: 1,3-butadiene, benzene, formaldehyde, and ethylene oxide.[^1] 2019 EPA assessments indicate a significant increase in hazardous air pollutants since 2014 in the communities of Allendale/Meadowbrook, Pecan Park, and Park Place.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This system addresses air quality monitoring challenges that can be exacerbated by climate-related factors affecting industrial emissions and pollutant dispersion patterns. The monitoring infrastructure provides critical data for understanding toxic air pollutant exposure in vulnerable communities experiencing elevated hazardous air pollution levels.

## Business Analysis

The implementation relies on a multi-source funding model combining federal and private foundation support. The Houston Health Department secured $500,000 from the EPA for enhanced air quality monitoring in East Houston communities.[^2] Additional funding comes through grants from the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency and the TCEQ for operating and maintaining the ambient air monitoring network.[^4] The Environmental Defense Fund provided grant funding for upgrading the department's original mobile lab air monitoring equipment.[^3] This diversified funding approach demonstrates a public-private partnership model that leverages federal environmental protection resources alongside philanthropic foundation support to expand community-level air quality surveillance capabilities.

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.houstonhealth.org/enhanced-air-quality-monitoring
- [^2]: https://www.houstonpublicmedia.org/articles/news/health-science/2023/07/11/456626/epa-grants-funding-for-air-monitoring-in-east-houston-targeting-communities-most-affected-by-pollution/
- [^3]: https://www.houstonhealth.org/news/news-releases/houston-health-department-expands-air-monitoring-capacity-new-mobile-laboratory
- [^4]: https://www.houstonhealth.org/services/pollution-control/environmental-monitoring
- [^5]: https://www.houstonchronicle.com/news/houston-texas/houston/article/Houston-develops-tool-to-better-monitor-levels-of-14383089.php
- [^6]: https://business.edf.org/climate-corps/projects/city-of-houston-akash-gulati-2020/
- [^7]: https://www.epa.gov/newsreleases/epa-announces-nearly-500000-grant-funding-high-risk-houston-communities
- [^8]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Houston-US/Huston-CAP-April2020.pdf
- [^9]: https://grist.org/accountability/after-a-houston-area-chemical-fire-toxic-benzene-lingered-for-weeks-endangering-residents/

# Research Report: DIY Air Cleaner Program for Wildfire Smoke Protection

## Solution Overview

The City of Vancouver expanded a DIY air cleaner pilot program in partnership with the Pacific Institute for Pathogens, Pandemics, and Society to provide supplies for 300 air cleaners and enable community members to build their own filtration devices.[^1] This initiative targets seniors and low-income residents to help them filter air in their homes during wildfire smoke events.[^2] The program was categorized as Critical priority and scheduled for completion by 2025.[^1]

## Technical Components

The DIY air cleaner design uses a box fan, a MERV 13 filter, and duct tape as its core components.[^5] The simplest formulation of this approach (box fan with taped on MERV 13 furnace filter) achieved a Clean Air Delivery Rate (CADR) of 111.2 ± 1.3 ft³/min.[^12] Large increases in CADR could be obtained by using a shroud (40%), using a 4″ thick filter (123%), using two filters in a wedge shape (137%), or using four filters in a Corsi-Rosenthal box design (261%).[^12] The smaller 1×1 design is optimal for people living in smaller spaces, easier to transport, and more straightforward to have workshop participants build on their own.[^7] CADR increased with increasing fan speed, increasing filter thickness, increasing filter rating (MERV), increasing number of filters used, and increasing particle size.[^12] Reported CADRs for DIY air cleaners range from 49 CFM to 825 CFM, depending upon the fan/filter features and particle characteristics.[^12] The CADR was greatly reduced with filters heavily loaded with smoke, pointing to the need for frequent filter changes during smoke events.[^12] Studies from the National Collaborating Centre for Environmental Health and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency that simulated the effects of wildfire smoke showed the DIY filters are about as effective as commercial ones at pulling particles out of the air.[^3][^6] DIY air cleaner designs with a cardboard shroud and multiple filters increased the cost effectiveness making them more effective at removing particles than higher-priced commercial units.[^11]

## Implementation Details

The pilot was initiated in 2023 as part of Vancouver's poor air quality adaptation actions.[^1] Since 2023, the City partnered with the Pacific Institute for Pathogens, Pandemics and Society and the BC Lung Foundation to build and distribute the air cleaners, with more than 230 air cleaners built and sent home with seniors and low-income residents who need them.[^2] The BREATHE Project, a collaboration by Simon Fraser University, the BC Lung Foundation, and The Pacific Institute on Pathogens, Pandemics and Society (PIPPS), helped communities protect themselves by teaching people to build their own air cleaners.[^8] Dr. Anne-Marie Nicol, an associate professor of professional practice at Simon Fraser University, started the BREATHE Project in 2023.[^5] The workshops are mainly funded by Simon Fraser University and the BC Lung Foundation, with additional support from the B.C. Health Ministry, health authorities and municipalities.[^5] The project began as a pilot in the Vancouver area and now runs workshops across B.C., showing participants how to build air cleaners.[^5] BREATHE has partnered with all of B.C.'s health authorities and hosted over 90 workshops so far this year in northern, rural and remote regions including the Cowichan Valley, Lower Mainland, Central Okanagan, the Kootenays and the Northern Rockies.[^9] Volunteer Lyra Murphy showed seniors at the Chinatown Plaza in downtown Vancouver how to build a DIY air cleaner as part of the B.C. Lung Foundation workshops for people who are more vulnerable to the effects of wildfire smoke.[^3] About 20 participants took part in the session at Chinatown Plaza making the filters and learning about how to keep their indoor air clean and wildfire smoke-free.[^3][^6] The project now focuses on targeted outreach through seniors' centres and community organizations, with groups able to apply to host a workshop through the BREATHE Project website.[^5] The B.C. Lung Foundation's DIY air cleaner design costs less than $100 to make, compared to store-bought air purifiers that can sell for a couple hundred dollars.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The project has helped build over 2,500 air cleaners.[^4] Workshop participants left with not just knowledge, but a functional air cleaner and the confidence to use it effectively during future wildfire smoke events.[^10] Participants in the DIY air cleaner workshops leave with a little less climate anxiety than they came in with, armed with knowledge about how to protect themselves during fire season.[^6] The distribution of air cleaners to vulnerable populations reduces exposure and negative health impacts of wildfire smoke and other air pollutants to those who do not have access to other forms of air filtration, in addition to improving community capacity and solutioning.[^1] These health-promotion and preparedness devices can drastically improve quality of life during wildfire smoke events, particularly for vulnerable populations without access to air filtration.[^1] EPA researchers evaluated a range of DIY air cleaner designs and a low-cost commercial air cleaner to identify the lowest cost and most effective design, with costs including the initial construction or purchase cost and estimated operating costs for electricity.[^11] UL tested five models of box fans with clean filters and with filters loaded with smoke and dust provided by EPA under several scenarios including an extreme scenario where the front and back of the fan were both blocked for an extended period of seven hours.[^11] Throughout the testing, temperatures of all fan components remained safely below recognized temperature safety standards, and none of the scenarios tested posed any observable fire hazards.[^11]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The do-it-yourself workshops were developed in response to evidence gathered during the acute phase of the COVID-19 pandemic that showed simple DIY air cleaners were very effective in reducing indoor airborne contaminants.[^8] BREATHE added additional resources for communities grappling with the co-exposure of wildfire smoke and extreme heat.[^9] BREATHE serves as a launchpad for research on the impacts of wildfire smoke on at-risk populations across the province.[^9]

## Business Analysis

Following a DIY air cleaner workshop pilot program in 2023, the SFU Air Cleaner team developed a 'train-the-trainer guide' in consultation with public health officials.[^7] The train-the-trainer guide is for organizations, municipalities, and emergency management personnel wanting to host their own DIY air cleaner workshops to build resiliency to wildfires and other indoor air pollution.[^7] The guide provides comprehensive information on the scientific evidence behind DIY air cleaners, along with logistical considerations and potential challenges.[^7][^8] The train-the-trainer guide provides information on the research and evidence behind the units, the logistics of hosting a workshop, the potential costs, and the challenges that may arise.[^7] The BREATHE Project provides step-by-step instructions translated into 11 languages including Spanish, French, Tagalog, Traditional Chinese, Simplified Chinese, Punjabi, Korean, Farsi, Portuguese, Vietnamese, and Japanese.[^7] EPA researchers are working with tribal and local partners to evaluate the effectiveness of air cleaner use in homes to improve indoor air quality and health measures during smoke events in the ASPIRE-Health Study.[^11] EPA tested the effectiveness of DIY air cleaners in removing smoke particles in laboratories in Research Triangle Park, NC using simulated wildfire smoke made from smoldering pine needles in a room-sized chamber to determine the Clean Air Delivery Rate.[^11]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://vancouver.ca/home-property-development/wildfire-smoke.aspx

# Research Report: Automated Water and Rain Monitoring System with Provincial Integration

## Solution Overview

The Huangshi Municipal Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau is constructing an automatic measuring and reporting system of water and rain that connects with the provincial flood monitoring system to realize information sharing through a special communication module.[^1] This approach aims to realize intelligent identification and prediction of disasters to provide basis for the government or drainage control department to formulate effective preventive measures.[^1] The implementation addresses critical gaps in Huangshi's current flood management, where information acquisition on flood disaster mainly relies on human beings, which is identified and verified based on experience and of low efficiency, making it difficult to predict disasters.[^1]

## Technical Components

The system integrates automatic water and rain measurement capabilities with provincial-level flood monitoring infrastructure through dedicated communication modules.[^1] To enhance asset management, the project developed a smart system that integrated a geographic information system database and online monitoring system, which surveyed 812 km of the urban wastewater network.[^2] Comparable implementations demonstrate the technical scope possible, as Fuzhou's Water Intelligent System covers 1000 reservoirs, lakes, pools, rivers, gates, stations and underground rainwater and sewage pipelines.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The automatic measuring and reporting system establishes direct connectivity with provincial flood monitoring infrastructure to enable real-time information sharing across jurisdictional boundaries.[^1] The geographic information system database and online monitoring system surveyed 812 km of the urban wastewater network as part of the asset management enhancement.[^2] This implementation addresses Huangshi's current reliance on human-based information acquisition, which operates at lower informatization levels and makes disaster prediction difficult.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The intelligent identification and prediction capabilities provide basis for the government or drainage control department to formulate effective preventive measures.[^1] Nationally, the annual average number of deaths and missing persons caused by flash floods has decreased by nearly 90% compared with the period before the system was built.[^3] By the end of 2022, China has built the world's largest and most extensive flash flood disaster monitoring and warning system, covering more than 2,000 counties in 31 provinces.[^3] Advanced warning systems demonstrate operational efficiency, as delivery time of high-level alerts to decision-makers averaged less than 1 minute, measured as the average time taken for the highest-level red alert to be delivered from the platform to the communication terminals of designated emergency managers at all levels.[^5] Annual reach of early warnings totaled 28 billion instances, representing the total number of warning messages delivered annually across all channels.[^5]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The automatic water and rain measuring system directly addresses flood hazards through intelligent disaster identification and prediction capabilities that enable proactive response measures.[^1] The provincial connectivity ensures coordinated flood monitoring across broader geographic scales, enhancing regional resilience to extreme precipitation events.[^1]

## Business Analysis

China operates a unified national emergency warning information release system that is vertically connected at the national, provincial, municipal and county levels, horizontally connected with 22 ministries, and uses the Common Alert Protocol.[^5] This institutional framework supports coordinated implementation across jurisdictions, as CMA issues risk warnings of geological hazards and flash floods jointly with ministries of natural and water resources.[^5] Comparable urban flood control investments demonstrate the financial scale of comprehensive systems, with Fuzhou's Water Intelligent System requiring total investments over 8.5 million EUR to cover 1000 reservoirs, lakes, pools, rivers, gates, stations and underground rainwater and sewage pipelines.[^4]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/China/Huangshi/Huangshi-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^2]: https://rksi.adb.org/publications/transforming-an-industrial-city-into-an-eco-friendly-tourist-city-2/
- [^3]: https://iwra.org/proceedings/congress/resource/IWRA2023_Beijing_China_20230913_SS_3_5_ORAL_4_Dongya_Sun_China_flash_flood_early_warning_systems.pdf
- [^4]: https://waterknowledgehub.org/case-study/china-digital-smart-flood-control-under-water-intelligent-system-fuzhou
- [^5]: https://wmo.int/media/magazine-article/governance-and-artificial-intelligence-keys-integrated-end-end-approach-early-warnings-all

# Research Report: MERIT Economic Impact Assessment Tool for Infrastructure Resilience

## Solution Overview

MERIT (Measuring the Economics of Resilient Infrastructure Tool) is an economic impact assessment tool developed by GNS Science, Market Economics and Resilient Organisations that models the economic impact resulting from a loss of lifeline services due to water, power, roading outages and other infrastructure disruptions.[^1] This dynamic economic analysis tool was specifically designed to evaluate 'shock' events and help resilience planners estimate the economic consequences of disruptive events.[^2][^1] The Wellington Lifelines Resilience Programme project was commissioned by the Wellington Region Lifelines Group to assess infrastructure vulnerabilities and investment priorities.[^4]

## Technical Components

The MERIT model is calibrated to a 2013 social accounting matrix for the Wellington region and is solved using the GEMPACK software.[^6] The system includes a detailed representation of the region's infrastructure networks, including electricity, gas, water, and transport, with the ability to model the impacts of disruptions to these networks on different sectors of the economy.[^6] The model has 35 sectors based on the Australian and New Zealand Standard Industrial Classification (ANZSIC).[^6] MERIT applies Social Accounting Matrices (SAMs) to understand economic value chains in greater detail, enabling planners to identify and mitigate pinch points in the economy that could delay response, recovery and rebuild.[^1] The tool includes an Inoperability MERIT module that provides data for short-run outages between 1-day and 1-week from localised small-scale disruption events such as electricity, gas, or telecommunication outages, designed to assess economic impacts associated with small-to-medium sized disruption events.[^5] MERIT runs comfortably over a 50-year time horizon, with the first 15-years calibrated against real-world data.[^5] The system reports various economic aggregate impacts including output, income, value added, GDP, factor prices, commodity prices, and welfare measures by industry for both the regional and national economy.[^5]

## Implementation Details

Smith N, McDonald G, Kim J-H, Ayers M, Brown C, and Seville E published the Wellington Resilience Programme Business Case: Modelling the Economics of Resilient Infrastructure Tool (MERIT) Assumptions Report in 2017.[^1] Brown C, Smith N, Buxton R, McDonald G, Daly M, and Sevill E published the Wellington Water resilience project report in 2016 for Wellington Water.[^1] The development of MERIT was halfway through a 4-year government funded development at the time of reporting.[^7] The modelling was carried out using MERIT as a state of the art economic modelling tool developed to estimate the economic impacts of infrastructure disruption.[^4] The preferred programme of investment comprises 25 resilience projects at an estimated total capital cost of $3.9 billion.[^3] MERIT capabilities were extended to cover 'welfare' impacts to enable decision-makers and planners to target resilience measures to the most vulnerable communities.[^1] The tool was further extended to provide decision-makers with assessments of physical and financial impacts of natural hazards as well as social and environmental impacts through dynamic assessment of multiple capitals.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

MERIT estimated that proposed infrastructure investment options valued at NZD2016 2.2–2.6 billion would result in reduced event losses of NZD2016 6 billion over 5 years for Wellington.[^2] The modelling revealed that if a magnitude 7.5 earthquake occurs on the Wellington Fault with no investment (the do-nothing scenario), the expected loss to New Zealand's GDP over a 5-year period will exceed $16 billion.[^3] If the preferred investment programme is implemented before the earthquake occurs, the expected economic loss reduces to $10 billion over a 5-year period, and a $6 billion impact to New Zealand's economy is avoided.[^3] A coordinated investment of $3.9 billion would save the nation $6 billion in the aftermath of a magnitude 7.5 earthquake on the Wellington Fault.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This economic modeling approach addresses climate adaptation by quantifying the financial consequences of infrastructure failures resulting from natural hazards, enabling evidence-based investment decisions for resilience improvements. The tool's capability to assess disruptions across water, power, transport, and other critical lifeline services provides decision-makers with comprehensive assessments of how infrastructure vulnerabilities compound economic losses during extreme events. By modeling both short-term outages and long-term recovery scenarios over a 50-year horizon, the system supports strategic planning for climate-related infrastructure risks.

## Business Analysis

The MERIT analysis demonstrated substantial economic returns on resilience investments, with a benefit-cost ratio showing that $3.9 billion in coordinated infrastructure improvements would prevent $6 billion in economic losses from a major seismic event.[^3] This financial modeling provides a compelling business case for proactive infrastructure investment, demonstrating that resilience measures deliver measurable economic value beyond traditional cost-benefit frameworks. The tool's integration with RiskScape, a multi-hazard risk assessment tool developed by GNS Science and NIWA that estimates damage and direct losses for assets exposed to natural hazards, enables comprehensive risk-informed investment planning.[^3] Government funding supported the 4-year development of MERIT, reflecting public sector recognition of the need for sophisticated economic modeling tools to guide infrastructure resilience investments.[^7] The system's ability to assess welfare impacts by household type enables targeted allocation of resilience resources to protect vulnerable communities, supporting equitable investment frameworks that address both economic efficiency and social equity objectives.[^1]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/87552930231195040
- [^3]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Asia & Asia Pacific Oceania/Wellington-New-Zealand/Wellington-Lifelines-PBC-MAIN-Combined-20191009.pdf
- [^4]: https://www.resorgs.org.nz/our-projects/risk-communication/wellington-lifelines-resilience-programme-business-case/
- [^5]: https://www.gns.cri.nz/research-projects/merit/

# Research Report: LED Streetlight and Smart Traffic Signal Infrastructure Upgrade

## Solution Overview

The City of Dallas is implementing a comprehensive infrastructure modernization program focused on converting its streetlight network to energy-efficient LED technology and upgrading traffic signal systems with intelligent network technologies.[^6] This approach aims to reduce energy consumption, lower operating costs, and enhance system reliability during severe weather events.[^6] The initiative encompasses both immediate lighting retrofits and advanced traffic management capabilities designed to improve urban infrastructure resilience.[^1][^2]

## Technical Components

The Smart Lighting initiative focuses on converting the City's 85,000 streetlights to energy-efficient LED technology, replacing existing high-pressure sodium or metal halide bulbs with LED fixtures.[^2][^1] The traffic signal network upgrade incorporates sensors that detect the presence of vehicles, with the system adjusting itself in real time across hundreds of intersections.[^4] Dallas signed an agreement with Ericsson, a provider of communications and Internet of Things infrastructure, to provide hardware and software upgrades for its traffic management systems.[^4] Ericsson's Intelligent Transport System technology is based on open platforms with application programming interfaces (APIs) and services, allowing developers and local stakeholders to easily access published APIs and make use of available data in a controlled and secure way.[^4] The Transportation Department implements the installation of fiberoptic cable through a price agreement with Durable Specialties, Inc., which was authorized in December 2018 by City Council Resolution No. 18-1787.[^3] The video integration phase of the traffic management system includes automatic camera focusing and information transmission back to the transportation center, with completion scheduled by summer.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The lighting plan is divided into two phases, with the first consisting of upgrading 1,000 city-owned lights from their current high-pressure sodium or metal halide bulbs to LED fixtures.[^1] Phase two includes infrastructure updates like traffic signals and pedestrian improvements in addition to converting 500 street lights and adding 190 new fixtures within five years.[^1] The city is converting 1,000 street lights to LED and adding almost 200 lights to poorly lit areas.[^1] Most of the 1,000 lights to be upgraded are in southern Dallas but a few are in the northern part of the city.[^1] The first project upgraded 76 lights to LED along South Malcolm X Boulevard near Marburg Street, a corridor in South Dallas.[^1] Phase two lighting updates are concentrated in five areas and funded in part by the county: South Walton Walker Boulevard, Lancaster Road, Simpson Stuart Road and South Buckner Boulevard in southern Dallas and Vickery Meadow in northeast Dallas.[^1] The City's Transportation Department implements the public street lighting improvement project through a service price agreement with Highway Intelligent Traffic Solutions, Inc., which was authorized in May 2020 by City Council Resolution No. 20-0821.[^3] The street lighting improvement project in the Skillman Corridor TIF District was scheduled to begin construction in March 2022 and complete construction in December 2022.[^3] On December 3, 2021, the Skillman Corridor TIF District Board of Directors reviewed the public street lighting improvement project and unanimously recommended City Council approval.[^3] The $500,000 LED upgrade project, already in progress, is estimated to take up to a year to complete.[^1] Dallas Transportation Director Gus Khankarli said it's going to take about five to seven years to convert based on the projected life of a bulb.[^5] City officials said they will examine the cost and negotiate with Oncor for a total conversion plan.[^5]

## Benefits and Impacts

The $500,000 project will save the city $12,000 annually.[^1] The benefits of LED street lights include energy preservation, more illumination and less frequent maintenance.[^1] LED lights are more expensive, but there is a potential long-term cost-saving advantage to changing with much lower LED energy costs.[^5] Michael Rogers, director of Dallas' transportation department, said that the department's biggest line item is the energy cost of the current lights: $17 million, which also includes Oncor and TXU Energy's fees.[^1] The Smart Lighting initiative aims to create a platform for other smart city applications, such as public Wi-Fi, environmental sensors, and security cameras.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This comprehensive retrofit program directly addresses climate resilience by reducing lighting outages during weather events through more durable LED technology and enhanced traffic signal network capabilities.[^6] The infrastructure improvements strengthen the city's ability to maintain critical transportation and safety systems during extreme weather conditions while simultaneously reducing energy consumption and operating costs.[^6]

## Business Analysis

Of nearly 95,000 streetlights in Dallas, just over one in 10 are LED lights now.[^5] The City has converted over 10,000 streetlights to LED technology.[^2] Around 15,000 Dallas streetlights are owned by the City and nearly half of those are LEDs.[^5] Electric provider Oncor owns and maintains 72,000 of the lights and less than one in 20 of the Oncor lights are LED.[^5] Oncor, the state's largest electric utility company, owns 75% of the 80,000 street lights in Dallas.[^1] Oncor replaces dimmer orange or white mercury vapor lights with LEDs when streetlights burn out.[^5] The phased implementation approach allows the city to manage capital expenditures while demonstrating cost savings, with the $500,000 initial project generating $12,000 in annual savings.[^1] County funding support for phase two projects demonstrates multi-jurisdictional investment in the infrastructure modernization program.[^1]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.dallasnews.com/news/2020/11/09/dallas-upgrading-1000-street-lights-to-led-expanding-wi-fi-to-needed-areas/
- [^2]: https://dallascityhall.com/departments/ciservices/smart-cities/DCH%20Documents/smart-city-initiatives-v2.pdf
- [^3]: https://cityofdallas.legistar.com/LegislationDetail.aspx?ID=5462543&GUID=AB64E209-A5C0-4419-BB34-3A4F802D4EFA&FullText=1
- [^4]: https://www.govtech.com/transportation/Dallas-Gives-Green-Light-to-New-Transportation-Management-System.html
- [^5]: https://www.nbcdfw.com/news/local/switch-to-brighter-led-dallas-streetlights-considered/2927278/
- [^6]: https://dallascityhall.com/government/Council%20Meeting%20Documents/qolac_2_city-environmental-and-climate-planning-efforts_combined_011419.pdf

# Research Report: Toyama City LoRaWAN IoT Sensor Network for Smart Infrastructure

## Solution Overview

The Toyama City Sensor Network was built as the FY2018 Toyama City Infrastructure Development Project for the Promotion of the Smart City.[^2] Using a group of LoRaWAN communication standard receiving antennas, the wireless communication network is expected to cover 98.9 percent of the population in the city because it is installed at government facilities such as schools and local community centers.[^2] By using this network, the city government can perform the role of a telecommunications carrier to offer a communication network that can be used via IoT sensors.[^2]

## Technical Components

The LPWA network was developed in 2018, and antennas were installed in about 100 public facilities in the city.[^3] The wireless communication network is installed at 100 places as of January 2020.[^2] This infrastructure consists of wireless communication networks that can be used via IoT sensors and others and the system to manage the information collected from sensors through a platform.[^2] The network covers 98.9% of the resident population.[^3] With the installation of a variety of sensors, the system collects information that includes the movement of people and things and environmental information such as river water level and atmospheric temperature.[^2]

## Implementation Details

Municipal authorities launched the network in 2018 as part of the city's smart infrastructure initiative.[^2][^3] The implementation strategically positioned receiving antennas at government facilities such as schools and local community centers to maximize population coverage.[^2] In FY2019, Toyama started a demonstration experiment that allows private businesses to utilise the sensor network free of charge, with 23 case studies (36 groups) adopted from applications in fields such as transportation/people flow, agriculture, remote monitoring/disaster prevention, welfare/nursing care, and performance evaluation/facility management.[^3] In Toyama City, school children carried GPS trackers in their school bags so that the local authorities could track their actual routes to and from school, and implement safety measures to specific points according to the results.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The network is expected to perform an important role as a monitoring device for disaster mitigation—for example, in the prevention of flooding during heavy rain by collecting information that includes the movement of people and things and environmental information such as river water level and atmospheric temperature.[^2] The demonstration experiment enabled diverse applications across multiple sectors, with 36 participating groups testing use cases ranging from transportation monitoring to disaster prevention.[^3] The GPS tracking application for school children enabled local authorities to implement targeted safety measures at specific points based on actual route data.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach directly addresses extreme precipitation and flooding hazards through real-time environmental monitoring capabilities. The system's ability to collect river water level data and atmospheric temperature information enables proactive flood prevention measures during heavy rain events.[^2]

## Business Analysis

The municipal government structured the initiative to function as a telecommunications carrier, offering IoT sensor connectivity as a public service.[^2] The FY2019 demonstration experiment provided free network access to private businesses, attracting applications from diverse sectors including agriculture, welfare, nursing care, and facility management.[^3] This public-private partnership model enabled 23 case studies across 36 participating organizations to test commercial applications without upfront infrastructure costs.[^3] The strategic placement of antennas at existing government facilities optimized capital deployment while achieving near-universal population coverage.[^2][^3]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.nec.com/en/global/insights/article/smart-city-world-congress-2022/index.html
- [^2]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Asia & Asia Pacific Oceania/Toyama-Japan/The-Development-Story-of-Toyama-Reshaping-Compact-and-Livable-Cities.pdf
- [^3]: https://www.jlgc.org.uk/en/news_letter/toyama-citys-smart-city-sensor-network/

# Research Report: InVEST Ecosystem Services Modeling for Climate Adaptation Planning

## Solution Overview

Istanbul Metropolitan Municipality staff received comprehensive training on the InVEST modeling program through SECAP workshops, which were conducted to build capacity in climate adaptation, mitigation, and monitoring.[^1] These workshops included training on technical tools and methodologies for assessing climate risks and ecosystem services.[^1] The initiative was developed under the EU4 Energy Transition: Covenant of Mayors in the Western Balkans and Turkey project.[^1]

## Technical Components

The InVEST (Integrated Valuation of Ecosystem Services and Tradeoffs) modeling program is a suite of free, open-source software models developed by Stanford University's Natural Capital Project.[^7] The Habitat Quality model within InVEST uses habitat quality and rarity as proxies to represent the biodiversity of a landscape, estimating the extent of habitat and vegetation types across a landscape and their state of degradation.[^7] The model combines maps of land use land cover (LULC) with data on threats to habitats and habitat response.[^7] Habitat quality and rarity in the InVEST model are functions of four factors: each threat's relative impact, the relative sensitivity of each habitat type to each threat, the distance between habitats and sources of threats, and the degree to which the land is legally protected.[^7]

## Implementation Details

IMM staff were given detailed information on the use of the InVEST modeling program as part of SECAP workshops.[^1][^2][^3] Using this technology, a map of Istanbul's biodiversity and habitat degradation areas was produced, and the relevant indexes were presented in a table.[^1][^2][^3] Through analysis and modeling, 10 vulnerable areas were identified in Istanbul's forests, serving as a basis for adaptation planning and awareness activities.[^8]

## Benefits and Impacts

The implementation produced comprehensive spatial analysis outputs that enable municipal staff to visualize biodiversity patterns and habitat degradation across Istanbul's landscape.[^1][^2][^3] The identification of 10 vulnerable forest areas provides a concrete foundation for targeted adaptation planning and awareness activities.[^8] The capacity-building approach equips municipal staff with technical expertise in assessing climate risks and ecosystem services, enabling ongoing monitoring and analysis capabilities.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach addresses climate adaptation needs by providing spatial assessment tools that identify vulnerable biodiversity and habitat areas requiring protection or restoration. The mapping of habitat degradation areas enables municipal authorities to prioritize climate adaptation interventions in forest ecosystems that provide critical services such as temperature regulation, water retention, and carbon sequestration. The identification of 10 vulnerable forest areas creates a targeted framework for implementing adaptation measures that strengthen ecosystem resilience against climate impacts.[^8]

## Business Analysis

The use of free, open-source software models from Stanford University's Natural Capital Project eliminates licensing costs and enables replicable implementation across municipal departments.[^7] The capacity-building model through SECAP workshops represents an investment in institutional knowledge that creates ongoing analytical capabilities without recurring software expenses.[^1] The EU4 Energy Transition: Covenant of Mayors in the Western Balkans and Turkey project provided the policy framework and likely financial support for this training initiative, demonstrating how international climate cooperation programs can facilitate technology transfer and capacity building.[^1]

## Sources

# Research Report: Multifamily Resilience and Solar Assessment Tool

## Solution Overview

The District of Columbia developed a Multifamily Resilience and Solar Assessment Tool to comprehensively identify opportunities to improve the resilience of affordable housing properties to climate change impacts.[^5] This initiative emerged from Washington, D.C.'s Department of Energy and Environment (DOEE) planning efforts to prepare the district for a changing climate, which included developing a tool to assess affordable multifamily housing for resilience.[^3] Kate Johnson, the chief of the green building and climate branch at DOEE, stated that one of the actions needed was a much deeper assessment of vulnerability of affordable housing after seeing maps showing how vulnerable the housing could be in the future due to flooding from severe storms.[^3]

## Technical Components

The assessment tool is comprised of three main components: a resilience assessment, an energy and water savings opportunity assessment, and a solar + storage assessment.[^2] This system enables affordable housing owners to assess the vulnerability of properties to climate change impacts and to identify opportunities to improve resilience, reduce energy use, install solar and install battery storage systems.[^1] The Cadmus Group LLC prepared a user manual for the DC DOEE Resilience Tool, Version 1.0, dated August 28, 2019.[^4] Following the creation of the tool and the pilot site assessments, New Ecology, Inc. (NEI), Enterprise Community Partners, and the National Housing Trust (NHT) commenced work on a user manual for the tool, to be presented during trainings to housing owners and professionals in order to grow the technical capacity for resilience planning in the affordable multifamily space.[^2]

## Implementation Details

DOEE awarded $250,000 to Enterprise Community Partners, in partnership with New Ecology, the National Housing Trust, and the Clean Energy Group, to develop the vulnerability assessment and resilience tool.[^1] The funding came from the District of Columbia's Department of Energy and Environment (DOEE) Solar for All program, which supported New Ecology, Inc. (NEI), Enterprise Community Partners, the National Housing Trust and Clean Energy Group to develop the vulnerability and resilience opportunity assessment tool for affordable housing owners to promote sustainability and resilience in their properties.[^2] The final component of this project is a strategic map to illuminate the routes to incorporating resilience assessment into existing funding, financing, and regulatory processes in the District, showing the key touch points and funding co-benefits for resilience planning for affordable housing management and development.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The project advances the goals of Climate Ready DC, a project led by Kleinfelder, and DC's plan to prepare for the impacts of climate change including heatwaves, flooding, and severe storms.[^2] This approach directly addresses the vulnerability of affordable housing to climate impacts that were identified through mapping exercises showing future flooding risks from severe weather events.[^3]

## Sources
