# Research Report: Urban Heat Island Mitigation Performance Index

## Solution Overview

The Urban Heat Island Mitigation Performance Index represents a decision-support framework developed to assist governments in establishing performance targets for planning control across Sydney and regional New South Wales.[^1][^2] This approach was carried out by UNSW Sydney and Swinburne University in collaboration with government and industry partners from January 2017 to December 2018, with funding from the CRC for Low Carbon Living.[^1][^2] The system indicates impact on street level temperature, health and mortality, and precinct level energy consumption to inform urban policy, development assessment and planning practices.[^1][^2][^4]

## Technical Components

The initiative comprises a web-based decision-support tool accessible at http://uhimitigationindex.fbe.unsw.edu.au/uhitool/index.html, alongside a Microclimate and Urban Heat Island Mitigation Decision Support Tool with restricted access.[^1][^2] Login credentials for the restricted tool can be requested from UHI-Index@unsw.edu.au.[^2] The system models various mitigation strategies including cool roofs, cool pavement, greenery with water irrigation, water-misting and outdoor shading to assess their combined effects on urban temperatures.[^3] Modelling capabilities evaluate subdivision scenarios to analyze how reducing lot sizes while increasing building density affects outdoor air and surface temperatures.[^3]

## Implementation Details

Government and Industry Partners and Steering Committee for the UHI-DS project comprised: City of Sydney, Greater Sydney Commission, OEH, Landcom, Parramatta City Council, Campbelltown City Council, WSROC, SSROC, Sydney Water, Inner West Council, Waverley Council, NSW Spatial Services, Stockland, BlueScope Steel, AECOM, PSMA, UNSW and Swinburne University.[^1] The project was funded by CRC for Low Carbon Living and completed between January 2017 and December 2018.[^1][^2] Regional applications extended beyond Sydney to include analysis of areas in Maitland and Dubbo.[^3] In Southlakes, Dubbo, various subdivision scenarios were analyzed to demonstrate how implementing a combination of mitigation strategies can effectively reduce the heat effects associated with increased building density.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

Modelling showed that the use of cool roofs, cool pavement, greenery with water irrigation, water-misting and outdoor shading could reduce air temperature by 1.6°C in the areas studied in Maitland and 2°C in the areas looked at in Dubbo.[^3] Surface temperature reductions exceeded 10°C in each location analyzed.[^3] Results from Southlakes indicate that while reducing lot sizes while increasing building density leads to increased outdoor air and surface temperatures, implementing a combination of mitigation strategies can effectively reduce the heat effects associated with increased building density.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach addresses urban heat island effects that create significant temperature disparities across metropolitan Sydney. Because of the intense development of the UHI in Sydney, Cooling Degree Days in Western Sydney are about three times higher than in the Eastern coastal zone.[^2] The performance index enables governments to establish planning controls that mitigate these temperature differentials and their associated health and energy consumption impacts.[^1][^2]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.unsw.edu.au/arts-design-architecture/our-schools/built-environment/our-research/clusters-groups/high-performance-architecture/projects/uhi-ds-microclimate-urban-heat-island-mitigation-decision-support-tool
- [^2]: https://www.unsw.edu.au/research/low-carbon-living/our-research/rp2023-microclimate-and-urban-heat-island-mitigation-decision-support-tool
- [^3]: https://www.unsw.edu.au/newsroom/news/2024/12/beat-the-heat-how-unsw-researchers-are-making-cities-cooler-and-safer
- [^4]: https://heathealth.info/resources/microclimate-and-urban-heat-island-mitigation-decision-support-tool-uhi-ds/

# Research Report: Crowdsourced Disaster Reporting Platform for Emergency Response

## Solution Overview

This crowdsourced disaster reporting platform aggregates real-time citizen reports and multi-source data to support emergency response coordination across Indonesian cities. The system enables residents to submit observations of flooding and other hazards through social media and mobile applications, which are then verified and disseminated to government agencies and humanitarian organizations for rapid response efforts.[^1][^2]

## Technical Components

The platform gathers point reports from multiple data streams including Twitter, Qlue (a citizen reporting platform), and Ditik (crowdsourced news reports from Indonesia).[^3] Jakarta's emergency management agency built its own feature called the Risk Evaluation Matrix (REM), which collates reports from multiple sources, verifies them, and then disseminates the information through the portal.[^1] The collected data were used by BPBD DKI Jakarta to cross-validate formal reports of flooding from traditional data sources, supporting the creation of information for flood assessment, response, and management in real-time.[^2]

## Implementation Details

During Tropical Cyclone Seroja in April 2021, residents of East Nusa Tenggara province used the platform to report collapsed bridges, washed away homes, and power outages.[^1] When massive floods inundated the city of Jakarta for weeks in early 2021, news media cited data from the system to keep residents informed.[^1] The gathered data and alerts are shared among government agencies, NGOs, and other stakeholders that use the platform for rapid response efforts.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The system enabled BPBD DKI Jakarta to cross-validate formal flooding reports from traditional data sources in real-time, supporting flood assessment, response, and management operations.[^2] During the April 2021 tropical cyclone event, the platform facilitated citizen reporting of critical infrastructure damage including collapsed bridges, destroyed homes, and power outages across East Nusa Tenggara province.[^1] News media organizations utilized platform data to maintain public awareness during the weeks-long Jakarta flooding event in early 2021.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach addresses extreme precipitation and flooding hazards by enabling real-time citizen reporting and multi-source data verification during disaster events. The system supports rapid emergency response coordination when tropical cyclones, monsoon flooding, and other climate-related hazards impact Indonesian communities.

## Sources

- [^1]: https://restofworld.org/2021/hi-im-disaster-bot/
- [^2]: https://icos.urenio.org/applications/petajakarta-org/
- [^3]: https://inasafe.org/petajakarta-version-2-launched/index.html

# Research Report: IoT Sensors and Cloud Platform for Flood Monitoring and Response

## Solution Overview

In May 2018, the city of Houston announced it was partnering with Microsoft to improve the city's quality of life and economy by connecting digital technology with the physical world.[^2] This approach helps Houston improve its disaster response and recovery systems by better integrating data between departments and City partners. Following Hurricane Harvey, which dumped more than 1 trillion gallons of water on Harris County in 2017 and flooded more than 96,000 homes according to a report by the Harris County Flood Control District, Houston migrated to Microsoft's Azure cloud platform.[^3][^20][^21]

## Technical Components

Following Hurricane Harvey, Houston migrated its computer systems to Microsoft's Azure cloud platform.[^3][^4] Houston has broadened a network of IoT environmental sensors that collect data on water and air quality, sewer outflow and flood detection.[^3] The Harris County Flood Control District installed 153 monitoring stations using a variety of water level and precipitation measurement technologies, including OTT Radar Level Sensors (OTT RLS), OTT Compact Bubbler Sensors (CBS), and pressure transducers.[^8] The District measures rainfall and water levels in real-time to provide up-to-date flood information to aid first responders, issue flood warnings, and inform decision makers, the media, and the citizens of the region.[^8] Houston's smart city initiatives include an analytics platform that monitors parking spot inventory in real time and a system that alerts residents during active emergencies.[^3] Following Hurricane Harvey, the city realized it needed a more precise understanding of how the storm impacted residents and so embarked on the Harvey Data Project.[^5]

## Implementation Details

In May 2018, Houston and Microsoft forged a wide-ranging alliance that included plans for Microsoft to assist the city in finding technological solutions to other challenges under the umbrella of the company's smart city initiatives.[^3] The city of Houston expanded its digital alliance with Microsoft in August 2020 to innovate in big data, artificial intelligence and the digital economy.[^6] Through their joint efforts, Microsoft and the city of Houston work to advance civic innovation and smart city initiatives, economic development and recovery, and digital skilling for workforce and youth.[^6] Microsoft and Houston are working with nearby Rice University to develop research into civic applications of IoT technologies.[^3][^4] Houston is considered a flagship city by partnering with Microsoft for the Microsoft Innovation Alliance initiative, and Verizon made Houston home to the first 5G implementation in the nation.[^1] The Smart City Advisory Council is charged with engaging community stakeholders, governments, academia and industry to develop a roadmap that will speed the adoption of technology and data-driven practices in the public realm.[^1] The Advisory Council was chaired by Elizabeth Brock, Director of External Engagement for CenterPoint Energy.[^1] Marissa Aho was appointed as Chief Resilience Officer for the City of Houston.[^1] The Smart City Advisory Council sought to align the roadmap to the Resilience Strategy the City was developing as a member of 100 Resilient Cities – pioneered by the Rockefeller Foundation.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

Houston Mayor Sylvester Turner said Thursday that the city's recovery from Hurricane Harvey has been an opportunity to become a globally competitive 'smart city' by putting more investments in cloud computing, public transportation and internet-connected environmental sensors.[^4] CrisisReady is a research response platform based at Harvard that collaborates with global academic partners and tech companies and agencies to embed data-driven decision making into local and global disaster planning.[^7]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Hurricane Harvey dumped more than 1 trillion gallons of rain in September 2017 and flooded more than 96,000 houses in the city of Houston.[^4] This implementation addresses extreme precipitation and flooding hazards by providing real-time monitoring capabilities and improved data integration for disaster response and recovery systems.

## Sources

- [^4]: https://statescoop.com/houston-hurricane-harvey-sylvester-turner-smart-city/

- [^6]: https://news.microsoft.com/source/2020/08/24/mayor-turner-and-microsoft-expand-digital-alliance-with-the-city-of-houston/

# Research Report: Smart Grid Energy Management Platform for Eco-Districts

## Solution Overview

The City of Paris developed an energy system management platform at the neighbourhood level in the Clichy-Batignolles eco-district to enable real-time monitoring and optimisation of energy production and consumption.[^5] This approach, known as CORDEES (Co-Responsibility in District Energy Efficiency & Sustainability), represents the first smart grid implementation in Paris's largest eco-district.[^1] The initiative combines digital monitoring technology with collaborative governance to address the gap between theoretical building performance commitments and actual energy efficiency outcomes.[^4]

## Technical Components

The Community Energy Management Platform serves as an interoperable and multi-user system that monitors, consolidates and analyses energy data in real time from all buildings (electricity and heat) and public facilities including electric vehicle stations, street lighting and automated waste collection.[^1][^2] The platform, also referred to as Smart-E, records the energy consumption of each building on an hour-by-hour basis for each type of equipment including heating, lighting, kitchen appliances, laundry, use of consoles and computers, and television, with everything modeled at district scale.[^7] The system detects drifts and suggests optimization scenarios for energy use across the district.[^4] Deployment of sensors (instrumentation) in buildings began at the end of February 2018, representing a key step of the project.[^1][^4] The facilitator uses this data analysis platform with systemic, real-time analysis to improve the coordination of production and demand.[^5] The network consolidates all energy data from 12 real estate programs including consumption, peak consumption, production, level of autonomy, and storage capacity.[^6]

The Clichy-Batignolles eco-district incorporates substantial renewable energy infrastructure to support the monitoring system. The heating network gets 85% of its power from renewable geothermal energy for heating and hot water.[^3][^7] Water collected from the Albien aquifer has a temperature of about 30°C, and heat pumps raise the water temperature to 65°C for domestic hot water production or to 50-65°C for district heating purposes.[^7] The heat exchange station was built 10 meters below the Earth's surface to protect the environment and save space.[^7] Additionally, 35,000 square meters of photovoltaic panels on roofs provide 40% of the eco-district's electricity consumption, generating 4,500 MWh per year.[^3][^7] Public lighting and elevator consumption are offset by electricity generated from 40,000 square meters of photovoltaic roofs spread over different buildings.[^6] The insulation and bioclimatic design of buildings lower heating needs to 15 kWh per square meter per year, equivalent to the German Passivhaus standard for passive houses.[^3] Paris City Hall established a 15 kWh/sq.m/year heating limit for Clichy-Batignolles based on an indoor temperature of 18°C.[^7] All buildings meet the requirements of the Paris Climate Plan with consumption of less than 50 kWh/m²/year for housing and offices.[^8]

## Implementation Details

The CORDEES project operates in the Clichy-Batignolles eco-district, a 54 hectare site under construction at the former SNCF rail yard in the north of the Batignolles neighbourhood in Paris 17th arrondissement.[^1][^3] Building began in 2010 and was scheduled to be finished in 2024.[^7] The district includes 3,400 housing units, 140,000 square meters of offices, 38,000 square meters of public facilities, 31,000 square meters of retail space and culture and leisure spaces.[^6] The environmental ambition of Clichy-Batignolles was conceived to go much further than the objectives of the Energy Master Plan assigned for the rest of Paris, as the creation of this 54 hectare district appeared to be an opportunity to contribute to the city's energy transition goals.[^4]

The project received 4.3 million euros allocated by the European Union through the Innovative Urban Actions call for projects, with a total ERDF budget of EUR 4,364,796.48.[^1][^6] The subsidy contract was signed by the Mayor of Paris in April 2017, with a kick-off event and press conference organized in Paris City Hall in May 2017.[^1] The consortium formed around the leadership of the City of Paris and composed of the urban developer, a public lab and two start-ups specialized in smart-grids and sustainable projects.[^4] Partnership members included Ville de Paris, Une autre ville (UAV) as a private company, Paris Batignolles Aménagement (PBA) as a public/private company, ARMINES as a research center, and EMBIX as a private company.[^1] The project was led by the City of Paris in partnership with Paris & Métropole Aménagement, the companies EMBIX and Une Autre Ville, and the engineering school MINES ParisTech through its research centre for energy efficiency of systems.[^3]

The consortium completed public procurement for the deployment of smart-grids in Clichy-Batignolles and planned to start implementation from September 2017, with interventions scheduled according to buildings' delivery to avoid temporary embarrassment.[^4] In June 2017, the project held its first workshop with inhabitants and local associations to get them involved and imagine future services.[^1] The project planned to deliver a BtoC community portal V1 by April 2019 and energy services developed for operators and end-users by June 2019.[^1] By October 2019, the project planned to have the final version of the Community Energy Management Platform developed, with standard clauses for new economic conditions and contracting scheme in place, and the Urban Energy New Deal and Urban Sustainability Trustee Facilitator elaborated.[^1]

Paris Batignolles Aménagement drove an expertise of the heating network in the Clichy-Batignolles district after delivery of the East side and discovered important drifts between the theoretical performances contractualized with real estate developers and the real performances when the buildings are occupied.[^4] The challenge of the project was to move from theoretical performances commitment with real estate developers to real performances commitment with all the actors of the energy chain, from the energy providers to the end-users passing by the building developers.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

The project aimed to reach the energy efficiency objective of 50 kWh per square meter and 90% less CO2 emissions in the Clichy-Batignolles eco-district.[^1][^2] Based on the Smart-E model, the district population was to be informed about the ecological actions they can take and, in parallel, studies would be conducted to balance heat and electricity supply and demand as effectively as possible.[^7] The CEMP development in Clichy-Batignolles was intended to pave the way for Paris's Scheme Manager for Digital City to improve digital services for citizens and public administrations, develop ICT for urban management, foster democratic participation and enable new services through open data.[^4] The Clichy-Batignolles eco-district was intended to be a trial neighborhood for the future urban districts of Greater Paris, with its 131 cities and population of 7.2 million.[^7]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The initiative addresses climate adaptation through comprehensive energy efficiency measures that reduce greenhouse gas emissions while building resilience into urban infrastructure. The project's focus on real-time monitoring and optimization of energy systems enables the district to respond dynamically to changing conditions and maintain performance standards. The integration of renewable energy sources and passive building design reduces vulnerability to energy supply disruptions while contributing to citywide climate transition goals.

## Business Analysis

The project established a first of its kind multi-stakeholders energy governance system called the Urban Energy New Deal (UEND), which defines on a collective basis the energy commitments and the contractual, financial and regulatory conditions necessary to its successful implementation.[^1][^9] This new deal was designed to move from theoretical performance commitments with real estate developers to real performance commitments with all actors of the energy chain.[^4] The project introduced a new actor called the Urban Sustainability Trustee Facilitator to provide technical recommendations, ensure stakeholder cooperation, end user empowerment and coordinate the creation and implementation of services.[^1] This energy facilitator for the neighbourhood proposes technical recommendations for the energy transition, ensures cooperation between stakeholders and assists users with the adoption of different schemes.[^5] The facilitator also coordinates the creation and implementation of new services for the neighbourhood, and the project was required to define a new business model that would ensure the energy facilitator becomes a permanent post.[^5]

The platform, led by a neighborhood energy facilitator, tested a new collaborative governance that involves the co-responsibility of actors involved in energy performance.[^6] From 2018, the project endeavored to meet these challenges by combining the new possibilities offered by digital technology with a new business model for energy governance and management at the neighbourhood level.[^5] This operation was used to test a new form of energy governance for all of the stakeholders in a neighbourhood.[^5] The City of Paris planned to work with network operators to carry out information and energy management campaigns directed at Parisians, relying on feedback from the CORDEES operation.[^5][^10]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.uia-initiative.eu/en/uia-cities/paris
- [^2]: https://portico.urban-initiative.eu/uia/cordees-co-responsibility-district-energy-efficiency-sustainability-closed
- [^3]: https://www.parisetmetropole-amenagement.fr/en/clichy-batignolles-paris-17th
- [^4]: https://www.uia-initiative.eu/en/news/projects-side-interview-cordees-project
- [^5]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Europe & Middle East/Paris-France/Paris_-_Paris_Climate_Action_Plan.pdf
- [^6]: https://www.thinksmartgrids.fr/en/actualites/smart-grids-in-paris-and-greater-paris/
- [^7]: https://www.planete-energies.com/en/media/article/clichy-batignolles-eco-district-energy-solutions
- [^8]: https://www.parisetmetropole-amenagement.fr/sites/default/files/2018-11/BD_CB_DossierPress_En_060317.pdf
- [^9]: https://portico.urban-initiative.eu/urban-stories/uia/implementing-cordees-project-paris-get-update-activities-carried-out-second-uia-expert-journal
- [^10]: https://cdn.paris.fr/paris/2019/07/24/1a706797eac9982aec6b767c56449240.pdf

# Research Report: Community-Based Open-Source Flood Risk Mapping Platform

## Solution Overview

Ramani Huria, meaning "Open Map" in Swahili, is a community-based mapping initiative launched in 2015 to create comprehensive and accurate maps of Dar es Salaam, with particular focus on the most flood-prone areas.[^6] This approach trains university students and local community members to create highly accurate maps of the city's most vulnerable neighborhoods using open-source tools.[^2] The initiative addresses critical data gaps by enabling communities formerly "off the map" to generate vital, accurate, and timely risk information for decision-makers.[^11]

## Technical Components

The mapping initiative uses open-source tools including Open Data Kit (ODK) and Open Map Kit (OMK) for data collection, which are easy to learn and can be used by anyone with an Android smartphone.[^4] For map production, the project employs QGIS and Java OpenStreetMap (JOSM), which are also open-source software.[^4] The technology incorporates GPS-enabled mobile apps, drones, and unmanned aerial vehicles to capture aerial and street-level imagery.[^9][^11] GPS accuracy of devices used by community mappers is checked to ensure it is usually less than 4 meters.[^8] Students collect critical data directly from community members using forms on smartphones (Open Data Kit) to precisely identify the extent of flooding within each city ward.[^5] The initiative initially used OpenMapKit for data collection in Hananasif ward and part of Kigogo ward, then switched to Open Data Kit (ODK) because polygon format data is more difficult to analyze, especially when producing heat maps that require data collected as points.[^8] Unmanned aerial vehicles or drones produce digital maps to assess flood-prone areas, take preventative measures to mitigate flooding impact before the rainy season, and identify appropriate upgrades to informal settlements.[^10] The project trained local pilots and worked with regulatory authorities to obtain permits, developing and flying a locally made bamboo drone to capture images whenever needed.[^15] Survey drones that cost a few thousand dollars can be deployed even when it's cloudy, replacing chartered flights that previously cost tens of thousands of dollars.[^15][^11]

## Implementation Details

Since 2015, the project mapped 49 out of 90 wards in Dar es Salaam, equivalent to approximately 50% of the city, comprising about 450,000 buildings and 1,700 schools.[^4] The initiative, funded by the World Bank and implemented by Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team (HOT), managed to map three-quarters of one of Africa's megacities between 2015 and 2020.[^15][^7] The first phase, which began in 2015, trained more than 150 students to create detailed maps of the city.[^15] A second phase started in 2017 to capture even more detailed and critical exposure and flood hazard information on the most at-risk neighborhoods, giving communities tools to build flood resilience themselves.[^3][^1] This second phase combined exposure data and flood hazard data to conduct risk analysis of potential future disasters.[^1] Using aerial and street-level imagery, GPS-enabled mobile apps, and drones, the project mapped 29 wards (home to 1.3 million citizens) and 1,254 kilometers of waterways and 3,396 kilometers of roads.[^9] Nearly 500 university students mapped a total of 228 sub-wards within 7 weeks to create a community asset and threat inventory, which is a collection of action maps, reports and open data for disaster management.[^4] Students mapped an area covering a total of 35 wards, home to the majority of Dar es Salaam's population.[^5] The initiative is a partnership between the World Bank, the Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team (HOT), Ardhi University in Dar es Salaam, and the city's municipal authorities.[^6] OpenMap Development Tanzania (OMDTZ) is the primary local organization with the team carrying out activities.[^4] The project is supported by the UK Department for International Development (DFID) in partnership with Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team, Tanzania Red Cross, Ardhi University, and the City of Dar es Salaam.[^14] The initiative is implemented in the frame of the "Building Climate Resilience in Tanzania Water Sector" project funded by the ACP-EU NDRR Program.[^2] Under a Memorandum of Understanding between GFDRR, the World Bank, the UK's aid-funded Urban Resilience Programme, and Tanzania's Ardhi University, the second phase trained about 300 Urban Planning and Geomatics students to capture community knowledge and help document the rapidly changing city more accurately.[^5]

The project trained nearly 1,000 students and local leaders to teach their communities how to collect data and play a part in flood mitigation programs.[^3] More than 1,000 students and 300 community members have been involved in the project.[^15] Specifically, 470 university students were trained to work on data collection and map production via the asset and threats project, and more than 300 Red Cross members were involved in the mapping.[^4] The training was conducted by fifteen student supervisors from Ardhi University, who prior to leading the training received three months of instruction with the initiative.[^17] Under the guidance of consultants from the Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team and ten other Tanzanian students from the project's pilot who became trainers, Ardhi students conducted the mapping work.[^5] The three days of training for new students included an introduction to OpenStreetMap (a free editable map of the world) and mapping tools such as field papers and the GPS Unit (eTrex 30), used to record tracks and waypoints.[^17] Training was given in JOSM software, an open-source offline editing tool of OpenStreetMap.[^17] The training included use of the OSM Tracker (an open-source Android phone application available in the Google Play Store) which does the same work as the GPS Unit for data collection but has a more user-friendly interface.[^17] Following the training session, the initiative successfully activated one hundred fifty new active OpenStreetMap users from two universities.[^17] The training took the form of 43 sessions with varying target audiences that covered topics including Java OpenStreetMap, InaSAFE, QGIS, data quality and quantity, and general map creation.[^13]

The team uses community mapping techniques to engage with local leaders and teach community inhabitants free, open-source data collection tools from their smartphones.[^1] The community mapping process begins by introducing the team to ward officers, explaining the project and providing an introductory letter addressed to Subward officials, then being introduced to the Wajumbe local leaders who operate at the most granular level of administration system that exists in Dar es Salaam.[^8] Ten cell leaders (Wajumbe) select a community member who owns a smartphone so that the phone can be installed with Open Data Kit (ODK) Collect and they can download the survey form from the server www.turkus.net, with the survey written in Swahili to avoid language barriers.[^8] A community member conducting data collection is provided with scratch cards to buy internet packages and a power bank.[^8] The trained students worked with communities living in informal settlements to carry out ground surveys to add different layers of data to the map - including drainage, infrastructure, and assets.[^15] The mapping teams consist of students from Ardhi University and local community members, known as "mappers", with students bringing technical skills and enthusiasm while community members provide essential local knowledge and context.[^6] By empowering students and local communities to do the mapping, the initiative was able to collect data at a much larger scale and at a much cheaper price than would have been possible with traditional surveyors.[^15] Community members in 21 wards collaborated to deliver the same information at a fraction of the price, accounting for 1.3 million of the city's 5.5 million residents.[^11]

The second phase focused on mapping the most flood-prone Wards such as Kigogo and Hananasif Wards to produce flood extent information which would later be used to inform decision making and flood mitigation plans.[^8] Six wards were selected from seventeen flood-prone wards for community mapping, including Mabibo, Mburahati, Makurumla, Makumbusho Msasani and Keko.[^17] In community surveys, respondents are asked questions such as "have you ever experienced flooding?", "how deep was the flood water?", "have you moved settlement because of flooding?", and "what do you think caused the flooding?".[^8] The team creates a new dataset for each year flooding is reported so that flood impact can easily be visualized by year.[^8] A community member who participated in the first phase stated that they used field papers and GPS devices to collect information which was time consuming and could result in data loss, but now with smartphones the data collection is quicker and less likely to lose data.[^8] For the second phase – which began in 2017 – map data was collected with mobile phone applications, such as Open Data Kit and Open Map Kit.[^15] Beyond drainage networks, students mapped health care services, toilets, water sources and various other elements of urban infrastructure to provide more information that can guide the Tanzanian government—from the local to national levels—in their efforts to improve flood resilience and service delivery across the city.[^5]

## Benefits and Impacts

The initiative enabled the collection of over 750,000 building footprints; 120 kilometers of imagery and surface models; 2,091 kilometers of roads; and tens of thousands of points of interest such as toilets, water points, schools and other critical infrastructure.[^13] Over 100 community members and 165 students of local universities were engaged in creating data and facilitating knowledge transfer on community-based flood and disaster issues.[^13] The World Bank's Tanzania Urban Resilience Program (TURP) used the data to develop detailed flood models for Dar es Salaam, with building footprint data combined with information on elevation and drainage systems allowing for the creation of highly accurate inundation maps that show which specific buildings and areas are likely to be affected by flooding of different magnitudes.[^6] The community mapped data supports flood modeling on a level of detail that is currently inaccessible in many data-scarce environments.[^12] The data collected enables people across all levels of society to improve flood mitigation plans and raise awareness and resiliency to natural threats.[^1] The maps provide valuable information about likely flood risk in vulnerable neighborhoods and reflect the knowledge of those most directly affected.[^9] Community members are now better informed about the risks in flood-prone areas, are able to access up-to-date community-based maps to get a better sense of the extent and impact of a flood, and know who to contact in order to coordinate response actions.[^14] The addition of Mjumbe boundaries and more accurate, detailed flood data from ODK from the second phase continues to inform and advise community members and government officials on focus areas for flood mitigation planning.[^8]

The project created data used in government risk assessments.[^3] At a later stage, the data collected and edited in OpenStreetMap will be used in InaSAFE (an open-source software used in QGIS) to help model disaster scenarios and will provide information to assist in allowing for better planning and response to floods by government authorities/offices.[^17] The raw data produced is fed into publicly accessible tools such as OpenStreetMap and InaSAFE, two "wiki" sites for the cartography and disaster relief communities.[^11] Sourcing information from OpenStreetMap and InaSAFE, authorities can identify areas at greatest risk of flooding and simulate disaster scenarios.[^11] Residents can locate latent water accumulation zones with drone-led aerial surveys of local topography.[^11] Residents can now know ahead of time which structures in their neighborhood stand to be impacted most in the event of disaster, and make a credible case for more resources when disaster strikes again.[^11] The project created the most detailed and comprehensive map of Dar es Salaam in existence, with this open dataset available for use by government agencies, NGOs, researchers, and the private sector to support a wide range of development and resilience-building activities.[^6] All data generated by this project is freely and publicly available and subsequently integrated into various governmental organizations, such as the National Bureau of Statistics and the Department for Disaster Management.[^16] The Dar es Salaam Multi-Agency Emergency Response Team (DarMAERT) uses the atlas to support field response during disasters, which was created back in 2018.[^7]

Based on the printed maps produced from the first phase, local people are establishing waste management initiatives to clean blocked drains and educate residents on the detrimental impact of littering.[^8] Community members explained how the first phase maps have been used to inform community cleanup initiatives to remove waste from blocked drains.[^8] The mapping contributes to better urban planning, better drainage and maintenance, and better community waste management.[^2] Green WastePro LTD – a trash collection company in Dar es Salaam – worked with the team to develop a dataset to enable tracking and locating of their clients for better service and revenue, with the company's revenue increasing significantly as they started using the datasets as a result of the training and support received.[^15] The project is harnessing the power of community to help local authorities like Ward Executive Officers better allocate resources – be it to repair a clogged water pipe or remove waste build-up from a flood-prone area – more effectively and cheaply than ever before.[^11] The data will further be used to inform other initiatives by the Bank's urban resilience umbrella team in Tanzania—its vast portfolio includes participatory climate risk plans and emergency planning for disaster response.[^5]

The initiative has been training students in Dar es Salaam and equipping them with industrial and technical skills for the 21st Century while generating vital high-precision, low-cost data for flood prediction and preparedness.[^1] The skills acquired by mappers are widely applicable to a number of areas, contributing to their ability to diversify their livelihood options and adapt to change.[^14] Volunteers are making direct contributions to the safety of their communities and gaining new skills along the way.[^11] Building on the inclusive and participatory model to resilience-building, TURP supported the Resilience Academy, which was first launched in 2019, and has since enabled 1,400 young Tanzanians to develop skills in data collection, community engagement, and mapping.[^9] A GFDRR-funded pilot supported Resilience Academy participants to produce detailed tree-cover maps of Dar es Salaam that have helped urban planners identify inequities in canopy coverage across the city.[^9] The project addressed sustainability by building local capacity at Ardhi University, creating a cohort of trained students and faculty who can continue the mapping work.[^6] Edward Anderson, Senior Technology & Innovation Specialist at the World Bank and manager of GFDRR's support of the initiative, says "We're not delivering risk maps to clients, we are building up capacity and institutions to do maps better and forever."[^11] Francis Ghesquiere, Head of GFDRR, explains "Tanzanians are taking the lead on Ramani Huria, which not only ensures its sustainability, but also paves the way for the next generation of disaster risk managers, computer scientists, and cartographers."[^11]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Dar es Salaam is vulnerable to floods, rising sea-levels, coastal erosion, water scarcity, and outbreaks of diseases, with the city of an estimated 6,368,000 people particularly vulnerable to climate change effects, caused among other things by poor planning and infrastructure, construction in hazardous lands and riparian areas.[^15] Severe flooding in December 2011 - January 2012 displaced at least 10,000 people and caused 40 deaths.[^1] Heavy rains in April 2014 led to widespread flooding and infrastructure damage.[^1] In Dar es Salaam, floods in 2009, 2011, and 2014 caused injury and loss of life, while also severely impacting diverse sectors including transportation, energy, and social infrastructures such as schools and health facilities.[^16] The city is home to nearly 5 million inhabitants, many of whom face the likelihood of flooding during Tanzania's two rainy seasons (in March/April and November).[^5] In the unplanned and informal settlements that abound in Dar es Salaam, flooding causes heavy losses in livelihoods and infrastructure, with flooded homes and roads causing at least nine casualties in one month.[^14] Around 70% of city residents live in informal settlements, and urbanization is largely unplanned.[^1] An estimated 70-80 percent of residents live in unplanned settlements.[^16] Buguruni kwa Mkanda and Tandale are among Dar es Salaam's countless unplanned, under-resourced, and largely unmapped informal settlements that sprawl across 80% of the city and suffer most when disaster strikes.[^11] Tanzania is the most flood-affected country in East Africa, and city governments across the country are underprepared to meet the challenges of urbanization, including the increased vulnerability to climate-related hazards.[^16]

## Business Analysis

The use of free and open-source software and local devices (students trained on smartphones) means that everything needed to continue the project and keep data updated as the city changes is already available in the community.[^14] The project demonstrated how low-cost tools such as OpenStreetMap, drones and in-situ sensors can be scaled and provide a platform for innovation.[^13] Data acquisition based on community mapping can be affordable, comprehensible, quality assured and open source, making it applicable in resource-strained contexts.[^12] The community mapping approach is appropriate for datasets that do not require extensive training, such as flood extent surveys where it is possible to cross-validate the quality of reports given a suitable number and density of data points.[^12] More technically advanced features such as dimensions of urban drainage system elements still require trained mappers to create data of sufficient quality.[^12] The approach proposed is based on proactive engagement of the local community that is trained to collect high quantities of flood-related data which allows for extensive flood model development and validation in an affordable way.[^12] The framework for urban flood modeling with community mapped data was demonstrated and validated on a case study in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania.[^12] The hypothesis was tested by developing a hydrodynamic model including detailed schematization of a community mapped drainage network and comparing the simulated flood depth with citizen's reports on flood depth for models run with and without the community mapped drainage network.[^12]

The World Bank is the main financier of the project, and Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team (HOT) is the primary organization managing projects.[^4] The initiative is supported by the World Bank through its Global Facility for Disaster Risk and Recovery (GFDRR) and its partners.[^11] GFDRR supported the initiative, a community-mapping project that trained university students and local community members to create open-source maps of Dar es Salaam's most flood-prone areas.[^9] Stakeholders include the Commission of Science and Technology (COSTECH), Dar es Salaam City Council, Global Facility for Disaster Reduction and Recovery (GFDRR), and the Red Cross.[^4] The World Bank has formed a partnership with the UK Department for International Development (DFID) and established the Tanzania Urban Resilience Program (TURP) in response to flooding issues.[^16] The Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team with its local partner OpenMap Development Tanzania has been contracted to build upon datasets collected during the first and second phases that started in 2015 and 2017 respectively.[^16] HOT and OMDTZ led a series of short urban mapping campaigns over a 6-month period starting in July 2020 that included verification and update of OpenStreetMap (OSM) data for COVID-19 hotspot analysis, remote digitization of buildings, and community flood history mapping.[^16]

Building on the foundation of the initiative, the Zuia Mafuriko project was initiated to specifically leverage this newly available data for flood resilience and early action, meaning "Stop Floods" in Swahili, implemented by the Tanzanian Red Cross Society (TRCS) in collaboration with the Climate Centre and the World Bank.[^6] The Zuia Mafuriko project focuses on two flood-prone wards: Tandale and Mwananyamala, which were selected based on their high vulnerability to flooding and the availability of detailed community-mapped data from the project.[^6] Consultancy contract for community upgrading in Tandale and Msimbazi Basin was signed in June 2019.[^18] The innovative, citizen-based participatory approach has already started to spread to other cities (such as Stone Town, Zanzibar) which have adopted the participatory mapping methods pioneered by the initiative.[^13] The project was showcased during the Africa Open Data Conference from September 2–5, 2016, which included over 450 participants from African governments.[^13] At the event, copies of the Risk Profile Atlas were disseminated and an agreement made to form committees on harmonizing data across Dar es Salaam, providing a foundation for a national framework of urban open data sharing.[^13] Ten additional workshops were delivered in collaboration with CSOs and universities—particularly the Tanzanian Red Cross Society, one of the key implementing partners—to establish community-led emergency response mechanisms in urban areas.[^13] The project was shortlisted for the "City to City Barcelona FAD Award" in 2018.[^4] One of Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team's biggest multi-year projects wrapped up in 2019.[^3] All datasets, maps, tools, learning materials and documentation of the project are being transferred to the Resilience Academy, who will be carrying the project forward.[^15] OMDTZ has been enriching OpenStreetMap through anticipatory actions and mapathons where volunteers update maps in collaboration with partners like dLab and CartONG.[^7] OMDTZ received funding from the Zayed Sustainability Prize with focus on working with communities and youth to update data, test new mapping methods, and push for digital tools that save lives when every second counts.[^7] The use of 360-degree imagery via Mapillary has enabled AI to detect features like traffic signs that are later added to OpenStreetMap.[^7] Much of the Atlas data was collected between 2015 and 2020, and rapid urban growth has left some areas unmapped or outdated.[^7]

## Sources

# Research Report: City Resilience Index Assessment and Measurement Framework

## Solution Overview

Los Angeles served as one of the first cities to pilot the City Resilience Index, a comprehensive tool that measures urban resilience in a systematic, globally applicable way.[^3][^4][^5] The City Resilience Index was developed by engineering consultancy Arup with support from The Rockefeller Foundation to provide cities with a quantitative measurement framework.[^3][^5][^6] This diagnostic tool enables municipalities to assess their resilience performance and identify priority areas for action rather than serving as a ranking system.[^5]

## Technical Components

The City Resilience Index is structured around four dimensions—Health and Wellbeing, Economy and Society, Infrastructure and Environment, and Leadership and Strategy—encompassing 12 goals and 52 indicators.[^6] The framework is based on the City Resilience Framework, with its 52 indicators grouped into the 12 drivers of the CRF.[^5] Participants complete 156 questions covering issues from health and wellbeing to economy and society, infrastructure and ecosystems to leadership and strategy.[^3] Any city can complete the CRI using an online tool that guides multiple users to collaborate over the input of data.[^3] The data are scored on a scale of 1 to 5, with 1 representing a very low level of resilience and 5 representing a very high level of resilience.[^5] The CRI is underpinned by a qualitative scoring methodology that enables cities to assess their resilience and develop a robust evidence base to support investment and programming decisions.[^6]

## Implementation Details

Los Angeles was selected to join 100 Resilient Cities, pioneered by The Rockefeller Foundation in 2014, and Mayor Garcetti appointed the City's first Chief Resilience Officer.[^1] The CRO and the Mayor's Office began the resilience strategy development process in 2016, holding an Agenda-Setting Workshop that brought together over 150 stakeholders from across the City to identify resilience challenges.[^1] Marissa Aho served as Chief Resilience Officer for the City of Los Angeles during the development of the Resilient Los Angeles strategy.[^1] The Mayor's Office developed the Los Angeles Resilience Strategy with the collaboration, financial, and technical support of 100RC, as well as community and expert input.[^2] AECOM served as a partner organization in developing the Resilient Los Angeles strategy alongside the City of Los Angeles and 100 Resilient Cities—Pioneered by The Rockefeller Foundation.[^1] Mayor Eric Garcetti, in partnership with 100 Resilient Cities, released Los Angeles' first citywide Resilience Strategy on March 22, 2018.[^2] Resilient Los Angeles is organized around four main pillars, which include 15 goals and 96 actions.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

The CRI provides a universal framework for cities to measure and monitor the multiple factors that contribute to their resilience and provides a common language for cities to understand and communicate their resilience.[^6] The tool's primary purpose is to help cities understand their resilience performance and identify priority areas for action.[^5] Los Angeles joins the ranks of approximately 40 cities around the world, including Paris, New York, and Bangkok, that have implemented similar resilience strategies.[^2] The 100 Resilient Cities program provided assistance through funding for a Chief Resilience Officer in each city to lead resilience efforts, resources for drafting a Resilience Strategy, access to private sector, public sector, academic, and NGO resilience tools, and membership in a global network of peer cities to share best practices and challenges.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The City Resilience Index enables Los Angeles to systematically assess its capacity to withstand and recover from climate-related shocks and stresses across multiple dimensions of urban systems.[^3][^4][^5] The comprehensive measurement framework addresses resilience factors spanning infrastructure and environment, health and wellbeing, economy and society, and leadership and strategy, providing a holistic approach to climate adaptation planning.[^6]

## Business Analysis

The 100 Resilient Cities program provided financial support for Chief Resilience Officer positions in member cities and resources for drafting resilience strategies, representing a philanthropic investment model in urban climate adaptation capacity.[^2] The program facilitated access to private sector, public sector, academic, and NGO resilience tools, creating a multi-stakeholder ecosystem for resilience planning.[^2] The CRI's evidence-based scoring methodology supports cities in developing robust justifications for investment and programming decisions related to resilience initiatives.[^6] Membership in the global network of peer cities enabled knowledge sharing and best practice exchange among approximately 40 cities worldwide that implemented similar strategies.[^2]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://lacity.gov/highlights/mayor-eric-garcetti-announces-plan-resilient-los-angeles
- [^3]: https://www.theguardian.com/cities/2016/may/18/city-resilience-index-expect-unexpected-how-measure-improve
- [^4]: https://www.rockefellerfoundation.org/wp-content/uploads/Institutionalizing-Urban-Resilience-A-Midterm-Monitoring-and-Evaluation-Report-of-100-Resilient-Cities.pdf
- [^5]: https://www.urban.org/sites/default/files/2022-09/Urban%20Resilience%20-%20From%20Global%20Vision%20to%20Local%20Practice_1.pdf
- [^6]: https://mercociudades.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/10/CRI-total-Booklet.pdf

# Research Report: Mapping Greater Manchester: Open Data Platform for Regional Planning

## Solution Overview

The Greater Manchester Combined Authority developed Mapping Greater Manchester, a series of open data maps designed to support an open and inclusive approach to sustainable and resilient development across the city-region.[^1] This digital mapping tool integrates infrastructure and growth data from multiple organizations to enable new ways of sharing risk information with different stakeholders and the public.[^4][^5] The platform brings together data on housing, planning, infrastructure, socio-economic, and demographic landscape to provide valuable insights for residents, businesses, academics, third sector organizations, and public sector professionals.[^1]

## Technical Components

The implementation utilizes ESRI's ArcGIS Online solution to create interactive maps, a transition completed in 2025 that brought the build and updating of the website back into the GMCA.[^2] The platform uses Ordnance Survey's MasterMap as the base map, with partners' asset data overlaid as a series of different layers.[^3] A project team led by Atkins and Ordnance Survey developed a secure, cloud-based platform to host the integrated data.[^3] The system provides a simple, intuitive interface that allows users to view and query the integrated data, with users able to switch different asset layers on and off, and click on an asset to get more information about it.[^3] Most of the datasets used are freely available for download, reflecting dedication to openness and community engagement.[^2]

## Implementation Details

The initiative was originally developed in 2014/15 to create a map of social and physical infrastructure across Greater Manchester, launching in 2015 with funding from the Cabinet Office.[^2][^4] The GMCA currently hosts three maps: ExploreGM, Integrated Pipeline, and Places for Everyone.[^2] The platform now contains over 60 datasets from 29 organizations.[^4] A pilot project brought together data from the ten GM local authorities, Transport for Greater Manchester (TfGM), utility companies, Ordnance Survey, and Atkins to create a single, shared, secure digital map of underground assets.[^3] The pilot area covered parts of Salford, Manchester, and Trafford, including the city centres and the Oxford Road Corridor.[^3] Utility company partners included Electricity North West, Cadent Gas, United Utilities, Virgin Media, and BT Openreach.[^3] The pilot project has been running since 2017.[^3] The platform is secure, with access restricted to authorized users from the partner organizations, and a robust governance framework has been developed to ensure that data is managed and used appropriately.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The map is used to identify land for development, assess planning applications, and engage the public in consultations on initiatives like the Greater Manchester Spatial Framework.[^4] The project team estimated that the use of MappingGM on the Salford Central scheme could have generated savings of between £2 million and £4 million, through reduced utility diversion costs and better coordination of works.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach enables new ways of sharing risk information with different stakeholders and the public through open data maps.[^5] The platform's integration of infrastructure, socio-economic, and demographic data provides valuable insights for assessing climate vulnerabilities and planning resilient development across Greater Manchester.[^1]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://greatermanchester-ca.gov.uk/media/4542/greater-manchester-resilience-strategy-2020-2030.pdf
- [^2]: https://mappinggm.org.uk/about/
- [^3]: https://greatermanchester-ca.gov.uk/media/3942/mappinggm.pdf
- [^4]: https://www.slideshare.net/slideshow/david-hodcroft/77428821
- [^5]: https://www.arup.com/globalassets/downloads/insights/digital-cities-resilient-cities-delivering-urban-resilience-through-digital-solutions.pdf

# Research Report: Geospatial Mangrove Monitoring and Hydrodynamic Modeling System

## Solution Overview

Geospatial monitoring tools have been deployed to track spatial and temporal changes in mangrove ecosystems along Thane Creek, Mumbai, combining satellite imagery analysis with hydrodynamic modeling to understand coastal dynamics.[^1] This approach enables systematic assessment of mangrove distribution patterns, species composition, and the ecological role these forests play in coastal stabilization and flood attenuation.[^1] The implementation addresses critical needs for evidence-based mangrove conservation in a rapidly urbanizing coastal environment where these ecosystems face competing pressures from development and natural processes.

## Technical Components

The monitoring system utilized Landsat series satellite imagery for decadal change analysis from 1990 to 2017, combined with Sentinel-2 imagery for detailed species distribution mapping in 2017, processed through Semi-automatic Mapping of Land Cover (SMLC) methodology with QGIS-SCP software.[^1] The ADCIRC hydrodynamic model was integrated to simulate tidal flooding and ebbing patterns toward mangrove areas, with validation conducted against in-situ observations of tides and currents.[^1] In November 2017, the Maharashtra Forest Department signed an agreement with the Indian Space Research Organization (ISRO) to implement satellite imagery monitoring for mangroves across the state, including Mumbai.[^3] The forest department utilizes ISRO's open source software to track mangrove changes, with images transmitted on a monthly basis to enable managers to identify locations of mangrove loss and gain.[^3][^4]

## Implementation Details

The monitoring project with ISRO required an investment of Rs40 lakh from Maharashtra state authorities.[^4] The geospatial analysis covered the entire Thane Creek mangrove system, which encompasses more than 59 square kilometers according to Forest Survey of India assessments.[^3] Satellite imagery analysis between 2008 and 2021 revealed that 325 hectares of dense mangrove cover transitioned to sparse mangrove cover or converted to intertidal mudflats due to excessive erosion and sedimentation processes.[^2] The monthly delivery schedule for satellite images provides forest managers with regular monitoring capabilities to detect changes in mangrove distribution patterns.[^3] Analysis documented that between 1988 and 2017, urban development encroached on mangroves in several areas along Thane Creek, with the Deonar landfill spreading significantly and new residential and commercial development emerging along the northern stretches.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

Mangrove forest area in Thane Creek almost doubled from 79.14 km² in 1990 to 154.5 km² in 2017, with regeneration occurring toward both landward and seaward directions.[^1] The hydrodynamic modeling demonstrated that with mangroves present, tidal currents attenuated 80% of their magnitude and favored sediment deposition in mangrove areas, which further enhanced mangrove growth and stabilized the coast.[^1] Between 2008 and 2021, approximately 305 hectares of mangroves increased in density.[^2] The sediment deposition process narrowed the channel and allowed some mangroves to spread closer to the center of Thane Creek over time.[^3] Analysis revealed that mangroves in Thane Creek reached a saturation stage and achieved maximum growth toward the landward direction.[^1] The dominance of Avicennia marina species confirmed increased anthropogenic activity along the landward side.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The monitoring system provides critical evidence of mangrove ecosystems' role in coastal flood protection, as modeling demonstrated that in the absence of mangroves, Thane Creek exhibited a perfect funneling effect with increased tidal amplification and strong flood currents, resulting in severe coastal inundation and erosion.[^1] The 80% attenuation of tidal current magnitude by mangrove presence directly reduces flood risk for adjacent urban areas while promoting sediment deposition that enhances coastal stability.[^1] Between 2010 and 2017, fluctuations in mangrove area distribution occurred due to different climatic conditions and anthropogenic activities, highlighting the need for continuous monitoring to track ecosystem responses to environmental pressures.[^1]

## Business Analysis

The Rs40 lakh investment in the ISRO partnership established a cost-effective monitoring framework leveraging existing satellite infrastructure and open source software platforms.[^4] The monthly satellite image delivery model provides operational efficiency by enabling proactive identification of mangrove loss and gain locations without requiring extensive ground-based survey resources.[^3] The Maharashtra Forest Department's agreement with ISRO represents a public-sector partnership model that distributes technical capabilities from national space agencies to state-level environmental management authorities.[^3] The comparison between 1990 and 2020 documented that Thane Creek on Mumbai's eastern side has been shrinking, with more than 14 square kilometers of coastline witnessing mud deposition over three decades, as observed by the Mangrove and Marine Biodiversity Conservation Foundation of Maharashtra in 2021.[^2]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Asia & Asia Pacific Oceania/Mumbai-India/Mumbai Climate Action Plan 2022.pdf
- [^3]: https://earthobservatory.nasa.gov/images/91333/monitoring-mumbais-mangroves
- [^4]: https://qz.com/india/886167/isros-satellites-will-now-protectively-watch-over-the-depleting-mangroves-around-mumbai

# Research Report: Food Access Geospatial Mapping Tool for Vulnerable Populations

## Solution Overview

The City and County of Honolulu is developing a geospatial mapping tool that overlays food system resources and assets with vulnerable and at-risk populations to assess community needs.[^3] This approach serves as a performance metric for the city's food security initiatives and emerged from expert consensus as a critical indicator for measuring food security in Hawaiʻi.[^2] The Resilience Office is drafting Honolulu's first-ever Food Systems Plan to guide municipal actions and investments toward creating a sustainable, prosperous, resilient, equitable, and community-based food system.[^1]

## Technical Components

The Food Access Map integrates multiple data layers to visualize factors affecting food access across Oʻahu, including federal nutrition programs like SNAP and WIC, emergency food assistance program locations such as food banks, and community and school garden sites.[^1] The Climate Ready O'ahu mapping tool provides the technical foundation for visualizing communities facing compounded socioeconomic and climate-related challenges.[^4] The Resilience Office's methodology layers social vulnerability measures with climate change impacts, including extreme heat, flooding, and sea-level rise.[^4] This multi-indicator approach was validated through a Delphi study engaging 24 food security experts in Hawaiʻi, which achieved a 58% response rate in the second round and reached consensus on 55 of 71 metrics, representing 78% agreement on measurement standards.[^2]

## Implementation Details

Municipal authorities conducted Food Access Micro Summits in Waiʻanae, Kalihi, and Waimānalo in partnership with the City's Office of Economic Revitalization to better understand how the City can support access to healthy and affordable food.[^1] The mapping tool serves as one of three performance metrics for Action 16, alongside convening a permanent Emergency Feeding Task Force and developing an O'ahu Emergency Food & Storage Plan.[^3] Implementation planning has been informed by the Emergency Feeding Systems Gap Analysis, which identifies weaknesses in Hawaii's emergency feeding systems, including gaps in food distribution networks and resource allocation during disasters.[^6] Data collection for vulnerable populations includes information from Aloha Harvest's recipient agencies, showing that end consumers include the houseless (22%), unemployed (36%), Native Hawaiians and Pacific Islanders (46%), children (29%), seniors (16%), and individuals with mental illness, substance abuse, or disabilities (10%).[^5]

## Benefits and Impacts

The mapping tool helps planners develop a more affordable, accessible, and equitable island food system by visualizing the intersection of food resources and vulnerable populations.[^1] This approach addresses a critical need on Oʻahu, where over 28% of residents are food insecure.[^1] The Climate Ready O'ahu mapping tool enables identification of communities confronting compounded socioeconomic and climate-related challenges, supporting targeted intervention strategies.[^4] The validated metrics framework provides standardized measurement capabilities, with expert consensus achieved on 78% of proposed food security indicators specific to Hawaiʻi's context.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The system directly addresses climate adaptation by layering social vulnerability measures with climate change impacts such as extreme heat, flooding, and sea-level rise.[^4] This integration enables planners to identify communities where food insecurity intersects with climate hazards, supporting resilience planning for emergency feeding systems during climate-related disasters.[^6]

## Business Analysis

The mapping tool operates within Honolulu's municipal food systems planning framework, guided by the first-ever Food Systems Plan being drafted by the Resilience Office.[^1] Implementation involves cross-departmental collaboration between the Resilience Office and the Office of Economic Revitalization, demonstrated through the Food Access Micro Summits conducted in multiple communities.[^1] The Emergency Feeding Systems Gap Analysis provides the analytical foundation for resource allocation decisions, identifying weaknesses in food distribution networks and resource allocation during disasters.[^6] The performance metrics framework established through the Delphi study provides a validated measurement system for tracking progress, with 78% expert consensus on food security indicators specific to Hawaiʻi.[^2]

## Sources

# Research Report: Smart Sensor Network for Flood Monitoring and Water Quality

## Solution Overview

Through collaboration with the French government, Tbilisi implemented an early warning system following a devastating flood in 2015.[^1] In 2022, preparations were underway to install 37 smart sensors throughout the city to strengthen resilience to the effects of climate change, including flood risks, and prevent the degradation of surface water quality.[^2][^3][^4] This approach transforms climate vulnerability into proactive disaster risk management through advanced monitoring infrastructure.

## Technical Components

The system provides advanced forecasting with 30-60 minute predictions and includes real-time rainfall and water level tracking.[^1] Technical infrastructure comprises 30 satellite antennas distributed across the city and 3 smart CCTV cameras for real-time river monitoring.[^1] The sensors were installed within the context of a technical assistance project implemented by the City Hall in cooperation with the Tbilservice Group SUEZ Group.[^2] The monitoring network targets the Mtkvari River at Tbilisi, which has a catchment area of 27,500 km2, an average annual discharge of 205 m3/s, a maximum discharge of 1,280 m3/s, and a minimum discharge of 35.0 m3/s.[^5] The National Environmental Agency conducts regular monitoring of the Mtkvari River basin's water quality at 67 points annually.[^8]

## Implementation Details

The €735,000 project showcases how cities can build resilience against climate challenges through smart technology and international partnerships.[^1] Following the 2015 flash flooding, the Tbilisi City Hall introduced regulations to prevent the issuance of building permits in risk areas and adopted a flood early warning system, installing a monitoring system in three landslide zones in 2016, which were consolidated with an additional monitoring system installed on Matchavariani Street and nearby slopes.[^2] The implementation addressed regional hydrological patterns, as the rivers of Eastern Georgia are characterized by spring floods caused by snowmelt and spring rains, with low water levels in summer and winter.[^5]

## Benefits and Impacts

The heavy rainfall of June 2015 in Tbilisi caused flash flooding from the overflowing of the riverbanks that claimed 21 lives and resulted in around USD 50 million in infrastructure damage.[^2] This system provides operational benefits through 30-60 minute advance warning capabilities, enabling emergency response teams to act before flood conditions materialize.[^1] Real-time monitoring across multiple parameters supports both immediate disaster response and long-term water quality management objectives.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach directly addresses extreme precipitation events and riverine flooding hazards that pose significant risks to urban populations and infrastructure. The system's capacity to monitor rainfall intensity, water levels, and provide short-term forecasting enables proactive evacuation and emergency response measures during flash flood conditions.

## Sources

# Research Report: Smart Zone-Based Water Pressure Management System

## Solution Overview

Cape Town implemented smart process controllers that enable zone-by-zone water pressure management to significantly reduce water consumption.[^6][^7] This approach addresses critical infrastructure challenges through advanced pressure control technology deployed across an extensive network of pressure management zones.[^1] The system played a vital role in helping the city avoid 'Day Zero' during the severe 2015-2017 drought by halving municipal water use.[^2]

## Technical Components

The implementation incorporated multiple advanced pressure control technologies working in coordination across the water distribution network. JOAT Group recommended i2O's Pressure Optimisation solution, oNet, which offered the ability to precisely control critical point pressure at pressure reducing valves (PRVs) and to manage the city's water more effectively through intelligent, self-learning technology.[^3] Utilitec Services, in partnership with the City of Cape Town, installed the Regulo PRV controller, an electronic controller which may be retrofitted to most models of hydraulic PRV.[^4] The Regulo records the inlet, outlet and critical point pressures, flow rate and valve position at 15 minute intervals.[^4] The Regulo has a dedicated internal modem and battery, allowing it to be deployed in underground chambers without the need for an external power supply or kiosk.[^4] The Regulo is capable of implementing both time and flow modulated control and can be remotely configured through the Technolog software platform.[^4] The Technolog software platform, Utilisoft, provides a web based interface that allows the City of Cape Town to remotely manage their advanced pressure control systems.[^4]

## Implementation Details

Cape Town established 170 pressure management zones covering 68 percent of the water network as of June 2021.[^1] The City of Cape Town has a well-established pressure management programme, with over 200 pressure management zones (PMZs).[^4] Utilitec Services, a Technolog group company, has been working in partnership with the City of Cape Town to install and commission over 100 advanced pressure control systems.[^4] The City of Cape Town has installed over 100 advanced pressure control systems, resulting in an estimated saving of 21 million m3 of water per annum.[^4] The City of Cape Town was continuing to expand its advanced pressure management programme, with a further 50 systems planned for installation in 2017.[^4] Cape Town's ultimate plan is to have about 90% of the water network under pressure management with more than 300 pressure management zones installed.[^5] JOAT ran a two-phase trial of oNet in the Eersterivier PRV zone, which comprises 89.3km of mains and 6,218 connections.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The pressure management programme addressed critical infrastructure challenges, as an average mains burst frequency of 21 per 100km per year and a 21% leakage rate meant the City of Cape Town needed to find more effective ways of managing pressure in its water distribution network.[^3] In the first phase, oNet successfully maintained a constant critical point pressure of 27m, which had previously averaged 34.5m during peak periods and 37m in off-peak.[^3] In the second phase, oNet was set to target critical point pressures of 27m during peak periods and 23m during off peak.[^3] In the Gugulethu DMA, implementing advanced pressure control reduced the average daily flow from 11,000 m3/day to 7,000 m3/day, a saving of 4,000 m3/day.[^4] The advanced pressure control programme has resulted in a significant reduction in burst frequencies, saving the City of Cape Town an estimated £1.2 million per annum in repair costs.[^4] The remarkable efforts of Capetonians to reduce their water consumption, and the City of Cape Town's pressure management programme, successfully halved municipal water use and helped the city avoid 'Day Zero'.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This pressure management technology directly addresses water scarcity challenges intensified by climate change, as demonstrated during the 2015-2017 drought when the system helped prevent complete reservoir depletion. The intelligent pressure control approach enhances municipal resilience to extended drought periods by maximizing water conservation through leak reduction and optimized distribution network performance. By reducing water losses and enabling more efficient resource allocation across pressure management zones, the system strengthens the city's capacity to withstand future climate-driven water stress events.

## Business Analysis

The implementation demonstrates a financially viable approach to water infrastructure optimization, with the City of Cape Town achieving an estimated £1.2 million per annum in repair cost savings through reduced burst frequencies.[^4] The advanced pressure control programme generated substantial operational returns through water conservation, saving an estimated 21 million m3 of water per annum across over 100 installed systems.[^4] The partnership model between Utilitec Services, a Technolog group company, and municipal authorities enabled systematic deployment and commissioning of advanced pressure control systems.[^4] The scalable nature of the technology, with retrofitting capabilities for most hydraulic PRV models and remote management through web-based platforms, supports cost-effective expansion across additional pressure management zones.[^4] The planned expansion to 300 pressure management zones covering 90% of the water network indicates sustained municipal investment in this infrastructure optimization approach.[^5]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://resource.capetown.gov.za/documentcentre/Documents/City%20strategies,%20plans%20and%20frameworks/Cape%20Town%20Water%20Strategy.pdf
- [^3]: https://en.i2owater.com/clients/city-of-cape-town/

- [^5]: https://www.news24.com/citys-pressure-checks-to-cut-down-water-losses-in-the-city-20210705
- [^6]: https://www.c40.org/case-studies/cities100-cape-town-better-management-prevents-water-stress/
- [^7]: https://www.wwdmag.com/utility-management/article/10936655/delaying-day-zero
