# Research Report: Radar-Based Flood Detection and Real-Time Alert System

## Solution Overview

Houston researchers developed flood detection sensors designed for installation on light poles and elevated surfaces to transmit real-time data and alert the public of dangerous conditions.[^12] This technology-enabled approach addresses the city's recurring flood challenges through multiple interconnected systems that provide early warnings and situational awareness during severe weather events. The implementation builds on decades of flood monitoring research and represents a comprehensive effort to protect critical infrastructure and vulnerable communities across Houston's major watersheds.

## Technical Components

The Flood Information and Response System (FIRST) was developed for the City of Houston as an end-to-end radar-based flood assessment and mapping tool for critical infrastructure.[^1] The system continuously ingests 15-minute radar rainfall data across four major watersheds in Houston, including Brays Bayou, White Oak Bayou, Hunting Bayou and Sims Bayou, and instantly displays flood risks in color through intuitive visual maps.[^6] FIRST integrates real-time radar with precision floodplain mapping to deliver forecasts that account for how rain moves through watersheds, analyzing how it interacts with infrastructure and how it puts people at risk.[^5][^6] The system provides data every 10 to 15 minutes and instantly displays flood risk in an easy-to-use visual map.[^5] If any region records over 5 inches of rain in a 24-hour period, FIRST pulls from an extensive floodplain map library (FPML) to simulate and display the expected inundation zones.[^6] The FPML contains 49 advanced flood scenarios per watershed, built using state-of-the-art modeling tools, allowing for precision flood forecasts based on both upstream and downstream rainfall levels ranging from 5 to 18 inches.[^6] SSPEED hydrologists built over 350 flood plain maps for FIRST showing all different combinations of rainfall, creating a flood plain map library using high-speed servers running hundreds of simulations to determine how quickly and where water will rise during intense rainfall events.[^7]

A team of senior engineering students at Rice University developed a real-time, web-enabled system to monitor flood levels throughout Houston, which has suffered three damaging floods in recent years, topped by the devastation of Hurricane Harvey.[^4][^8][^9] The students developed wireless stations that communicate with a base to report on flooding at their locations, consisting of a solar-powered wireless transmitter that can ride high atop a utility pole, with a rubber conduit that stretches down the side and connects the station to a water-level rain gauge and pressure sensor.[^8][^9] The flood monitoring system uses off-the-shelf components and students designed and built several simple PC boards to connect sensors and solar cells to battery packs and a wireless transmitter.[^4] The individual sensors store and send information to a central location using radio, and that location will then parse through and send the data off through a cellular connection, which can then be accessed from any web interface.[^4][^8][^9] Initially, the sensor nodes are set to report local conditions every five minutes, but that can be adjusted as needed.[^4][^8][^9]

Rice University engineers Jamie Padgett and Pranavesh Panakkal developed an automated data fusion framework called OpenSafe Fusion (Open Source Situational Awareness Framework for Mobility using Data Fusion) that leverages existing individual reporting mechanisms and public data sources to sense quickly evolving road conditions during urban flooding events.[^2] The framework uses data from sources like traffic alerts, cameras and traffic speed, and leverages machine learning and data fusion to predict whether a road is flooded or not.[^2] Other data sources that could be used in the OpenSafe Fusion framework include water-level sensors, citizen portals, crowdsourcing, social media, flood models and a factor referred to as 'human-in-the-loop.'[^2] The OpenSafe Fusion framework builds off of and is inspired by longtime collaboration with colleagues in the SSPEED Center at Rice, who have been developing state-of-the-art flood alert systems.[^2]

FIRST was designed by flood expert Dr. Philip Bedient at Rice University, with assistance from Dr. Nick Fang and Dr. Baxter Vieux.[^1] SSPEED's Nick Fang, associate professor of civil engineering at the University of Texas at Arlington, was instrumental in developing FIRST's web interface and software for incorporating radar data.[^7] The FIRST system was developed over decades in collaboration with fellow researchers, including Nick Fang at the University of Texas at Arlington.[^5] FAS3 provides specific local flood warnings by monitoring key watersheds using a combination of cameras, rain gauges, radar, and digital modeling, and is interactive with real time updates every five minutes.[^10]

## Implementation Details

An upgraded FIRST system was designed for the city of Houston and implemented in 2020.[^6] Initially developed for the Brays Bayou Watershed to protect the Texas Medical Center 20 years ago, the new FIRST system was developed for the city of Houston in 2020.[^1][^6] The SSPEED Center is researching Houston's major flood-prone watersheds through partnerships with the Texas Medical Center, the City of Houston, and community organizations.[^1] SSPEED currently has flood monitoring projects on White Oak, Hunting, Brays, and Sims Bayous in Houston.[^1] Initially created to protect Houston's Texas Medical Center following Tropical Storm Allison in 2001, the system has expanded to cover four major watersheds in the Houston area and could be scaled to serve vulnerable communities across Texas.[^5] FIRST covers several flood-prone and at-risk communities in Houston, including Kashmere Gardens, Gulfton and Sunnyside.[^7]

Rice University's Houston Solutions Lab announced funding in June 2018 to support three projects focusing on flooding issues and a sustainability plan for the city's fleet of vehicles.[^3] The Houston Solutions Lab, a partnership between Rice's Kinder Institute for Urban Research and the city of Houston, is sponsoring the initiative being developed as a senior capstone project at the university's Oshman Engineering Design Kitchen.[^4][^8][^9] A team from the civil and environmental engineering department created low-cost flood sensors that were tested on Rice's campus in spring 2019.[^11][^13] The sensors are currently being tested on the campus of Rice University with the goal to scale to areas with frequent localized street flooding.[^12] The team created relatively low-price sensors and pitched the system to several entities interested in having real-time street-level flooding information.[^11] The flood sensor researchers will work with Steve Costello, Houston's chief resiliency officer, to ensure the sensor platform is robust and meets requirements for broader city deployment.[^3]

Each station shouldn't cost more than a few hundred dollars, including waterproof casing to protect the electronics – and less if the product is someday made in bulk quantities.[^4][^8] The students plan to have a small set of sensors set up around campus by the end of the spring semester, and hope to deploy a larger set in a Houston neighborhood prone to flooding, like Meyerland, over the summer.[^4][^8][^9] Most of the FIRST flood plain map library was created by Rice undergraduates in fall 2020, after Bedient trained them in summer 2020 on how to build map libraries using hydrologic software.[^7] The project started when two Rice professors, civil engineer Leonardo Dueñas-Osorio and computer scientist Devika Subramanian, looked in vain for commercial versions of what the students have developed for their own projects.[^4]

To test the OpenSafe Fusion process, researchers used historical flooding data observed during Hurricane Harvey in 2017 to recreate the scenario in the framework, consisting of around 62,000 roads in the Houston region.[^2] During Hurricane Harvey in 2017, many people in Houston including emergency responders resorted to manually examining data sources to infer probable road conditions due to the lack of reliable real-time road condition data.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

FIRST is an integrated system that predicts inundation levels caused by heavy rainfall events in order to provide lead-time for flood-response decisions.[^1] Philip Bedient, designer of the FIRST system, director of the SSPEED Center and the Herman Brown Professor of Engineering at Rice, stated that FIRST was designed to give decision-makers the earliest possible warnings, ideally hours before flash flooding takes place.[^6] The system is designed to assist the City of Houston in addressing emergency management and operations, including emergency closures, evacuation, as well as rescue operations.[^1] FIRST was created to serve hospitals, nursing homes, fire stations and communities vulnerable to flooding, helping city and county officials and emergency responders determine where and when to issue warnings, close roads or evacuate neighborhoods.[^6] FIRST's maps show the location of hospitals, nursing homes and fire stations in the four watersheds, and the system includes tools that allow emergency managers to establish warning thresholds so they have access to flood level information during rain events.[^7]

The FIRST system performed well during thunderstorms in May 2021, and SSPEED engineers planned to refine its performance with data from those and other storms.[^7] FAS accurately predicted the peak surge of nearby Brays Bayou during and immediately after Hurricane Ike, showing that the Texas Medical Center building was not at risk of flooding and saving the medical center from a costly and unnecessary evacuation.[^10] The OpenSafe Fusion study considered flooding impacts on community access to critical facilities such as hospitals and dialysis centers during a natural disaster, giving community members or emergency responders an understanding of which roads are flooded and how to safely navigate to a location.[^2] The ultimate goal of the piloted flood sensor array is to collect flood level data in real-time for neighborhood streets, which can be used to aid evacuation and transportation in storms and to refine models for future flooding.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Houston has experienced recurring severe flooding events, including three damaging floods in recent years topped by the devastation of Hurricane Harvey in 2017.[^4][^8][^9] In mid-April 2016, a low-pressure system produced record-breaking rain in and around Houston, Texas, resulting in widespread and severe flooding that was the worst since Tropical Storm Allison in 2001, with eight people losing their lives and nearly 1,200 homes and apartments inundated with water.[^10] The integrated flood monitoring and early warning systems address these extreme precipitation and flooding hazards by providing real-time situational awareness and advance warnings to protect critical infrastructure and vulnerable communities during severe weather events.

## Business Analysis

Rice University and the City of Houston have joined forces to create Houston Solutions Lab, building on other recent efforts to bring together research and policy.[^11] The Houston Solutions Lab partnership between Rice's Kinder Institute for Urban Research and the city of Houston provides institutional support for developing and deploying flood monitoring technologies.[^4][^8][^9] The initiative received funding announced in June 2018 to support three projects focusing on flooding issues.[^3] The low-cost sensor approach developed by students uses off-the-shelf components and simple PC boards, with each station costing no more than a few hundred dollars including waterproof casing, and potentially less if produced in bulk quantities.[^4][^8] This cost structure makes the technology accessible for broader deployment across flood-prone neighborhoods. The collaboration model between university researchers, student teams, and city officials demonstrates a public-private partnership approach that leverages academic research capacity to address municipal resilience challenges while maintaining affordability through innovative design and component selection.

## Sources

- [^1]: https://sspeed.rice.edu/flood-alert-systems
- [^2]: https://news.rice.edu/news/2024/rice-engineers-develop-ai-system-real-time-sensing-flooded-roads
- [^3]: https://news2.rice.edu/2018/06/26/kinder-institute-announces-2018-houston-solutions-lab-funding/
- [^4]: https://oedk.wildapricot.org/news/8792166
- [^5]: https://news.rice.edu/news/2025/rices-bedient-details-flood-warning-system-solutions-texas-joint-special-session
- [^6]: https://news.rice.edu/news/2025/rice-sspeed-centers-first-system-provides-critical-flood-warnings-real-time
- [^7]: https://news.rice.edu/news/2021/rice-builds-first-flood-alert-system-city-houston
- [^8]: https://news2.rice.edu/2019/04/04/rice-u-students-focus-on-floods/
- [^9]: https://news2.rice.edu/2019/04/04/rice-u-students-focus-on-floods-2/
- [^10]: https://hazards.colorado.edu/article/houston-we-have-a-solution-the-birth-of-a-new-generation-of-flood-warning-systems
- [^11]: https://kinder.rice.edu/research/scalable-and-robust-prototype-sensor-network-real-time-street-level-flood-measurement
- [^12]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Houston-US/Resilient-Houston-20200518-double-page.pdf

# Research Report: Self-Healing Grid Algorithm for Flood-Resilient Power Distribution

## Solution Overview

Barcelona has implemented a self-healing algorithm within its electrical distribution grid as part of a comprehensive flood impacts reduction strategy.[^1] Spanish DSO Endesa is advancing investment in the ongoing upgrading and digitalisation of Barcelona's electricity infrastructure to transform it into the 'digital city of the future'.[^2] This approach represents one of seven key measures designed to enhance urban resilience against flooding events, working alongside improvements to surface drainage, sewer system capacity, sustainable urban drainage systems, early warning systems, and waste container stability measures.[^1]

## Technical Components

The Location of Breakdowns and Restoration of Supply (LARS) system serves as an essential feature of this digitalisation process, enabling more rapid and accurate determination of line incidents and reactions to them.[^2] The LARS system integrates historical data, maps of street works and weather forecasts to more accurately and quickly determine where on a line an incident has occurred and react more quickly.[^3] Endesa is creating a Network Digital Twin for Barcelona, an exact digital replica of the existing network that can be used to generate simulations in all possible conditions, control component operation in real time, carry out preventive maintenance, and interact with field operators.[^3] The digital twin enables the location of a potential fault in the grid to be detected, assisting prevention, and the most efficient way to restore supply in the event of an extreme weather event to be simulated, helping to reduce restoration times and improve customer service quality.[^8] The modeling of the Barcelona electrical system in RESCCUE was carried out based on heavy historical rains, the flooding of the rivers and the sea.[^4] As part of the automation of the MV grid, new remote control systems are planned, which should enable quicker location of faults and the ability to manage the network without the need to send out personnel to the location.[^2]

## Implementation Details

By the end of 2023, 74% of Barcelona's transformer centres were due to be sensorised as part of Endesa's grid modernization plan.[^3] The remote control of 321 transformer centres was planned in Barcelona as part of the City Plan.[^3] By the end of 2023, 40% of the installations were expected to be automated.[^2] In the current year, Endesa expects to invest €46 million ($50 million) in Barcelona's grid digitalisation.[^2] The main implementing actors for improving the resilience of Barcelona's electricity grid are Ajuntament de Barcelona (Urban Ecology), Endesa, and Red Eléctrica de España (REE).[^5] Through RESCCUE, Endesa can simulate possible contingencies and cascading effects in any of the potentially flooded electrical installations including primary substations, secondary substations and their interconnections.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

The automation and digitalisation of Barcelona's electricity grid will enable a 20% reduction in the time clients are affected by an issue, whatever may be the cause.[^3] The self-healing algorithm objective is to minimize the load unsupplied after a fault event in the distribution grid.[^9] Endesa's role focuses on quantifying the impact of climate change on the capacity to recover power supplies and the interaction of this with the water cycle.[^7] The project will analyse the improvements smart grids and, specifically, micro-grids (small-scale systems that combine power generation, storage and distribution) can make to the resilience of cities.[^7]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This system directly addresses climate-related electrical grid vulnerabilities from multiple flooding sources. In Barcelona, the impact of climate change has been established to be an increase of 7–26% at peak rainfall intensity, depending on rainfall duration.[^10] The impact of atmospheric discharges on medium voltage lines generates overvoltages which can produce interruptions in the supply of energy and damage to electric infrastructures.[^4]

## Sources

# Research Report: Mumbai Automated Weather Station Network for Rainfall Monitoring

## Solution Overview

Decadal rainfall data from the Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation's Automated Weather Stations reiterates the increasing frequency of very heavy and extremely heavy rainfall events in Mumbai.[^1] The BMC completed the installation of 60 additional automatic weather stations throughout Mumbai, increasing the total number of stations to 120.[^2] The rainfall data collected through this expanded network helps the BMC send warnings to citizens and strategically prepare and implement disaster plans during heavy rainfall.[^2]

## Technical Components

The automatic weather stations provide information related to rainfall, wind speed, temperature, humidity, and wind direction.[^3] These stations collect information on 28 parameters in all and are connected to the Emergency Operation Centre at the BMC headquarters.[^4] The weather stations are connected to a central server located at the Worli data centre of the civic body.[^5] The data is sent to this central server at regular intervals and updated every 15 minutes on the civic disaster management portal.[^4][^5] The gathered information is updated on dm.mcgm.gov.in.[^6] Additionally, 165 automatic rain gauges installed by the Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology, BMC, and IMD are used to collect rainfall data.[^7] The successful bidder for the 60 new AWS was expected to integrate the new stations with the existing disaster management website and mobile app.[^4]

## Implementation Details

Around 43 AWS were installed by December 2023, while the remaining 17 were set up in the past three months, with the installation cost of each AWS up to INR 7 lakh.[^2] The BMC had planned to install 60 additional automatic weather stations across Mumbai during the monsoon, but only 43 were set up initially and 17 were underway due to technical difficulties and security reasons.[^6] The National Centre for Coastal Research recommended installation of 97 additional AWS throughout Mumbai to obtain accurate rainfall measurement and enhance the flood prediction ability of the Integrated Flood Warning System developed by NCCR.[^8] Due to cost and security issues, the civic authority decided to install only 60 AWS.[^2] The NCCR submitted grids to BMC for identifying locations where the AWS would be installed, and the civic body's disaster management department estimated about 60 feasible grids.[^4] Most of the automatic weather stations were installed on rooftops of BMC or government buildings, while a few were installed on ground.[^4] The installation work started at locations such as schools, residential societies, and hospitals.[^6] Specific installation sites included the municipal school on Gokhale Road at Dadar (West), Khar Danda pumping station, Versova in Andheri (West), and Pratiksha Nagar school in Jogeshwari (West).[^2] Many of the existing AWS were installed at fire brigade stations as they are the first respondents during disasters.[^4] The contractor is responsible for maintaining the AWS as well as the disaster management portal for three years.[^2] The BMC floated tenders for installation of the 60 AWS, with the bidder also responsible for displaying real-time rainfall data and maintaining the Disaster Management website and app.[^8]

## Benefits and Impacts

Decadal data from 2011-2020 accessed from 37 weather stations showed that Mumbai experiences an average of 6 heavy, 5 very heavy, and 4 extremely heavy rain events per year.[^1] Between 2017 and 2020, there was a steady increase in extremely heavy rainfall events.[^1] The analysis showed that most extreme rainfall events tend to last only a single day, while 17% of very heavy and 21% of extremely heavy events last more than a day.[^1] Spatially, most extreme rainfall events tend to occur as localized clusters in western and central areas, such as Worli-Dadar, Kurla, and Andheri.[^1] The weather stations are installed at a distance of every four square kilometers to provide citizens with micro-level information.[^6] After installations are complete, there is an AWS every three square kilometers as opposed to the previous eight square kilometers.[^3] According to the IMD, Mumbai has one of the largest weather monitoring networks in the country.[^3] The BMC's Disaster Management app currently gives weather data—temperature, wind, humidity, and pressure—in 140 locations in the city.[^7] Through the BMC's Disaster Management app, areas likely to be waterlogged are flagged anywhere between six to 72 hours in advance, along with the probable height of floodwater and location-specific risks.[^7]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The analysis initially focused on examining the pattern of extreme rainfall events across various regions of Mumbai, using data obtained from AWS.[^9] The Integrated Flood Warning System was installed in Mumbai in 2020 and factors in the amount of rainfall, tide levels, topography, land use, drainage, population, and the level of water in the rivers.[^7] Last year, the BMC asked IMD to provide warnings specific to an area, time, and intensity of rainfall, following an incident on August 29, 2017, when it predicted extremely heavy rainfall in two locations, but the city came to a grinding halt after receiving more than 200 mm of rain at over 20 locations.[^3]

[^2]: Source 2

# Research Report: Smart Sensor Network for Urban Tree Monitoring and Care

## Solution Overview

Street Lab operates as Copenhagen's testing ground for smart city solutions in real urban space, based on the award-winning Copenhagen Connecting concept.[^1] This three-year initiative under Copenhagen Solutions Lab deploys state-of-the-art measuring sensors and city wifi infrastructure to collect data providing unique insights into Copenhagen's current urban conditions.[^2][^3] The system enables city gardeners to remotely monitor soil humidity and temperature around trees through sensor technology, improving plant care management across the urban landscape.[^3][^4][^5]

## Technical Components

The implementation utilizes state-of-the-art measuring sensors integrated with Copenhagen's city wifi network to enable remote monitoring capabilities.[^2] These sensors measure soil humidity and temperature conditions around trees throughout the Street Lab testing area.[^3][^4][^5] The sensor infrastructure allows city gardeners to access environmental data remotely, supporting data-driven decisions for urban plant care management.[^6]

## Implementation Details

Street Lab is positioned at Copenhagen City Hall Square, serving as the municipality's designated testing area for smart city technologies.[^1] Copenhagen municipality functions as a key cooperator in this three-year project operating under Copenhagen Solutions Lab.[^1][^3] The initiative supports the city's broader ambition to plant 100,000 additional trees before 2025.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The sensor-based monitoring approach improves plant care by providing city gardeners with remote access to critical environmental data around urban trees.[^3][^4][^5] This technology enables more responsive maintenance practices by delivering real-time soil humidity and temperature measurements.[^6] The data collection infrastructure provides unique insights into Copenhagen's current urban conditions, supporting evidence-based urban management decisions.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Urban tree networks serve as critical infrastructure for climate adaptation, and this sensor-enabled monitoring system supports the maintenance and expansion of Copenhagen's urban forest. The remote measurement capabilities help ensure tree health and survival as the city works toward its goal of planting 100,000 additional trees before 2025, strengthening urban resilience through enhanced green infrastructure.[^3]

# Research Report: Urban Heat Vulnerability Mapping and Green Infrastructure Planning

## Solution Overview

Through continued technical assistance provided by Earth Economics and the Urban Green Infrastructure Lab, preliminary urban heat mapping and a high-level analysis of vulnerabilities were completed for Broward County.[^1][^2][^4] This approach identified communities experiencing elevated temperatures due to urban heat island effects and assessed the populations most vulnerable to heat-related health impacts. The heat vulnerability mapping information has been summarized in a brochure for distribution and served as the basis for grant applications to support development of heat mitigation strategies inclusive of green infrastructure.[^4]

## Technical Components

Earth Economics conducted an Urban Heat Island Analysis for Broward County, Florida.[^1] The heat vulnerability mapping identified communities where there is both an increase of 2 degrees Fahrenheit or more of urban heat island, as well as average per capita incomes less than 200% the federal poverty level.[^3] The highest risk areas identified in Broward County's heat mapping meet criteria of 2°F+ urban heat island increase, income less than 200% federal poverty level, plus have a high percentage of population younger than 14 and older than 65.[^3] The model estimated that 13 to 44 lives are lost in the County annually due to excessive heat.[^5] Analysis in Broward County indicates that the areas with the highest average summer temperatures and the communities most vulnerable to these temperatures are concentrated in eastern Broward surrounding major highways such as the I-95 and I-595.[^3]

## Implementation Details

The preliminary urban heat mapping and vulnerability analysis were completed through continued technical assistance provided by Earth Economics and the Urban Green Infrastructure Lab.[^1][^5] The analysis revealed that in Broward County, an Earth Economics study found that urban heat islands increase maximum temperatures by as much as 5°F above the local baseline.[^2] Broward is exploring the potential to develop a network of temperature and humidity sensors that would trigger localized alerts upon reaching heat watch and warning advisory thresholds.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The mapping initiative identified that 90,000 people live in neighborhoods where the urban heat islands increase temperatures by 2°F or more and where average per capita incomes are less than 200% the federal poverty level.[^2] The public health burden of urban heat islands was confirmed to be disproportionately levied on the lowest income households, with approximately 90,000 residents with income significantly below the poverty level living in neighborhoods where temperatures are higher than the rest of the county.[^4][^5] One third of the 90,000 low-income residents in high-temperature neighborhoods are at higher risk to heat health impacts due to their age.[^4] Specifically, 33,000 people live in areas that meet the above criteria and have a high percentage of population in high risk age groups (under age 14 or over age 65).[^2] The model estimated that 13 to 44 lives are lost in Broward County annually due to urban heat island effects.[^4] In the Broward County's most severe urban heat islands, all-cause mortality rates are estimated to increase by 4.7% (2.4% - 7%) when baseline temperatures exceed 90°.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

In Broward County, the average temperature in 2021 was 3.7 degrees Fahrenheit warmer than it was in 1921.[^3] The number of days above 88°F has increased 36% since 2000, to 90 days per year, with projections suggesting 144 days per year by 2050.[^5] Broward County's heat map outlines the types of heat risk reduction strategies it is advancing – from stronger building codes to green infrastructure to cooling centers.[^3]

## Business Analysis

The heat vulnerability mapping information served as the basis for grant applications to support development of heat mitigation strategies inclusive of green infrastructure.[^4] The analysis provided critical data to support policy and investment decisions targeting the approximately 90,000 residents with income significantly below the poverty level living in neighborhoods where temperatures are higher than the rest of the county.[^4][^5] Broward County's heat map outlines multiple heat risk reduction strategies it is advancing, including stronger building codes, green infrastructure, and cooling centers.[^3]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://www.broward.org/resilience/Maps/Pages/heat.aspx
- [^3]: https://southeastfloridaclimatecompact.org/news-post/broward-county-completes-heat-analysis-and-predictions/
- [^4]: https://www.broward.org/Climate/Pages/News.aspx
- [^5]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Broward-US/CCAP_2020_ADA.pdf

# Research Report: City Resilience Index Monitoring Framework

## Solution Overview

The City of New Orleans piloted the City Resilience Index (CRI) to serve as a holistic framework for monitoring across sectors and scales.[^3][^6][^7] This monitoring system was developed by Arup with support from The Rockefeller Foundation and provides a universal framework for cities to measure and monitor the multiple factors that contribute to their resilience.[^1] The approach is structured around four dimensions, 12 goals and 52 indicators, and is underpinned by a qualitative scoring methodology that enables cities to assess their resilience and develop a robust evidence base to support investment and programming decisions.[^1]

## Technical Components

The CRI framework encompasses four core dimensions: Health and Wellbeing, Economy and Society, Infrastructure and Environment, and Leadership and Strategy.[^1] This holistic articulation of city resilience operates through 52 indicators distributed across 12 goals, providing comprehensive measurement capabilities.[^1] The qualitative scoring methodology embedded within the system enables systematic assessment of resilience factors and supports evidence-based decision-making for investment and programming.[^1] New Orleans has been listed among the cities in the Americas where the CRI has been applied, demonstrating its operational deployment.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The City Resilience Index implementation was established as a supporting action under the 'Launch City Resilience Index' initiative led by the City of New Orleans in partnership with the Rockefeller Foundation.[^3] Management of the CRI was assigned to the Office of Performance and Accountability (OPA), which oversees the monitoring framework across sectors and scales.[^3] As the City moved towards implementation of the resilience strategy, they gained continuing access to a $100 million pool of tools and services from the 100RC network and platform partners.[^2] Ongoing updating and monitoring of CRI metrics became part of the City's practice of resilience and plays a critical role in measuring the ultimate impact of New Orleans' resilience building efforts.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The monitoring framework supports the broader Resilient New Orleans strategy, which is comprised of 41 actions that are ambitious and programmatic, as well as short and long-term goals.[^8] This portfolio of policies combines environmental, social, and infrastructure systems to reduce climate risk and socio-economic inequality.[^4] The strategy includes a 50% CO2 reduction goal by 2030 with resilient New Orleans.[^4] The City proposes to establish a Resilience Center to serve as a central point of coordination for the City's comprehensive resilience strategy and as a hub for resilience innovation and leadership.[^5][^4] The strategy was developed in partnership with over 350 stakeholders, including representatives from the Foundation for Louisiana, Greater New Orleans Foundation, Lower 9th Ward Center for Sustainable Engagement and Development, Neighborhoods Partnership Network, Institute for Women and Ethnic Studies and Broad Community Connections.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

More than a decade after Hurricane Katrina flooded 80% of the city and the homes and businesses of one million people, New Orleans moved forward with its comprehensive strategy to reduce inequality and climate change vulnerability.[^4] Climate change is expected to disproportionately affect poor communities, so to increase resiliency, New Orleans is investing in the most vulnerable neighborhoods.[^4] The monitoring framework enables systematic tracking of resilience-building efforts across multiple dimensions, supporting evidence-based adaptation planning and investment decisions.[^1][^3]

## Business Analysis

The implementation was led by the city's Chief Resilience Officer with process guidance from 100 Resilient Cities — Pioneered by the Rockefeller Foundation and HR&A Advisors.[^8] The City Resilience Framework (CRF) highlights the interconnectedness of systems in building resilience and guided the Resilient New Orleans strategy development process from the beginning.[^8] The strategy is focused on advancing social cohesion and mobility, with more than 52% of black men unemployed or underemployed, and a workforce development program in progress to accompany environmental projects.[^4] The $100 million pool of tools and services from the 100RC network and platform partners provides substantial resource support for ongoing implementation and scaling of resilience initiatives.[^2]

## Sources

- [^4]: https://www.c40.org/case-studies/cities100-new-orleans-comprehensive-strategy-for-equality-and-resilience/
- [^5]: https://nola.gov/nola/media/Stormwater/NDRC_Phase2_NewOrleans_Narrative_Graphics.pdf
- [^6]: https://www.datacenterresearch.org/reports_analysis/the-new-orleans-index-at-twenty-measuring-greater-new-orleans-progress-toward-resilience/

# Research Report: Multi-Risk Monitoring Centre for Climate Event Anticipation

## Solution Overview

The Direction de la sécurité civile et de la résilience intends to create a multi-risk monitoring centre mandated to anticipate events more quickly and effectively, mobilize the concerned actors, and collate all monitoring data.[^1][^2] This approach will make it possible to gain a comprehensive and accurate picture of situations presenting minor impacts with the potential to become major impacts.[^1] The initiative responds to the city's experience with several major weather events in recent decades, from the 1998 Ice Storm, to flooding in 2017, to a major heatwave in 2018.[^3]

## Technical Components

A Bureau de la résilience pilot project is already underway to develop a dashboard for meteorological risks that will serve to track forecasts and probable impact thresholds online, and issue alerts to emergency responders.[^1] This software application represents the technical foundation for the broader monitoring centre infrastructure. The system is designed to collate all monitoring data from multiple sources into a centralized platform.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The time horizon for the multi-risk monitoring centre project is medium term.[^1] Principal partners for this initiative include Ville de Montréal (Bureau de la résilience, Centre de sécurité civile, Ville intelligente et numérique, Service des technologies de l'information, Centre de gestion de la mobilité urbaine, Service de police de la Ville de Montréal, Service de sécurité incendie de Montréal, Service de l'environnement), Direction de la santé publique, Environment and Climate Change Canada, Wood, 100 Resilient Cities, citizens, and others to be determined.[^1] The collaborative structure brings together municipal departments, public health authorities, federal environmental agencies, private sector consultants, and international resilience organizations to support the centre's development and operations.

## Benefits and Impacts

The monitoring centre will enable authorities to anticipate events more quickly and effectively while mobilizing the concerned actors across multiple agencies and departments.[^1][^2] The system's ability to gain a comprehensive and accurate picture of situations presenting minor impacts with the potential to become major impacts provides operational benefits for emergency response coordination.[^1] The meteorological risk dashboard pilot project will issue alerts to emergency responders, improving response times and situational awareness.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This monitoring approach directly addresses the city's vulnerability to multiple climate hazards demonstrated through historical events including the 1998 Ice Storm, flooding in 2017, and a major heatwave in 2018.[^3] The multi-risk framework enables comprehensive tracking of meteorological threats and their probable impact thresholds, supporting proactive adaptation measures.[^1]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Montréal-CA/Montreal-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^2]: https://www.rockefellerfoundation.org/wp-content/uploads/Institutionalizing-Urban-Resilience-A-Midterm-Monitoring-and-Evaluation-Report-of-100-Resilient-Cities.pdf
- [^3]: https://changingclimate.ca/map/montreals-resilient-city-strategy/

# Research Report: Community Solar Microgrid for Emergency Response and Communications

## Solution Overview

Following Hurricane María in 2017, Casa Pueblo in Adjuntas, Puerto Rico coordinated a diaspora-driven emergency response that distributed solar-powered lamps across multiple municipalities and maintained critical radio communications between the island and mainland United States.[^3][^5] This community-based initiative leveraged Casa Pueblo's existing solar infrastructure, which had operated independently since going off-grid months before the hurricane, to provide emergency power, communications, and humanitarian aid distribution across Puerto Rico's central highlands.[^2][^1] The approach demonstrated how decentralized renewable energy systems combined with community networks could maintain essential services during prolonged grid failures.

## Technical Components

Casa Pueblo's community center operated on solar power infrastructure that included 42 solar panels of 260 watts each, producing approximately 16,000 annual kWh, combined with a Deka Unigy II 48AVR95-93 battery system.[^12] The organization's Radio Casa Pueblo station broadcast on 1020AM in the central zone and via internet, operating entirely on renewable energy through a project implemented in collaboration with Maximo Solar Industries.[^12] Casa Pueblo worked with locally owned solar company Sol de la Montaña to install small alternating-current (AC) solar energy systems on families' roofs as part of the Cucubano project.[^10] In May 2024, workers from Sol de la Montaña installed 12 solar panels (320 watts each) on one family's home roof in Adjuntas, along with inverters and two batteries.[^10] The organization pioneered solar-powered streetlights (Postirriquenos) and solar refrigerators crucial for preserving perishable medicines like insulin and foods in the warm climate.[^4] Casa Pueblo also provided satellite phone service access for thousands of Puerto Ricans left without cell phone service.[^9]

## Implementation Details

Casa Pueblo installed its first solar panels in 1999 and went off the electric grid entirely just months before Hurricane María struck in September 2017.[^2] The organization was founded in the 1980s in opposition to government mining operations and developed into a cultural center advocating for protection of resources and community self-management.[^11][^10] After María made landfall on September 20, 2017 with winds exceeding 155 miles per hour, members of Puerto Rico's diaspora contacted Casa Pueblo to ask how they could help.[^2][^13] Casa Pueblo told diaspora members to send solar lamps instead of money, and over the next six months, the organization distributed 14,000 lamps via postal service to Adjuntas, Utuado, Lares, Peñuelas, and Ponce.[^2][^3] Casa Pueblo received $15,000 in October 2017 focused on procurement and distribution of solar-powered lights to replace the devastated power grid in the central part of the island.[^6] The organization received an additional $50,000 in July 2018 to install solar powered electrical systems for families whose medical needs depended on constant power in the rural highlands of central Puerto Rico.[^6] The Radio Casa Pueblo solar project cost nearly $73,000.[^12] Casa Pueblo made collaborative arrangements with North American institutions in cultural centers of New Jersey, Chicago, and many places through the Puerto Rican diaspora.[^9] Organizations from the mainland U.S. that collaborated with Casa Pueblo's post-María efforts included the Sierra Club, Grassroots Boston, JPB Foundation, and David Galarza NY Foundation.[^9] Solar panels installed by Casa Pueblo were paid for by foundation donations and proceeds from locally grown coffee sold by the organization.[^11] Rebecca Rodríguez Banch served as community liaison for Casa Pueblo, managing the Cucubano project and selecting project participants based on need without asking anything from the families.[^10] In the six years following Hurricane María, Casa Pueblo helped fund and install more than 350 solar energy systems on buildings across Adjuntas, including in an assisted living facility, a grocery store, the local fire station, and many homes in the poorest neighborhoods.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

After Hurricane María, Casa Pueblo distributed 14,000 solar lamps, lighting 70 percent of the houses and businesses in Adjuntas.[^9] The organization also distributed solar lamps in Utuado, Jayuya, Humacao, Loíza, Vieques, and Salinas, along with 200 roof tarps and around 1,500 water filters for those without potable water.[^9] During the outages following María, Adjuntas residents gathered at Casa Pueblo where they were able to charge phones, run dialysis machines, and store medications in the center's refrigerators, with one neighbor coming daily to administer her son's asthma treatment.[^2] In the region of Adjuntas where Casa Pueblo had invested in systems to provide solar-powered capacity to homes and businesses, hundreds of people managed better during months without access to electricity through the island's power grid.[^4] Casa Pueblo's home base became an oasis for people in the southern and central regions of the island to charge their phones and communicate with loved ones outside the island.[^7] Radio Casa Pueblo played a significant role in informing inhabitants of the island's central region during Hurricane María, serving as a vital source of news and information when communication was nearly impossible for millions on the island.[^12][^4] People in or from Adjuntas could email voice messages to Casa Pueblo to be delivered to family members.[^1] In the central highland region of Adjuntas, the store Colmado Cielomar had just had a solar panel installed by Casa Pueblo, and throughout the blackout, the proprietor managed to offer roasted chicken and perishable goods, as well as refrigeration and a lighted place to gather.[^4] When Hurricane Fiona hit in 2022, the local fire station in Adjuntas with solar power became a regional response center, intercepting calls from a station in Ponce, 15 miles to the south, which had lost power.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Hurricane María struck Puerto Rico as a category 4 storm in 2017, ultimately killing over 4,000 people in the 90 days after the storm made landfall, with most deaths occurring not from wind and rain but from months without power, clean water, and a functioning health system.[^10][^13] After María, communications collapsed for several reasons including destroyed antennas, impassable access roads, and lack of fuel for power generators.[^12] Casa Pueblo's solar-powered infrastructure and diaspora coordination network demonstrated how decentralized renewable energy systems could maintain critical services during catastrophic grid failures caused by extreme weather events.

## Business Analysis

Puerto Rico's power grid remained fragile and underinvested, with its operation privatized in 2021 as a supposed solution to grid problems and the Puerto Rico Electric Power Authority's $9 billion public debt.[^10] Puerto Rican power customers paid about twice as much as U.S. customers and saw seven electric rate increases in the year prior to 2024.[^10] Casa Pueblo's financing model combined foundation donations, proceeds from locally grown coffee sales, and targeted grants to fund solar installations without requiring payment from recipient families.[^11][^6] The organization partnered with Oxfam after the hurricane, with Oxfam supporting Casa Pueblo's leadership toward energy self-sufficiency and ecological preservation.[^4] Casa Pueblo's community-based approach emphasized autogestion (self-governance), providing energy security regardless of families' circumstances or ability to pay.[^10] The model demonstrated how community organizations could leverage diaspora networks, foundation support, and local revenue streams to build climate resilience infrastructure independent of centralized utility systems.

## Sources

# Research Report: AutoCASE Triple Bottom Line Infrastructure Analysis Tool

## Solution Overview

Oakland plans to explore digital cost-benefit tools to demonstrate the triple bottom line benefits of green infrastructure projects, with AutoCASE being useful for determining triple bottom line returns associated with infrastructure projects at sizes ranging from small (e.g., $500,000) to extremely large ($1 billion+).[^2] This low-cost, web-based analysis tool automates triple-bottom-line analysis of a proposed project's social, environmental and financial impacts.[^1] The technology automates calculation of a proposed project's net present value based on its likely social, environmental and financial impacts.[^1]

## Technical Components

AutoCASE functions as a cloud-based plug-in for the Autodesk Infrastructure Design Suite, letting users bring TBL analysis to AutoCAD Civil 3D building information models.[^1] The system uses several data sources in its calculations, with a key one comprising the 60 sustainability criteria from Envision, a sustainable-infrastructure rating system managed by the Institute for Sustainable Infrastructure.[^1] This approach enables users to continue sustainable planning through the design phase by integrating triple bottom line analysis directly into building information modeling workflows.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The City will explore using digital cost-benefit tools to demonstrate the triple bottom line (economic, social, and environmental) benefits of GI projects.[^2] AutoCASE is useful for determining triple bottom line returns associated with infrastructure projects at sizes ranging from small (e.g., $500,000) to extremely large ($1 billion+).[^3] Traditional triple-bottom-line analysis costs $50,000 to $150,000 per project and can usually only be afforded by large firms on a per-project basis.[^1] This web-based tool provides a low-cost alternative that automates the traditionally expensive process of analyzing a proposed project's social, environmental and financial impacts.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The technology enables comprehensive evaluation of infrastructure investments across economic, social, and environmental dimensions simultaneously.[^2] By automating calculation of a proposed project's net present value based on its likely social, environmental and financial impacts, the system provides quantitative assessment capabilities that were previously cost-prohibitive for many organizations.[^1] This approach makes triple-bottom-line analysis accessible at a fraction of the traditional cost, which ranges from $50,000 to $150,000 per project.[^1]

[^1]: [Source 1]
[^2]: [Source 2]
[^3]: [Source 3]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Oakland-US/Oakland-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^3]: https://cao-94612.s3.amazonaws.com/documents/Oakland-GSI-Plan-Final-20190930_sm.pdf

# Research Report: Smart Water Meter System with AI-Powered Consumption Analytics

## Solution Overview

The Water Supplies Department is exploring the use of Automatic Meter Reading (AMR) coupled with mobile applications to enhance consumers' awareness of their water consumption on a timely basis, thereby facilitating behavioural changes to save water.[^3] This approach addresses a critical consumption pattern identified through big data analytics, which revealed that approximately 1% of domestic customers account for over 15% of the city's total domestic water consumption.[^1] Each Hong Kong resident currently consumes an average of about 130 litres of water per day.[^1]

## Technical Components

The system utilizes smart water meters equipped with high-precision readers and AI-powered technologies to analyse customers' water usage data, enabling identification of whether domestic water usage is mainly for cooking, laundry, or showering, and comparing these findings with the averages of other households.[^1] In addition to enabling automatic reading of water meters, AMR provides customers with timely water consumption data and related information.[^2] The system can detect possible water leakage in consumers' plumbing systems by analysing water consumption patterns and send alert messages for early follow-up action.[^4] Once installed, the smart water meter readers start tracking water usage for eight weeks and transmit the information to a server located in the University of Hong Kong campus.[^1] The radio frequency emitted by the smart water meter is of very low power and is classified as non-ionising radiation, with RF emission levels well below the safety limit set by the International Commission on Non-Ionizing Radiation Protection (ICNIRP).[^4] Water consumption data is encrypted during transmission to ensure data security, and WSD handles all personal data in accordance with the Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance.[^4]

## Implementation Details

WSD has been conducting a pilot project on smart water metering for approximately 400 households in Tseung Kwan O, Sai Kung, Sheung Shui and Kwun Tong since 2018.[^4] The department planned to install smart water meters for all consumers in Hong Kong by phases starting from 2021.[^4] The implementation strategy includes installing AMR in appropriate, new public and private developments, with an aim to install about 430,000 smart water meters for the period from 2021 to 2031.[^2] Customers participating in the programme engage in an eight-week water conservation campaign where the taskforce installs smart water meter readers on the existing meters, without requiring any pipe modifications or affecting the water supply.[^1] The smart water meter is provided and installed by WSD free of charge.[^4] WSD plans to pilot the use of smart water meters and Automatic Meter Reading System to work in conjunction with WIN to help identify problems.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

Among the first batch of programme participants in the domestic sector, more than 80% of participants were able to reduce their water consumption.[^1] The use of AMR raises customers' awareness about water conservation and alerts them of abnormal water consumption which may be caused by leakage in water mains inside their premises.[^2] Consumers can access their water consumption information, such as hourly, daily and monthly consumption data, through the WSD website or mobile app anytime and anywhere.[^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This water conservation technology addresses climate adaptation challenges by promoting efficient water resource management in response to increasing water scarcity risks. The system's ability to detect leakage and reduce consumption through behavioural change supports resilience against drought conditions and water supply disruptions that may intensify with climate change.

## Business Analysis

The implementation model involves municipal investment in infrastructure deployment, with WSD providing and installing smart water meters free of charge to consumers.[^4] The phased rollout approach from 2021 to 2031 targeting 430,000 installations represents a significant capital investment in water infrastructure modernization.[^2] The programme operates through a public service delivery model where the government agency manages both the technical infrastructure and consumer engagement components. The integration with the Water Intelligent Network (WIN), which includes establishment of a total of 2,400 DMAs and PMAs,[^5] demonstrates a comprehensive approach to water system optimization that combines consumer-level metering with network-wide monitoring infrastructure.

## Sources

- [^5]: https://www.wsd.gov.hk/en/core-businesses/major-infrastructure-projects/water-intelligent-network-win/index.html

# Research Report: Digital Surface Flood Warning System with Multi-Sensor Network

## Solution Overview

Manchester City Council has committed to investing in remote monitoring systems at known flooding sites to enable a more effective response.[^5] The Digital Surface Flood Warning System (DSFWS) will integrate data from existing river gauges, new low-cost sensors, weather radar, and social media to provide more accurate, timely, and localised flood warnings.[^1] This approach addresses the critical need for enhanced flood prediction and response capabilities in areas vulnerable to surface water flooding.

## Technical Components

The system architecture includes procurement and installation of a network of new, low-cost sensors for monitoring river levels, soil moisture, and surface water.[^1] The project will focus on surface water level monitoring using new bollard sensors and rainfall, soil moisture and gully sensors.[^3] These compact, battery-powered sensors collect data continuously, transmitting it via a mobile data network to a cloud-based platform.[^6] The networks are low energy and low-frequency allowing the transfer of data from remote, battery-powered sensors to user-friendly dashboards.[^3] The network of sensors would transmit real-time surface water levels to a central hub and create an early warning system of potential flooding.[^3] Data from the Environment Agency and the Met Office will be accessed through existing data sharing agreements.[^1] The system will use machine learning algorithms to continuously improve its predictive capabilities, learning from each new event.[^1] Development of a user-friendly interface (dashboard) for flood incident managers will provide real-time data visualisation and decision support tools.[^1] The scope also includes development of a public-facing web portal and mobile app for disseminating flood warnings and advice.[^1] The core data platform and forecasting models will be developed by a blended team of LCC staff and specialists from the University of Leeds, building on the research and development work already undertaken through the iCASP project.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The total estimated cost for the development and implementation of the DSFWS is £500,000 over two years.[^1] The DSFWS capital costs include hardware (sensors, servers) at £150,000, software development at £250,000, and project management and contingency at £100,000.[^1] Ongoing operational costs are estimated at £25,000 per annum.[^1] The ongoing operational cost of £25,000 per year includes staff time for system maintenance and operation at £15,000, data hosting and communication costs at £5,000, and sensor maintenance and replacement at £5,000.[^1] The scope includes a pilot implementation of the system in a sub-catchment of the River Aire to test and refine its performance.[^1] The DSFWS will provide improved warnings for an area containing 5,000 residential properties and 1,000 commercial properties at risk of flooding.[^1] A post-project evaluation will be carried out 12 months after the system becomes fully operational to assess the extent to which the project has achieved its objectives and delivered its intended benefits.[^1] A further evaluation will be conducted after five years to assess the long-term impacts and benefits of the system.[^1]

Comparable projects demonstrate the feasibility of large-scale sensor deployment. United Utilities is planning to install over 20,000 sensors at key points on the sewer pipes across the North West region, providing live information back to teams so they can monitor, identify and resolve any issues quickly.[^2] The plan is to have all 20,000 sensors installed by the end of 2022.[^2] The sensor installation normally takes just a few hours with no digging or noisy machinery required.[^2] Ongoing maintenance of the sensors involves simple straightforward checks inside the manhole once a year or less in some cases to ensure the sensor is working as it should.[^2] Plans are underway to create a network of sensors across West Yorkshire to give people earlier warnings about the risk of surface water flooding following a successful bid for £97,000.[^3]

Technical implementations in other contexts provide additional insights. The Community Flood Telemetry project developed a low-cost, open-source telemetry system that could be built, installed, and maintained by community flood action groups to monitor local water levels, with a total cost of approximately £115 per monitoring station.[^4] The Community Flood Telemetry system is based on a Raspberry Pi single-board computer, connected to an ultrasonic sensor to measure water level and a camera to provide visual verification, with a 3G dongle used to transmit data to a central server.[^4] The Community Flood Telemetry system has an ongoing cost for a data-only SIM card for the 3G dongle, which is typically around £5-£10 per month depending on the network provider and data allowance.[^4] A public-facing website (www.communityfloodtelemetry.co.uk) was developed to display the data in near real-time, showing the latest water level and image from each monitoring station, as well as interactive graphs of historical data.[^4] The main challenges encountered with the Community Flood Telemetry system were related to power supply, with the system currently requiring a mains power source, and intermittent mobile signal in some locations.[^4] Recommendations for future work include exploring alternative power sources such as solar, investigating more robust sensor technologies, developing a mobile app with alert functionalities, and expanding the network to more at-risk communities.[^4]

Metasphere's Sense Level technology, a radar-based level sensor, was selected to monitor water levels within key components of the Mansfield SuDS scheme including downpipe planters, raingardens, bioswales, detention basins and specific points within the sewer network.[^6] The real-time water level data captured by Metasphere's monitoring solutions enables accurate calculation of drainage volumes and supports sophisticated hydrological analysis and modelling, providing a deeper understanding of the system's behaviour under varying conditions.[^6]

## Benefits and Impacts

The project aims to increase the lead time for flood warnings from 2 hours to 6 hours within 2 years of implementation for a 1-in-100-year flood event.[^1] The project aims to achieve a 25% reduction in the number of false alarms and missed warnings within 3 years of implementation.[^1] The project aims to reduce annual average damages from flooding in the target area by 10% over the 10-year appraisal period.[^1] The improved warning system is expected to lead to a 10% reduction in damages, based on evidence from previous studies.[^1] The annual average benefit is calculated as £775,000 per year based on avoided damages to residential and commercial properties.[^1]

Operational efficiency improvements include a 20% reduction in the time taken to deploy temporary flood barriers within 2 years of implementation.[^1] The project aims to increase public awareness by raising the percentage of residents in at-risk areas signed up to receive flood warnings from 50% to 75% within 2 years of implementation.[^1] The sensors help United Utilities reduce the risk of flooding or pollution from the pipework by discovering potential issues in the sewers before they become a problem.[^2]

User experience data from comparable systems indicates significant value in visual monitoring capabilities. Community members reported that the camera image was consistently highlighted as the most useful feature of the system, providing a richer and more intuitive understanding of the situation than numerical data alone and allowing them to spot debris and potential blockages.[^4] The most requested feature from community users was the ability to set custom alert thresholds to receive email or text message notifications when water levels reach pre-defined points.[^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This monitoring infrastructure directly addresses the increasing frequency and intensity of surface water flooding events driven by climate change. Enhanced early warning capabilities enable communities and emergency responders to take protective actions before flood conditions develop, reducing both property damage and risks to public safety. The system's integration of multiple data sources and machine learning capabilities allows for adaptive improvement as precipitation patterns continue to shift under changing climate conditions.

## Business Analysis

The Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) for the project is estimated at 4.1, with a Net Present Value (NPV) of £1.55 million over a 10-year period.[^1] The payback period for the initial investment is within 3 years of the system becoming operational.[^1] Sensitivity analysis demonstrates project robustness across varying scenarios. A 20% increase in project costs would reduce the BCR to 3.4.[^1] A 20% decrease in project benefits would reduce the BCR to 3.3.[^1] If the damage reduction is only 5% instead of 10%, the BCR falls to 2.05.[^1] These financial metrics indicate strong economic viability even under conservative assumptions, supporting the case for municipal investment in flood monitoring infrastructure as a cost-effective climate adaptation measure.

[^1]: DSFWS Project Documentation
[^2]: United Utilities Sensor Installation Programme
[^3]: West Yorkshire Surface Water Flooding Sensor Network
[^4]: Community Flood Telemetry Project Report
[^5]: Manchester City Council Climate Adaptation Strategy
[^6]: Metasphere Mansfield SuDS Monitoring Case Study

## Sources

- [^1]: https://icasp.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/sites/13/2024/03/LDF-BUSINESS-CASE-v1.1-Digital-Smart-Flood-Warning-Systems-business-case-26-Jan-2024.pdf
- [^2]: https://www.unitedutilities.com/help-and-support/wastewater-services/sewer-improvement-work-in-your-area/innovation-leads-the-way/
- [^3]: https://icasp.org.uk/projects-2-2/west-yorkshire-flood-innovation-programme-flip/digital-surface-water-flood-warning-system-lorawan/
- [^4]: https://thefloodhub.co.uk/wp-content/uploads/2018/12/FT-Q-R58-Community-Flood-Telemetry-1-2.pdf
- [^5]: https://www.manchester.gov.uk/download/downloads/id/21707/action_planpdf.pdf
- [^6]: https://metasphere.co.uk/news/

# Research Report: Multi-Hazard Early Warning System with Hydrometeorological Network

## Solution Overview

Georgia has experienced repeated extreme flooding events in recent years, prompting the development of modern monitoring equipment and detailed hazard and risk maps as the foundation for a new multi-hazard early warning system.[^1] A feasibility study identified locations for installing observation equipment to create an observation network of meteorological and hydrological parameters in the Mtkvari River Basin for fluvial flood monitoring and across the city for surface water and pluvial flood monitoring.[^4] This approach addresses the flash floods and mudflows occurring on small rivers as a result of heavy rains that impact various parts of Tbilisi, causing heavy damage and human casualties.[^2]

## Technical Components

The observation network will comprise more than 140 observation devices to be purchased and installed as part of a significantly expanded hydrometeorological observation network.[^7] For quality data processing and storage, a high-performance computer was purchased for the National Environment Agency, which has been operating since 2021.[^5] The monitoring infrastructure targets both the Mtkvari River Basin for fluvial flood monitoring and city-wide locations for surface water and pluvial flood monitoring.[^4] The Municipality initiated various infrastructure projects in the affected area following the 2015 flood, including river monitoring systems.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The Multi Hazard Risk Assessment & Early Warning System is being implemented by the Italy–Georgia Bilateral funded project through the National Environment Agency at the Ministry of Environmental Protection and Agriculture.[^4] UNDP and the Government of Georgia, with funding from the Green Climate Fund, Swiss and Swedish governments are implementing a 7-year programme dedicated to reducing the risk of climate-driven disasters in Georgia with an overall budget of US$74 million.[^5] The programme comprises Scaling-up Multi-Hazard Early Warning System and the Use of Climate Information in Georgia with US$27 million in funding from the Green Climate Fund.[^5] The MHEWS project started in December 2018 and has an end date of July 2025, receiving total contributions of $31,843,756 from Green Climate Fund and Swiss Agency for Development & Cooperation.[^3] The design and introduction of MHEWS covers all 11 river basins of Georgia.[^3] Tbilisi City Hall and NEA secured technical and financial assistance from IFIs to conduct risk assessment and early warning system feasibility studies, with implementation planned for Medium Term 2022-2025.[^4] An earlier flood mitigation assessment project in Tbilisi had a budget range of $200-250k, with the Ministry of Environmental Protection and Agriculture serving as the National Designated Entity and HYDROC Environment and Development as implementor.[^2] The flood risk management plan for the Vere River basin is assigned to Tbilisi City Hall and the Ministry of Environmental Protection and Agriculture with a timeline of 2020-2025 and an estimated cost of 20,000,000 GEL.[^6]

## Benefits and Impacts

The observation network provides the foundation for a new multi-hazard early warning system designed to address repeated extreme flooding events.[^1] A technology transfer project in Tbilisi included recommendations for eventual establishment of regular hydro-meteorological observations and monitoring as part of flood mitigation measures.[^2] The flood risk management plan for the Vere River basin includes a combination of structural and non-structural measures, such as the construction of flood defenses, the restoration of the river channel, and the implementation of an early warning system.[^6]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This monitoring network directly addresses flash floods and mudflows occurring on small rivers as a result of heavy rains that impact various parts of Tbilisi, causing heavy damage and human casualties.[^2] The system targets both fluvial flooding from the Mtkvari River Basin and surface water and pluvial flooding across the city.[^4] The multi-hazard early warning system provides critical infrastructure for reducing the risk of climate-driven disasters in Georgia.[^5]

## Business Analysis

The implementation demonstrates diverse financing mechanisms, with the MHEWS project receiving $31,843,756 from Green Climate Fund and Swiss Agency for Development & Cooperation.[^3] The broader programme secured US$74 million from UNDP, the Government of Georgia, Green Climate Fund, and Swiss and Swedish governments.[^5] The Italy–Georgia Bilateral funded project provides additional support through the National Environment Agency.[^4] Tbilisi City Hall and NEA secured technical and financial assistance from international financial institutions to conduct risk assessment and early warning system feasibility studies.[^4] The flood risk management plan for the Vere River basin represents a 20,000,000 GEL investment from 2020-2025.[^6] Earlier feasibility work operated at a smaller scale with a budget range of $200-250k.[^2]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://climatepromise.undp.org/news-and-stories/how-climate-information-and-early-warning-systems-protect-lives-and-economies
- [^2]: https://www.ctc-n.org/technical-assistance/projects/assessment-suitable-flood-mitigation-measures-tbilisi
- [^3]: https://www.undp.org/georgia/projects/early-warning-climate-information
- [^4]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Europe & Middle East/Tbilisi-Georgia/Tbilisi_Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^5]: https://www.undp.org/georgia/projects/climate-change-disasters
- [^6]: https://com-east.eu/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/tsibilisi-georgia_adaptation_en.pdf
- [^7]: https://mepa.gov.ge/En/Projects/Details/158

# Research Report: Urban Heat Island Mitigation Index Tool

## Solution Overview

An online, interactive urban heat mitigation index was developed by the Cooperative Research Centre for Low Carbon Living (CRC LCL) in 2019 to provide high-level guidelines for urban planning decisions to mitigate urban heat in varying climates and development typologies.[^3] This approach bridges the gap between urban climate research and the practical application of heat reduction strategies in public spaces through simulations tailored to microclimates across different regions in different cities.[^2] The initiative was designed by researchers at UNSW in partnership with the CRC for Low Carbon Living, with project leadership from Associate Professor Lan Ding from UNSW's Faculty of Built Environment.[^1][^2]

## Technical Components

The project produced two distinct online tools as its main outcomes.[^5] The publicly available Urban Heat Island Mitigation Index tool is accessible at http://uhimitigationindex.be.unsw.edu.au/ and provides high-level guidelines for varying climates and development typologies to support urban planning decisions across different urban contexts.[^3][^4] The companion Microclimate and Urban Heat Island Mitigation Decision Support Tool has restricted access, with login credentials available by request from UHI-Index@unsw.edu.au.[^4][^5] This decision-support system brings together scientific models, case studies and broad guidelines to help government and built environment industry reference top heat mitigation strategies and effects, relative to their city.[^4][^5] The technology integrates scientific models with a range of mitigation techniques to perform urban heat island mitigation analysis across both building and urban scales, such as building coatings and roofs, urban form and density, greenery and infrastructure.[^4][^5] The system combines scientific models, case studies and broad sustainability guidelines into an easy to use 3D interface.[^1] Users can visualize the effects of heat mitigation techniques such as adding trees or incorporating cool roofs and pavements by overlaying them onto maps of existing infrastructure.[^1] According to Dr Ding, the tool is unique because it integrates scientific models with a range of mitigation techniques to provide urban heat island mitigation analysis across both urban and building scales, such as urban form, parks, greenery, waterways and cool roofs.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The Urban Heat Island Mitigation Decision Support tool research project was led by the University of New South Wales through the Cooperative Research Centre for Low Carbon Living (CRCLCL).[^6] Project RP2023 ran from January 2017 to December 2018 under the leadership of Dr Lan Ding from UNSW, with funding provided by the CRC for Low Carbon Living.[^4][^5] The research project came to a conclusion in FY19.[^6] The Microclimate and UHI Mitigation Decision-Support Tool was developed by the Low Carbon Living Cooperative Research Centre, whose 45 members include the CSIRO, Bluescope, and five Australian universities.[^2] CRCLCL chief executive officer Professor Deo Prasad stated that the tool is the result of collaboration between multiple research streams and stakeholders, including state and local governments, the built environment industry and academia.[^1] Landcom was involved in the collaborative research as a member of the project steering committee, and also made their projects available as test beds for the practical application of the research.[^6] During FY19, testing of the UHI-DS Tool was completed at Green Square Town Centre and Macarthur Heights in Sydney, two very different geographic areas (inner Sydney and greater Sydney) with distinct microclimates and building types.[^6] The project report incorporates several previously published reports and case studies in addition to two new case studies in Parramatta and Macarthur Heights.[^4] The tool has been put to work demonstrating UHI mitigation scenarios of development projects at Sydney's Green Square Town Centre, Parramatta Civic Link, and Macarthur Heights greenfield development.[^2] Specifically, the system has been used to demonstrate heat mitigation scenarios in Sydney development projects such as the Green Square Town Centre redevelopment by Landcom and the City of Sydney, and the Parramatta Civic Link redevelopment by Parramatta City Council.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The tool can be used by people without any technical background by simply clicking to explore development alternatives and view the potential of mitigation options to reduce the impact of urban overheating.[^2] Project leader and associate professor at UNSW, Lan Ding, stated that the tool's simple design means you don't need a technical background to take advantage of it.[^1] Associate Professor Lan Ding from UNSW's Faculty of Built Environment said the tool will provide scenario analysis to better address the heat and will inform future urban policy, development assessment and planning practices related to mitigate urban overheating, improve outdoor thermal comfort and protect the health of the vulnerable.[^2] The system was expected to become a useful point of reference for decision making by governments, developers and planners.[^1] The project developed an Urban Heat Island Mitigation Performance Index to support governments in establishing performance targets for their planning control, indicating impact on street level temperature, health and mortality, and precinct level energy consumption.[^4][^5][^6] At Macarthur Heights the difference in roof surface temperature between a light grey and white roof is almost 10°C, with darker roofs at the hottest part of the day reaching up to double the temperature of a white roof.[^6] The urban heat mitigation index is part of a suite of UHI mitigation research conducted by CRC LCL and UNSW to provide in-depth insights for the City of Sydney in developing the most effective mitigation strategies for specific urban contexts.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The initiative addresses urban overheating through scenario analysis that informs future urban policy, development assessment and planning practices to mitigate urban heat island effects, improve outdoor thermal comfort and protect the health of vulnerable populations.[^2] The Performance Index supports governments in establishing planning control performance targets that indicate impact on street level temperature, health and mortality, and precinct level energy consumption.[^4][^5][^6]

## Business Analysis

The suite of urban heat mitigation research conducted by the CRC LCL and UNSW can provide in-depth insights for the City of Sydney in developing the most effective mitigation strategies for specific urban contexts.[^3] The publicly available Urban Heat Island Mitigation Index tool provides accessible high-level guidelines for urban planning decisions across varying climates and development typologies, while the restricted-access Microclimate and Urban Heat Island Mitigation Decision Support Tool serves government and built environment industry users.[^3][^4][^5] The collaborative funding model involved the CRC for Low Carbon Living supporting the research project, with participation from 45 member organizations including the CSIRO, Bluescope, and five Australian universities.[^2][^5] Landcom's involvement as a member of the project steering committee and provider of test bed projects demonstrates the public-private partnership approach to development and validation.[^6] The tool was expected to become a useful point of reference for decision making by governments, developers and planners, having already been applied to major Sydney development projects including Green Square Town Centre, Parramatta Civic Link, and Macarthur Heights.[^1][^2]

## Sources
