# Research Report: Tsunami Risk Assessment and Disaster Management Strategy

## Solution Overview

A tsunami simulation conducted by 100RC Platform Partner Swiss Re confirms that Byblos' Old Port is at risk from coastal flooding.[^1][^2][^3] This assessment forms the foundation for Action 37, which involves developing a disaster risk reduction and management strategy that identifies the risks facing the city and outlines a plan for mitigating these risks and responding to emergencies.[^1] Byblos currently lacks a comprehensive disaster risk reduction and management strategy, despite the city's location in a seismically active zone that makes it vulnerable to earthquakes and tsunamis.[^1]

## Technical Components

The tsunami simulation conducted by Swiss Re generated two different tsunami scenarios based on earthquakes in the region.[^2] Based on these scenarios, it is predicted that the largest tsunami could create a 6 meter wave in the port area with a return period of around 2,000 years, while a smaller tsunami of 1-3 meters may re-occur every 300-1,000 years.[^2] The modeling approach incorporated regional seismic data to assess coastal flooding vulnerability at the Old Port location.[^1][^2][^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The simulation addresses multiple climate hazards facing Byblos, as the city's coastal location makes it vulnerable to sea-level rise and other climate-related risks.[^1] The areas at risk of tsunami will increase as global mean sea level rises, with estimates for 2100 ranging from approximately 20 cm to nearly 1 m according to the IPCC (2013).[^4] In Lebanon, tsunami hazard is classified as medium, meaning there is more than a 10% chance of a potentially-damaging tsunami occurring in the next 50 years.[^4]

[^1]: Source 1
[^2]: Source 2
[^3]: Source 3
[^4]: Source 4

# Research Report: Landslide Monitoring System with Robotic Geodetic Equipment

## Solution Overview

Tbilisi City Hall installed a monitoring system in three landslide zones in 2016, which was consolidated with an additional monitoring system on Matchavariani Street and nearby slopes.[^3] The instrumental observation network was launched to determine the mechanism and intensity of landslides across these large landslide areas.[^4] This approach provides accurate measurements of ground movement levels and directions through advanced geodetic equipment deployed across vulnerable slope areas in the capital.[^1]

## Technical Components

The monitoring infrastructure includes a robotic S9 Trimble apparatus installed in Tbilisi, which provides accurate movement levels and directional data.[^1] The system integrates tiltmeters and piezometers to facilitate real-time monitoring of ground movements and pore pressure changes, offering valuable insights into evolving geotechnical conditions.[^1] This technical configuration enables continuous instrumental observations across the three large landslide areas where the monitoring network was established.[^4]

## Implementation Details

Municipal authorities installed the monitoring system in three landslide zones in 2016, which were later consolidated with an additional monitoring system on Matchavariani Street and nearby slopes.[^3][^5] The Vashlijvari slope landslide zone near Machavariani Street received dedicated monitoring infrastructure from Tbilisi City Hall.[^2] The implementation targeted the Vashlijvari-Lisi road section, located on the right bank of Mtkvari valley on the southern slope of the sub-lateral extended and flattened Lisi ridge.[^5] The monitored area is bordered by temporarily cut V-shaped ravines and characterized by slope inclinations varying from 10° to 15°.[^5] Visual and measurement studies of landslide dynamics from March 1 to March 17, 2021, identified multiple cases of regressive expansion of the landslide body, resulting in most observer GPS points being deformed or already within the landslide body.[^5]

## Benefits and Impacts

The monitoring network enables determination of the mechanism and intensity of landslides through instrumental observations across the three large landslide areas.[^4] Real-time monitoring of ground movements and pore pressure changes provides valuable insights into evolving geotechnical conditions.[^1] The robotic S9 Trimble apparatus delivers accurate measurements of movement levels and directions for ongoing assessment.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This monitoring approach directly addresses landslide risks exacerbated by extreme precipitation events in Tbilisi. The heavy rainfall of June 2015 triggered flash flooding from overflowing riverbanks that claimed 21 lives and resulted in approximately USD 50 million in infrastructure damage, including a large-scale landslide between the villages of Tskneti and Akhaldaba.[^3] The monitoring system was installed following these events as part of regulations to prevent building permits in risk areas and the adoption of a flood early warning system.[^3]

## Sources

# Research Report: Automated Water Meter and Leak Detection System

## Solution Overview

The San Francisco Public Utilities Commission deployed automated water meter technology and leak detection systems to reduce potable water losses from its distribution system.[^3] For more than a decade, SFPUC has used advanced metering infrastructure (AMI) to collect hourly water meter readings from all retail customers.[^2] The agency was the first large utility in California to fully automate its meters, allowing it to provide more timely water use data to customers.[^1]

## Technical Components

San Francisco Public Utilities Commission installed the STAR network from Aclara across the city's 180,000 meters to combat drought and save water through hourly meter reading.[^4] The data collected from the city's meters by Aclara's two-way fixed network powers the utility's system for letting customers track usage online.[^4] Aclara generates a report that allows SFPUC to identify customers who may have leaks inside their homes.[^4] Most of Aclara's California customers, including SFPUC, are looking at installing the STAR ZoneScan system, which allows utilities to pinpoint underground leaks.[^4] With hourly usage data transmitted from the meter directly to SFPUC systems, account holders for single family and small multi-family homes in San Francisco are automatically alerted of a potential leak if usage is consistently more than one cubic foot (about 7.5 gallons) every hour in a two-day period.[^2] The Leak Alert program alerts customers when they have three days of non-stop water use, which could indicate a plumbing leak.[^1] For commercial, industrial, and multi-family properties more than three dwelling units, the SFPUC also screens for increases of 50 percent and more in water use over the past 90 days, as well as spikes in nightly water use, in addition to constant use monitoring.[^1] The expanded leak detection program notifies customers with intermittent overnight use of a possible leak when the resulting nighttime consumption ratio is greater than 2, and for properties that usually have regular overnight use, the criterion triggers a notification when the ratio is greater than 4.[^2] San Francisco customers who register to pay their bills online through MyAccount can track their water use on a daily or even hourly basis to detect water waste.[^1]

## Implementation Details

A few years ago, SFPUC wanted to upscale its leak detection program beyond residential and small multi-unit buildings to include approximately 10,000 multi-family homes with six or more residential units and 17,000 commercial, industrial, and institutional accounts.[^2] SFPUC hired Woodard & Curran to help develop a system of detecting possible leaks for large multi-family buildings and CII accounts with more certainty.[^2] Woodard & Curran partnered with local civil engineering firm Lotus Water to collect, analyze, and report on water use trends for SFPUC's large retail water service customers.[^2] Given that large multi-family buildings and CII accounts constitute 54 percent of San Francisco's water consumption, there was a massive amount of data to analyze and determine water use trends.[^2] The SFPUC expanded its Leak Alert program in August 2021 to include commercial and industrial properties and larger multi-family buildings, in addition to single and small multi-family properties and irrigation accounts.[^1] The notification system leverages a customized version of the communications software SFPUC uses for emergencies and runs automatically, with notifications sent through all available communication methods, first prioritizing SMS text and email, followed by automated phone call and mailed letter.[^2] The utility reaches out to those who show up on the report by sending weekly postcards notifying them of a possible leak.[^4] It monitors the reports and notes which accounts have come off the list, assuming that they have responded to the utility's notice and fixed the leak.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

The automated water meter technology is estimated to save 54 million gallons per year by notifying customers about leaks.[^3] In the last fiscal year, the Leak Alert program saved SFPUC customers an estimated 47 million gallons of water.[^1] Since adding large multi-family units and CII accounts to SFPUC's leak detection program, the agency has realized an additional 30 million gallons of water savings per year.[^2] This brings the total savings across the leak detection program, including single family, small multi-family, and irrigation accounts, to approximately 76 million gallons per year.[^2] Depending on the customer sector, this represents a 20 to 50 percent reduction in volume of water lost due to leaks.[^2] Approximately 11 percent of CII and 19 percent of large multi-family accounts are notified of a potential leak every year.[^2] Of those notified in the last year, 88 percent stopped constant use within four weeks.[^2] The King George Hotel in the City's Union Square District is one the local businesses that has benefitted from the expanded Leak Alert program, after the SFPUC notified the hotel following analysis of water usage data, which indicated that the business was using more than 200 gallons per hour of water than typical.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The SFPUC provides drinking water to 2.7 million people throughout the Bay Area.[^1] This leak detection technology deployment reduces losses of potable water from the SFPUC distribution system as part of the Innovations Program, addressing water conservation needs during drought conditions.[^3] The SFPUC's water loss control program is described in Section 6.2.5 as part of DMM 5 Programs to Assess and Manage Distribution System Real Loss, with non-revenue water representing the difference between the volume of water that enters the distribution system and the volume of water that is billed to customers and comprised of both apparent and real losses.[^5]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://www.woodardcurran.com/projects/san-francisco-public-utilities-commission-leak-alert-program/
- [^3]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/San-Francisco-US/09_cap_water_addendum.pdf
- [^4]: https://www.waterworld.com/drinking-water-treatment/distribution/article/16199336/sfpuc-saves-water-combats-drought-with-advanced-metering-technologies
- [^5]: https://www.sf.gov/sites/default/files/2024-02/31_sfpuc_2021_uwmp.pdf

# Research Report: Swiss Re CatNet Global Natural Hazard Risk Assessment Platform

## Solution Overview

Swiss Re's CatNet platform serves as a global location intelligence tool that enables organizations to assess and visualize natural hazard exposure for any location worldwide.[^2] This proprietary risk assessment system is specifically designed to provide insurers with comprehensive overviews and assessments of natural hazard exposures, including flood, earthquake, tsunami, and wind and storm surge.[^4] The platform allows users to analyze exposure to natural catastrophe risk by combining hazard, loss, exposure and clients' insurance information with selected background maps and satellite imagery, enabling risk assessment from individual locations to entire portfolios.[^4][^3]

## Technical Components

The CatNet platform integrates multiple sophisticated data layers and modeling capabilities to deliver comprehensive risk assessments. Swiss Re announced the addition of Fathom's advanced Global, UK, and Japan Flood Maps to the CatNet risk assessment platform in July 2024.[^1] This integration followed the earlier incorporation of Fathom's US Flood Map, representing a significant development in managing and understanding flood risks.[^1] By integrating Fathom's flood maps, Swiss Re's clients gained access to high-resolution, accurate datasets covering the entire planet.[^1] The incorporation of Fathom's flood maps enables sophisticated climate-driven present-day flood risk assessment, instead of relying on historical data alone.[^3] Fathom's independently validated, high-resolution flood maps are now available across multiple regions within the platform, including the Global Flood Map, US Flood Map, UK Flood Map and Japan Flood Map.[^3] The platform enables insurers to view multiple flood-risk models on a single platform, facilitating more thorough risk assessments.[^1] This integration allows users to leverage multiple flood risk models simultaneously, facilitating more comprehensive risk assessments.[^3]

## Implementation Details

The integration of Fathom's flood maps into CatNet marked a significant milestone in enhancing the platform's existing flood modeling resources to supply clients with the highest quality dynamic flood risk assessments.[^3] Andrew Smith, chief operating officer at Fathom, highlighted that cutting-edge models produced together with academia are being made accessible to insurers within the CatNet platform.[^1] The natural catastrophes data from CatNet can be integrated into insurer's underwriting platforms, allowing automated and predictive underwriting.[^4] The data can also be used in pricing calculations to standardize pricing in non-modelled regions.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

The platform delivers substantial operational benefits for insurance organizations conducting natural catastrophe risk analysis. By combining hazard, loss, exposure and clients' insurance information with selected background maps and satellite imagery, CatNet allows insurers to assess risk from individual locations to entire portfolios.[^4] The integration enables users to leverage multiple flood risk models simultaneously on a single platform, facilitating more comprehensive risk assessments.[^3] The natural catastrophes data can be integrated into insurer's underwriting platforms, allowing automated and predictive underwriting, and can also be used in pricing calculations to standardize pricing in non-modelled regions.[^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The platform addresses multiple climate hazards through its comprehensive natural hazard assessment capabilities. Swiss Re offers this range of proprietary geo risk tools specifically designed to provide insurers with overviews and assessments of natural hazard exposures worldwide, including flood, earthquake, tsunami, and wind and storm surge.[^4] The incorporation of Fathom's flood maps enables sophisticated climate-driven present-day flood risk assessment, instead of relying on historical data alone.[^3]

## Business Analysis

The CatNet platform serves the insurance and reinsurance sector by providing essential risk assessment capabilities for underwriting and pricing decisions. Organizations use this proprietary risk assessment tool to analyze their exposure to natural catastrophe risk.[^3] The natural catastrophes data can be integrated into insurer's underwriting platforms, allowing automated and predictive underwriting.[^4] The platform supports standardized pricing in non-modelled regions through its comprehensive data integration capabilities.[^4] The integration of Fathom's independently validated, high-resolution flood maps marked a significant milestone in enhancing the platform's existing flood modeling resources to supply clients with the highest quality dynamic flood risk assessments.[^3]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://www.hyperexponential.com/blog/swiss-re-and-hyperexponential-integrating-catnet-with-hx-renew/
- [^3]: https://www.fathom.global/newsroom/fathom-in-catnet/
- [^4]: https://geospatialworld.net/prime/driving-digital-insurance-solutions-through-risk-assessment-2/

# Research Report: Wastewater Treatment Plant Microgrid with Tier 4 Diesel Generators

## Solution Overview

A microgrid is a local energy grid that can disconnect from the traditional grid and function autonomously without disrupting operations.[^1] During the 23rd Ave Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) Power Redundancy study, Phoenix partnered with APS to install a microgrid that would serve both the city's power redundancy needs and APS's long-term goals.[^1] This approach improves the sustainability and resilience of the surrounding community's electricity grid, particularly for critical water and wastewater treatment infrastructure.[^1]

## Technical Components

The microgrid runs on diesel generators, which APS identified as the optimal fuel source to ensure reliable operation.[^2] As part of the installation, Tier 2 generators were replaced with more stringently regulated Tier 4 generators which significantly reduce emissions.[^1] The bidirectional substation allows the microgrid to take energy from the main grid, while also letting it flow back.[^2] APS built in the ability to add batteries and photovoltaic systems to the microgrid very quickly and easily, with everything already in place for plug-and-play installation.[^2] The microgrid was constructed with renewable energy and storage in mind, allowing for swift conversion to 100% renewable energy if Aligned Data Centers requests it.[^2] When there is a fluctuation outside of the norm, the microgrid machines will automatically turn on and support the grid as well as securing the site.[^2] In times of crisis, the microgrid can use its own local energy generation from solar energy generation systems, emergency generators or an on-site battery system.[^1]

## Implementation Details

In 2016, Arizona Public Service Co. announced it would build a 63 MW microgrid with Aligned Data Centers, as part of the utility's push to build the 'grid of the future.'[^2] The 63 MW APS microgrid was completed by 2017, one year after its announcement.[^2] While Aligned paid for part of construction, APS recovered the remaining costs from ratepayers.[^2] Arizona regulators approved a number of programs for APS to recover costs for new technologies through the rate base in the past, including an in-house rooftop solar program.[^2] The APS microgrid was constructed on top of the roof of a converted building.[^2] Additional power redundancy studies will be conducted at different facilities and microgrids will be installed at those facilities identified to show a benefit to the power redundancy needs at those locations.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The microgrid can take the load from the data center offline to provide 24/7 power in times of outages.[^2] Once the crisis is resolved, the microgrid can then be connected to the traditional grid.[^1] The microgrid is useful if energy generation in the surrounding community is disrupted and can provide resiliency and stability to the grid.[^1] The microgrid illustrates how the utility is finding ways to relieve congestion on transmission lines, leverage grid services and boost the resiliency of the grid.[^2] In times of crisis, this capability is important to the continued operation of water and wastewater treatment plants.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The implementation addresses power disruption risks during extreme weather events and other climate-related crises. Comparable projects demonstrate the critical need for power redundancy, as Hurricane Sandy left the service area of the Passaic Valley Sewerage Commission (PVSC) in Newark, New Jersey, without power for 72 hours.[^3] Phoenix's approach promotes development of community-wide energy projects, including microgrids, that improve the sustainability and resilience of the surrounding community's electricity grid.[^1]

[^1]: City of Phoenix. (n.d.). *Phoenix Climate Action Plan*.
[^2]: Utility Dive. (2017). *APS completes 63 MW microgrid in Phoenix*.
[^3]: Passaic Valley Sewerage Commission. (n.d.). *Hurricane Sandy Impact Report*.

## Sources

- [^1]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Phoenix-US/Phoenix-2021ClimateActionPlanEnglish.pdf
- [^2]: https://www.utilitydive.com/news/case-study-aps-invests-in-microgrid-as-phoenix-metropolitan-area-grows/503687/
- [^3]: https://www.epa.gov/sites/default/files/2016-03/documents/160212-powerresilienceguide508.pdf

# Research Report: Automated Water Meter Infrastructure for Real-Time Leak Detection

## Solution Overview

San Francisco's automated water meter infrastructure enables real-time leak detection and customer notification to reduce water waste across the city's 170,000 retail customer accounts.[^1][^9] The San Francisco Public Utilities Commission (SFPUC) was the first large utility in California to fully automate its meters, allowing it to provide more timely water use data to customers.[^3] For more than a decade, SFPUC has used advanced metering infrastructure (AMI) to collect hourly water meter readings from all retail customers.[^2]

## Technical Components

The automated water meter system employs the Aclara STAR Network technology, which uses low-frequency radio signals to collect hourly water use and transmit that data four times daily to the SFPUC billing system.[^7][^5] A total of 81 Data Collection Units are located throughout the city on SFPUC facilities and City-owned poles and rooftops to transmit the meter readings to the SF Water, Power and Sewer's AWMP database.[^8] The new meters track consumption and send the data wirelessly to the San Francisco Public Utilities Commission four times a day.[^6] The system provides frequent transmission of more precise water consumption data allowing for quicker and more precise identification of high usage and suspected leaks through a leak detection notification program.[^8] The SFPUC partnered with the Department of Public Health to complete an evaluation of RF transmission levels associated with the Meter Transmission Unit System, which confirmed that the signals are well below government limits with respect to human exposure to RF energy.[^8][^7]

## Implementation Details

San Francisco started replacing its 175,000-plus water meters in June with high-tech devices as part of the SFPUC automated water meter program, a two-year effort that began on June 7.[^7] The San Francisco Public Utilities Commission began citywide installation of the Aclara STAR Network system in early calendar 2010.[^5] The program, launched on May 21, has placed the meters in 180,000 homes and businesses as California tries to cope with an extended drought.[^6] San Francisco has spent $56 million on smart water meters that allow residential and commercial customers to track how much water they're using.[^6] The $50 million program is being funded by a 91-cent fee that's being tacked onto ratepayers' monthly bills.[^7] SFPUC hired Woodard & Curran to help develop a system of detecting possible leaks for large multi-family buildings and CII accounts with more certainty.[^2] Woodard & Curran partnered with local civil engineering firm Lotus Water to collect, analyze, and report on water use trends for SFPUC's large retail water service customers.[^2] The customer Web interface for viewing water usage online is under development and is expected to be available within two years of deployment, though the exact timeframe is uncertain.[^7]

The Leak Alert Program provides proactive customer notification of potential on-site leaks based on meter data.[^4] The notification system leverages a customized version of the communications software SFPUC uses for emergencies and runs automatically.[^2] Notifications are sent through all available communication methods, first prioritizing SMS text and email, followed by automated phone call and mailed letter.[^2] Account holders for single family and small multi-family homes in San Francisco are automatically alerted of a potential leak if usage is consistently more than one cubic foot (about 7.5 gallons) every hour in a two-day period.[^2] The Leak Alert program alerts customers when they have three days of non-stop water use, which could indicate a plumbing leak.[^3] For commercial, industrial, and multi-family properties more than three dwelling units, the SFPUC also screens for increases of 50 percent and more in water use over the past 90 days, as well as spikes in nightly water use, in addition to constant use monitoring.[^3]

The expanded leak detection program employs two criteria for identifying potential leaks. The first criterion takes the average number of hours of zero consumption from 1:00 to 4:00 a.m. over a 90-day period to sort accounts into two categories – those that tend to have no consumption overnight and those with continuous use comparable to other hours of the day.[^2] The second criterion is the ratio of consumption from 1:00 to 4:00 a.m. on the last two days divided by the average use in the same hours within the prior 90 days, where spikes in the ratio indicate an increased water usage during typically low-usage hours.[^2] The expanded leak detection program notifies customers with intermittent overnight use of a possible leak when the resulting nighttime consumption ratio is greater than 2, and for properties that usually have regular overnight use, the criterion triggers a notification when the ratio is greater than 4.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

San Francisco deployed automated water meter technology estimated to save 54 million gallons per year notifying customers about leaks.[^9] The total savings across the leak detection program (including single family, small multi-family, and irrigation accounts) is approximately 76 million gallons per year.[^2] In the last fiscal year, the Leak Alert program saved SFPUC customers an estimated 47 million gallons of water.[^3] Since adding large multi-family units and CII accounts to SFPUC's leak detection program, the agency has realized an additional 30 million gallons of water savings per year.[^2] The leak detection program represents a 20 to 50 percent reduction in volume of water lost due to leaks, depending on the customer sector.[^2] The San Francisco Public Utilities Commission's Leak Alert Program has a goal of saving 0.1 MGD (million gallons per day) between 2020-2025.[^4]

Of those notified in the last year, 88 percent stopped constant use within four weeks.[^2] Approximately 11 percent of CII and 19 percent of large multi-family accounts are notified of a potential leak every year.[^2] The King George Hotel in the City's Union Square District is one the local businesses that has benefitted from the expanded Leak Alert program, as the SFPUC notified the hotel after analyzing water usage data, which indicated that the business was using more than 200 gallons per hour of water than typical.[^3] Hotels make up approximately 6 percent of SFPUC's commercial accounts, but use more than a quarter of total commercial consumption due to extensive laundry, dishwashing, cooling, and kitchen operations, amenities such as pools and restaurants, and large number of overnight guests.[^2]

San Francisco residents can register for My Account to track water usage data, view daily and hourly water usage, identify unusual spikes in water use, and receive notifications of possible leaks faster by updating contact information with cell phone or email.[^1] San Francisco customers who register to pay their bills online through MyAccount also can track their water use on a daily or even hourly basis to detect water waste.[^3] Consumers can look at the information as it's received and compare consumption to past use as well as city averages.[^6] Most San Francisco residents use about 40 gallons of water per person per day, which serves as a baseline for identifying unusual water usage patterns through the My Account system.[^1] Customers will eventually be able to log on to their own accounts with the SFPUC to monitor their own water consumption habits and billing.[^5]

The automated water meter system eliminated the need for manual meter reading, as currently most residential water meters in San Francisco are read once every two months and commercial meters are read monthly, with meter readers required to individually visit and read each meter manually.[^5] Aclara said the manual meter reading process makes it difficult to detect silent leaks, which can result in significant water loss and high water/sewer bills for customers.[^5] SFPUC General Manager Ed Harrington stated that automated meters will give the SFPUC and customers more accurate information about water consumption that will help detect leaks faster and conserve more.[^5]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The automated water meter infrastructure addresses water scarcity challenges intensified by extended drought conditions in California.[^6] The Environmental Protection Agency estimates that the average household's leaks can waste nearly 10,000 gallons of water every year, and 10% of homes have leaks that waste 90 gallons or more per day.[^1] By enabling rapid leak detection and customer notification, this system helps conserve limited water resources during periods of climate-driven water stress.

## Business Analysis

Large multi-family buildings and CII accounts constitute 54 percent of San Francisco's water consumption, representing a significant market opportunity for water conservation interventions.[^2] The San Francisco Public Utilities Commission's Leak Alert Program is an ongoing program that continues to be implemented in-house.[^4] The program operates through a customized version of the communications software SFPUC uses for emergencies, leveraging existing infrastructure to minimize operational costs.[^2] The automated water meter system was funded through a $56 million capital investment, with a 91-cent monthly fee added to ratepayers' bills to support program financing.[^6][^7] This fee-based financing model distributes costs across the customer base while enabling immediate implementation without requiring upfront municipal bond issuance.

## Sources

# Research Report: AIRLAB Open Innovation Laboratory for Air Quality Solutions

## Solution Overview

AIRLAB functions as an open innovation laboratory for air quality, founded by Airparif to accelerate the development and implementation of innovative solutions to improve air quality.[^1] Officially created on 17 September 2017 by decision of the Airparif Board of Directors, this initiative brings together an ecosystem of stakeholders, including startups, researchers, local authorities, and large companies, to co-create, experiment, and evaluate new technologies and services.[^1][^8] The creation of the AirLab within Airparif increases the number of measures and innovations to improve air quality, in collaboration with major private sector companies as well as start-ups.[^12]

## Technical Components

The laboratory operates through a comprehensive evaluation methodology for low-cost sensors based on the principle of the challenge, coupling metrology and ergonomics criteria.[^2] The evaluation process involves rigorous testing protocols conducted by the Laboratoire National de Métrologie et d'Essais (LNE) for laboratory assessment, followed by field evaluation at Airparif's reference station located at the quai des Célestins in Paris 4th arrondissement.[^9] Sensors are installed on the roof of the station at a height of 3 meters above the ground for real-world testing.[^9] The Challenge evaluates the performance of air quality sensors for the measurement of PM10, PM2.5, NO2, O3 and CO2.[^9] Multiple projects involving fixed, mobile, or portable low-cost sensor systems are currently deployed by Airparif to draw initial insights into the potential gain obtained through sensor multiplication in terms of monitoring, personal exposure characterization, and education.[^2] For its 2025 edition, the AIRLAB Microsensors Challenge offers a more advanced evaluation by incorporating two new outdoor testing sites in Accra (Ghana) and Bangalore (India), along with a new underground railway environment in Paris (France).[^6]

## Implementation Details

A consortium was formed for the creation of AIRLAB in 2017 with ten founding members including the State (Regional Prefecture), Local Authorities (Region, City of Paris and Metropolis), a Union (Île-de-France Mobilité), and Companies (SNCF, Véolia, Icade, EDF and Engie).[^8] Airparif created AIRLAB with the support of the Île-de-France Region for the prefiguration, as the laboratory of open innovation on air quality issues in Île-de-France.[^8] The initiative operates as an ecosystem and brings together around Airparif partners who are committed to a common goal, namely to improve air quality in Île-de-France, with institutional stakeholders (local authorities, agencies, institutes, etc.), academic research, large companies, SMEs, start-ups and representatives of civil society.[^6] With more than 70 members ranging from start-ups to large groups, AIRLAB promotes a model of open innovation functioning as an ecosystem.[^10] In 2018, AIRLAB added 10 new projects following the call for experimentation Air Quality from URBAN LAB and AIRLAB with the City of Paris and Paris&Co and with the support of ADEME and the Banque des Territoires.[^8] In March 2022, AIRLAB launched a new website.[^8] Since its creation, AIRLAB has tested 162 technological or behavioral change solutions as part of AIRLAB-labeled projects involving 107 organizations.[^8] The total number of sensors to be evaluated is limited to 50 per challenge cycle.[^9]

## Benefits and Impacts

The 2021 AIRLAB Microsensors Challenge evaluation lasted nearly 10 months and involved 40 candidate devices from around the world, of which 19 were selected to undergo laboratory and real-world testing at Airparif's reference stations.[^1] Performance improvements have been substantial across successive challenge editions, with 80% of tested particulate matter sensors achieving the highest score (1/5) in 2023, compared to 60% in 2022 and only 20% in 2021.[^7] For nitrogen dioxide sensors in the 2023 Challenge, only one sensor, the AQS-1 from Vaisala, achieved a good score (2/5), while other sensors showed lower performance with scores ranging from 3/5 to 5/5.[^7] The 2023 edition evaluated 17 sensors measuring fine particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10) and nitrogen dioxide (NO2).[^7] Airparif published the results of its Microsensors Challenge for the fifth consecutive year in 2023, an initiative supported by the Île-de-France Region and ADEME.[^7] The AIRLAB Microsensors Challenge provides a unique opportunity to test a large number of sensors under the same conditions in both laboratory and field settings.[^11] One AIRLAB project relating to indoor air quality involves testing different categories of microsensors used indoors to give users unbiased information about the product's suitability for its intended application.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Air quality monitoring and improvement directly addresses climate adaptation challenges by providing critical data on atmospheric conditions that affect public health during extreme weather events. Enhanced sensor networks enable real-time tracking of pollution episodes that intensify during heat waves and stagnant air conditions associated with climate change. The multiplication of monitoring points through validated low-cost sensors strengthens the region's capacity to respond to air quality degradation linked to changing climate patterns.

## Business Analysis

The Challenge is part of the AIRLAB ecosystem, an accelerator of innovative solutions for air quality, supported by Airparif, the Île-de-France Region, the City of Paris and the Greater Paris Metropolis.[^9] The 2023 Microsensors Challenge was conducted in partnership with Atmo France, the French Metrology Laboratory (LNE), and the French Agency for Ecological Transition (ADEME).[^7] Airparif operates with 65 staff members and an annual budget of €8.5 million in 2016, providing the institutional foundation for AIRLAB's innovation activities.[^3] The platform supports innovation through which economic actors, research bodies and representatives from public organizations seek to test and assess innovative pollution measurement and treatment approaches.[^4] The evaluation schedule includes laboratory evaluation from October 2023 to March 2024, field evaluation from April 2024 to September 2024, and publication of the evaluation report in October 2024.[^9] This structured timeline provides manufacturers and technology developers with clear pathways for product validation and market entry in the growing air quality monitoring sector.

## Sources

# Research Report: Smart Leak Detection Cables for Water Network Monitoring

## Solution Overview

Wellington Water implemented smart leak detection cables throughout its water network to address significant water loss challenges across the region's aging infrastructure.[^4] This technology enables rapid identification and repair of leaks in a drinking water network comprising 2,543km of pipeline, much of which is 30-100 years old.[^1] The approach forms part of a broader smart water monitoring solution designed to cover multiple parameters within the water distribution system.[^4]

## Technical Components

Wellington Water's monitoring infrastructure includes a network of 1,520 water meters deployed throughout the regions to detect unusual water use patterns.[^1] These meters identify potential leaks by monitoring for anomalies such as sustained water flow during nighttime hours when usage typically drops.[^1] The smart monitoring solution was developed through collaboration with Applied Instruments, which Wellington Water approached in 2021 to create a comprehensive system covering multiple network parameters.[^4] In 2024, Wellington Water completed a smart meter technical feasibility study on behalf of Greater Wellington Regional Council to investigate how meters could improve overall management of the wider network.[^5]

## Implementation Details

Wellington Water's enhanced leak repair efforts began in late January 2024, supported by increased funding from councils across the region.[^1] The organization fixed nearly 4,000 leaks during 2024 as reported in November.[^3] Within Wellington City specifically, over 566 leaks were repaired since July 1, demonstrating the positive impact of the Council's increased operational funding on network performance.[^2] All Wellington metropolitan councils included work to implement residential water meters in their 2024-34 Long-Term Plans in response to the need for water conservation and the increasing risk of future water shortages and restrictions.[^5]

## Benefits and Impacts

The implementation achieved substantial water conservation outcomes across multiple Wellington suburbs during the 2024/25 financial year. In Brooklyn, water leakage was reduced by 51,000 litres per day, benefiting 533 homes.[^2] Johnsonville experienced a reduction of 58,000 litres per day, benefiting 766 homes.[^2] Melrose saw water leakage reduced by 6,000 litres per day, benefiting 242 homes.[^2] Latest annual water loss estimates show that the average level of water loss dropped from 41% to 37% across the 2024/25 financial year, which includes both public and private leaks.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This water conservation technology directly addresses climate adaptation challenges by reducing strain on water supply systems facing increasing risk of future water shortages and restrictions.[^5] The enhanced leak detection and repair capabilities strengthen network resilience against drought conditions and water scarcity scenarios projected under climate change.

## Business Analysis

The implementation reflects a significant shift in municipal investment priorities, with Wellington metropolitan councils allocating increased operational funding to support enhanced leak repair efforts beginning in late January 2024.[^1] The Council's increased operational funding has positively impacted network performance, as evidenced by the repair of over 566 leaks in Wellington City since July 1.[^2] All Wellington metropolitan councils incorporated residential water meter implementation into their 2024-34 Long-Term Plans, establishing a decade-long policy framework supporting water conservation infrastructure.[^5] The 2024 smart meter technical feasibility study provides the analytical foundation for future network management investments and strategic planning decisions.[^5]

## Sources

# Research Report: Integrated Air Quality Monitoring and Forecasting Network

## Solution Overview

This air quality monitoring and forecasting approach integrates data from multiple monitoring networks operated by the Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation (BMC) and the System of Air Quality and Weather Forecasting And Research (SAFAR) to conduct trend analysis of pollutant concentrations across Mumbai.[^3] The framework enables identification of pollution patterns at both city-wide and station-specific levels through systematic assessment of monitored data.[^3]

## Technical Components

SAFAR-Mumbai operates through a network of 10 Air Quality Monitoring Stations (AQMS) established across Greater Mumbai and Navi Mumbai, strategically positioned to represent different microenvironments including industrial, residential, background/cleaner, urban complex, agricultural zones, and urban downtown areas as per international guidelines.[^5][^1] The monitoring stations measure pollutants including PM1, PM2.5, PM10, Ozone, CO, NOx (NO, NO2), SO2, BC, Methane (CH4), Non-methane hydrocarbons (NMHC), VOC's, Benzene, and Mercury through automatic analyzers.[^1][^4] Air quality indicators are monitored at approximately 3 meters height from the ground with online sophisticated instruments that operate round the clock, with data recorded and stored at every 5-minute interval for quality check and further analysis.[^1] Meteorological parameters including UV Radiation, Rainfall, Temperature, Humidity, Wind speed, Wind direction, and solar radiation are simultaneously monitored.[^1] The system employs a state-of-the-art Atmospheric Chemistry Transport Model (ACTM) to forecast air quality of various pollutants along with weather parameters with inner domain resolution of 1.67 km x 1.67 km.[^5] The framework includes six different components from observations and modeling to outreach, incorporating data from air quality and weather monitoring stations network, inventory of emissions to track pollution sources, and the Air Quality Index among others.[^6][^4] Data for calculating Mumbai's AQI is collected from 30 Air Quality Monitoring Systems (AQMS) stations set up across various locations in Mumbai and Vasai by three different agencies: BMC, MPCB, and IITM.[^3]

## Implementation Details

SAFAR-Mumbai was dedicated to the nation on June 23, 2015, as a joint venture of the BrihanMumbai Municipal Corporation (BMC), Indian Meteorology Department (IMD) Mumbai, and the Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology (IITM) Pune for the Mumbai Metropolitan Region.[^5][^3] The framework was first developed and implemented for Delhi in 2010, used in Pune from 2013, extended to Mumbai in 2015, and to Ahmedabad in 2017.[^4] IITM successfully developed and implemented the system to understand current air pollution levels and forecast them 24-48 hours in advance during the Commonwealth Games 2010 held in Delhi.[^7] The Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology (IITM), an autonomous institute under the Union ministry of earth sciences, coordinates and leads the development effort while managing day-to-day SAFAR operations in Mumbai, Pune, Ahmedabad and Delhi.[^7][^2] The Metropolitan Air Quality and Weather Service project had a total budget requirement of 90.00 crores rupees allocated from 2012-13 to 2016-17, with 25.00 crores in 2012-13, 30.00 crores in 2013-14, 20.00 crores in 2014-15, 10.00 crores in 2015-16, and 5.00 crores in 2016-17.[^7] Specific monitoring locations include the Chembur station at the International Institute for Population Sciences, Govandi Station Road, Near Govandi Station Over Bridge, Deonar, Chembur, Mumbai-400088 at coordinates 19.04946°N, 72.9162°E.[^1] The Bandra Kurla Complex station is located at Nandadeep Park, Bandra East Skywalk, Bandra East, Mumbai-400051 at coordinates 19.053536°N, 72.84643°E.[^1] The Colaba station operates at the Indian Meteorological Department, Nananbhai Moosa Marg, Near RC Church, Colaba, Mumbai 400005 at coordinates 18.89806°N, 72.81269°E.[^1] BMC has set up air quality monitoring stations at five different places in Mumbai namely Byculla, Ghatkopar, Kandivli, Sewri and Shivaji Nagar.[^3] The Maharashtra Pollution Control Board (MPCB) operates at least 11 continuous ambient air quality monitoring stations (CAAQMS) in Mumbai, which typically record lower pollution levels than SAFAR's devices.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

The system provides current and 1-3 days' advance forecast of air quality with associated health advisories in terms of Air Quality Index.[^5] SAFAR can give air quality forecasts 24, 48 and 72 hours in advance, and in case of extreme pollution events like dust storms or stubble burning issues, extended range forecasts give forecasts five days in advance.[^4] The Model Output Statistics (MOS) application enhanced operational forecast ability of the numerical model, resulting in improved accuracy for specific seasons, particularly winter.[^6] The Normalized Gross Error (NGE) of PM2.5 for Mumbai is approximately 13-20%, significantly lower than Delhi's 35%.[^6] On January 16, SAFAR data showed Mumbai's AQI to be 306 in the 'very poor' category, while Delhi's was at 242 indicating 'poor' air quality.[^2] The System of Air Quality and Weather Forecasting And Research (SAFAR) Project under the Ministry of Earth Sciences received a global nod for the first official indigenous framework to forecast air quality in Delhi, Mumbai, Pune and Ahmedabad.[^4] SAFAR's forecasting model is comparable to the framework by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (US-EPA).[^4] The SAFAR project is adopted as a pilot study of GURME, World Meteorological Organization.[^6]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The air pollution monitoring and forecasting system addresses climate-related air quality challenges by enabling trend analysis of pollutant concentrations at city level and around specific monitoring stations, supporting evidence-based decision-making for urban environmental management.[^3] The framework's capability to provide multi-day advance forecasts with health advisories allows municipal authorities and residents to prepare for and respond to extreme pollution events that may be exacerbated by climate conditions.[^5][^4]

## Business Analysis

After the successful demonstration of SAFAR project in Delhi, the Indian government decided to expand it to three more major cities of India: Pune, Mumbai and Ahmedabad, demonstrating scalability and policy support for the framework.[^6] The deliverable is to develop and dedicate the System of Air Quality Forecasting & Research (SAFAR) to nation as an operational service in at least 6 Metropolitan cities.[^7] The framework can be easily replicated in 132 cities across the country with a population of over 10 lakh, indicating substantial market expansion potential.[^4] As per World Meteorological Organisation research, in a city where the population is between 50 lakh to 1 crore, a minimum of 10 to 12 stations is a requirement, establishing technical standards for implementation.[^4] The system is proposed to extend to other metropolitan cities including Mumbai, Delhi, Pune, Chennai, Kolkata, Bangalore, and Hyderabad using both dense observational network and high resolution air chemistry-transport forecasting model.[^7] The objective is to develop a System of Air Quality Forecasting and Research (SAFAR) in major metropolitan cities of India including Mumbai under the studies of Atmospheric Chemistry and Climate.[^7] The project operates through a public-sector partnership model involving BMC, IMD, and IITM, with government funding supporting infrastructure deployment and operational costs.[^3][^7]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://safar.tropmet.res.in/monitoring%20system-10-3-Details
- [^2]: https://www.hindustantimes.com/cities/mumbai-news/mpcbs-response-to-city-s-air-crisis-shift-aqi-monitors-where-air-s-cleaner-101673983970144.html
- [^3]: https://citizenmatters.in/explainer-mumbai-air-quality-data-monitoring/
- [^4]: https://indianexpress.com/article/cities/mumbai/safar-framework-one-stop-solution-for-air-quality-management-7536259/
- [^5]: https://www.tropmet.res.in/project_details.php?project_id=3&position=0
- [^6]: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1364815221002462
- [^7]: https://www.moes.gov.in/programmes/metropolitan-air-quality-and-weather-service

# Research Report: Resilience Maturity Model for Urban Climate Adaptation Assessment

## Solution Overview

The Resilience Maturity Model is a methodological approach to define the degree of resilience attained by cities, based on objective, measurable and comparable parameters.[^4] This strategic tool was designed to provide cities with a roadmap towards enhanced resilience, offering a holistic overview of the resilience building process and helping end users to understand resilience as a multidimensional objective.[^1] The model enables cities to assess their current maturity stage and to identify the policies to implement in order for the city to evolve and move to the next maturity stage.[^1]

## Technical Components

The Resilience Maturity Model is structured in five stages of maturity: (1) starting, (2) moderate, (3) advanced, (4) robust and (5) vertebrate.[^5] These five incremental stages guide local governments through the ideal path for building-resilience: Starting, Moderate, Advanced, Robust, and Vertebrate.[^3] The model includes 38 policies that cities should implement to build resilience, grouped into six dimensions: (1) leadership and strategy, (2) stakeholder engagement, (3) awareness, (4) risk management, (5) resource management and (6) emergency management.[^5] Each maturity stage includes a description of the objectives of each stage, the stakeholders actively involved in each maturity stage, and a list of policies that should be followed.[^3] The revised version was implemented in a software tool, the Resilience Maturity Model Assessment Tool, and it was tested by the cities of the project.[^5]

## Implementation Details

This approach is one of five resilience-building tools that comprise the European Resilience Management Guideline, which were co-created as part of the Smart Mature Resilience (SMR) project.[^7] The SMR project worked closely with the cities of Glasgow, Donostia, Kristiansand, Riga, Rome, Vejle and Bristol to develop, test and finally use the project tools.[^6] The European Resilience Management Guideline comprising a toolbox of five well-integrated tools was developed iteratively and validated through a co-creation process with the project end users.[^7] The final version of the Resilience Maturity Model (Version 3.0) was completed on 29 June 2017.[^5] The model underwent significant revisions following advice from project-external participants of the standardization process.[^3] Through involvement of German standardization institute DIN, the project developed three CEN Workshop Agreements based on the project outputs.[^3] The Resilience Maturity Model was documented as CWA 17301 City Resilience Development – Maturity Model, a CEN Workshop Agreement developed through the SMR project.[^3] The SMR project went through a whole process of standardizing the results so that they could be replicated and implemented by the cities more easily, according to Marta Iturriza, a doctoral student at Tecnun.[^9] The standardization process brought external participants into the process of documenting their tools and benefits.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

Rome used the Resilience Maturity Model to assess its current resilience level and to identify areas for improvement.[^2] The city has used the SMR tools to enhance its resilience to these challenges.[^2] The paper defines five steps for integrating standardization in research projects with a focus on (city) resilience and shows the benefits of the standardization process for fostering the engagement of relevant stakeholders.[^8] The SMR project integrated several European cities in its co-creative approach for developing city resilience tools and the related standards series CWA 17300 on 'City Resilience Development'.[^8]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Rome is one of the few cities that benefits from several resources at the same time to support urban resilience, including the 100RC initiative and the European project SMR - Smart Mature Resilience, under the Horizon 2020 Program.[^4] The Resilience Maturity Model provides cities with a structured framework to systematically build resilience capacity across multiple dimensions including risk management and emergency management.[^5]

# Research Report: Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Stations (CAAQMS)

## Solution Overview

Bengaluru plans to procure 13 new Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Stations (CAAQMS) and transition existing manual stations to CAAQMS systems by 2035.[^1] This expansion forms part of a broader infrastructure development strategy to create a robust evidence-based air quality management paradigm through enhanced monitoring, evaluation, feedback loops, and knowledge dissemination to encourage behavioral shifts.[^1] The initiative addresses critical gaps in the city's air quality monitoring network, where inconsistencies in pollutant observations remain a challenge in tracking air quality trends.[^1]

## Technical Components

The CAAQMS infrastructure represents a significant technological upgrade from manual monitoring stations, which currently operate at approximately Rs 3 lakh per station compared to several crores for CAAQMS setups.[^7] The Karnataka State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB) established a robust system for the calibration and maintenance of air quality monitoring stations between 2024-2025, with support from the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB).[^5] Data from 12 monitoring stations across Bangalore, collected between 2013 and 2021, were analyzed to identify key pollutants, seasonal variations, and their impact on buildings.[^6] A forecasting model with 93% accuracy was developed to predict AQI levels in Bangalore, demonstrating a strong correlation between predicted and actual values.[^6] The monitoring network feeds into an Air Quality Early Warning System (AQEWS), with Bengaluru joining seven other Indian cities—Ahmedabad, Jaipur, Pune, Mumbai, Kolkata, and Hyderabad—that currently have similar operational systems.[^3]

## Implementation Details

The Bangalore City Action Plan (BCAP) targets increasing the number of continuous ambient air quality monitoring stations to 50 by 2025.[^5] KSPCB expanded the network of CAAQMS across Bengaluru between 2024-2025, with support from CPCB and the Bruhat Bengaluru Mahanagara Palike (BBMP).[^5] The action plan for control of air pollution in Bangalore city was prepared by the Central Pollution Control Board in association with Karnataka State Pollution Control Board, approved by CPCB, and sent to KSPCB for implementation.[^4] Under the National Clean Air Programme (NCAP), launched by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change in January 2019, Bengaluru received Rs 541.1 Crores in total fund released, with Rs 5.47 Crores utilized.[^2] The NCAP framework aims to improve air quality in 131 cities across 24 States by engaging all stakeholders.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

Bengaluru aims to achieve 40% reduction in PM10 concentration and 30% reduction in PM2.5 concentration by 2026 from 2017-18 levels as per revised targets prescribed by NCAP.[^1] The revised national targets seek to achieve reduction in PM10 level up to 40% or achievement of national standards (60 µg/m³) by 2025-26, updated from the original NCAP goal of 20-30% reduction in PM concentration over baseline in year 2017 by 2024.[^2] Current monitoring data reveals that PM10 and PM2.5 are the primary pollutants in Bangalore, with concentrations peaking during summer and winter, while monsoon seasons show lower levels.[^6] Both PM10 and PM2.5 concentrations were highest between October and May during the study period of 2016-21.[^1] The pollutant concentrations remain highest during morning and evening peak hours across all monitoring stations.[^1] Specific locations demonstrate persistent air quality challenges: Bapuji Nagar, Jayanagar 5th Block, and Peenya crossed the daily CPCB permissible limits for PM2.5 for more than one-third of the year 2021—58%, 36% and 34%, respectively.[^1] Areas such as Majestic, Silk Board and Peenya crossed the annual PM10 permissible limits for most of the years between 2016 and 2021.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Under the current policy landscape and projected population and economic growth, ambient PM2.5 levels in Bengaluru are projected to increase to 45.9 µg/m³ by 2050, exceeding national air quality standards and resulting in air that contains 9 times the concentration of air pollution that the WHO deems safe.[^1] The expanded monitoring infrastructure addresses this climate-related air quality challenge by enabling evidence-based interventions and real-time tracking of pollution trends. Enhanced monitoring capacity supports adaptive management responses to seasonal variations in air quality and helps identify pollution hotspots requiring targeted mitigation measures.

## Business Analysis

The financial structure of CAAQMS deployment involves substantial capital investment, with operational costs reaching several crores per station compared to Rs 3 lakh for manual monitoring stations.[^7] Bengaluru's implementation benefits from the National Clean Air Programme funding mechanism, which allocated Rs 541.1 Crores to the city, though utilization remained at Rs 5.47 Crores as of the reporting period.[^2] The multi-agency partnership model involves KSPCB as the primary implementing authority, with technical and financial support from CPCB and operational coordination with BBMP.[^5] This collaborative framework distributes implementation responsibilities across central, state, and municipal levels, enabling resource sharing and technical capacity building. The procurement of 13 new CAAQMS by 2035 represents a phased expansion approach aligned with the broader target of reaching 50 monitoring stations by 2025.[^1][^5]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Asia & Asia Pacific Oceania/Bengaluru-India/BCAP_Summary Report.pdf
- [^2]: https://www.pib.gov.in/PressReleasePage.aspx?PRID=1989207
- [^3]: https://www.ceew.in/publications/designing-effective-air-quality-decision-support-and-pollution-warning-alert-systems
- [^4]: https://prana.cpcb.gov.in/ncapDashboard/downloadEmissionFiles/Bangalore.pdf
- [^5]: https://apps.bbmpgov.in/bcap/PDF/BCAP_Sector%20Actions_01042024.pdf
- [^6]: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-00814-9
- [^7]: https://citizenmatters.in/low-cost-air-pollution-monitoring-sensors-iit-madras-chennai-research/

# Research Report: Solar PV with Battery Storage for Grid Resilience

## Solution Overview

New York City is advancing climate resilience through the integration of solar photovoltaic systems with battery energy storage technologies across critical infrastructure and municipal facilities.[^2] This approach enables facilities to maintain operations during grid outages while simultaneously supporting greenhouse gas emission reduction targets and relieving stress on the electric grid from growing power demands.[^2] The technology provides backup power during blackouts, brownouts, or storms when electric service is interrupted, while also generating emissions-free energy during normal operations.[^4]

## Technical Components

The implementation encompasses diverse solar configurations including traditional rooftop installations, solar canopies over parking lots and wastewater processing tanks, ground-mounted systems, and elevated canopy arrays.[^6][^11] Battery storage systems are wired to power critical functions during outages, including roll-up doors, communication devices, fuel pumps, lighting, and electrical outlets.[^4] The NYC Installed Energy Storage Systems map layer provides detailed information on battery chemistry type, system size, location, solar pairing status, installation date, and project developer for permitted and verified systems.[^3] NREL utilized the REopt modeling platform to optimally select and size resilient power options, incorporating revenue streams from displacing grid energy purchases, reducing peak demand charges, shifting energy consumption to low-cost periods, and avoiding grid interruption costs.[^14] The analysis placed a monetary value on resilience equal to the cost of grid interruptions, modeling a new revenue stream for avoided outage costs.[^14]

## Implementation Details

Between 2021 and the end of 2023, the Department of Citywide Administrative Services more than doubled solar capacity on City property from 11 MW to over 24 MW.[^6] As of 2024, the Clean Energy Program had over 40 MW of solar projects in progress and 30.5 MW of completed installations across 187 facilities.[^6] In 2024, DCAS assessed all City-owned buildings larger than 10,000 gross square feet for solar readiness and identified nearly 29 MW of rooftop solar potential.[^6] Construction began in fall 2023 to install solar systems at over 60 City-owned buildings in Brooklyn and Queens, adding over 30 MW of solar generating capacity and up to 10 MW of large-scale battery storage through an agreement between the City and the New York Power Authority.[^10][^12] The Wards Island Wastewater Resource Recovery Facility project represents the City's largest energy storage installation to date, featuring 10 MW of solar capacity and 10 MW of battery storage.[^9] This installation, currently in the design phase with construction expected from June through completion in June 2027, will be the largest clean energy installation at a wastewater treatment facility globally, at no upfront capital cost to the City.[^9][^16] Through the power purchase agreement, NYPA's competitively selected developer Ameresco is responsible for installing, owning, and maintaining assets for 20 years while the City purchases clean power at a fixed rate.[^9][^16] The joint project between NYPA and DCAS generated more than 25 MW of power from rooftop solar arrays and included up to 6.6 MW of energy storage.[^11] Six firehouses now feature rooftop solar systems paired with battery banks that remain charged during outages, selected for upgrade because they had all recently experienced power interruptions.[^4] The installation of these firehouse solar systems totaled $2.4 million, funded by DCAS and managed by DCAS and the New York Power Authority.[^4] Energy storage projects must obtain permits from the Fire Department of New York and the Department of Buildings and are subject to inspections throughout construction and operational phases.[^1] The City is working to streamline the permitting process to reduce project costs and timelines.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The solar arrays generate more than 25 MW of solar power, enough to power 1,297 NYC residences, and help offset more than 7,100 metric tons of CO2 equivalent annually, equivalent to removing more than 1,553 cars from city streets.[^11] The Wards Island project is projected to reduce nearly 4,000 metric tons of carbon dioxide, equivalent to removing close to 860 cars from the road, and will reduce energy usage by nearly 42,000 MMBtu, equivalent to the energy consumption of approximately 3,000 New York City households.[^9] By the end of 2025, DCAS installations will provide the annual electricity equivalent of roughly 11,500 NYC homes.[^10] Unlike generators, the solar panels with batteries provide quiet and continuous energy regardless of fuel shortages.[^4] During normal grid operations, resilient PV systems help host sites generate revenue or reduce electricity bill charges, while during grid outages they provide critical emergency power that can help people in need and ease demand on emergency fuel supplies.[^2] Analysis results demonstrated that solar plus storage is economically viable for NYC's critical infrastructure and may be similarly beneficial to other commercial buildings across the city.[^2][^14] Energy storage can provide backup power during grid outages, which are expected to become more frequent and severe due to climate change, and can stabilize supply during peak electric usage while helping keep critical systems online during outages.[^1][^5]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

As NYC fortifies its building stock against future storms of increasing severity, resilient PV plays an important role in disaster response and recovery.[^2] The technology addresses the growing frequency and severity of grid outages expected from climate change impacts.[^1] DCAS is currently planning new future resilient solar PV storage projects with emphasis on prioritizing additional firehouses, police stations, libraries, and other critical facilities to enhance emergency response capabilities.[^4]

## Business Analysis

The City established a goal of installing 100 MW of solar photovoltaic on City-owned buildings by the end of 2030 and 150 MW by the end of 2035, as set forth by Local Law 99 of 2024.[^6] New York City set a target of deploying 1,000 MW of energy storage by 2030.[^1] At the state level, New York's Climate Leadership and Community Protection Act codified a goal of 1,500 MW of energy storage by 2025 and 3,000 MW by 2030.[^5] On June 20, 2024, the New York Public Service Commission approved the Order Establishing Updated Energy Storage Goal and Deployment Policy, formally expanding the State's goal to 6,000 MW of energy storage by 2030.[^5] The Order authorized funds for NYSERDA to support 200 MW of new residential-scale solar, 1,500 MW of new commercial and community-scale energy storage, and 3,000 MW of new large-scale storage.[^5] The Order specifies that at least 35% of the benefits of these new energy storage projects will accrue to disadvantaged communities in accordance with the Climate Act.[^5] Energy storage could employ at least 30,000 New Yorkers by 2030.[^5] These projects will reduce projected future statewide electric system costs by nearly $2 billion, in addition to improved public health from reduced exposure to harmful fossil fuel pollutants.[^5] There is approximately 2,000 MW of economic potential for rooftop solar in NYC, with the majority on large commercial and industrial buildings that have high electricity rates and large, unshaded rooftops.[^7] There is over 11,000 MW of technical potential for rooftop solar in NYC, enough to meet over 20% of the city's annual electricity needs.[^7] A typical residential solar system in NYC can cost between $15,000 and $25,000, with a residential solar plus storage system costing an additional $10,000 to $15,000.[^7] In a survey of NYC residents conducted for the Roadmap, over 70% of respondents said they would be willing to pay more for a solar plus storage system that could provide backup power during an outage.[^7] NYC has some of the most stringent safety codes and standards for energy storage in the world, including requirements for fire detection and suppression, ventilation, and thermal management.[^1] On July 28, 2023, Governor Kathy Hochul announced the creation of a new Inter-Agency Fire Safety Working Group to ensure the safety and security of energy storage systems across the state.[^5] Residential storage systems can be eligible for Inflation Reduction Act tax credits.[^5]

## Sources

# Research Report: Healthcare Resilience Through Electronic Records & Facility Preparedness

## Solution Overview

New York has implemented a comprehensive healthcare resilience strategy centered on electronic health records systems, telecommunication infrastructure redundancy, and facility preparedness protocols to protect medical services during climate-related disasters. This approach emerged from lessons learned during Hurricane Sandy, which forced the evacuation of more than 6,400 patients from 37 healthcare facilities in New York City.[^2] The initiative addresses critical vulnerabilities exposed when major hospital centers including Bellevue Hospital Center and Coney Island Hospital suffered catastrophic damage and were forced to close for an extended period of time.[^2]

## Technical Components

The technical infrastructure centers on the State Health Information Network – New York (SHIN-NY), which New York State has invested deeply in developing over more than a decade of planning since the attacks of September 11, 2001, with additional support from the Health Information Technology for Economic and Clinical Health (HITECH) Act.[^1] The New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene (DOHMH) Primary Care Information Project (PCIP) assists more than 9,000 clinicians, mainly in low-income communities with high proportions of Medicaid-insured and uninsured patients, adopt electronic health records (EHRs) and achieve meaningful use standards for the federal EHR Incentive Program.[^4] The telecommunication redundancy component includes strengthened emergency broadcast capabilities and coordination with local media outlets to ensure they are able to continue operating during a disaster.[^3]

## Implementation Details

Municipal authorities have worked with the State to develop new regulations that require healthcare facilities to have more robust emergency plans in place.[^2] The New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene (DOHMH) has developed a range of tools and protocols to help healthcare facilities prepare for and respond to climate-related events.[^2] The City launched a public awareness campaign to educate New Yorkers about how to stay connected in an emergency by Q2 2014, developed in partnership with telecommunications providers and community organizations, with responsible parties including DoITT, NYCEM, and Department of Consumer Affairs (DCA).[^3] This campaign provides practical tips and information to ensure that New Yorkers have access to reliable information during an emergency, even if their own telecommunications services are down.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

During Hurricane Sandy, the electronic health records system demonstrated remarkable resilience, with only one report of records being lost among EHR users in the greater New York City area, occurring in a small clinic that was actually in the process of converting their paper records into an EHR system.[^1] In contrast, there were widespread reports of paper records being lost during the same event.[^1] This performance proved critical when Long Beach Medical Center was taken completely offline by the storm surge, with its basement flooded with more than ten feet of water, affecting heating, electrical, fire alarm, and communications systems.[^1] The digital infrastructure enabled continuity of patient care information despite catastrophic physical damage to healthcare facilities.

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach directly addresses vulnerabilities to storm surge, flooding, and extended power outages that climate change intensifies in coastal urban healthcare systems. The system's effectiveness during Hurricane Sandy, when facilities experienced basement flooding exceeding ten feet and complete system failures, demonstrates its capacity to maintain critical patient information access during extreme weather events that are projected to increase in frequency and severity.

## Business Analysis

The implementation leveraged federal financing through the Health Information Technology for Economic and Clinical Health (HITECH) Act to support the substantial state investment in health information exchange infrastructure development.[^1] The Primary Care Information Project's focus on assisting more than 9,000 clinicians in low-income communities with high proportions of Medicaid-insured and uninsured patients demonstrates a public health equity financing model that prioritizes vulnerable populations.[^4] The regulatory framework requiring healthcare facilities to maintain more robust emergency plans creates ongoing compliance-driven demand for EHR systems and telecommunication redundancy solutions.[^2] The partnership model for the public awareness campaign, involving telecommunications providers, community organizations, and multiple city departments, represents a collaborative governance approach that distributes implementation costs across public and private sector stakeholders.[^3]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3959913/
- [^2]: https://www.nyc.gov/assets/sirr/downloads/pdf/Ch_8_Healthcare_FINAL_singles.pdf
- [^3]: https://www.nyc.gov/assets/sirr/downloads/pdf/Ch_9_Telecommunications_FINAL_singles.pdf
- [^4]: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3953763/

# Research Report: Interactive Sustainability Mapping Platform for Citizen Engagement

## Solution Overview

The Hague has developed an interactive digital platform that enables residents to map and share sustainability initiatives across the city.[^7] This approach serves as a collaborative tool where citizens can contribute their own projects and ideas related to energy transition and climate action.[^7] The platform supports the city's broader goal to become climate-neutral by 2030.[^4]

## Technical Components

The interactive map functions as a web-based platform that organizes initiatives by different sustainability topics.[^7] Students developed the technical infrastructure to catalog and present 150 sustainable initiatives operating in and around The Hague.[^6] The platform includes explanatory content that connects each initiative to the UN Sustainable Development Goals and provides justification for why specific projects qualify as sustainable.[^6] The system was designed to allow ongoing citizen contributions, enabling residents to add new initiatives and ideas directly to the map interface.[^7] The Hague Climate Accord platform operates as a complementary tracking system that monitors progress on climate deals and provides updates on current and future agreements through its website.[^5]

## Implementation Details

The initiative emerged from a collaboration between Duurzaam Den Haag and resident initiatives, commissioned by the municipality of The Hague.[^1] The Haags Bewonersplatform Energietransitie has been meeting every 6 to 8 weeks since 2016 to share knowledge and experience among participants.[^1] This platform brings together resident initiatives, Duurzaam Den Haag, and municipal authorities along with other parties to realize and accelerate projects throughout the city.[^1] Students compiled the initial 150 initiatives and shared them through both the website and a printed booklet.[^6] The municipality allocated €3,700,000 from its Municipal Budget for 2024-2027 specifically for creating renewable energy sources, energy grids, and storage.[^2] An additional €30,804,000 was designated for creating sustainable built environment during the same budget period.[^2] The city established a sustainability scheme with a budget of €1.4 million, offering grants of up to €8,000 to citizens with ideas for improving the environment or reducing energy use and emissions in their neighborhoods.[^9] From 2015 onwards, €200,000 per year was made structurally available to support active groups of citizens in transforming ideas into actionable plans.[^9]

## Benefits and Impacts

The platform provides transparency by making 150 sustainable initiatives visible and accessible to residents across The Hague.[^6] The European Union recognized The Hague with the Climate-Neutral and Smart Cities label in October 2024.[^3] The tracking functionality enables residents to monitor progress on climate deals and stay informed about ongoing and future agreements through continuous online updates.[^5] The educational component helps citizens understand how local initiatives connect to global sustainability frameworks through the UN Sustainable Development Goals.[^6]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The interactive mapping system supports The Hague's climate-neutral goal by 2030 through enhanced citizen engagement and knowledge sharing.[^4] The platform facilitates the acceleration of energy transition projects by connecting resident initiatives with municipal resources and other stakeholders.[^1] Similar digital engagement approaches have been proposed for targeted feedback mechanisms, such as designing digital maps where citizens can leave suggestions, ideas, or pictures at specific geographic points.[^8]

## Business Analysis

The municipality structured its financial support through multiple mechanisms to sustain citizen-led climate action. The €1.4 million sustainability scheme provides direct grants up to €8,000 for neighborhood-level environmental improvements.[^9] The structural allocation of €200,000 annually since 2015 creates a predictable funding stream for transforming citizen ideas into implemented plans.[^9] The substantial municipal budget allocations of €3.7 million for renewable energy infrastructure and €30.8 million for sustainable built environment during 2024-2027 demonstrate long-term policy commitment to the energy transition.[^2] The collaborative governance model between Duurzaam Den Haag, resident initiatives, and municipal authorities establishes a public-private partnership framework that leverages citizen expertise alongside government resources.[^1] The regular meeting structure every 6 to 8 weeks since 2016 provides institutional stability for knowledge exchange and project acceleration.[^1]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://eu-mayors.ec.europa.eu/en/Building-trust-and-powering-change-The%20Hague-path-to-a-just-energy-transition
- [^3]: https://thehague.com/partners/en/news/the-hague-climate-week-initiatives-accelerate-climate-neutrality-2030
- [^4]: https://www.impactcity.nl/en/discover-the-initiatives-in-the-field-of-new-energy-with-the-energy-map/
- [^5]: https://www.justpeacethehague.org/en/story/working-together-towards-the-future-the-haag
- [^6]: https://www.universiteitleiden.nl/en/news/2021/05/sustainable-the-hague-sustainable-initiatives-in-your-local-area
- [^7]: https://netzerocities.app/_content/files/knowledge/4441/ccc_the_hague.pdf
- [^8]: https://thehagueacademy.com/news/how-to-plan-for-meaningful-citizen-participation-in-local-governance/
