# Research Report: Grid-Integrated Demand Response for Municipal Water Heating and Cooling

## Solution Overview

The City and County of Honolulu has committed to advancing flexible energy demand response programs for municipal operations as part of its climate action strategy.[^3] This approach leverages controllable loads—particularly water heaters and air conditioning systems—to provide grid services during periods of high electricity demand, supporting grid stability while reducing reliance on fossil fuel generation.[^1] Given that electricity generation creates approximately 35% of O'ahu's total carbon emissions and about 80% of the island's electricity comes from fossil fuels, demand response programs represent a critical tool for managing peak loads and facilitating renewable energy integration.[^3]

## Technical Components

Hawaiian Electric's EnergyScout program operates through a one-way paging network to control approximately 34,000 water heaters, delivering approximately 10 MW of controllable peak demand.[^1] The utility has successfully deployed multiple grid-interactive water heater pilot projects with Shifted Energy over the last five years, demonstrating confidence in the technology's capabilities.[^1] In partnership with Open Access Technology International, Inc. (OATI) and Hawaiian Electric, Shifted Energy provided 2.5 MW of grid-interactive water heater capacity through a Grid Services Purchase Agreement starting in August 2019.[^1] The Residential Direct Load Control program, active since 2009, cycles participants' central air-conditioning and electric water heaters on and off for brief intervals during high demand periods.[^4] The Commercial & Industrial Direct Load Control program, also active since 2009, targets small and medium commercial customers by cycling air-conditioning and other loads during peak periods.[^4]

## Implementation Details

Hawaiian Electric launched its Grid Services Purchase Agreement in 2018 to competitively procure approximately 16 MW of capacity in response to the Hawaii Public Utilities Commission's request to explore new methods for procuring demand response, regulating reserves, and providing fast frequency response.[^1] Hawaiian Electric Company, Maui Electric Company, and Hawai'i Electric Light Company submitted their Integrated Demand Response Portfolio Plan to the Hawai'i Public Utilities Commission on July 28, 2014 under Docket No. 2007-0341.[^4] The Fast Demand Response pilot program provided fast-responding grid services including contingency reserve and regulating reserve from aggregated customer loads for large commercial and industrial customers from 2014 to 2015.[^4] Savings from demand response implementations are guaranteed by the contractor Johnson Controls and financed through a third party, resulting in zero out of pocket spending by the city or taxpayers.[^2] The Time of Use Rates program, active since 2006, offers different rates for electricity used during on-peak, mid-peak, and off-peak periods for residential and small to medium commercial customers.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

The EnergyScout program delivers approximately 10 MW of controllable peak demand through control of approximately 34,000 water heaters, providing significant capacity for grid management.[^1] Grid-interactive water heater deployments added 2.5 MW of capacity through the Grid Services Purchase Agreement starting in August 2019.[^1] The competitive procurement process launched in 2018 targeted approximately 16 MW of total capacity across demand response, regulating reserves, and fast frequency response services.[^1] These programs support operational efficiency by cycling loads during high demand periods while maintaining customer comfort and service quality.[^4] The guaranteed savings model ensures financial benefits without requiring upfront municipal or taxpayer expenditure.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Demand response programs directly address climate adaptation needs by reducing peak electricity demand and associated fossil fuel combustion, which generates approximately 35% of O'ahu's total carbon emissions.[^3] By enabling greater grid flexibility and supporting renewable energy integration, these programs advance Hawai'i's mandate to achieve 100% of electricity net sales from renewable sources by 2045.[^3] The initiative contributes to Honolulu's goal to reduce emissions 45% from 2015 levels by 2025, addressing the climate impacts of electricity generation while enhancing grid resilience during extreme weather events that drive peak demand.[^5]

## Business Analysis

Hawaiian Electric's competitive Grid Services Purchase Agreement model, launched in 2018, established a market-based procurement framework for approximately 16 MW of grid services capacity.[^1] The financial structure through Johnson Controls guarantees savings while utilizing third-party financing, eliminating capital expenditure requirements for municipal participants.[^2] The Hawaii Public Utilities Commission's directive to explore new procurement methods for demand response and grid services created regulatory support for innovative business models and technology deployments.[^1] The Critical Peak Pricing pilot program, which ran from 2014 to 2015, tested pricing mechanisms that charge higher rates during critical peak periods for residential customers.[^4] Long-term program sustainability is demonstrated through the Residential and Commercial Direct Load Control programs, both active since 2009 and continuing operations, along with the Time of Use Rates program active since 2006.[^4]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://sepapower.org/knowledge/two-birds-one-water-heater-how-shifted-energy-and-hawaiian-electric-are-helping-hawaii-meet-its-clean-energy-goals/
- [^2]: https://www.sustainablehonolulu.com/
- [^3]: https://resilientoahu.org/energy-climate-action-electricity
- [^4]: https://puc.hawaii.gov/wp-content/uploads/2014/08/HECO-Companies-Integrated-Demand-Response-Portfolio-Plan-07-28-14.pdf

# Research Report: Building Energy and Water Benchmarking Performance Standards

## Solution Overview

Building Efficiency 305 (BE305) represents Miami-Dade County's comprehensive strategy to increase energy and water efficiency in large public and private buildings countywide.[^1] This innovative approach uses the deployment of building performance data to drive decision making and transform the marketplace.[^6] The initiative helps ensure a more resilient city in relation to existing buildings and addresses the mandate from the Miami City Commission to mitigate emissions of greenhouse gases.[^5]

## Technical Components

The program requires commercial and multifamily buildings 20,000 square feet and above to benchmark their electricity, natural gas, and water consumption every year.[^2] Building owners must annually measure and report whole-building energy and water use data, building characteristics, and gross floor area using EPA's ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager as the reporting mechanism.[^3] The Building Performance Standards apply to public and private buildings 20,000 square feet and larger, using ENERGY STAR Score and/or Site Energy Use Intensity (EUI) as performance metrics, with standards set by property type to ensure that buildings are compared to their peers and that targets are equitable and achievable.[^3] The City of Miami procured Touchstone IQ as the energy and water benchmarking solutions provider, which provides the software platform for the data processing and tracking, along with support for a help desk.[^2] One of the components includes the Building Performance Ordinance which mandates benchmarking and retuning, also known as retro commissioning.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The initiative is managed by the Office of Resilience for large existing buildings in Miami-Dade County and is part of the national City Energy Project initiative.[^4][^1] The City of Miami adopted the building energy and water consumption benchmarking and retuning ordinance in June 2021.[^2] The ordinance went into effect in a phased approach with buildings 100,000 square feet or larger required to comply by June 30, 2022, buildings between 50,000 and 99,999 square feet by June 30, 2023, and buildings between 20,000 and 49,999 square feet by June 30, 2024.[^2] Building owner reporting was phased in overtime by square footage, with reporting requirements for 100,000 square feet and above beginning in 2023.[^6] Data verification is required for a subset of buildings every three years to ensure data quality.[^3] The implementation requires one full staff person to manage this program and one part-time staff person to support.[^2] The cost of the software program was an estimated $500,000 which is required for the building tracking and technical support.[^2] Miami-Dade County planned to create a Quality Assurance Verifier position to support the guidelines and applicability of the Building Performance Ordinance.[^4] Florida Power and Light (FPL) established a pilot program for properties in the City of Miami that must comply with the benchmarking requirement.[^2]

The recommendations were developed through an extensive stakeholder engagement process that included one-on-one interviews with more than 40 key stakeholders, including building owners and managers, developers, affordable housing providers, community-based organizations, contractors, and utility representatives.[^3] A series of four public workshops with over 100 participants gathered input on the design of the proposed policies and programs.[^3] The recommendations were scheduled to be presented to the Miami-Dade County Board of County Commissioners in early 2022.[^3] IMT served in a leadership role in the City Energy Project, a $20 million, multi-year initiative that provided human and financial resources to major U.S. cities to improve the energy efficiency of buildings.[^7]

## Benefits and Impacts

Once fully implemented, the program is projected to achieve annual countywide outcomes of $37 million to $112 million in energy and water bill savings.[^1] The initiative is projected to achieve an annual countywide reduction of 234,000 to 1.1 million metric tons in climate pollution emissions.[^1] Additionally, the program is projected to achieve an annual countywide reduction of 300 billion gallons in water usage.[^1] Miami-Dade County established a target to benchmark 1.3 billion building square footage communitywide by 2026.[^4] The county also set a target to retune 1.1 billion building square footage communitywide buildings by 2030.[^4] The program seeks to promote improvements in building performance through a suite of strategies that increase energy and water efficiency in large, existing private and public buildings.[^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Buildings account for nearly 60% of community-wide greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions in Miami-Dade County.[^3] Out of the 1.03 million housing units in Miami-Dade County, 82% were built before 2000, which is before the Florida Building Code was enacted in 2002.[^4] The program addresses this legacy building stock challenge by ensuring that 80% of the building stock that was built before the Florida Building Code came into place in 2002 meets modern efficiency standards.[^4]

## Business Analysis

The program includes comprehensive support mechanisms through a BE305 Resource Center that provides a one-stop-shop for information, technical assistance, and guidance through a website, help desk, trainings, and direct support to building owners and managers.[^3] Financial resources connect building owners with existing rebates and incentives from utilities and other sources, as well as exploring new financing options such as a green bank or commercial property assessed clean energy (C-PACE) program.[^3] The initiative includes workforce development through training and certification programs to build the local workforce of qualified contractors, building operators, and other professionals needed to deliver high-quality energy and water efficiency projects.[^3] Equity strategies include workforce development programs focused on creating pathways to high-quality jobs for residents of disadvantaged communities.[^3] In addition to policy development, the program includes a voluntary performance challenge (the BE305 Challenge) that provides resources, training, and technical assistance to help building owners and managers improve their performance and prepare for future requirements.[^3]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.miamidade.gov/global/environment/resilience/building-efficiency-305.page
- [^2]: https://miamibeach.novusagenda.com/AgendaPublic/CoverSheet.aspx?ItemID=34331&MeetingID=1343
- [^3]: https://southeastfloridaclimatecompact.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/02/Miami-Dade-County_-Building-Efficiency-305_Summary.pdf
- [^4]: https://www.miamidade.gov/global/environment/climate-strategy/energy-buildings-approach-1.page
- [^5]: https://www.miami.gov/My-Government/Climate-Change-in-the-City-of-Miami/Climate-Change-Action/Building-Efficiency-305-BE305
- [^6]: https://www.benchmark305.com/
- [^7]: https://imt.org/public-policy/policy-design-and-adoption/city-energy-project/

# Research Report: Deep Lake Water Cooling District Energy System

## Solution Overview

Toronto's Deep Lake Water Cooling (DLWC) system represents the world's largest implementation of renewable lake-based district cooling technology, harnessing the naturally cold temperatures at the bottom of Lake Ontario to provide air conditioning for downtown buildings.[^3][^10][^7] This low-carbon thermal energy network plays a critical role in enhancing the city's resilience to climate change while substantially reducing carbon emissions through an innovative public-private partnership between Enwave Energy Corporation and the City of Toronto.[^11][^3] The system has been identified as a key strategy within the City of Toronto's TransformTO Climate Action Plan, which aims to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by 65% by 2030 and achieve net-zero emissions by 2040.[^1][^2][^5]

## Technical Components

The DLWC system operates through three high-density polyethylene intake pipes, each extending 5 kilometres into Lake Ontario to draw water at a constant temperature of 4°C from a depth of 83 metres.[^9][^7][^8] The cold lake water travels first to the Toronto Island Filtration Plant for treatment as potable water, then flows by gravity to the John Street Pumping Station and the Energy Transfer Station located at the Metro Toronto Convention Centre.[^7][^9][^4] At the Energy Transfer Station, a series of large plate and frame heat exchangers transfer thermal energy from the lake water to a closed chilled water loop owned by Enwave, with the two water loops never mixing.[^7][^9][^6] The system was originally built with 11 large Alfa Laval plate heat exchangers and has since been expanded to include 14 heat exchangers.[^9] The lake water, now slightly warmed to 9°C, continues into the City's potable water supply system.[^7][^4] The chilled water circulates through four interconnected downtown plants and 25 miles (40 kilometers) of underground pipes to deliver cooling to connected buildings.[^2] In 2020, Enwave installed an innovative 8.5-million-litre temperature-controlled thermal battery tank underneath the lowest parking level of The Well development at Spadina and Front Streets, which stores energy at night during off-peak times and discharges during peak times, easing strain on the electricity grid and reducing overall costs.[^3][^5][^2]

## Implementation Details

The DLWC system was launched in July 2004 after more than 15 years of planning, research and development, with pre-construction funded by the Federation of Canadian Municipalities beginning in 1997 and environmental assessment completed by R.V. Anderson Associates Limited starting in 1997.[^8][^7][^6] The original construction costs exceeded $200 million, funded by the Toronto District Heating Corporation with financing from OMERS and the City of Toronto, with some federal funding supporting a portion of construction costs.[^4][^6][^10] The system began with only a handful of customers in 2004 but has since expanded rapidly to serve more than 100 buildings in downtown Toronto, including hospitals, commercial buildings, residential buildings, data centres, City Hall, entertainment venues, and Queen's Park.[^10][^3][^5] Construction on a major expansion of the system began in 2021 and was commissioned in August 2024, involving a $100 million investment to lay three kilometres of pipe into Lake Ontario through a newly built tunnel extending under the harbour to Enwave's John Street Energy Centre, along with installation of new heat exchangers.[^3][^5][^6] The expansion project faced significant challenges, including drilling a three-kilometre tunnel 300 feet below surface during the pandemic and encountering atypical ground conditions, with key contractors including BTY, Modern Niagara, WSP, Armstrong, C&M McNally Tunnel Contractors, Sprint Mechanical and Trivolt.[^3] The expansion increased the system's cooling capacity by 60 percent, with the capability of providing service to an additional 40 buildings throughout Toronto's downtown, bringing total deep lake water cooling raw water capacity to 704 million litres per day.[^5][^3][^6] The DLWC System expansion project received financial support from a grant from Canada's Low Carbon Economy Fund, Champions Stream, and a $600 million loan commitment from the Canada Infrastructure Bank (CIB) supporting projects across Enwave's portfolio.[^5][^1] In 2024, the Canada Infrastructure Bank and Enwave completed a long-term agreement valued at $1.4 billion to accelerate and advance district energy projects in the Cities of Toronto and Mississauga, with the CIB committing $600 million to allow Enwave to accelerate and scale the build-out of its district energy systems.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The DLWC system achieves remarkable environmental and operational performance, reducing electricity consumption for cooling by up to 90% compared to conventional air conditioning systems.[^7][^9][^4] The system delivers a 74% to 79% reduction in greenhouse gas emissions from conventional configurations, eliminating between 22,000 and 79,000 tonnes of CO2 emissions annually—equivalent to taking 15,800 cars off the road.[^4][^7][^9] The system saves 90,000 megawatt-hours of electricity use annually, roughly enough to power a town of 25,000 people, and avoids drawing over 60 megawatts of peak electrical demand from Ontario's grid.[^10][^3][^5] Water conservation benefits are substantial, with the system saving 220 million gallons (832 million litres) of water annually—equivalent to nearly 350 Olympic-size swimming pools—by eliminating the evaporation associated with traditional cooling tower systems.[^5][^10][^7] The system has also eliminated the need for harmful ozone-depleting CFC refrigerants and reduced water usage by 1.2 billion litres per year.[^7][^9] Specific building performance demonstrates the system's effectiveness, with Scotiabank Arena using approximately 3 million kilowatt-hours less electricity annually than if it cooled using traditional methods—a reduction of about 70 percent.[^10] The system now serves over 200 buildings covering more than 40 million square feet of downtown real estate, including hospitals, commercial offices, hotels, condominiums, institutions, and data centers.[^2][^3] Notable clients include Toronto-Dominion Centre, Royal Bank Plaza, RBC Centre, Metro Toronto Convention Centre, University Health Network, Scotiabank Arena, Brookfield Place, and the Fairmont Royal York.[^6][^2] The DLWC System has been harnessed to create Enwave's Green Heat program, enabling the provision of low-carbon heat to Toronto's downtown district energy grid and allowing the Fairmont Royal York, a nearly 100-year-old historic building, to achieve net zero certification from the Canada Green Building Council.[^5] The projects are expected to help reduce greenhouse gas emissions by more than 67,000 tonnes every year, actively mitigating climate change and supporting Toronto developers in reaching their GHG reduction goals while providing a cost-effective means to achieving compliance with the Toronto Green Standard.[^1][^5]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Low-carbon thermal energy networks such as Toronto's deep lake water cooling system are important in making the city more resilient to climate change and decreasing carbon emissions, particularly as urban areas face increasing cooling demands during extreme heat events.[^11] The system enhances the supply of potable water with a clean water source, decreases electrical demand and consumption, increases employment opportunities, and helps businesses and Toronto residents reduce greenhouse gas emissions and improve outdoor air quality.[^8] This unique partnership directly supports Toronto's ambitious strategy to reduce GHG emissions overall and achieve net-zero emissions by 2040, with the Downtown Plan policies stating that Downtown will contribute to the achievement of the City's energy and emissions targets through near-zero emissions development that minimizes electricity demand and, where possible, by connecting development to the deep lake water cooling system and other low-carbon thermal energy networks.[^5][^11]

## Business Analysis

The hybrid public-private business model served as the catalyst for implementing Toronto's DLWC system, which had been planned since the early 1980s but was challenged by a lack of major private investment.[^4][^8] The opportunity emerged when excessive silt in drinking water extracted from Lake Ontario led the city to plan for a deeper, longer pipe to reduce filtering costs, creating an incentive for the city to partner with Enwave through restructuring of the originally non-profit publicly owned Toronto District Heating Corporation that had restricted powers in securing long-term financing.[^8][^4] In March 2000, the Toronto District Heating Corporation changed its name to Enwave District Energy Limited, and Enwave Energy Corporation is now a private corporation owned by Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan Board and IFM Investors since its acquisition by Brookfield Asset Management in 2012.[^4][^1] The success of the private-public partnership was driven by early recognition of the opportunity for partnership through the connection of drinking water and water for cooling supply, with the City of Toronto's involvement as investor and promoter helping create confidence in the project and contributing to increased confidence among potential customers who signed long-term contracts, some of nearly 20 years.[^4][^8] Regulators granted Enwave an exemption from water extraction fees—particularly important as Enwave only extracts the cold from the water, not the resource itself—which was key for ensuring the project's commercial viability while securing long-term benefits for the City of Toronto as well as revenues from the project's later privatization.[^8][^4] The partnership aligns Enwave's vision of clean energy growth with its owners' commitments to achieve net-zero emissions by 2050, and the new investment represents a unique opportunity for Enwave to deploy sustainable technologies, such as wastewater heat recovery and Geoexchange, in delivering community-scale low-carbon energy for new networks in Toronto and Mississauga.[^1] District energy projects help decarbonize connected buildings by reducing typical electricity consumption by up to 80 percent and carbon emissions by up to 60 percent, with low-carbon thermal networks connected to energy-efficient buildings serving as key ingredients for near-zero emissions development.[^1][^11] The projects leverage Enwave's expertise and strengths in design, construction, operations, and maintenance of district energy networks, and Enwave is expanding its chilled water system with a new plant at the Metro Toronto Convention Centre South that will add 16,000 tons of capacity to meet growing demand.[^1][^7] The United Nations under its United 4 Sustainable Smart Cities initiative published a case study of Enwave's DLWC system in A Guide to Circular Cities, praising its water consumption, energy efficiency and cost savings, and citing the system's accomplishment in achieving its objective to transform the way buildings are cooled to reduce environmental impact while providing value to the city and fostering economic development.[^3][^5]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://cib-bic.ca/en/medias/articles/1-4-billion-committed-to-district-energy-projects-in-toronto-and-mississauga/
- [^2]: https://www.enwave.com/locations/toronto
- [^3]: https://canada.constructconnect.com/dcn/news/infrastructure/2024/09/enwave-commissions-deep-lake-water-cooling-system-expansion
- [^4]: https://c2e2.unepccc.org/kms_object/enwave-energy-corporation/
- [^5]: https://www.enwave.com/enwave-commissions-expansion-of-world-renowned-deep-lake-water-cooling-system-internationally-recognized-by-the-united-nations-in-partnership-with-toronto-water
- [^6]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deep_Lake_Water_Cooling_System
- [^7]: https://www.nyc.gov/html/unccp/gprb/downloads/pdf/Toronto_DLWC.pdf
- [^8]: https://www.c40.org/case-studies/c40-good-practice-guides-toronto-enwave-energy-corporation/
- [^9]: https://www.alfalaval.com/globalassets/documents/local/canada/campaign/alfa-laval-enwave-case-study.pdf
- [^10]: https://www.washingtonpost.com/climate-solutions/interactive/2021/toronto-deep-latke-water-cooling-raptors/
- [^11]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Toronto-CA/toronto-resilience-strategy.pdf

# Research Report: 100-Year Storm Flood Risk Mapping Tool

## Solution Overview

The San Francisco Public Utilities Commission (SFPUC) launched the 100-Year Storm Flood Risk Map in 2018 to inform property owners and tenants about stormwater flood risk so they can take actions to protect their property and belongings.[^1] This informational tool identifies more than 4,000 properties that are vulnerable to storm runoff occurring during a 100-year storm—a term used to describe an extreme storm that has a 1 percent chance of happening during any given year.[^5] The approach was developed to help property owners, developers, and City agencies understand and address the potential for flooding.[^8]

## Technical Components

The SFPUC created this mapping tool using computer modeling that simulates the citywide operation of the stormwater system during a 100-year storm to identify areas subject to flooding.[^4][^6][^10] The sophisticated computer model simulates how rainfall flows through the City's sewer system and on its streets, with the model calibrated and validated using data from past storms to ensure its accuracy.[^3] The modeling platform utilizes InfoWorks ICM software, which includes a two-dimensional (2D) module that generates a surface mesh using ground surface elevation data and can route overland flows by solving the surface flow transport equations for each mesh element in extreme storm events.[^2] InfoWorks ICM also features a Real Time Control (RTC) module that allows programming different types of pump stations, gate and valve structures and their operational set points for different treatment pathways during varying storm conditions.[^2] The modeling was based on historical storm data to simulate flooding scenarios.[^1] The map shows the depth of potential flooding, ranging from 1 to 3 inches to over 2 feet.[^3] The Flood Map depicts areas that experience flooding at least six inches deep during a 100-year storm.[^1] The tool specifically shows parcels that are highly likely to experience deep and contiguous flooding during a 100-year storm, where deep and contiguous flooding means flooding that is at least 6 inches deep, spanning an area at least the size of half an average city block.[^7] The Flood Map, updated in December 2024, now shows the flood elevations for these areas in San Francisco, by flood elevation zones.[^7] Flood elevation refers to the height (rounded to the nearest foot) that stormwater flooding is likely to reach at a specific location during a 100-year storm, measured from the San Francisco City Datum, a consistent reference point for vertical measurements used by the design and development industry in San Francisco.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The preliminary flood risk maps presented by the SFPUC highlight areas prone to deep and contiguous flooding across multiple neighborhoods.[^9] Areas with stormwater flooding risks include the Islais Creek area (Cayuga/Alemany), South of Market, Inner Mission, and Civic/Center Western Addition.[^4] The SFPUC identified neighborhoods with the greatest flood risks and prioritized projects in those areas, with proposed upgrades to the flood-prone neighborhoods totaling $700 million.[^5] The agency emphasized the importance of prioritizing projects based on flood risk, with a focus on high-risk areas marked in red on the maps, with the goal to eliminate these high-risk zones over time, although it was acknowledged that not all neighborhoods could receive immediate attention due to budget constraints and competing infrastructure needs.[^9] The SFPUC is investing over $600 million to expand the capacity of the system in specific low-lying areas.[^1] The agency is actively working on several capital projects, with over $700 million proposed for flood management initiatives, including ongoing construction in neighborhoods like Ingleside Terrace and Sunnyside.[^9] To tackle these challenges, the SFPUC is planning to invest approximately $1.6 billion in capital projects over time to enhance the city's infrastructure to better manage stormwater and minimize flooding risks.[^9] Following the map's launch, a 2019 ordinance requires sellers and landlords to disclose if a property lies in the Flood Map's risk zone.[^1] San Francisco law requires sellers or landlords of property in San Francisco to disclose to buyers or tenants if the property is within the 100-year storm flood risk zone shown on the SFPUC's Flood Map.[^7] Flood elevations are not required to be disclosed but are provided to help renters and owners take steps to protect their properties and belongings and minimize potential flood impacts.[^6]

## Benefits and Impacts

The 100-Year Storm Flood Risk Map serves as a valuable tool for residents, helping them understand their flood risk and take steps to protect their properties, contributing to a more flood-resilient city.[^6] The mapping tool functions as a planning tool and does not affect flood insurance requirements.[^8] The map shows flood risk from heavy rainfall that can temporarily overwhelm the capacity of the sewer system, also known as stormwater flooding.[^3] The Flood Map shows flooding due to stormwater runoff, not other potential flooding sources, such as water coming in from San Francisco Bay or the Pacific Ocean.[^1] This Draft Map shows flood risk from storm runoff only and does not consider flood risk in San Francisco from other causes such as inundation from the San Francisco Bay or Pacific Ocean.[^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

San Francisco faces unique flooding challenges due to its topography and infrastructure configuration. The city has extremely steep hills, low valleys, and lots of flat low-lying areas with paved surfaces, which means rain has no place to go other than their combined sewer system before being discharged to the San Francisco Bay or the Pacific Ocean.[^2] San Francisco is the only coastal city in California with a combined sewer system that collects and treats both wastewater and stormwater in the same network of pipes.[^2] A 100-year storm is a very large storm that has a 1% chance of happening in any given year and would bring intense rainfall over a short period of time that could overwhelm the City's sewer system and cause flooding.[^3] The SFPUC is using the latest climate science to understand how flood risk will change in the future and to plan for a more resilient San Francisco.[^3]

## Business Analysis

The SFPUC owns and operates close to 1,000 miles of sewer mains, 3 treatment facilities, 200 million gallons of storage, 26 pump stations and 36 combined sewer discharge outfalls.[^2] The agency's capital investment strategy reflects a comprehensive approach to flood management, with proposed upgrades to flood-prone neighborhoods totaling $700 million and over $600 million specifically allocated to expand system capacity in low-lying areas.[^5][^1] The SFPUC is planning to invest approximately $1.6 billion in capital projects over time to enhance the city's infrastructure to better manage stormwater and minimize flooding risks.[^9] The implementation includes ongoing construction in neighborhoods like Ingleside Terrace and Sunnyside as part of the over $700 million proposed for flood management initiatives.[^9] The regulatory framework supporting this investment includes the 2019 ordinance requiring property disclosure, which creates market transparency around flood risk and incentivizes property-level adaptation measures.[^1]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://www.autodesk.com/blogs/water/2023/06/12/how-san-francisco-public-utilities-commission-uses-infoworks-icm-to-model-a-combined-system/
- [^3]: https://www.sfpuc.gov/sites/default/files/learning/SFPUC_100YearStormFloodRiskMap_InfoFAQs.pdf
- [^4]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/San-Francisco-US/HCR_FullReport_200326_0.pdf
- [^5]: https://localnewsmatters.org/2019/02/21/sf-supervisors-vote-that-buyers-and-renters-must-be-provided-with-flood-risk-info/
- [^6]: https://www.sfpuc.gov/about-us/news/san-francisco-asks-residents-and-businesses-prepare-rainy-season
- [^7]: https://www.sfpuc.gov/learning/emergency-preparedness/flood-maps
- [^8]: https://www.sfpuc.gov/sites/default/files/learning/100YRFloodRiskMapParcelReviewProcess.pdf

# Research Report: Sagrera District Heating and Cooling via Groundwater Heat Pumps

## Solution Overview

Barcelona City Council and Barcelona Sagrera Alta Velocidad are promoting the development of a new centralised heating and cooling system for Sagrera Station and its surroundings.[^1] This approach utilizes high-efficiency heat pumps powered by groundwater from the Besòs River and solar energy to provide thermal services across a major urban development area.[^1][^2][^3] The system is designed to achieve maximum energy efficiency while significantly reducing fossil fuel consumption and carbon emissions in one of Barcelona's key infrastructure projects.[^1]

## Technical Components

The production of heat and cold is foreseen through high-efficiency heat pumps that extract thermal energy from groundwater sources in the Besòs River.[^1][^2][^3] Solar energy capture is integrated through multiple infrastructure elements, including the Solar Forest at La Sagrera Station—a steel pergola constructed from structural steel tubes of 500mm diameter rising to 16 meters that incorporates a solar energy capture system.[^4] The station features 14,000 square meters of photovoltaic panels on its roof, which utilize waste heat from train platforms to warm station areas and incorporate natural ventilation systems.[^4] The centralised air-conditioning network is designed to enable 85% of the energy needed for heating, cooling, and domestic hot water to be self-generated from renewable energies without environmental impact.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The system planned for La Sagrera Station and the surrounding area is currently in the design stage.[^2] The initial project proposes deployment with a planned investment of 21 million euros.[^1] Coverage will extend across the Sagrera intermodal station, the entire Sagrera tertiary area, and the Prim urban development sector through an agreement with ADIF.[^1] The aim is to provide energy coverage for a total of 920,000 square meters, including future expansion into the new urban fabric with neighbourhood and productive uses planned for the site of the former Mercedes-Benz factory.[^1] Once the initial project has been configured, the dossier will be prepared for this heating and cooling network to be implemented, operated, and maintained in a public-private partnership format.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The new plant will reduce annual CO2 emissions by the equivalent of taking 19,160 vehicles off the road and save 53,000 MWh per year in electricity consumption.[^1] The system's design enables 85% of required energy for heating, cooling, and domestic hot water to be self-generated from renewable sources.[^1] This approach builds on Barcelona's experience with Districlima, which was formed in 2002 to perform the first urban network of heat and cold distribution in Spain.[^3] Districlima provides thermal energy through its urban network to more than 90 buildings, saving more than 17,500 annual tons of CO2, representing approximately 60% reduction in the consumption of fossil energies.[^3] The Districlima project required total investment costs of 55,000,000 euros before tax, and in 2005, following the award of a public competition, extended to the 22@ technology district with a concession of 27 years.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This system addresses climate adaptation by reducing dependence on fossil fuel-based heating and cooling systems that contribute to urban heat island effects and greenhouse gas emissions. The integration of renewable energy sources—groundwater thermal extraction and solar capture—provides resilient thermal services that can operate independently of conventional energy grids during climate-related disruptions. The approach demonstrates how major infrastructure developments can incorporate climate-responsive design that reduces environmental impact while meeting growing urban energy demands.

## Business Analysis

The initial project investment is planned at 21 million euros for the La Sagrera network.[^1] The implementation will follow a public-private partnership format for operation and maintenance, similar to the Districlima model that operates under a 27-year concession awarded in 2005.[^1][^3] The Districlima precedent demonstrates the financial viability of district energy systems in Barcelona, with total investment costs of 55 million euros before tax supporting services to more than 90 buildings.[^3] The network extends its layout based on urban development of the area and the needs of connecting new users, providing a scalable business model.[^3] The significant energy savings—53,000 MWh per year in electricity consumption—and carbon reduction equivalent to removing 19,160 vehicles annually create strong value propositions for both public authorities and private operators.[^1]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://www.energia.barcelona/en/saving-energy/heating-and-cooling-networks
- [^3]: https://www.construction21.org/luxembourg/city/es/districlima-urban-network-of-heat-and-cold-in-barcelona-and-sant-adria-de-besos.html
- [^4]: https://www.ayesa.com/en/projects/la-sagrera-station-a-new-gateway-to-barcelona-for-100-million-people/

# Research Report: Urban Ecosystem Services Modeling for Carbon Drawdown Prioritization

## Solution Overview

The City of Boulder is collaborating with University of Colorado researchers to develop an ecosystem services modeling system designed to identify and prioritize urban carbon drawdown opportunities that maximize multiple environmental benefits.[^3][^5][^6] This modeling approach emerged from a multi-year effort by Climate Initiatives and City Planning staff working with a broad consortium of partners to explore strategies for managing urban landscapes to both capture carbon and enhance urban ecosystem services including shade, stormwater infiltration, air quality, and biodiversity.[^3] The initiative operates within a context where the city and Boulder County collectively own more than 75,000 acres of open space, including nearly a third of the county's agricultural holdings.[^1]

## Technical Components

The ecosystem services modeling system represents a technical framework for analyzing and prioritizing nature-based interventions across urban landscapes.[^3][^5][^6] A previous MENV capstone team created a process guide outlining methodologies to identify and model carbon drawdown opportunities in cities, establishing foundational technical protocols for this work.[^2] The modeling approach integrates multiple ecosystem service parameters including carbon sequestration capacity, shade provision, stormwater infiltration potential, air quality improvements, and biodiversity enhancement to generate comprehensive assessments of landscape management strategies.[^3] The toolkit titled 'Designing Resilient Urban Landscapes: Nature-Based Solutions for Local Governments' provides practical guidance for local governments on using sustainable, ecosystem-driven approaches that are specific to local geography and climate conditions.[^7]

## Implementation Details

The City of Boulder and the Urban Sustainability Directors Network jointly formed the Urban Drawdown Initiative (UDI) to accelerate the development of carbon drawdown strategies that simultaneously improve the resilience, productivity, and equity-based community development of urban areas.[^2] UDI has been working with cities and other leading climate action organizations to build tools and systems that identify opportunities to harness and amplify the abilities of living landscapes in and around cities.[^2] The initiative sought three MENV students to be trained in leading-edge practices and tools to identify climate action opportunities for mitigation, adaptation, resilience, and equity in managing urban landscapes and resources.[^2] The city and county launched extensive research and field trials of soil-based carbon drawdown, hoping to replicate successful approaches on other public and private lands.[^1] This project was funded by the voter-approved Boulder County Sustainability Tax, a 0.125% sales and use tax that runs through the end of 2034.[^7] The initiative has since evolved, with Nature-based Climate Initiatives (NCI) – formerly the Urban Drawdown Initiative – now bringing together cities, resource specialists, community-based organizations, scientists, innovators, land managers, and others to accelerate implementation of carbon removal strategies that simultaneously improve the social, economic, and environmental resilience of local communities.[^4] By 2025, the city aimed to develop the capability to value and monitor ecosystem services for City of Boulder and broader community activities.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The modeling system enables prioritization of urban carbon drawdown efforts based on their capacity to maximize multiple ecosystem services benefits simultaneously.[^3][^5][^6] This approach addresses shade provision, stormwater infiltration, air quality improvements, and biodiversity enhancement alongside carbon capture objectives.[^3] The extensive research and field trials of soil-based carbon drawdown aim to identify successful approaches that can be replicated across other public and private lands within the region.[^1]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The ecosystem services modeling system directly addresses climate adaptation by prioritizing landscape interventions that enhance urban resilience through multiple pathways including stormwater management, heat mitigation through shade provision, and air quality improvements.[^3] The integration of carbon drawdown with ecosystem services creates nature-based solutions that simultaneously address climate mitigation and adaptation objectives across Boulder's extensive public land holdings.[^1][^3]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://www.colorado.edu/menv/city-boulder-urban-drawdown-initiative
- [^3]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Boulder-US/Boulder-June_8_Study_Session_FINAL.pdf
- [^4]: https://web.tplgis.org/udi/

- [^7]: https://bouldercounty.gov/news/toolkit-integrating-nature-into-urban-planning-now-available/

# Research Report: Flood Risk Management Platform with Integrated Municipal Data

## Solution Overview

CivicDataLab, in collaboration with the Open Contracting Partnership and Bangkok Metropolitan Administration (BMA), has developed a Flood Risk Management Platform to strengthen mitigation planning and oversight.[^1] This comprehensive one-stop online hub consolidates different types of disaster and hazard information into a unified digital infrastructure.[^3][^4][^5] The initiative addresses Bangkok's acute vulnerability to flooding, which stems from the city's low elevation, rapid urbanisation, and extensive canal network, with shifts in rainfall patterns and intense rainfall events often overwhelming existing drainage systems.[^1]

## Technical Components

The platform integrates data from multiple municipal sources into one cohesive and user-friendly interface.[^1] Technical inputs include flood-related citizen complaints from Traffy Fondue, flooding, rainfall, drainage, and canal data from the Department of Drainage and Sewerage (DDS), and flood-related budget and procurement data from the Budget Bureau.[^1] The system architecture supports the integration of disease surveillance data, including dengue fever, COVID-19, influenza, monkeypox, and drug-related issues, with disease information used for internal management and protected under the Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA).[^2] Complementing this infrastructure, the Geo-Informatics and Space Technology Development Agency (GISTDA) has expanded access to open geospatial data via the National Geographic Information System (NGIS), with these datasets supporting the development of digital twins—virtual simulations of cities that can model disaster impacts and guide risk-informed urban planning.[^6]

## Benefits and Impacts

The platform provides a holistic view of the ground situation, aimed at improving BMA's flood mitigation efforts through better internal coordination and supervision, reduced data-sharing burdens, enhanced evidence-based decision-making, and greater public engagement.[^1] This integrated approach enables municipal authorities to monitor multiple disaster-related data streams simultaneously while maintaining data protection standards for sensitive health information.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This system directly addresses Bangkok's heightened susceptibility to flooding driven by climate-related factors.[^1] The platform responds to the challenges posed by shifts in rainfall patterns and intense rainfall events that disrupt water supply, housing, public health, and essential public services by overwhelming existing drainage infrastructure.[^1]

## Sources

# Research Report: Smart Stormwater Harvesting and Irrigation Optimization System

## Solution Overview

The City of Sydney has implemented a comprehensive water management approach combining stormwater harvesting infrastructure, real-time irrigation monitoring systems, and smart sensor networks across its parks to reduce reliance on potable water and enhance drought resilience.[^4] This initiative encompasses water reuse schemes at twenty parks, providing 80,000 litres of recycled water daily during summer, alongside advanced IoT-enabled irrigation optimization.[^2][^4] The approach represents a city-wide strategy to drought-proof water supply while addressing urban heat island effects and improving environmental outcomes.[^1]

## Technical Components

The Sydney Park water reuse project features a stormwater treatment system designed to harvest up to 850 million litres of stormwater annually from the Munni Catchment north of the park.[^1] The system diverts water from the Munni Channel through a new underground pipe into a series of four constructed wetland ponds.[^27][^6] Treatment processes include a pollution trap that removes litter, coarse sediment and organic matter through physical screening, followed by a bioretention system that collects water in shallow depressions and filters it through plant roots and soil.[^38][^39] Additional filtration and ultra-violet cleansing processes treat water as it is drawn from the system for reuse.[^15] The infrastructure includes elevated terracotta channels that aerate and distribute water throughout the wetland system while reflecting the site's industrial history.[^48] The SIMPaCT pilot project at Sydney Olympic Park deployed 260 smart IoT sensors delivering live environmental data and capturing highly localised microclimates across the park.[^3] This installation comprised over 50 devices measuring temperature and humidity, 13 weather stations, and over 200 smart-soil devices across Bicentennial Park.[^49] The system uses soil moisture outcomes to learn over time the most efficient irrigation regime, working with recycled water to optimize over 200 individual irrigation stations based on sensor data.[^21][^34] The technology is designed to easily scale up or down in complexity, allowing replication in any urban irrigation project from parks to botanic gardens and sports fields.[^24]

## Implementation Details

The Sydney Park water reuse project was completed in October 2015 with a budget of $11 million AUD (approximately $8.3 million USD), funded jointly by the City of Sydney and the federal government.[^8][^44][^58] The project started in April 2013 and represents the second phase of stormwater harvesting at the 109-acre Sydney Park in St Peters, following a much smaller first phase implemented in 2010-2011.[^58][^12][^41] The initiative was initiated by a series of connected events including the Millennium Drought, the Detailed Master Plan for Sydney Park (2006, Aspect Studio), Sydney 2030 strategic plan, and the City's Decentralised Water Master Plan.[^47] In 2017, the new stormwater treatment plant had to be relocated to accommodate motorway construction and was scheduled to re-open in late 2020.[^62] The SIMPaCT pilot at Bicentennial Park started in Q3 2021 and was completed in Q3 2023 with a total cost of $3.677 million, including $2.47 million funded by the Smart Places Acceleration Program.[^7][^25] The project was co-delivered by a coalition including UTS's Institute for Sustainable Futures, Western Sydney University, Sydney Olympic Park Authority, NSW Department of Planning and Environment, Sydney Water, and several industry stakeholders.[^56] Project suppliers included Australasian Railway Consultancy Services (ARCs Group), Meshed, Hydrology and Risk Consulting (HARC), Eratos, CentraTech Systems, and Total Water.[^50] ISF's role encompassed overseeing live data capture and delivery, writing the project Blueprint, and leading development of a post-pilot Roadmap for scalability.[^61] Sydney Water participated as a sponsor with a specific request that the artificial intelligence module be upscalable and downscalable for any irrigation system.[^60] Implementation challenges at Sydney Olympic Park included managing one of the largest irrigation systems in the country on uncapped landfill with thin soil that makes plants highly susceptible to drought.[^55]

## Benefits and Impacts

The Sydney Park system currently harvests 114 million litres of recycled water annually for improved pond circulation, irrigation of the 44-hectare park, and non-potable water supply to the neighbouring depot.[^13][^33] Water quality monitoring in Wetland 4 demonstrated a 68% reduction in copper, 50% reduction in nickel, 85% reduction in nitrogen, and 68% reduction in zinc, meeting local guidelines for water quality.[^36] Between 2012 and 2016, three out of four wetlands improved in microbial water quality, with increases ranging from 6% to 49% in the number of water samples meeting local criteria for good to fair quality.[^54] The project supported a 24% increase in bird species recorded from 2013 to 2019, with new habitat areas including a 5.21-acre (119%) increase in Sydney freshwater wetlands habitat and addition of 0.95 acres of gully forest habitat.[^63] The twenty-park water reuse scheme provides 80,000 litres of non-potable water per day during summer across the city.[^11] The SIMPaCT project focuses on making Bicentennial Park a 'cool island' with far-reaching thermal benefits for communities downwind, helping mitigate the Urban Heat Island effect in adjacent residential precincts.[^22][^23] City-wide water use was 1,090,000 kL in 2022/23, representing 22% lower consumption than the 2006 baseline.[^62][^65] In 2019-20, the City exceeded its water reduction target for the first time in a decade due to implemented measures, increased rainfall, and COVID-19 related closures of water-intensive facilities.[^61] The SIMPaCT project won multiple high-profile awards in 2023, including IoT Alliance awards for 'IoT for good' and 'Research', InnovationAus awards for 'Govtech' and 'People's Choice', and the NSW Banksia Award for 'Climate Technology Impact'.[^37][^26][^59][^60]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This integrated water management approach directly addresses drought vulnerability by reducing reliance on potable water through local stormwater capture and reuse.[^1] The project supports the City of Sydney's goal of reducing potable water reliance by 30% by 2030 and meeting 10% of water demand through local water capture and reuse by that same year.[^6][^10][^23] The smart irrigation and urban cooling components help mitigate extreme heat impacts through optimized water delivery and creation of cool island effects that benefit surrounding communities.[^22][^23]

## Business Analysis

The Sydney Park project represents the City of Sydney's largest water harvesting system, part of a broader portfolio that includes stormwater harvesting schemes at Victoria Park, Prince Alfred Park, Pirrama Park, and Paddington Reservoir, all delivering recycled water for irrigation and other non-potable uses.[^14][^16][^17][^18][^19] The City aims to keep potable water use below 2006 levels despite the area of parks and open spaces requiring irrigation increasing by more than 50% and growth in the property portfolio.[^67] The City will reduce potable water use by 10% by 2030 from 2018/19 levels in the local area.[^66] Municipal authorities are continuing to deliver the water sensitive urban design program and investigating opportunities to expand water reuse schemes.[^57][^32] The City is working with Sydney Water to deliver a recycled water scheme in Green Square.[^42] The SIMPaCT system's scalable design enables replication across diverse urban irrigation contexts, with Sydney Water specifically requesting upscalable and downscalable artificial intelligence modules for any irrigation system.[^24][^60] The project received substantial government support through the NSW Government's Smart Places Acceleration Fund, demonstrating public sector commitment to innovative water management technologies.[^51][^7]

## Sources

# Research Report: Solar-Battery Backup Power for Critical Facilities

## Solution Overview

San Francisco is implementing emergency backup power systems at critical facilities that integrate on-site solar energy generation with battery storage to support both climate action objectives and disaster preparedness.[^1][^2] This approach provides fuel-free emergency power during grid outages while advancing the city's transition to 100% renewable electricity by 2030 and net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2040.[^2][^3] The initiative serves as a national model for integrating solar and energy storage into existing disaster preparedness plans.[^4][^7]

## Technical Components

The systems utilize grid-tied rooftop solar arrays paired with battery energy storage systems (BESS) that enable "islanding" capability, allowing buildings to disconnect from the main grid during power outages and operate independently by drawing on stored solar energy.[^5] The San Francisco Public Utilities Commission installed a 95-kilowatt grid-tied rooftop solar array with battery storage at the San Francisco Public Library's Catalog & Technical Services building, marking the SFPUC's first resiliency project.[^5] All BESS project plans must comply with rigorous standards from UL, NFPA (including NFPA 855), and local fire and building codes, with the San Francisco Fire Department reviewing all project plans.[^1] The installations are designed to provide energy for critical loads for up to five days to help the city survive major disasters such as earthquakes.[^6] These systems support critical services including refrigeration for medical supplies, lighting for shelters, communications, and backup of critical information from the main library.[^5][^6]

## Implementation Details

In 2015, the City and County of San Francisco Department of the Environment received $1,321,200 in funding from the U.S. Department of Energy's Solar Market Pathways program, with an additional awardee cost share of $288,971, for the Solar+Storage for Resiliency project.[^4][^7] A three-year study funded with $1.3 million from the Department of Energy and $300,000 in San Francisco city funds examined solar-plus-storage installations at community centers.[^6] City officials are considering installing solar-plus-storage systems at as many as 12 community buildings.[^6] Consulting group Arup, hired to study the viability of the systems, estimated the price at $40 million, which could be reduced to approximately $26 million if the city secures a private sector partner for the installations.[^6] The project produced four case studies explaining how solar+storage would function at a fire station, a police station, a building used for shelter, and another critical power-need building.[^4] Proposed updates to Chapter 7 of the Environment Code require implementing solar and solar storage capabilities at Critical Community Institutions to provide emergency power without gas-powered generators, applying to new construction, HVAC replacement, or electrical updates at public safety facilities, health clinics, community centers, libraries, and emergency management facilities.[^3] CleanPowerSF is procuring energy storage to meet state mandates and reliability needs.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The implementation ensures greater energy reliability and resiliency amidst a changing climate while reaffirming the SFPUC's contribution to achieving net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2040.[^5] These new regulations help the city prepare for and recover from energy disruptions with minimal use of additional fossil fuels.[^3] By generating electricity at the point of use, rooftop solar reduces the need for costly and time-consuming upgrades to the electricity grid.[^2] Solar and storage can be aggregated and dispatched to support the grid during periods of high demand, reducing the need for expensive and polluting gas peaker plants.[^2] The approach addresses a critical gap in current planning, as much of San Francisco's existing resiliency planning relies on diesel generators, which could face fuel supply difficulties during major disasters that disrupt supply chains.[^6] The city aims to create a model that other neighborhoods in San Francisco can follow and a planning document that other cities nationwide can use to implement similar solar and storage emergency backup power systems.[^8]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Grid outages are expected to become more frequent and severe due to climate change, making solar paired with battery storage a resilient source of backup power during these disruptions.[^2] The systems address the need for resilient power at critical facilities including hospitals and emergency services, with deployment aligning with the city's climate and resilience goals.[^1] The initiative strengthens a network of community hubs throughout the city where residents can access information, resources, and support during disasters by providing them with backup power, emergency supplies, and training for staff and volunteers.[^9]

## Business Analysis

The Solar Market Pathways program provided the primary funding mechanism, with the Department of Energy contributing $1,321,200 and the city providing $288,971 in cost share for the initial project development.[^4][^7] The estimated $40 million implementation cost for 12 community buildings represents a potential public-private partnership opportunity, with projected cost reduction to $26 million through private sector collaboration.[^6] San Francisco is actively exploring and deploying BESS to support climate action goals, enhance grid resiliency, and integrate renewable energy, with CleanPowerSF procuring energy storage to meet state mandates and reliability needs.[^1] The regulatory framework includes proposed updates to Chapter 7 of the Environment Code mandating solar and solar storage capabilities at Critical Community Institutions, creating a compliance-driven market for these installations at public safety facilities, health clinics, community centers, libraries, and emergency management facilities.[^3]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.sfgov.org/lafco/sites/default/files/LAFCO_Intro_to_BESS-07-19-24.pdf
- [^2]: https://www.sfenvironment.org/sites/default/files/fliers/files/sfe_ee_solar_storage_roadmap.pdf
- [^3]: https://onesanfrancisco.org/cap-plan-2024/affordability
- [^4]: https://www.energy.gov/eere/solar/project-profile-city-and-county-san-francisco-department-environment-solar-market
- [^5]: https://www.sfpuc.gov/about-us/news/sfpucs-first-resiliency-project-san-francisco-public-library
- [^6]: https://www.utilitydive.com/news/san-francisco-studying-solar-plus-storage-systems-to-boost-local-resiliency/505828/
- [^7]: https://www.energy.gov/eere/solar/solar-market-pathways
- [^8]: https://rmi.org/blog_2015_10_13_san_francisco_prepares_for_the_big_one_with_microgrids/
- [^9]: https://resilientcitiesnetwork.org/downloadable_resources/Network/San-Francisco-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf

# Research Report: Community Microgrid Emergency Resilience Network

## Solution Overview

San Francisco completed a strategic initiative to develop criteria for high-priority neighborhoods where microgrids can provide critical community energy emergency resilience, identifying up to 10 neighborhoods and specific areas for microgrid development with implementation and funding plans for each location.[^3] This approach addresses the city's need to rapidly decarbonize its buildings and transportation sectors, which together account for 91% of the city's core greenhouse gas emissions, with building energy representing 49% and transportation 42% of San Francisco's core emissions in 2019.[^1][^7] The implementation supports San Francisco's ambitious climate goals of transitioning to 100% renewable electricity by 2030 and achieving net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2040.[^1]

## Technical Components

The San Francisco Department of Environment worked with the Department of Emergency Management to conduct a survey of city departments to develop a list of emergency facilities and their energy use, then mapped out the city to identify buildings with critical and essential power demands and group them into solar+storage microgrids where proximity allows.[^4] These maps covered the 11 supervisory districts in the city and identified power needs should a disaster strike, buildings with solar that were already part of disaster preparedness plans, and areas where a microgrid could potentially be installed.[^4] When paired with battery storage, solar provides a resilient source of backup power during grid outages, which are expected to become more frequent and severe due to climate change.[^1] The Hunters Point Community Microgrid Project was designed to transform the Bayview and Hunters Point areas of San Francisco into a world-class community microgrid.[^2]

## Implementation Details

The San Francisco Department of Environment received $1,321,200 in funding from the Department of Energy's Solar Market Pathways program for the Solar+Storage for Resiliency project, with an awardee cost share of $288,971.[^4] The Hunters Point Community Microgrid Project received a $150,000 boost in December 2015 and was being conducted in collaboration with Pacific Gas & Electric.[^2] The cost of SFPUC Hetch Hetchy Power's Renewable/Generation Power Program is approximately $10.0 million through FY2033.[^5] The implementation team produced four case studies explaining how solar+storage would work at a fire station, a police station, a building used for shelter, and another critical power-need building.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

Once deployed, the Hunters Point Community Microgrid Project is expected to bring $100 million in local wages to the Bayview-Hunters Point community, while reducing greenhouse gas emissions by 1.5 billion pounds over the next 20 years.[^2] The project will provide 25 percent of the community's electric energy needs.[^2] The strategic microgrid planning approach enables the city to identify and prioritize neighborhoods where distributed energy resources can deliver the most significant resilience benefits during emergency situations.[^3]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This microgrid implementation strategy directly addresses the increasing frequency and severity of grid outages expected due to climate change impacts.[^1] The October 26, 2019 Public Safety Power Shutoff event cut power to more people than live in the entire city of San Francisco, demonstrating the scale of vulnerability that distributed energy systems can help mitigate.[^6]

## Business Analysis

The financial structure combines federal grant funding, local cost-sharing, and utility partnerships to support distributed energy infrastructure development across priority neighborhoods.[^4][^2] The Hunters Point project's economic model demonstrates significant local economic benefits through job creation and wage generation, with $100 million in local wages expected alongside substantial greenhouse gas reductions.[^2] The SFPUC's Renewable/Generation Power Program allocated approximately $10.0 million through FY2033 to support renewable energy infrastructure development.[^5] The collaboration with Pacific Gas & Electric on the Hunters Point Community Microgrid Project represents a public-private partnership model for implementing community-scale distributed energy systems.[^2]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.sfenvironment.org/sites/default/files/fliers/files/sfe_ee_solar_storage_roadmap.pdf
- [^2]: https://clean-coalition.org/community-microgrids/hunters-point-community-microgrid-project/
- [^3]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/San-Francisco-US/HCR_FullReport_200326_0.pdf
- [^4]: https://www.energy.gov/eere/solar/project-profile-city-and-county-san-francisco-department-environment-solar-market
- [^5]: https://onesanfrancisco.org/cap-plan-2024/infrastructure-streets-enhancement-projects
- [^6]: https://votesolar.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/01/Resilient_Clean_Energy_for_California-REPORT.pdf

# Research Report: Digital Twin Platform with 5G IoT Infrastructure

## Solution Overview

Belfast is developing an integrated digital infrastructure approach centered on digital twin platforms built upon 5G and Internet of Things deployments, combined with the city's expertise in cybersecurity and artificial intelligence technologies.[^5] This approach aims to ensure strategic city assets including housing, energy, transport, water, and telecommunications are resilient by design.[^5] The initiative represents a comprehensive framework for data-driven urban management and climate adaptation across critical infrastructure systems.

## Technical Components

The technical foundation includes a £30 million investment in advanced wireless infrastructure to support enhanced innovation, productivity, and competitiveness.[^1] Belfast Harbour partnered with BT in 2021 to build the UK and Ireland's first 5G private network for ports, delivering a series of 5G-led innovations to accelerate digital transformation.[^1] The roll out of 5G enabled sensors monitors air quality and other environmental factors, supporting Belfast Harbour and the wider city in achieving sustainability ambitions.[^1] A £5 million shared urban data platform and architecture enables businesses, industry, academia, and the public sector to generate, manage, and analyse data in ways that spur collaboration and open innovation, supporting data-enabled solutions, products, and services in areas such as sustainable mobility, energy management, and urban planning.[^4] The UK Digital Twin Centre, which officially opened in Belfast in May 2025, has convened capabilities across six enabling technology areas: intelligence, data services, immersive and UX, cyber-physical systems, integration, and security, making this emerging technology more accessible and interoperable across sectors and suppliers.[^3] Queen's University is harnessing 5G, Building Information Modelling, Digital Twin technology, and Augmented Reality to support the design and construction of a £100 million, state-of-the-art Advanced Manufacturing Innovation Centre.[^6] Based at Queen's University Belfast, CSIT serves as a global research and innovation hub for cyber security and the UK's Innovation and Knowledge Centre for cyber security research.[^9]

## Implementation Details

The Smart Belfast Urban Innovation Framework, published by Belfast City Council in December 2022, aims to nurture a collaborative environment in which innovators from across industry, academia, and the public sector can work together with citizens and communities to find new ways to address Belfast's major urban and economic challenges.[^1] The framework runs to 2026 and incorporates learning from society's response to the global pandemic and opportunities from the £120 million Belfast Region City Deal digital investments planned for the coming decade.[^7] Belfast Region City Deal and Innovate UK announced a £37.6 million investment in the UK Digital Twin Centre on 01 May 2024.[^2] The UK Digital Twin Centre was delivered by Digital Catapult and funded by the Belfast Region City Deal and Innovate UK, with £15 million of funding from the Belfast Region City Deal helping support core research and the building of skills and capability.[^2] Led by Digital Catapult, the Centre launched with co-investment from three major industry partners: Thales UK, Spirit AeroSystems, and Artemis Technologies.[^2] The Belfast region, led by Belfast City Council, secured £3.8 million wireless innovation funding from the UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology to deliver an exciting range of innovative projects powered by 5G.[^6] Partners in the Belfast 5G Innovation Region include five other councils in the Belfast Region City Deal area: Antrim and Newtownabbey, Ards and North Down, Lisburn and Castlereagh, Mid and East Antrim, and Newry, Mourne and Down.[^6] The Innovation City Belfast partnership includes Belfast Harbour, Belfast Metropolitan College, Catalyst, Queen's University Belfast, Ulster University, and advisory partner Invest Northern Ireland to establish Belfast as a globally significant destination for innovation.[^7] The Belfast Region City Deal will invest £125 million in the Digital Programme which will include the Belfast Region Innovation Challenge Fund, Enabling Infrastructure, and Regional Innovation Hubs.[^8] £40 million of funding in enabling infrastructure will stimulate the conditions for innovation and business growth by targeting investment in digital connectivity, data infrastructure, and other enabling infrastructure to support the Belfast Smart District and wider regional innovation.[^8]

## Benefits and Impacts

The UK Digital Twin Centre project is projected to reach £62 million in GVA for the economy over the next decade.[^3] Belfast's city centre based Smart District, led by Belfast City Council's City Innovation Office as part of their Smart Belfast programme, fosters the conditions for collaborative innovation focused on Belfast's major urban challenges such as climate change, sustainable urban mobility, the future of the high street, and healthy urban neighbourhoods.[^1] The Belfast 5G Innovation Region programme delivered innovative projects powered by advanced wireless technologies, a 5G funding competition for SMEs, and an Enablement Programme to support the advanced wireless ecosystem in the Belfast region.[^7] £270,000 of funding was awarded by the Belfast 5G Innovation Region programme to Manfreight, Retinize, and Stena Line to support them to harness the power of 5G and digital technologies to deliver enhanced operations and business growth.[^6] Stena Line, which transports over 500,000 freight units through Belfast Port for customers across the UK and Ireland each year, is using 5G connectivity, connected cameras, Push-to-Talk, and advanced tracking technologies to deliver more efficient operations at its Belfast Harbour based freight trailer yard by optimising the movement of trailers to and from the ferry.[^6] Logistics company Manfreight is using 5G connectivity, connected cameras, machine learning, scanning, and Push-to-Talk technology to enhance its chilled distribution hub operations at Belfast Harbour by optimising the movement and storage of lorries, trailers, and product across its warehouse facility.[^6] Translink, Northern Ireland's public transport provider, is exploiting advanced wireless connectivity to enable enhanced operations and network planning along its Glider bus routes to further improve services, maximise and optimise resources, and ensure continued development of high quality, sustainable, and attractive Glider services.[^6] Belfast Harbour is harnessing 5G-led innovations to accelerate the digitisation of port operations including automatic bulk freight handling and safety improvements which aligns with their strategic ambition to become the world's best regional Smart Port.[^6]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The Digital Innovation Pillar of the Belfast Region City Deal prioritises resilience and sustainability, with a core commitment to triple bottom line delivery in all of its programs taking into account social, economic, and environmental sustainability factors.[^5] Specific programs within the Belfast Region City Deal will target funding of innovation in energy transition, integrated transport solutions, and building technology in this decade.[^5] Belfast's Smart District will benefit from a proportion of a £34 million mission-orientated Innovation for Societal Impact Fund and £20 million Digital Innovation Venture Fund through the Belfast Region City Deal; these funds aim to grow the region's most innovative industry sectors, support collaborative innovation, and tackle some of the city's most complex urban challenges.[^4]

## Business Analysis

The Centre's first six-month Accelerator Programme, now open for pre-application expressions of interest, will support partnerships between tech SMEs and industry to fast-track digital twin adoption in maritime, aerospace, and defence.[^3] The partners delivered an initial six use cases to demonstrate the potential of digital twins in transforming the maritime, aerospace, and defence sectors, enabled by core investment from Innovate UK into this new world-class Centre and the associated programmes.[^2] Belfast 5GIR worked with Digital Catapult to develop a 5G Adoption Toolkit to support businesses and industries in Northern Ireland to harness 5G to drive innovation and economic growth.[^6] Belfast 5GIR worked with Digital Catapult to deliver an Enablement Programme to foster Northern Ireland's advanced wireless ecosystem through a range of outreach and engagement activities to attract investment, build skills and capacity, share knowledge, and identify opportunities for economic growth.[^6] The Belfast 5G Innovation Region programme, led by Belfast City Council's City Innovation Office, is working with businesses, academia, and the public sector to support the adoption of 5G across the Belfast Region.[^6] Queen's University's 5G Construction use case is harnessing 5G, Building Information Modelling, Digital Twin technology, and Augmented Reality to support the design and construction of a state-of-the-art Factory of the Future at Queen's University's Advanced Manufacturing Innovation Centre.[^7]

## Sources
