# Research Report: Data-Driven Urban Tree Planting and Shade Infrastructure Program

## Solution Overview

Phoenix has developed a comprehensive street tree planting and shade infrastructure program that leverages geographic information systems and data-driven prioritization to combat extreme urban heat.[^1][^6] The initiative strategically targets high-pedestrian corridors and vulnerable neighborhoods through the Cool Corridors program, which aims to establish 100 designated segments by 2030.[^1][^5] This approach combines urban forestry investments with built shade structures to create a network of thermally comfortable pathways for pedestrians, bicyclists, and transit users across the city's districts.[^6]

## Technical Components

City staff and researchers developed a ranking system using four variables to identify optimal tree planting locations: neighborhood heat vulnerability, transit dependency, pedestrian use, and existing shade coverage with temperature measurements.[^6] Cool Corridors are defined as one-quarter to half-mile segments, walkways, or trails adjacent to arterial streets designed to provide relief from high temperatures.[^6] The Street Transportation Department implements the Right Tree, Right Place framework, planting desert-adapted, low water-use species including Mulga, Ghost Gum, Southern Live Oak, Chinese Elm, and Blue Palo Verde.[^6] American Forests provided forestry expertise to select native tree species with greatest potential for long-term survival in a changing climate while maximizing shade benefits.[^7] Phoenix further increases cooling impact using techniques that reflect solar heat, such as cool paving materials.[^7] The Heat Vulnerability Map and Cooling Solutions Webtool provides heat data across site, neighborhood, city, and regional scales, along with vulnerability data for each census tract in Maricopa County.[^8] ASU's Decision Theater created the map interface for this analytical tool.[^8]

## Implementation Details

The Street Transportation Department implemented the Cool Corridors program in 2022 with approval of $1.4 million to plant up to 200 trees per mile, totaling 1,800 new trees across project areas citywide.[^6] The 2025 budget added funding for 1,800 more trees to plant in nine cool corridors, with nine additional corridors planned each year.[^5] The Shade Phoenix Plan commits $50 million in local, federal, and private funds over five years to accelerate shade creation and maintenance.[^3] Phoenix's heat office estimated costs of $17 million for new trees, $15 million for maintenance, $18 million for new built shade, and $2 million for workforce development and programming.[^3] The plan will result in 25,000 new trees and 500 new shade structures, with approximately half going to low- and moderate-income neighborhoods and most of the remainder to middle-income sections.[^3][^4] Much of the federal funding allocated requires spending in low-income areas.[^3] The city is pursuing a large grant from the $1.5 billion dedicated to urban forestry in the Inflation Reduction Act to scale the Tree Equity work.[^7] Corporate partners including Microsoft, American Express, Salesforce, Bank of America, Aspiration, and iHeartMedia have leveraged significant public funds from the city and other philanthropic dollars secured by American Forests.[^7] More than 50 organizations formed a Phoenix Metro Urban Forestry Roundtable, uniting diverse actors from the Mayor's office to local neighborhood organizations to set goals together and partner on every aspect of the work.[^7] The department's program is supported by continuous research, prioritization, and assessment through a partnership with Arizona State University and other partners.[^6]

## Benefits and Impacts

As part of one project, 259 drought-resistant, arid-adapted trees were planted along a corridor to create shade and resilience.[^9] The city chose the Baseline Road corridor based on American Forests' tree equity tool, which ranked the Laveen-area tract's shade 677th out of 965 census block groups, with existing canopy covering only 5% of the area.[^5] When trees planted in this location reach maturity, they may shade up to half of the sidewalk area, according to the city's heat response officer.[^5] American Forests' interactive tool allows users to click on and learn about any neighborhood's shade coverage and rankings.[^5] A study quantified shade coverage on sidewalks in Phoenix and Tempe to identify neighborhoods not meeting the minimum 20% shade coverage recommendation outlined in the Maricopa Association of Government's Active Transportation Plan.[^1] Only 8% of all sidewalks in the Phoenix-Tempe metropolitan area met the recommended minimum of 20% shade coverage at all times during a typical summer day.[^1] The program explicitly focuses on the city's most vulnerable residents and places where people are outside the most.[^3] Phoenix used American Forests' Tree Equity Score as a foundational tool for setting its Tree Equity goal and for prioritizing where to increase tree planting and tree care.[^7] American Forests is building a Tree Equity Score Analyzer tool for Phoenix to provide actionable data down to the granular level of individual properties.[^7]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The summer of 2020 was the hottest on record for Phoenix, with 53 days reaching 110°F or higher and an unprecedented number of heat-related deaths.[^2] The Phoenix City Council passed a motion in 2018 to address urban heat, which led to the formation of the Urban Heat Island/Tree and Shade Subcommittee as part of the Environmental Quality and Sustainability Commission.[^2] The city's Office of Heat Response and Mitigation, which launched in October 2021, inherited a largely ignored 2010 shade plan and was tasked with overhauling it.[^3]

## Business Analysis

Phoenix aims to invest in street trees to reach 25% tree canopy coverage by 2030, becoming the first city to pledge Tree Equity with particular focus on increasing canopy in economically disadvantaged communities.[^1] Phoenix is currently working toward achieving Tree Equity by 2030, making it the first city in the United States to set this public goal.[^7] The city strategically mapped out and focused investment on 100 cool corridors that align with high pedestrian use and low tree canopy.[^7] The Shade Phoenix Plan, first aired before City Council in early June, could be formally adopted before the end of the year and includes 37 specific actions aimed at 13 strategic priorities.[^4] Those priorities were developed by 26 city departments and agencies and include input from some 2,000 residents who commented and made suggestions through meetings, online surveys, and other outreach efforts.[^4] Heat Director Dave Hondula planned to present the final proposal to City Council in the fall.[^3] The city Office of Heat Migration asked all 15 village planning committees to weigh in by September 30 on the plan to spend $50 million over five years to increase shade.[^4] The new plan calls for a citywide shade inventory and re-establishment of a cross-departmental team meant to advance the plan, two components of the 2010 plan that fizzled out.[^3] The city should include a significant allocation for the Cool Corridors program in future General Obligation Bond programs presented to voters, framed as a critical investment in public health, safety, and infrastructure resilience.[^2] The Heat Vulnerability Map tool can be included in federal grant applications to demonstrate how projects ensure Justice40 requirements by investing in marginalized areas to benefit underrepresented groups.[^8] Phoenix's heat office aims to increase shade on city-owned property such as bus stops and parks, but city-owned land only accounts for approximately 8% of Phoenix, requiring private property owner engagement for significant progress.[^3]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://www.phoenix.gov/content/dam/phoenix/oepsite/documents/eqsc/Cool%20Corridors%20Policy%20Recommendations%20for%20Phoenix%20UHITS_2021%20Apr%2008.pdf
- [^3]: https://www.azcentral.com/story/news/local/phoenix/2024/06/12/phoenix-debuts-50-million-plan-to-improve-shade-plant-25k-trees/72746820007/
- [^4]: https://www.ahwatukee.com/news/phoenix-advancing-50-million-shade-program/article_edb5aa24-4de5-11ef-ad59-5b2dc92f7120.html
- [^5]: https://www.azcentral.com/story/news/local/arizona-environment/2022/04/17/beating-heat-phoenix-plants-first-cool-corridor/7308133001/
- [^6]: https://www.phoenix.gov/administration/departments/streets/initiatives/cool-corridors.html
- [^7]: https://www.americanforests.org/article/tree-equity-is-growing-in-phoenix/

# Research Report: Msimbazi River Water Level and Conductivity Monitoring System

## Solution Overview

A water level logger was installed underneath the Selander Bridge at the outfall of the Msimbazi River to measure water levels and conductivity (salinity) for a period of at least one month.[^1][^2][^4] This monitoring technology formed part of a comprehensive survey campaign conducted by the Tanzania Urban Resilience Program, which employed an innovative combination of Light Detection and Range (LiDAR) UAV surveying and bathometric surveying to support flood modelling within the flood-prone Msimbazi river basin.[^5] The approach addressed critical data gaps in understanding flood dynamics in an area experiencing frequent, severe flooding at least twice a year during every rainy season, with nine major flood events taking place in 2019 alone.[^9]

## Technical Components

The water level logger installation prioritized vertical referencing to link measured levels to terrain heights, a consideration deemed very important for accurate flood modeling.[^1] Water level and salinity measurements were executed as part of an integral survey campaign that also included setting out Ground Control Points and Benchmarks, RTK-GNSS River bathymetry profile measurements, UAV (Drone) LiDAR Survey and UAV (Drone) Photogrammetry Survey.[^2] OpenMap Development Tanzania (OMDTZ) teamed with Resilience Academy partner Delft University of Technology (TU Delft) to investigate if a new low-cost, dual-frequency GNSS receiver could be deployed for collecting very precise geographic position data for river cross-section surveys.[^3] OMDTZ deployed two units that use the real-time kinematic (RTK) mode of the new, low-cost hardware that are able to provide centimeter precise surveys.[^3] A team of 6 people (4 mappers and 2 technicians) was deployed to the field equipped with an Android smartphone with Open Data Kit (ODK) installed for data collection of Msimbazi River cross-sections.[^3]

## Implementation Details

The entire survey campaign including the water level logger installation took place from 14th until 20th of February 2019, with only the retrieval of the water level logger done after this period.[^1] The German International Cooperation Agency (GIZ) worked with OpenMap Development Tanzania (OMDTZ) and Dutch knowledge institute Deltares to collect cross-section surveys of the Msimbazi River, extending an existing flood model for the Lower Msimbazi to include a much larger upstream portion of the river.[^3] In collaboration with Deltares and with funding from GIZ, OMDTZ collected accurate and precise river cross-section surveys of the Msimbazi River using an innovative, low-cost technique developed by the Resilience Academy and OMDTZ.[^3] OMDTZ developed a hydrological data collection protocol for ODK with Deltares, and carried out their first field survey with Deltares associates Bobby Russell and Hessel Winsemius, both of whom were experienced in hydrological surveys.[^3] This first field exercise enabled OMDTZ to refine methods and safety precautions, after which the team was able to scale up and rapidly collect over 80 cross-sections of the river.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The OMDTZ survey of river cross-sections and hydraulic structures along the Msimbazi River supported the creation of a comprehensive hydraulic model of the area that has led to the development of a more realistic flood hazard map of the most vulnerable areas.[^3] This improved flood modeling capability addressed critical information gaps in the Msimbazi River Basin, which is located in Dar es Salaam Region of Tanzania, begins in Kisarawe ward, Kisarawe District and eventually drains into Zanzibar Channel at the border of Upanga West ward of Ilala MC and Hananasif ward of Kinondoni MC.[^8] The 35-kilometer river system had been experiencing increasingly severe flooding over the past decade, with major flood events experienced in seven out of the last 10 years.[^8][^9]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This monitoring and surveying approach directly addressed extreme precipitation and flooding hazards in the Msimbazi Basin, where the middle and lower portions had been experiencing frequent, severe flooding at least twice a year during every rainy season.[^9] The comprehensive hydraulic modeling enabled by the water level logger data and associated surveys provided essential information for understanding flood dynamics in this economically important and flood-prone area. The Government of Tanzania, with a $200 million credit from the World Bank Group's International Development Association (IDA), established the Msimbazi Basin Development Project (2022-2028) to strengthen flood resilience and integrate urban development efforts in this area of Dar es Salaam.[^9]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Africa/Dar-es-Salaam-Tanzania/Bathymetric-and-Topographic-Surveying-of-Flood-Prone-Rivers-in-Dar-Es-Salaam-Comparative-Case-of-UAV-Photogrammetry-and-UAV-LiDAR-for-Generation-of-Digital-Terrain-Models-for-Flood-Modelling-Purposes-Msimbazi-River-Dar-es-Salaam.pdf
- [^2]: https://cdr-international.nl/portfolio/tanzania-worldbank-topographic-and-bathymetric-surveying-of-flood-prone-rivers-in-dar-es-salaam/
- [^3]: https://www.hotosm.org/updates/using-low-cost-equipment-to-gather-high-precision-data-for-flood-modeling/
- [^4]: https://documents.worldbank.org/curated/en/310991567574404522/text/Bathymetric-and-Topographic-Surveying-of-Flood-Prone-Rivers-in-Dar-Es-Salaam-Comparative-Case-of-UAV-Photogrammetry-and-UAV-LiDAR-for-Generation-of-Digital-Terrain-Models-for-Flood-Modelling-Purposes-Msimbazi-River-Dar-es-Salaam.txt
- [^5]: https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/video/2019/05/15/drones-helping-to-understand-flood-risk-in-tanzania

- [^7]: https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/532691567573088477/pdf/Development-of-a-Hydrological-and-Hydrodynamic-Model-of-the-Msimbazi-Basin-Inception-Report.pdf
- [^8]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Msimbazi_River

# Research Report: Storm Alert System with Real-Time Precipitation Monitoring

## Solution Overview

Buenos Aires developed a Storm Alert System (SAT) as part of its Hydraulic Plan to anticipate accurately and in real time the arrival of storms, southeast blows, and different extreme climate phenomena.[^4] The system predicts with 90 to 120 minutes of anticipation which zones of Buenos Aires City will be most affected by storms, with a precision of 4 kilometers.[^1] This approach was designed, installed, and became operational by December 31, 2020, with the goal of providing early warning access to 3,000,000 people.[^6]

## Technical Components

The infrastructure incorporates a weather radar and 34 automatic meteorological and hydrological stations distributed across the City and AMBA (Buenos Aires Metropolitan Area).[^4][^5] Among these stations, 13 are automatic hydrological stations monitoring different points of the Vega, White, Ugarteche, Medrano, Radio Antiguo, Elías Iguazú Erézcano, Ochoa, San Pedrito streams and the Maldonado relief channel.[^1] Seven stations are both meteorological and hydrological, located on the Maldonado and Cildañez streams, while four additional meteorological stations operate in the neighborhoods of Colegiales, Parque Centenario, Parque Avellaneda, and Pompeya.[^1] In Buenos Aires Province, seven automatic meteorological stations function in the localities of Escobar, San Fernando, Campo de Mayo, Palomar, Morón, Lanús, and Ezeiza, plus one meteorological and mareological station in Magdalena.[^1] All stations have solar panels and batteries that give them autonomy in case of possible power outages.[^3] The stations can measure the speed, flow rate, and water level in the streams of the City and the water level in the Río de la Plata.[^3] A satellite reception antenna receives information captured by the American meteorological satellite GOES-R, launched in 2016, which offers storm forecasts, real-time lightning activity mapping, and tornado alerts.[^1][^3] All information from the radar, satellite, and stations converges at the CUCC (Centro Único de Coordinación y Control) located in the Chacarita neighborhood, with data arriving through fiber optic connections and radio links to avoid cuts and interruptions.[^1][^3] At the CUCC, a team of 15 professionals analyzes and processes the data with software that allows cross-referencing with other information sources, including international prediction models, maps from the City's Geographic Information Systems Unit, and data from the National Meteorological Service and Naval Hydrography Service.[^1]

## Implementation Details

The monitoring system measures changes in rainfall, temperature, humidity, wind direction and speed, and stream water level to generate community alerts and inform real-time emergency decisions.[^2] The implementation involved installing 30 information collection points, with more sensors under development as of the reporting period.[^2] The system creates a real-time information repository shared with AMBA's emergency teams.[^4] Implementation actions included entering into agreements with telephony companies to generate warnings through the UMTS and with Google to include geolocation of individuals and warning agents.[^4] The Early Warning System was under implementation and was expected to be completed by June 2019.[^6] Component 1: Institutional Development for Flood Risk Management had a cost of $10.00 million.[^6] The broader project, which included the early warning system as part of flood risk management efforts, had a total cost of US$326 million, with US$200 million coming from a World Bank loan and the remaining US$125 million being financed by the Buenos Aires city government.[^8] The project is part of the city's hydraulic master plan, which outlines a series of flood-mitigation actions until 2050 to reduce the risks and effects of floods in the city.[^8]

## Benefits and Impacts

The monitoring system enables the city to automatically operate pumping stations and floodgates, issue warnings to key institutions and the public, and send cleaning crews to compromised drains.[^2] The approach allows Buenos Aires to analyze and evaluate storm predictions over the City, warns about probable floods, and improves emergency situation management.[^5] Data derived from sensors along the Medrano Stream helps the city make important planning and emergency response decisions.[^2] The hydrometeorological monitoring system provides a precise, real-time evaluation of the comprehensive state of the stormwater system.[^5] The monitoring system protects approximately 6 million people, including residents and daily commuters.[^2] By reducing the impact of flooding due to rain and providing drainage infrastructure, the project was expected to benefit some 3 million people.[^8] This implementation addresses critical needs following April 2013, when Buenos Aires suffered a record rainfall causing more than $300 million in damages.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This system directly addresses extreme precipitation and flooding hazards by providing 90 to 120 minutes of advance warning with 4-kilometer precision for storm impacts.[^1] The technology enables pre-emptive emergency management by predicting and monitoring weather phenomena, allowing the city to automatically operate flood control infrastructure and deploy resources before storms arrive.[^2] The approach improves resilience to southeast blows and different extreme climate phenomena that threaten the metropolitan area.[^4]

## Business Analysis

The World Bank Board approved a US$200 million loan for the project to strengthen flood risk management in Buenos Aires and improve drainage systems in the city's Cildáñez, Vega and Maldonado river basins.[^8] The financing structure combined international development funding with local government investment, with the Buenos Aires city government contributing US$125 million to the US$326 million total project cost.[^8] The institutional development component specifically allocated $10.00 million for flood risk management capacity building.[^6] The broader national context demonstrates significant public investment in meteorological infrastructure, with the SINARAME project demanding a total investment of 6,400 million pesos since 2011.[^7] INVAP, based in Bariloche, was contracted for the construction and installation of radar systems, with the National Meteorological Service serving as the main user for the elaboration of immediate alerts based on radar information.[^7] Since 2019, the Ministry of Public Works has contracted INVAP to carry out the operation and maintenance of the SINARAME radars and computer equipment.[^7]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.clarin.com/ciudades/funcionara-sistema-predecira-tormentas-90-minutos-anticipacion_0_HJIFOjb0G.html
- [^2]: https://www.c40.org/case-studies/cities100-buenos-aires-monitoring-climate-data-for-flood-prevention/
- [^3]: http://buenosaires.gob.ar/desarrollo-urbano-y-transporte/noticias/el-sistema-de-alerta-de-tormentas-sat-una-herramienta
- [^4]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/Latin America & Caribbean/BuenosAires-Argentina/Buenos-Aires-Resilience-Strategy-English.pdf
- [^5]: http://buenosaires.gob.ar/cambioclimatico/preparados-para-las-tormentas
- [^6]: https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/783991590670922154/pdf/Disclosable-Version-of-the-ISR-AR-Flood-Risk-Management-Support-Project-for-the-City-of-Buenos-Aires-P145686-Sequence-No-10.pdf
- [^7]: https://www.invap.com.ar/en/the-installation-of-new-weather-radars-to-monitor-storms-in-the-country-is-moving-forward/
- [^8]: https://www.bnamericas.com/en/news/world-bank-pledges-us200mn-for-argentina-flood-risk-project

# Research Report: Crowdsourced Property Condition Survey Using Street-Level Photography

## Solution Overview

New Orleans developed a rapid property condition survey methodology that captures street-level photography using car-mounted cameras and engages community members to evaluate each parcel through a crowdsourcing application.[^3][^7] The system was developed as the city approached the 10th anniversary of Hurricane Katrina, inspired by Intel's Code for Good commitment to the White House Climate Data Initiative.[^6][^2] This approach addresses the urgent need for comprehensive parcel data after the USPS deleted nearly every blighted home from its address count, leaving the city without reliable indicators of blight and increasing the necessity for a process to track the status of every property with regularity.[^9]

## Technical Components

Staff drove every street of the city capturing photos of each building with two consumer grade cameras with wide angle lenses purchased from the local tech store, equipped with an integrated GPS that tagged the location to each photo taken.[^5] Photos are taken in one-second intervals, with the speed of the vehicle regulating the number of images in which a property appears.[^6] The city uploaded the building pictures to a data layer and joined them to corresponding parcel location data.[^5] Municipal authorities have come up with innovative ideas for photo shoots, such as attaching cameras to firetrucks or street cleaners.[^6] Esri created the Photo Survey app for New Orleans, which any local government can use to publish street-level photo collections.[^5] The simple 10-second survey asks six questions: 1) Is there a structure on the property? 2) Is the lot overgrown? 3) Is there apparent damage to the siding or walls? 4) Is there apparent damage to the windows or doors? 5) Is there damage to the roof? 6) What's the foundation type?[^5] The system is designed to collect data about the status of the city's 150,000 properties by showing building photos and asking survey questions about property condition.[^5] Scoring of properties is randomized across the city, reducing the likelihood of bias where stakeholders in one neighborhood might score properties on a different threshold than stakeholders in a different neighborhood.[^2]

## Implementation Details

The city did not have the resources to actually complete the survey for these properties, so it asked Esri to build a crowdsourcing solution.[^5] Photo Survey began with Esri's commitment to the White House's agenda to combat climate change, according to Chris Buscaglia, Esri local government solutions manager.[^6] Much of the initiative came from New Orleans officials, such as Greg Hymel, enterprise GIS manager.[^6] Esri provided geospatial technology and expertise to 12 cities to build maps and apps that the communities can use to plan for the impacts of climate change, including New Orleans, Louisiana.[^1] The methodology allows boots-on-the-ground post-disaster to be limited to 2 or less staff for 2 weeks for a city the size of New Orleans by driving the camera-on-car on every street.[^2] Volunteers can be anywhere at any time, and scoring of each property takes less time than sidewalk surveys.[^2] Locals might still do the majority of the crowdsourced scoring for baseline data, but in the event of a catastrophic event, e-volunteers from around the world can join in.[^2] The system is cost-effective enough to be repeated frequently, providing baseline data for normal planning and resource allocation, and supporting rapid-rebound and strategic recovery after shocks.[^2] On the city side of the app, managers have a dashboard with a gauge showing the number of surveys completed, bar charts showing the number of problem types reported such as damaged roofs, and color-coded dots indicating parcels that have been surveyed.[^5]

## Benefits and Impacts

This approach is designed to cut down on the amount of time cities and volunteers spend surveying neighborhoods and documenting conditions such as graffiti and rundown buildings.[^6] The crowdsourcing photo-survey tool helps ensure that the burden of data collection post-disaster will never again fall squarely on citizens who are already struggling to rebuild their homes and neighborhoods.[^4] After a disaster, the imagery can be quickly collected and used to rapidly assess recovery needs and support services, such as mobile first aid clinics, food and water distribution, and temporary housing.[^3] During disaster recovery, a citywide property condition census conducted at regular intervals can provide crucial progress updates and be used to strategically deploy public services and case management.[^3] Having comprehensive pre-storm data can facilitate more efficient reimbursement from FEMA and private insurers.[^3] Outside of crises, the property condition data can be used to develop strategies for risk reduction, code enforcement, and reuse of vacant property.[^3] The Office of Performance and Accountability, which oversees BlightStat with Oliver Wise serving as director, tracked 7,671 blighted property addresses remediated through city intervention since 2010, which reduced to fewer than 7,200 unique addresses once duplicates were eliminated.[^8] As of March 2012, there were an estimated 35,700 blighted homes and empty lots in New Orleans, down from 43,755 in September 2010.[^9]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This rapid assessment system directly addresses climate adaptation needs by enabling efficient post-disaster property condition evaluation and recovery planning. The methodology supports both baseline risk assessment for normal planning and rapid response capabilities after climate-related shocks, providing the data infrastructure necessary for strategic recovery and resource allocation following extreme weather events.

## Business Analysis

After Hurricane Katrina, government agencies, universities, nonprofits, and neighborhood associations struggled to survey the city parcel-by-parcel, with collection methods that varied, data that didn't integrate with each other or municipal data sets, and most imagery and data not shared with the public.[^2] Neighborhood residents and legions of volunteers organized field data-collection efforts to document the condition of storm-damaged buildings.[^4] Only a massive operation that goes door-to-door to inspect every parcel in the city can provide an accurate count of the number of blighted properties in New Orleans.[^9] The city will need to begin coordinating citywide data collection efforts to track blight going forward, as USPS data will provide less reliable indicators over time.[^9] The crowdsourcing model addresses resource constraints by enabling distributed volunteer participation while maintaining data quality through randomized scoring protocols, creating a scalable approach that can be replicated in other municipalities facing similar post-disaster assessment challenges.

## Sources

- [^4]: https://medium.com/@ObamaWhiteHouse/ten-years-after-katrina-new-orleans-recovery-and-what-data-had-to-do-with-it-3df0bb2467e9
- [^5]: https://www.esri.com/about/newsroom/insider/crowd-sourcing-apps-whittle-away-at-huge-data-projects

# Research Report: Meteorological Risk Dashboard for Emergency Response Alerts

## Solution Overview

Montréal established a meteorological risk dashboard as part of its climate resilience infrastructure, representing the first such implementation by a Canadian city participating in the 100 Resilient Cities program.[^1][^2] This software application tracks forecasts and probable impact thresholds online while issuing alerts to emergency responders.[^2] The initiative emerged from the city's participation in the Rockefeller Foundation's global resilience program, which began in 2013 and led to Montréal's induction as the first Canadian participant in 2014.[^1]

## Technical Components

The dashboard operates as a software application designed to monitor meteorological forecasts and establish probable impact thresholds for tracking purposes.[^2] This system enables online tracking capabilities and automated alert distribution to emergency response personnel.[^2] The technical infrastructure supports multi-risk monitoring across the city's geographic area.[^2]

## Implementation Details

The project originated when the Rockefeller Foundation launched funding for the Resilient Cities program in 2013, with Montréal selected from over 300 applicants and inducted as the first Canadian city in 2014.[^1] The city used the awarded funds to create an Office of Resilience (Bureau de la Résilience) and hire a Chief Resilience Officer.[^1] A Bureau de la résilience pilot project was already underway to develop the meteorological risk dashboard.[^2][^3] The implementation follows a medium-term time horizon and represents Action 26 to establish multi-risk monitoring in Montréal.[^2] Principal partners include multiple Ville de Montréal departments (Bureau de la résilience, Centre de sécurité civile, Ville intelligente et numérique, Service des technologies de l'information, Centre de gestion de la mobilité urbaine, Service de police de la Ville de Montréal, Service de sécurité incendie de Montréal, Service de l'environnement), Direction de la santé publique, Environment and Climate Change Canada, Wood, 100 Resilient Cities, citizens, and others to be determined.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

The system provides emergency responders with automated alerts based on meteorological forecast thresholds.[^2] This approach enables proactive response coordination by tracking probable impact levels online.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Montréal's participation in the 100 Resilient Cities program specifically targeted increasing resilience to extreme weather hazards.[^1] The meteorological risk dashboard addresses this vulnerability by providing real-time monitoring and alert capabilities for weather-related threats.[^2]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://changingclimate.ca/map/montreals-resilient-city-strategy/

- [^3]: https://www.wri.org/update/new-data-dashboard-shows-climate-change-risks-in-cities

# Research Report: GIS-Based Urban Tree Canopy Mapping for Heat Mitigation

## Solution Overview

Fort Lauderdale will use GIS mapping and other tools to evaluate shade and cooling available at pedestrian, transit (train and bus) and bicycle facilities, and assess whether the urban tree canopy is sufficient to protect people from heat and pollution.[^5] This approach builds on Broward County's broader initiative to map and monitor the percentage of tree canopy county-wide, establishing a baseline that enables urban foresters to evaluate the present extent of tree canopy and monitor changes over time.[^4] The methodology provides critical data for addressing urban heat islands, which increase maximum temperatures by as much as 5°F above the local baseline in Broward County.[^6]

## Technical Components

Broward County created a series of maps using GIS software that showed the 32 cities within Broward County and their percentage of tree canopy, amount of green space and other indicators of a healthy urban environment to identify the areas with the lowest tree canopy.[^1] The County completed the latest survey of the urban tree canopy coverage in 2018, using Geographical Information Systems (GIS).[^3] The imagery used for the tree canopy project were color infrared scanned photographs taken in January 1996, and it was decided that the 1996 imagery would be mapped and classified as a baseline for tree canopy in Broward County.[^4] In a pilot test, digital image processing techniques took 4 hours to create a coverage for a one-mile section, compared to approximately 4 weeks for one staff member to digitize tree polygons manually in the same area.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The Broward County Board of Commissioners established the Broward Urban Forest Initiative in 1999 to stem the loss of tree canopy from Broward's urban landscape.[^4] The tree canopy survey provides the technical basis for the County's Urban Forest Management Plan.[^3] Broward County will perform a tree canopy study every five years as part of efforts to reduce urban heat island effect.[^2] The County will work with community and governmental partners to identify needs and involve community members in the policy and planning process for urban tree canopy enhancement.[^2] The survey will be used to develop targets for future tree canopy by the various municipalities who are trying to develop achievable goals, and to evaluate achievable canopy coverage for various zoning and land use categories.[^3] The County will establish support for tree maintenance to encourage long-term canopy growth and tree retention as part of the urban tree canopy enhancement initiative.[^2]

## Benefits and Impacts

The analysis found that certain communities in central, inland Broward County had less tree canopy than the rest of Broward County, and these cities also had some of the lowest income per capita in the region.[^1] Urban vegetation offers shade and evapotranspiration, which can lower peak summer temperatures 2-9°F.[^6] In Broward County, 90,000 people live in neighborhoods where the urban heat islands increase temperatures by 2°F or more and where average per capita incomes are less than 200% the federal poverty level.[^6] Additionally, 33,000 people live in areas that meet the above criteria and have a high percentage of population in high risk age groups (under age 14 or over age 65).[^6] One-third of the 90,000 Broward County residents living in higher-temperature neighborhoods are at higher risk to heat health impacts due to their age.[^2] The County aims to increase the urban tree canopy to at least 40% county-wide as part of Action 30 to reduce urban heat island effect.[^2] The initiative will enhance the tree canopy equitably countywide.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Broward County experiences 74 days above 88°F per year, on average.[^6] The number of days above 88°F has increased 36% since 2000, to 90 days per year, with projections suggesting 144 days per year by 2050.[^2] In the County's most severe urban heat islands, all-cause mortality rates are estimated to increase by 4.7% (2.4% - 7%) when baseline temperatures exceed 90°.[^6] The urban heat model estimated that 13 to 44 lives are lost in the County annually due to excessive heat.[^2] On the current trajectory, Broward County will experience 144 days above the temperature threshold by 2050, and 223 days above the threshold by 2100.[^6]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://blog.nwf.org/2019/08/tree-equity-in-broward-county/
- [^2]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Broward-US/CCAP_2020_ADA.pdf
- [^3]: https://www.broward.org/NaturalResources/LandStewardship/UrbanForest/Pages/TreeCanopyCoverage.aspx
- [^4]: https://proceedings.esri.com/library/userconf/proc01/professional/papers/pap273/p273.htm
- [^5]: https://southeastfloridaclimatecompact.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/02/City-of-Fort-Lauderdale_2040-Comprehensive-Plan.pdf
- [^6]: https://www.broward.org/resilience/Maps/Pages/heat.aspx

# Research Report: Grid-Independent Solar EV Charging with Battery Backup

## Solution Overview

Pittsburgh has developed a network of resilient electric vehicle charging stations that operate independently from the electrical grid through solar generation and battery backup systems.[^4] This approach ensures that residents maintain access to vehicle and device charging during grid failures while supporting the city's broader vehicle electrification goals.[^4] The infrastructure addresses the challenge that Pittsburgh neighborhoods typically lack driveways and garages, making home charging prohibitive for many city residents.[^4]

## Technical Components

Each EV charging location consists of 2 dual-port Level 2 charging stations, which can accommodate up to 4 vehicles to charge at once.[^1] The City of Pittsburgh acquired 5 EV ARC™ solar standalone charging stations that are not connected to the grid and serve as a source of emergency power backup, supporting neighborhood resilience goals.[^2] The EV ARC™ solar standalone charging station enabled the City to provide easy and quick to install renewable power, in a way that visually and functionally met the needs of the neighborhood.[^2] Additionally, the City and the Parking Authority will install 25 Level 2 charging stations on a DC microgrid.[^3] These charging stations receive power through a solar canopy tied into the local district energy microgrid.[^3] One of Beam Global's deployment experts can set up an EV ARC in as little as a few minutes.[^5]

## Implementation Details

The City of Pittsburgh was awarded federal grant funding from Round 1B of the Charging and Fueling Infrastructure Grant Program to install publicly accessible electric vehicle charging stations.[^1] Pittsburgh's CFI Grant enables Phase 1 installation of 4 community park sites with 16 ports and 2 community parking facility sites with 16 ports.[^1] Pittsburgh's CFI Grant enables Phase 2 installation of 3 community parking facility sites with 20 ports and 12 community curbside sites with 48 ports.[^1] The City is leading the effort to install 12 public curbside chargers where the private sector typically doesn't invest.[^1] Pittsburgh's EV charging station locations were selected based on federal guidelines that focused on equity and environmental justice census block data as well as existing electrical capacities.[^1] Key requirements of the grant program include ensuring 24/7 public accessibility, supporting environmental justice with a focus on Justice40 communities, and providing accessibility for individuals with disabilities.[^1] The City of Pittsburgh is working closely with Duquesne Light Company's Community, Fleet, & Transit Charging Program to determine the most cost-effective method to install and energize each charging station.[^1] The Pittsburgh Parking Authority evaluated 22 lots where new chargers could fill network gaps and serve residents in need.[^1] The Pittsburgh Parking Authority manages 34 surface lots and has already installed 35 public chargers in city garages.[^1] The charging stations will be open to the public during the day and will recharge the City of Pittsburgh's Department of Permits, Licenses, and Inspections vehicles at night.[^3] In 2021, the City published the Public Facilities EV Charging Strategic Plan, creating the mission for public EV charging throughout Pittsburgh.[^1] The City of Pittsburgh Office of Management and Budget put out a Request for Proposal for a Mobile Electric Charging Station in conjunction with the Alternative Fuels Incentive Grant Program.[^2] The City, U.S. Department of Energy, and the National Energy Technology Lab signed a historic agreement to research, develop and deploy district energy and microgrid systems throughout Pittsburgh.[^3] Beam Global's EV ARC systems start at $59,400.[^5] Beam Global says its customers' savings on construction and electric work exceeds the cost of the EV ARC in almost every instance.[^5] Over the past 10 years, Beam Global has deployed hundreds of EV ARCs.[^5] The City has a $5 million budget to purchase 10 electric motorcycles, 81 electric sedans, 14 electric medium-duty SUVs and 107 Level 2 charging stations over a three-year period beginning in 2017.[^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The system builds resilience and functions even if the alternating current grid goes down.[^3] As an EPA air quality nonattainment zone, vehicle electrification comes with the very important co-benefit of improving local air quality by reducing tailpipe emissions.[^4] The Hubs could be utilized to improve the ease of commuting via less carbon intensive transit modes, as a place to purchase bus tickets, post City transit option maps, and connect people to car, van and bike pools.[^4] Using the Resilient EV Charging Hubs as a reporting station for idling or other air quality violations would enable residents to make the connection between the carbon reduction benefits of electrification and their direct health.[^4] The kiosks can provide space for advertising to offset the cost of energy and maintenance.[^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Neighborhood charging stations operated with off-grid solar generation and battery backup provide a resilient hub for residents to gather and charge devices or vehicles in times of grid failure.[^4] The charging infrastructure is not connected to the grid so is a source of emergency power backup, supporting neighborhood resilience goals.[^2] In addition to providing clean energy, the system will build resilience and function even if the alternating current grid goes down.[^3]

## Business Analysis

Beam Global's EV ARC systems start at $59,400, and in almost every instance, the company says its customers' savings on construction and electric work exceeds the cost of the EV ARC.[^5] The kiosks can provide space for advertising to offset the cost of energy and maintenance.[^4] The City of Pittsburgh was awarded federal grant funding from Round 1B of the Charging and Fueling Infrastructure Grant Program to install publicly accessible electric vehicle charging stations.[^1] Key requirements of the grant program include ensuring 24/7 public accessibility, supporting environmental justice with a focus on Justice40 communities, and providing accessibility for individuals with disabilities.[^1] The City has a $5 million budget to purchase 10 electric motorcycles, 81 electric sedans, 14 electric medium-duty SUVs and 107 Level 2 charging stations over a three-year period beginning in 2017.[^3] Over the past 10 years, Beam Global has deployed hundreds of EV ARCs, which represent a growing list of incredibly compelling use cases, including otherwise impossible charging locations.[^5]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://the-atlas.com/projects/pittsburgh-electric-fleet
- [^3]: https://smartpittsburgh.org/programs/electric-avenue
- [^4]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Pittsburgh-US/Pittsburgh_Climate_Action_Plan_3.0.pdf
- [^5]: https://chargedevs.com/features/solar-powered-off-grid-ev-charging-stations-offer-surprisingly-attractive-cost-advantages/

# Research Report: Automated Fleet Fuel Tracking and Vehicle Data Management System

## Solution Overview

The Department of Citywide Administrative Services (DCAS) expanded automated fuel tracking technology across New York City's municipal fleet operations, building on successful pilot programs in the Parks and Police departments.[^1] This approach enables comprehensive monitoring of fuel consumption and vehicle deployment patterns across approximately 200 general use fueling sites citywide.[^1] The implementation represented a systematic effort to optimize fleet management through real-time data collection and analysis capabilities.

## Technical Components

The city deployed EJ Ward's CANceiver fuel management and vehicle data system as the core technology platform for this initiative.[^1] The system was installed across all 200 general use fueling sites and on individual vehicles throughout the municipal fleet.[^1] The technology captures automated fuel consumption data and vehicle operational information, providing fleet managers with detailed insights into usage patterns across different city agencies.

## Implementation Details

DCAS completed installations for all city agencies except the Department of Sanitation (DSNY), with DSNY installations scheduled for completion by the end of 2015.[^2][^3] The Parks and Police departments had already been using automated fuel tracking prior to the citywide expansion, serving as pilot implementations that informed the broader rollout.[^1] The deployment encompassed all 200 general use fueling sites across the city's fleet operations, representing a comprehensive infrastructure upgrade.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

Fleet staff gained enhanced capacity to study fuel consumption patterns and optimize vehicle deployment strategies across city operations.[^1] The Parks and Police departments demonstrated the system's potential for improving operational efficiency through data-driven fleet management decisions.[^1] The technology provided municipal agencies with detailed analytics capabilities to identify opportunities for reducing fuel waste and improving resource allocation across diverse vehicle operations.

## Sources

- [^2]: https://comptroller.nyc.gov/reports/ten-years-after-sandy/
- [^3]: https://www.nyc.gov/assets/sirr/downloads/pdf/Ch18_Southern_Manhattan_FINAL_singles.pdf

# Research Report: Cooling Tower Water Softening and Disinfectant System

## Solution Overview

Engineers at Phoenix Sky Harbor International Airport developed an innovative cooling tower water treatment system that combines water softening technology with an environmentally friendly disinfectant generator.[^2][^3] This approach addresses one of the Southwest's most significant water consumption challenges, as cooling towers can account for up to half of all water use in some commercial buildings in the region.[^1][^4] The pilot project demonstrated that softening the make-up water increases the cycles of concentration and reduces water use by 20%.[^4][^5][^6]

## Technical Components

The system integrates a water softening mechanism with an environmentally friendly disinfectant generator installed on cooling towers at Terminal 3, Terminal 4, and the Rental Car Center.[^2][^3] This combination reduces the amount of chemicals needed to keep pipes clean and increases the number of times water can cycle through the towers before being discarded.[^1] A principal factor of this technology is that it can be scaled up or down in size to accommodate almost any size cooling tower.[^4] The water meter providing make-up water for Terminal 4's cooling towers ranks as one of Phoenix's highest volume water meters, highlighting the scale of water consumption at these facilities.[^4]

## Implementation Details

The technology was pioneered at Phoenix Sky Harbor International Airport through ingenuity and experimentation, with engineers achieving dramatic results.[^2][^3] The project was so successful that the systems became a standard central plant design, with similar installations completed at the Terminal 3 Central Plant and Rental Car Center Central Plant.[^4] This cooling water treatment system was also installed as part of the Terminal Modernization Project, and a system was being installed in the Rental Car Center.[^4] The successful implementation at the airport became the basis for a broader grant program when Mayor Kate Gallego and the Phoenix City Council members voted unanimously to make funding available to companies interested in using leading-edge technology to save water and protect the environment through the new 'Blue Bank' partnership.[^2] BlueCommons and BWS provide loans to qualified companies that want to incorporate cooling tower projects in their own buildings and campuses.[^2][^3]

## Benefits and Impacts

The Cooling Tower System Upgrade was successfully completed, reducing water use by 20%.[^4] The large-scale cooling tower water treatment technology at Phoenix Sky Harbor International Airport has saved 31 million gallons of water at one of the terminals since implementation more than three years ago.[^1] Initial estimates inferred that the water savings would be 10 million gallons per year.[^4] More than two years after the project was concluded, results showed more than 21 million gallons of water and thousands of pounds of chemicals saved in 2019.[^4] The cost savings of these upgrades are achieved by the elimination of purchasing biocide chemicals, reduced water use, and increased system life.[^4] Relatively simple upgrades to existing cooling systems can substantially increase the number of cycles during which the same water can be used in cooling.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This technology directly addresses water scarcity challenges intensified by climate change in the Southwest, where cooling demands place enormous pressure on limited water resources. By enabling cooling towers to operate with significantly less water consumption, the system enhances Phoenix's resilience to drought conditions and reduces vulnerability to water supply disruptions. The scalability of the approach allows for widespread adoption across commercial buildings, multiplying water conservation benefits throughout the urban environment.

## Business Analysis

The highly successful large-scale cooling tower water treatment technology pioneered at Phoenix Sky Harbor International Airport serves as the basis for the grant program.[^2][^3] The Blue Bank partnership provides a financial mechanism for scaling the technology, with BlueCommons and BWS offering loans to qualified companies that want to incorporate cooling tower projects in their buildings and campuses.[^2][^3] The business model demonstrates strong financial viability through multiple revenue streams, including elimination of biocide chemical purchases, reduced water utility costs, and increased system life.[^4] The technology's ability to be scaled up or down to accommodate almost any size cooling tower expands the addressable market across diverse commercial building types.[^4] Municipal support through unanimous City Council approval signals strong policy backing for water conservation investments, creating a favorable regulatory environment for adoption.[^2]

## Sources

- [^1]: https://www.aztechcouncil.org/phoenix-businesses-can-receive-loans-to-conserve-water-in-their-buildings/
- [^2]: https://www.amwua.org/blog/phoenixs-blue-bank-partnership-fosters-innovation-in-water-conservation
- [^3]: https://inbusinessphx.com/government-compliance/mayor-gallego-announces-blue-bank-to-foster-innovation-in-water-conservation
- [^4]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/resilience.urbantech.info/data/North America/Phoenix-US/Phoenix-2021ClimateActionPlanEnglish.pdf
- [^5]: https://airport.ifma.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/Day3_04_PHX_Central-Plant-20230505-Ellen-Crews.pdf
- [^6]: https://www.skyharbor.com/media/di5fx30h/phxsustainabilityreport_external.pdf

# Research Report: IoT-Based Elderly Monitoring System for Isolated Death Prevention

## Solution Overview

Seoul operates an IoT-based monitoring system that installs environmental sensors and smart devices in the homes of elderly residents living alone to detect emergencies and prevent isolated deaths.[^1][^2][^3] The system analyzes real-time data on daily activities, temperature, humidity, and motion patterns to identify dangerous situations and trigger immediate welfare responses.[^4][^5][^6] This approach addresses the vulnerability of isolated senior citizens through continuous remote monitoring combined with rapid intervention protocols.

## Technical Components

The monitoring infrastructure consists of environmental data-collecting sensors that detect motion, temperature, humidity, and luminous intensity installed in the residences of vulnerable senior citizens.[^3][^7] In the IoT care system, sensor devices are installed in the home of a senior who lives alone, and the information gathered from these sensors is sent in real time to relevant offices and officials.[^8] The data gathered from households are monitored in real-time on screens at autonomous and metropolitan institutions as well as mobile devices of life supporters in charge.[^9] Since late 2020, Seoul has been operating a smart plug program under which electricity usage by those who live alone is monitored through internet connections to their televisions, microwaves, or other frequently-used home appliances.[^10] The program involves installation of a monitoring device plugged into an electrical outlet that measures the amount of electricity consumed by connected home appliances.[^11] The system also incorporates companion robot dogs that keep company with elderly persons living alone and call 911 if an emergency occurs.[^12]

## Implementation Details

The Seoul Metropolitan Government launched the IoT service for 2,800 households in 2018 to better care for senior citizens living alone and in need of constant attention and care.[^13] The number of Seoul households with the IoT-enabled electronic monitoring system increased to 5,000 in 2019.[^14] The city planned to install IoT sensors in the homes of 10,000 elderly people living alone by the end of 2019.[^15] Seoul expanded distribution of IoT devices annually, installing and supporting 12,500 devices by 2022 through the Safety/Health Care Solution for Senior Citizens Living Alone project.[^16] The Seoul Metropolitan Government broadened the number of beneficiaries of the safety management solution project for vulnerable senior citizens to 10,000 households by 2020.[^17] The number of beneficiaries of the IoT care service gradually increased to 12,500 households by 2022.[^18] The Seoul City Government established the smart plug program in October 2021 to address the growing number of elderly people living by themselves.[^19] During its initial rollout seven months after October 2021, a little over 2,700 households were enrolled into the smart plug program.[^20] Another 2,000 single-person households in Seoul were added to the smart plug program.[^21] For the expanded smart plug program, the Seoul City Government selected vulnerable households in 25 districts with elderly living alone given priority.[^22] On March 6, 2024, the Seoul Metropolitan Government announced plans to incorporate cutting-edge technology such as AI, robots, and IoT devices into its elderly care services in response to the super-aged society era.[^23] Beneficiaries are elderly citizens who need safety monitoring due to isolation, depression, and old age among the beneficiaries of customized care services for senior citizens.[^24]

## Benefits and Impacts

Under the IoT care service, the Seoul Metropolitan Government took preemptive measures by detecting a total of 135 emergency cases during the period from January 2018 to July 2020.[^25] A total of 135 dangerous situations were detected through the project and preemptive actions were taken.[^26] During the period from January 2018 to July 2020, there were no elderly solitary deaths reported when a dead body is discovered more than 72 hours after the presumed time of death under the IoT care service.[^27] There has been not a single lonely death in houses supported by the care service through IoT devices since the project launched.[^28] Municipal officials rescued many lone and diseased elders suffering from exhaustion and other disorders thanks to the electronic monitoring system.[^29] A total of 154 emergency cases were detected and taken care of during the monitoring period, with most cases involving either people in poor health because of alcohol abuse, those with restricted movement in need of aid, or those who cannot feed themselves.[^30] The data on temperature and humidity detected by IoT devices led to amelioration of housing environments of elderly living alone, such as installation of insect-proof nets in doorways, using resources from the local community.[^31] When indoor temperature is higher than 32℃, life supporters educate the elderly about guidelines for coping with heatwaves, provide items to keep cool, and improve humid housing environments of the elderly through association with local social resources using objective data.[^32]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The system directly addresses climate-related health risks by monitoring extreme indoor temperatures and humidity levels that threaten vulnerable elderly populations during heatwaves.[^33][^34] When abnormal changes in temperature, humidity, or light are detected, welfare officials take emergency measures such as calling the home or sending 119 rescue squads for assistance.[^35][^36] This capability enables rapid response to heat-related emergencies and prevents heat-related mortality among isolated senior citizens during extreme weather events.

## Business Analysis

The Seoul Metropolitan Government operates this safety management solution project for vulnerable senior citizens as a municipal welfare service.[^37] Currently, 16,000 households in the capital are in welfare blind spots.[^38] About 1.6 million single households in South Korea are aged 65 or older.[^39] Some 43% of people who died of a lonely death in 2019 were elderly.[^40] The system addresses this growing demographic challenge through systematic expansion, with the government distributing safety management devices to elderly citizens living alone to monitor their health issues.[^41] The initiative operates through coordination between metropolitan and autonomous institutions with life supporters and caseworkers providing direct response services.[^42] When no movement is detected over a certain period of time or if abnormal signs are detected in terms of temperature, humidity, or luminous intensity, the caseworker in charge immediately contacts and visits the house and takes emergency measures such as calling 119.[^43] In the event of a sudden drop in a person's electricity use, the city's social welfare officials get alerts to immediately inquire after or visit the person to ensure the person's safety.[^44]

## Sources

# Research Report: RiskScape Multi-Hazard Infrastructure Resilience Assessment Platform

## Solution Overview

This integrated modeling approach combines multi-hazard risk assessment with economic impact analysis to evaluate infrastructure resilience investments for major seismic events in Wellington.[^3][^5] The system links physical damage predictions with service outage mapping and socio-economic consequence modeling to quantify the benefits of infrastructure resilience improvements.[^3][^5] An economic impact assessment was carried out for a Mw7.5 Wellington Fault Scenario using damage modeling largely produced by RiskScape.[^1]

## Technical Components

RiskScape is a multi-hazard risk assessment tool developed by GNS Science and NIWA that estimates damage and direct losses for assets exposed to natural hazards.[^3] This award-winning technology has been developed by GNS Science, NIWA and the Natural Hazards Commission Toka Tū Ake with software team, Catalyst.[^4] To calculate risk, RiskScape requires the user to provide geospatial data about the hazard, geospatial data about assets that stand to be damaged, and consequence functions that estimate loss based on the characteristics of the hazard and assets.[^4] Outputs may include the probability of building damage, infrastructure damage, injuries to people, or fatalities.[^4] RiskScape is available two ways, via the online Platform and the Engine, where the Engine is standalone with users constructing their own model using their data in a RiskScape pipeline of code, and the Platform is hosted full service with custom end-to-end risk analysis solutions including models and dashboards in a cloud-based online environment.[^4] RiskScape outputs of damage are used to create service outage maps, which are an input to MERIT.[^3][^5] MERIT is a suite of integrated, spatial decision-support tools that estimate the socio-economic consequences across sectors and wellbeings associated with disruption events.[^5] MERIT runs comfortably over a 50-year time horizon, with the first 15-years calibrated against real-world data.[^5]

## Implementation Details

GNS Science's RiskScape and Post Disaster Cities (PDC) teams modelled and mapped infrastructure service outages and restoration after a M7.5 Wellington Fault event, and its associated perils including fault rupture, ground shaking, liquefaction, landslides, lateral spreading, and subsidence.[^2] The infrastructure types included in the modelling process were road, rail, port, airport, electricity, telecommunications, potable water, wastewater, fuel, and gas.[^3] MERIT was developed under the Economics of Resilient Infrastructure research programme and is being extended under Resilience to Nature's Challenges.[^2] The likely scale and timeframe of infrastructure service losses under current investments were compared with that which might occur should further investments be made in infrastructure resilience.[^2] In 2019, the Natural Hazards Commission Toka Tū Ake provided funding to expedite development and make the model fully probabilistic to account for the inherent uncertainty associated with modelling natural hazards.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

With the preferred programme of investments, the MERIT modelling demonstrated a multi-billion-dollar reduction in economic losses and improved community outcomes in the event of a major seismic event in Wellington.[^2] The Natural Hazards Commission Toka Tū Ake are using RiskScape as a replacement for their loss modelling tool due to RiskScape's ability to model a wide range of natural perils.[^4] The Natural Hazards Commission Toka Tū Ake and MBIE funded RiskScape to develop a customised risk modelling dashboard to support the response efforts to Cyclone Gabrielle in 2023.[^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This approach addresses multiple natural hazards including seismic events, liquefaction, landslides, and coastal hazards. In the Wellington Region risk assessment, tsunami was rated as 'Possible' likelihood with 'Catastrophic' consequence, resulting in a risk score of 15 and 'High' risk rating.[^6] The system's ability to model infrastructure service disruptions and economic consequences enables evidence-based decision-making for resilience investments across multiple climate and natural hazard scenarios.

## Sources

# Research Report: Cost of Doing Nothing Assessment Toolbox for Municipal Climate Decisions

## Solution Overview

Vancouver's Climate Change Adaptation Strategy includes an action to generate localized cost-implication estimates from inaction on climate adaptation, using ICLEI's Cost of Doing Nothing toolbox for municipal governments.[^1] In 2024, staff began a Cost of Doing Nothing study in partnership with ICLEI Canada, with results expected in mid-2025.[^3] This study will provide Vancouver-specific estimates of the costs of climate inaction across different sectors and hazards, helping to inform future adaptation investment decisions and build a stronger business case for climate adaptation funding.[^3]

## Technical Components

ICLEI Canada developed the Cost of Doing Nothing toolbox to provide municipalities with guidance on collecting locally-relevant data to weigh the costs of inaction when investing in climate action.[^4] The toolbox includes eight national impact statements that form the basis for the cost of doing nothing assessment used in the municipal template.[^4] A Data Collection Tracking Tool is provided as an editable Excel spreadsheet that suggests potential sources of climate, costs, and impacts data for each of the eight national impact statements, and provides guidance on how best to attain and track the collection of this data.[^4] Appendix A includes a summary table of publicly available national and subnational resources and datasets to support municipalities in understanding and evaluating climate change projections, costs and impacts across sectors and climate change hazards.[^4] The methodology involves analyzing the costs of inaction by identifying the direct and indirect costs of climate impacts on the asset or service.[^6] The approach then analyzes the costs of adaptation by identifying the direct and indirect costs of potential adaptation actions.[^6] The methodology compares the costs of inaction to the costs of adaptation to determine the net benefit or cost of adaptation.[^6]

## Implementation Details

Vancouver is a participating municipality in the Economic Tools to Advance Adaptation project, working with ICLEI Canada to apply the Cost of Doing Nothing report tailored to climate hazards and risks specific to their community.[^2] The study is conducted in partnership with ICLEI Canada.[^3] The ETAA project is funded by Natural Resources Canada's Climate Change Adaptation Program.[^2] The CODN Toolbox was made possible with funding from the Ontario Ministry of Environment, Conservation and Parks alongside financial support from the Government of Canada through the federal Department of Environment and Climate Change Canada as part of the Advancing Adaptation Project.[^4] For this Strategy update, the Pacific Climate Impacts Consortium at the University of Victoria prepared downscaled climate data for the City of Vancouver based on an ensemble of nine global climate models used in the IPCC's most recent AR6 report.[^1]

## Benefits and Impacts

The tool can help understand what the costs of climate inaction are at the local level, and what the distribution of those costs is across different sectors and society.[^1] The equity outcome for the cost estimation action is that improved data will help with making more equity-centred decisions on climate adaptation moving forward.[^1] Following CODN analysis, municipalities identify and prioritize adaptation actions based on the results of the CODN analysis.[^6] The methodology supports developing an implementation plan for the prioritized adaptation actions, including timelines, responsibilities, and funding sources.[^6] The approach integrates the adaptation plan into other municipal plans and processes, such as asset management plans, capital budgets, and community plans.[^6] Comparable implementations demonstrate the value of this approach: the City of Selkirk, Manitoba, undertook a CODN analysis to support their decision-making process for a new wastewater treatment plant.[^6] The analysis found that the cost of inaction, which included the risk of non-compliance with provincial regulations and the potential for nutrient loading to cause algae blooms in Lake Winnipeg, was significantly higher than the cost of building a new, more resilient plant.[^6] The CODN analysis helped to build a strong business case for the new plant and secure funding from the provincial and federal governments.[^6] The City of Windsor completed a technical analysis of 19 possible climate change impacts in November 2019 as part of their climate adaptation planning process using the CODN approach.[^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

Vancouver experienced a devastating heat dome in 2021 followed by atmospheric rivers.[^5] Vancouver's first Climate Change Adaptation Strategy was released in 2012 and renewed in 2018.[^5] This study will provide Vancouver-specific estimates of the costs of climate inaction across different sectors and hazards, helping to inform future adaptation investment decisions and build a stronger business case for climate adaptation funding.[^3]

[^1]: City of Vancouver Climate Change Adaptation Strategy
[^2]: ICLEI Canada ETAA Project Documentation
[^3]: Vancouver Climate Adaptation Progress Report 2024
[^4]: ICLEI Canada Cost of Doing Nothing Toolbox
[^5]: Vancouver Climate Change Adaptation Strategy Introduction
[^6]: ICLEI Canada Cost of Doing Nothing Methodology Guide

## Sources

# Research Report: Integrated Environmental Monitoring and Warning Platform

## Solution Overview

Building upon the existing Huangshi Geological Environment Information Platform Project, this approach represents a comprehensive expansion to integrate multiple environmental monitoring and warning capabilities into a unified system.[^1][^2] The enhanced platform consolidates geological hazards monitoring, meteorological warning systems, urban geology data, and groundwater pollution tracking to provide integrated environmental intelligence for climate adaptation and disaster risk management.[^3][^4]

## Technical Components

The expanded platform integrates four core functional domains to create a comprehensive environmental information system. The technical architecture encompasses geological hazards monitoring capabilities, meteorological warning systems, urban geology data management, and groundwater pollution tracking infrastructure.[^1][^2] This integrated approach builds upon the foundational Huangshi Geological Environment Information Platform Project, extending its original capabilities to support multi-hazard environmental monitoring and early warning functions.[^3][^4]

## Implementation Details

The implementation strategy focuses on functional expansion and enhancement of the existing Huangshi Geological Environment Information Platform Project infrastructure. Development priorities include perfecting current system capabilities while integrating additional modules for geological hazards, meteorological warning, urban geology, and groundwater pollution monitoring.[^1][^2] The platform development approach emphasizes comprehensive environmental information integration to support coordinated monitoring and response across multiple environmental domains.[^3][^4]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

This integrated environmental monitoring system addresses multiple climate-related hazards through coordinated data collection and early warning capabilities. The platform's meteorological warning functions support preparedness for extreme precipitation events, while geological hazards monitoring enables response to climate-intensified landslides and ground instability.[^1][^2] Groundwater pollution tracking and urban geology monitoring provide essential data for managing climate impacts on water resources and urban infrastructure resilience.[^3][^4]

## Sources

# Research Report: Building-Scale Hydrodynamic Flood Modeling with Sea Level Rise Scenarios

## Solution Overview

New York City employs advanced building-scale hydrodynamic modeling to simulate coastal flooding induced by historical hurricanes under various 21st-century sea level rise scenarios.[^2] This approach utilizes computational models that account for nonlinear interactions between rising seas and urban flood dynamics, revealing how floodwater depths and velocities change in ways that cannot be predicted through simple linear addition of sea level rise to baseline flood conditions.[^2] The modeling framework further implements human vulnerability assessments to quantify how physical risks to individuals respond to sea level rise effects on flood hazards across the city's built environment.[^2]

## Technical Components

The building-scale hydrodynamic model conducts numerical experiments to quantify the effects of various sea level rise scenarios on flood hazards throughout New York City's urban landscape.[^2] Model simulations demonstrate that floodwater depth under a sea level rise scenario can substantially differ from depths estimated through linear addition of sea level rise to control-run floodwater depths, due to complex nonlinear interactions within the urban environment.[^2] The modeling framework incorporates projections relative to the Mean Higher High Water tidal datum at The Battery, NYC, based on the 1983-2001 tidal epoch.[^3] Under the RCP8.5 greenhouse gas concentration trajectory, the median projection of 2100 relative sea level around New York City reaches 0.96 m, with the likely range extending from 0.7 to 1.3 m by century's end.[^2]

## Implementation Details

The New York City Panel on Climate Change, a special advisory group of academic and private-sector experts, provides the city with up-to-date science information on future climate change-related risks and adaptation recommendations.[^1] New York City actively employs earlier NPCC results from 2015 as the current scientific basis for the city's ongoing and planned coastal resiliency programs.[^1] City officials are working with the New York City Panel on Climate Change to develop a new set of coastal protection standards for all new and substantially improved infrastructure in the floodplain, based on the latest climate science to ensure critical infrastructure protection against future flood risks.[^4] For planning purposes, the City uses the NPCC's high-end projections for the 2020s and 2050s to ensure preparation for the full range of possible climate futures and that investments made today will protect the city for decades to come.[^4]

## Benefits and Impacts

The hydrodynamic modeling reveals dramatic increases in flood hazard intensity under sea level rise scenarios. Under a 1.04 m sea level rise scenario, the increase in maximum floodwater speed exceeds 2.7 m/s—representing a 1271% increase—in 5% of the area that was flooded under the no-sea level rise control run scenario.[^2] The modeling approach enables quantification of how physical vulnerability of individuals to flooding responds to sea level rise effects on flood hazards, providing critical data for emergency planning and infrastructure protection decisions.[^2] By revealing nonlinear flood dynamics that differ substantially from simple linear projections, the modeling provides more accurate hazard assessments for coastal protection planning.[^2]

## Climate Adaptation Relevance

The enhanced dynamic coastal flood modeling directly addresses New York City's escalating sea level rise and coastal flooding risks. By the 2030s, the 1-year coastal flood is projected to reach 2.5 feet above Mean Higher High Water at The Battery—slightly higher than the 99th percentile of observed monthly high tide floods during the 2000-2020 period of 2.3 feet.[^3] Projections indicate the 1-year coastal flood will reach 3.3 feet above MHHW by the 2060s and 5.4 feet by 2100.[^3] The NPCC projects sea level could rise by an additional 11 to 24 inches by the 2050s, with a high-end projection of up to 31 inches.[^4] Under the Antarctic Rapid Ice Melt upper-end scenario—which incorporates recent ice loss trends, improved ice sheet-ocean-atmosphere modeling, and potential ice sheet destabilization—sea level rise could reach up to 2.1 m by the 2080s and up to 2.9 m by 2100 under high greenhouse gas emissions.[^1] By 2100, the 1% annual chance floodplain could cover one-third of the city's total area under ARIM, with around one-fifth of the area potentially flooded during monthly high tides.[^1]

## Business Analysis

The ARIM scenario depicts an alternative physically credible upper-end, yet very low probability late 21st century sea level rise scenario, which could become more likely over longer time horizons.[^1] This modeling approach supports the city's decision to use high-end projections for infrastructure planning, ensuring that investments made today account for the full range of possible climate futures rather than median scenarios alone.[^4] The development of new coastal protection standards based on building-scale hydrodynamic modeling provides a scientific foundation for regulatory frameworks governing floodplain development and infrastructure improvements.[^4] By quantifying nonlinear flood dynamics and human vulnerability under various sea level rise scenarios, the modeling enables more accurate cost-benefit analyses for coastal protection investments and helps justify expenditures on resilience infrastructure designed to withstand extreme upper-end scenarios.[^2]

## Sources

- [^2]: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0378383921001356

- [^4]: https://a860-gpp.nyc.gov/downloads/1n79h624q?locale=en
